topics.title: Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Basic Concepts # Module 1 — Basic Concepts of Real Time Systems ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > A **Real-Time System (RTS)** is a computer system whose correctness depends not only on the logical results of computation (the *functional* or *logical correctness*) but **also on the physical time at which these results are produced** (the *temporal correctness*). In other words, a real-time system must respond to externally generated stimuli (inputs or events) **within strict, deterministic, and bounded time intervals** known as *deadlines*. Mathematically, if a task $\tau$ produces an output $y$ from an input $x$, then the system is "real-time" only if: $$ y = f(x) \quad \land \quad t_{response} \leq D_{deadline} $$ Where $D_{deadline}$ is the maximum permissible response time. Missing the deadline is treated as a **system failure**, not merely a performance degradation. ## 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy Imagine the **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** of a car. When a driver slams the brake pedal: - The sensor detects wheel lock in a few **milliseconds**. - The Electronic Control Unit (ECU) must release and reapply brake pressure within **5–10 ms**. - If the ECU takes 200 ms (which is "fast" for a normal computer but deadly slow for ABS), the car will skid, and the system is considered to have **failed**, even if it eventually produces the correct braking pattern. A real-time system is like an **air traffic controller** — issuing the right instruction at the wrong time can be just as catastrophic as issuing the wrong instruction. This "timing-correctness" duality is what separates RTS from general-purpose computing. ## 1.3 Distinguishing RTS from Conventional Computing > [!NOTE] > **General Purpose Computer (e.g., Desktop PC):** > Goal → Maximize **average throughput** and minimize *average* response time. Late results are inconvenient but tolerable. > [!NOTE] > **Real Time System (e.g., Flight Controller):** > Goal → Guarantee **worst-case response time** is within bounds. Late results are **catastrophic**, and predictability is more important than raw speed. | Property | Non-Real-Time System | Real-Time System | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Primary Goal | Average throughput | Predictable worst-case latency | | Late Output | Tolerable / Acceptable | System Failure | | Metric of Success | Speed / FLOPS | Determinism / Deadline Adherence | | Example | Web Server, Spreadsheet | Autopilot, Pacemaker | ## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Real-Time Correctness For every output of an RTS, three conditions must be satisfied simultaneously: 1. **Logical Correctness** — The output value must be mathematically correct. 2. **Temporal Correctness** — The output must be produced at (or before) the deadline. 3. **Reliability/Availability** — The system must consistently deliver both over long operational periods (often expressed as a **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures of **>10,000 hours** for safety-critical systems). ## 1.5 Examples of Real-Time Systems in Modern Engineering - **Avionics**: Fly-by-wire systems in Airbus A350, Boeing 787. - **Automotive**: Engine Control Unit (ECU), ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems). - **Medical**: MRI machines, Insulin pumps, Robotic Surgery (Da Vinci system). - **Industrial**: SCADA, PLC-based assembly lines, Robotics in manufacturing. - **Consumer**: Smartphones (audio/video playback buffering), IoT wearables. - **Defence**: Missile guidance, Radar tracking, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs). # Module 1 — Deep Theoretical Analysis ## 2.1 Classification of Real-Time Systems The classification is based on the **severity of consequences** when a deadline is missed. KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this taxonomy heavily. ### 2.1.1 Hard Real-Time System > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Hard Real-Time System** is one where missing a deadline is considered a **catastrophic, total system failure** that may result in loss of life, significant property damage, or irreversible environmental harm. - **Consequence of missed deadline:** Catastrophic (e.g., death, massive financial loss). - **Acceptance criterion:** Deadline miss probability $\leq 10^{-9}$ (for ultra-critical systems). - **Example:** Airbag deployment system, Nuclear reactor shutdown, Pacemaker. ### 2.1.2 Soft Real-Time System > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Soft Real-Time System** is one where missing a deadline causes **performance degradation** but does **not threaten system survival** or safety. The usefulness of the result degrades after the deadline. - **Conformance criterion:** Maximizing the percentage of deadlines met (e.g., 95% or 99%). - **Example:** Video streaming (a dropped frame reduces QoS but the system continues), Airline reservation system. ### 2.1.3 Firm Real-Time System > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Firm Real-Time System** sits between Hard and Soft. Missing a deadline is treated as **zero value** (the late result is discarded), but unlike soft systems, there is **no progressive degradation penalty** — only a binary useful/useless classification. - **Example:** Weather forecasting data that arrives after the forecast broadcast is useless but does no harm. - **Use case:** Financial market data feeds, Stock quote systems. ### 2.1.4 KTU High-Yield Classification Table | System Type | Deadline Miss Impact | Quality Metric | Tolerance Level | Canonical Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Hard Real-Time** | Catastrophic Failure | Zero misses allowed | Extremely Low | Airbag ECU | | **Firm Real-Time** | Result discarded | Statistical acceptance | Moderate | Weather Data Feed | | **Soft Real-Time** | Performance degradation | Average response time | High | Video Streaming | ## 2.2 Three-Layer Reference Model of Real-Time Systems The KTU syllabus references the classic **Three-Layer Reference Model** proposed by Stankovic and Ramamritham. It is foundational for Module 1. 1. **Layer 1 — Environment Layer (Outer World)** - Represents the physical process being monitored/controlled. - Generates stimuli/events. - Receives actuator commands. 2. **Layer 2 — Real-Time Computer System Layer (RCS)** - The hardware + RTOS that responds to stimuli. - Composed of sensors, processors, memory, and actuators. 3. **Layer 3 — Real-Time Software Layer** - The application programs implementing the control logic. > [!NOTE] > **Why this model matters:** It formalizes the feedback loop — the environment is continuously sensed, processed, and actuated upon, all within bounded time. ## 2.3 Characteristics (Features) of Real-Time Systems A KTU-favored list. Examiners frequently ask to "List and explain characteristics of RTS": 1. **Determinism** — The system's response time must be predictable (worst-case bounded). 2. **Responsiveness** — How quickly the system acknowledges an input. 3. **Reliability** — Long-term MTBF typically **>10,000 hours**. 4. **User Control** — Direct application-domain control over system parameters. 5. **Concurrency** — Multiple tasks/threads must be scheduled simultaneously. 6. **Pre-emptability** — Lower priority tasks can be interrupted by higher priority tasks. 7. **Maintainability** — Often modular to allow upgrades without re-validation. 8. **Safety-Critical Awareness** — Fail-safe design with redundancy (Triple Modular Redundancy — TMR). 9. **I/O Latency Bounded** — Sensor reads and actuator writes must complete in bounded time. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Expression | Meaning / Usage | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Deadline Condition** | $t_{response} \leq D$ | Fundamental RTS correctness | seconds (s) | | **Worst-Case Execution Time** | $WCET$ | Maximum computation time | seconds (s) | | **Slack Time** | $Slack = D - WCET$ | Available margin before deadline | seconds (s) | | **CPU Utilization** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Fraction of CPU consumed | dimensionless (0–1) | | **Schedulability (Liu & Layland)** | $U \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | Rate Monotonic bound | dimensionless | | **Hard RT Tolerance** | $P_{miss} \leq 10^{-9}$ | For ultra-critical systems | probability | | **Jitter** | $\Delta t_{jitter}$ | Variation in periodic release times | seconds (s) | | **MTBF** | $\frac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Reliability metric | hours | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Pipe Rule Note:** All absolute values and conditional expressions inside the table have been written using $\vert$ LaTeX commands instead of the literal `\|` symbol to preserve table formatting. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace Engineering:** Flight control software must guarantee actuation deadlines — uses Rate Monotonic Scheduling. - **Automotive:** ECU firmware using OSEK/VDX RTOS standard. - **Medical Devices:** IEC 62304 compliance mandates deterministic software lifecycle. - **Industrial IoT:** Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) standards now extend RTS concepts to Ethernet. ## 2.6 Common Real-Time Task Parameters Each task $\tau_i$ in an RTS is described by a tuple: $$ \tau_i = (\phi_i, \ p_i, \ e_i, \ d_i, \ D_i) $$ Where: - $\phi_i$ — Phase (release offset of first instance) - $p_i$ — Period - $e_i$ — Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) - $d_i$ — Relative Deadline - $D_i$ — Absolute Deadline > [!NOTE] > For a **periodic task**, $p_i = d_i$ is standard. For **aperiodic/sporadic tasks**, $d_i \leq p_i$. # Module 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Utilization Bound This is a **classic 14-mark KTU question**. We derive Liu & Layland's upper bound on CPU utilization for RMS to be schedulable. ### Assumptions - $n$ periodic tasks with no resource contention. - All tasks have $p_i = d_i$ (deadline equals period). - All tasks are independent. - WCET $e_i$ is constant. ### Step 1: Define Total Utilization $$ U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i} $$ This is the fraction of CPU time consumed if each task ran continuously at its worst-case length. ### Step 2: Define the Schedulability Test For RMS (static priority = inverse of period), the **sufficient (not necessary) condition** is: $$ U \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$ ### Step 3: Derivation Sketch (Liu & Layland, 1973) The proof uses the concept of the **critical instant** — the worst-case release time, where all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with task $\tau_i$. Let the worst-case response time $R_i$ of task $\tau_i$ satisfy: $$ R_i = e_i + \sum_{j: p_j < p_i} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{p_j} \right\rceil e_j $$ For the system to be schedulable, $R_i \leq d_i$ for all $i$. The utilization bound is the maximum $U$ such that even in the critical instant, no deadline is missed. Setting $e_i = p_i$ at the limit (worst case): $$ \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693 $$ Hence, RMS can guarantee schedulability up to approximately **69.3%** of CPU utilization for large $n$. ### Step 4: Numerical Example > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example (KTU Pattern):** > Given 3 periodic tasks: > - $\tau_1$: $p_1 = 20$ ms, $e_1 = 4$ ms > - $\tau_2$: $p_2 = 50$ ms, $e_2 = 10$ ms > - $\tau_3$: $p_3 = 100$ ms, $e_3 = 15$ ms Compute the utilization and verify against the RMS bound. #### Step 4.1: Compute Utilization $$ U = \frac{4}{20} + \frac{10}{50} + \frac{15}{100} $$ $$ U = 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.150 = 0.550 $$ #### Step 4.2: Compute RMS Bound for $n = 3$ $$ U_{bound} = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) $$ $$ 2^{1/3} \approx 1.2599 $$ $$ U_{bound} = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 \approx 0.7797 $$ #### Step 4.3: Compare $$ U = 0.550 \quad \text{vs} \quad U_{bound} \approx 0.7797 $$ Since $0.550 < 0.7797$, the task set is **schedulable under RMS**. > [!NOTE] > **Examiners Note:** The bound is *sufficient*, not necessary. Some task sets with $U > 0.7797$ may still be schedulable, but the bound provides a safe analytical guarantee. ## 3.2 Derivation: Slack Time Calculation Slack time represents the maximum time a task can be delayed without missing its deadline. ### Definition $$ S(t) = D - (t - R) - C_{remaining} $$ Where: - $D$ — Absolute deadline - $t$ — Current time - $R$ — Release time - $C_{remaining}$ — Remaining computation ### Step-by-Step Calculation Example A task is released at $t = 0$, has deadline $D = 100$ ms, and has 30 ms of computation remaining at current time $t = 50$ ms. $$ S(50) = 100 - (50 - 0) - 30 = 100 - 50 - 30 = 20 \text{ ms} $$ The scheduler has **20 ms of slack** before the deadline is jeopardized. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: RTS vs. Conventional Systems | Analytical Parameter | General Purpose | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Metric of Interest** | Average throughput | Worst-case latency | Average QoS | | **Predictability** | Low | Absolute | Statistical | | **Hardware Cost** | Moderate | High (specialized) | Moderate | | **Operating System** | GPOS (Linux, Windows) | RTOS (VxWorks, QNX) | Modified GPOS | | **Software Design** | Throughput optimized | Determinism optimized | Hybrid | | **Failure Consequence** | Annoyance | Loss of life | Reduced experience | | **Redundancy** | Optional | Mandatory (Triple Modular Redundancy) | Often optional | # Module 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Three-Layer Reference Model (Mermaid Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Environment Layer
Physical Process
Sensors and Actuators] -->|Stimuli and Events| B[Real Time Computer System Layer
Hardware Processor Memory RTOS] B -->|Processed Data| A C[Real Time Software Layer
Application Programs
Control Logic] -->|Control Commands| B B -->|Sensor Data| C ``` **Explanation of the diagram:** - The **Environment Layer** contains the physical system being controlled (e.g., a robotic arm, chemical plant). - The **Computer System Layer** digitizes sensor signals and routes them to software. - The **Software Layer** runs the deterministic control algorithms. - The arrows form a closed feedback loop, the core of any RTS. ## 4.2 RTS Classification Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD Start[Real Time System] --> Q1{What happens
if deadline missed?} Q1 -->|Catastrophic
Loss of Life| Hard[Hard Real Time
Airbag ECU
Nuclear Control] Q1 -->|Performance Loss
No Safety Risk| Q2{Is the late
result useful?} Q2 -->|No Discarded
Zero Value| Firm[Firm Real Time
Weather Feed
Stock Quotes] Q2 -->|Yes Degraded
QoS| Soft[Soft Real Time
Video Streaming
Reservation System] ``` **Explanation:** This flowchart visually maps the decision logic used to classify any given real-time system. Start from the root, ask "what happens if the deadline is missed?", and traverse down to the appropriate category. ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RTS ```mermaid graph LR subgraph ExternalWorld [External Physical World] Sensor1[Sensor Input A] Sensor2[Sensor Input B] Actuator1[Actuator Output X] end subgraph RTProcessing [Real Time Processing Unit] Digitizer[Analog to Digital Converter] RTOSCore[RTOS Scheduler Kernel] AppTask1[High Priority Task Tau1] AppTask2[Medium Priority Task Tau2] AppTask3[Low Priority Task Tau3] Buffer[Shared Memory Buffer] end Sensor1 --> Digitizer Sensor2 --> Digitizer Digitizer --> Buffer Buffer --> RTOSCore RTOSCore --> AppTask1 RTOSCore --> AppTask2 RTOSCore --> AppTask3 AppTask1 --> Actuator1 AppTask2 --> Actuator1 AppTask3 --> Actuator1 ``` **Explanation of Block Architecture:** - The **External World** block contains all physical I/O devices. - The **RT Processing Unit** houses the RTOS scheduler at its core, which deterministically allocates CPU to tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$ based on priority. - Data flows: Sensors $\rightarrow$ ADC $\rightarrow$ Buffer $\rightarrow$ RTOS Dispatch $\rightarrow$ Tasks $\rightarrow$ Actuators. ## 4.4 Task Lifecycle State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Dormant Dormant --> Ready : Released by Event or Timer Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatches Running --> Ready : Preempted by Higher Priority Task Running --> Blocked : Waiting on I/O or Resource Blocked --> Ready : Resource Available Running --> Completed : Execution Finished Completed --> [*] ``` **Explanation:** Every real-time task passes through these states. The transitions must be time-bounded — for instance, the Ready $\rightarrow$ Running transition (dispatch latency) must have a strict worst-case bound. # Module 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]** ### Question 1 **Q: Define a Real-Time System. Differentiate between Hard and Soft Real-Time Systems with one example each.** **Model Answer:** A Real-Time System is a computer system in which the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical correctness of the output but also on the time at which the output is produced. The output must be generated within a predefined deadline. | Aspect | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Deadline Miss | Catastrophic | Performance degradation | | Example | Airbag Deployment System | Video Streaming | | Design Priority | Predictability | Throughput | | Failure Consequence | Loss of life / equipment | Reduced quality | *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for comparison table.)* ### Question 2 **Q: List any three characteristics of a Real-Time System and briefly explain the concept of Determinism.** **Model Answer:** Three characteristics of an RTS are: 1. **Determinism** — The system must produce the same response within a bounded time for the same input, irrespective of system load. 2. **Responsiveness** — How quickly the system reacts to an input event. 3. **Reliability** — The system must function correctly for extended periods (high MTBF). **Determinism explained:** Determinism refers to the property that the *worst-case execution time* of any task is bounded and predictable. It is the most critical characteristic because it underpins the system's ability to guarantee deadlines. A non-deterministic system cannot be classified as real-time. *(3 marks: 1 mark per characteristic with concise explanation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern, Module 1 Coverage]** ### Question A — Part B Option 1 **Q: (a) [7 Marks] Explain the three-layer reference model of a Real-Time System with a neat diagram. Discuss how it differs from the conventional computing model.** **Model Answer:** The Three-Layer Reference Model proposed by Stankovic (1990) decomposes an RTS into three logical layers: **Layer 1 — Environment (Outer World):** This layer represents the physical process being monitored and controlled. It includes physical equipment such as motors, valves, sensors, and actuators. The environment generates continuous stimuli that the RTS must sense and respond to. **Layer 2 — Real-Time Computer System Layer:** This layer comprises the hardware (CPU, memory, I/O interfaces) and the Real-Time Operating System (RTOS). It serves as the bridge between the physical environment and the software. It digitizes sensor data, manages task scheduling, and dispatches control commands. **Layer 3 — Real-Time Software Layer:** This layer contains the application software that implements the control algorithms. The software is structured as real-time tasks with priorities, deadlines, and periods. **Differences from Conventional Computing:** | Aspect | Conventional Computing | Real-Time Computing | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Primary Concern | Logical correctness | Logical + temporal correctness | | Resource Allocation | Maximize throughput | Guarantee deadlines | | Interaction | Human-driven | Environment-driven (sensor/actuator) | | Operating System | GPOS | RTOS with deterministic scheduling | **Diagram (as per Section 4.1):** A flowchart showing the closed feedback loop between Environment $\leftrightarrow$ Computer System $\leftrightarrow$ Software. *(7 marks: 3 marks for layer explanation, 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for differences.)* --- **(b) [7 Marks] Explain the three types of Real-Time Systems (Hard, Soft, Firm) with two examples each. How does the system design philosophy change across these types?** **Model Answer:** **Hard Real-Time System:** Missing a deadline is catastrophic — it can lead to loss of life or major financial loss. The system must guarantee that all deadlines are met under all possible scenarios, including worst-case CPU load and maximum concurrent events. - *Examples:* Airbag deployment ECU, Fly-by-wire flight control system. **Soft Real-Time System:** Missing a deadline causes performance degradation but does not threaten system safety. The goal is to minimize the average number of late completions. - *Examples:* Video conferencing application, Online ticket reservation. **Firm Real-Time System:** This is a hybrid category where late results have *zero value* (they are discarded) but do not cause harm. The system aims to maximize the proportion of useful results. - *Examples:* Weather forecasting data feed (used for a specific broadcast), Stock market quote system. **Design Philosophy Differences:** | Design Aspect | Hard | Firm | Soft | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Resource Reservation | Static / Reserved | Statistical | Dynamic | | Scheduling Algorithm | Rate Monotonic / Deadline Monotonic | RMS with admission control | EDF / Priority-based | | Worst-Case Bound | Mandatory | Statistical | Best effort | | Failure Handling | Redundant failover (TMR) | Discard late job | Log warning | *(7 marks: 2 marks per type with examples, 3 marks for design philosophy table.)* --- ### Question B — Part B Option 2 (Internal Choice) **Q: (a) [7 Marks] Define the term "Deadline" in the context of Real-Time Systems. Explain the four categories of deadlines with appropriate examples and diagrams.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** A *deadline* is the latest time by which a real-time task must complete its execution. Failure to complete by this time constitutes a deadline miss, with consequences determined by the system's criticality class. **Four Categories of Deadlines:** 1. **Hard Deadline:** Missing it is a *system failure*. The result after the deadline is not only useless but also potentially harmful. - *Example:* The deadline for releasing an airbag is approximately 10–20 ms after collision detection. 2. **Firm Deadline:** Missing it results in the result being *discarded*, but no cascading damage occurs. - *Example:* Real-time stock quote that arrives after the trade decision window is simply discarded. 3. **Soft Deadline:** Missing it causes a *graceful degradation* in service quality, but the late result may still retain partial value. - *Example:* A video frame that arrives 50 ms late still contributes to visual continuity, though with minor jitter. 4. **Impossible / Pseudo Deadline:** A theoretical deadline that cannot be met under any realistic scheduling — used only for academic analysis. **Deadline Timeline Diagram (Text Representation):** ``` Release Time ----[Execution Window]---- Deadline ----(Late) | | t=0 t=D ``` The execution window between release time and deadline must accommodate the worst-case execution time (WCET). *(7 marks: 2 marks for definition, 4 marks for four categories with examples, 1 mark for diagram.)* --- **(b) [7 Marks] "A Real-Time System must be designed for worst-case, not average-case, scenarios." Critically analyze this statement with reference to scheduling, hardware selection, and software architecture.** **Model Answer:** **Critical Analysis:** The statement reflects the foundational principle of Real-Time System design: **determinism over optimization**. **1. Scheduling Implications:** - Average-case scheduling (e.g., minimizing mean turnaround time) is *insufficient* for RTS. - Real-time scheduling algorithms such as **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** and **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** are designed for worst-case guarantees. - Liu & Layland proved that RMS can guarantee schedulability up to **69.3% CPU utilization** in the worst case for large task sets. **2. Hardware Selection Implications:** - Worst-case design forces the use of: - **Faster processors** than average-case demand requires. - **Specialized deterministic hardware** such as FPGAs for ultra-low latency. - **Memory hierarchies** with predictable access times (avoiding caches with non-deterministic hit/miss behavior). - *Trade-off:* Higher hardware cost is justified by safety and predictability. **3. Software Architecture Implications:** - Software must be structured to bound execution time: - Avoid unbounded loops and recursion. - Use *static* rather than dynamic memory allocation. - Implement *resource locking protocols* (e.g., Priority Inheritance Protocol) to bound priority inversion. - Modular architecture with **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** and voting logic is common. **Counter-Argument (Nuanced View):** For purely **soft real-time systems** (e.g., video streaming), average-case optimization may suffice because occasional deadline misses are tolerable. The statement thus holds strictly for *hard* real-time systems and *firm* real-time systems. **Conclusion:** The statement is fundamentally correct for safety-critical RTS, where the cost of a single missed deadline (e.g., $1M in damages or a human life) vastly outweighs the cost of over-engineering for the worst case. *(7 marks: 2 marks for introductory framing, 4 marks for three-area analysis, 1 mark for counter-argument and conclusion.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones for Module 1 — Basic Concepts:** > > 1. **Confusing Hard with Firm Real-Time:** Many students write "Hard = late result is discarded." This is wrong — *Hard* means catastrophic failure, while *Firm* means discarded. This single confusion costs **2 full marks** if asked directly. > > 2. **Forgetting the Time-Correctness Clause:** When defining RTS, students often define it like a generic computer system. The KTU examiner expects the phrase "**within a bounded time**" or "**before a deadline**" explicitly. Missing this costs **1 mark** out of the 3-mark definition question. > > 3. **Three-Layer Model Diagram Mistakes:** Drawing only two layers or omitting the closed-loop arrow. The diagram MUST show bidirectional feedback. Missing arrows = **−1 mark**. > > 4. **Numerical Computation Errors:** When asked to compute RMS utilization, students often use the wrong formula (e.g., $U = C_i \times T_i$ instead of $U = \sum C_i / T_i$). This is an **automatic 0** in numerical parts. > > 5. **Mixing up $p_i$ and $d_i$:** Period and deadline are often equal in standard problems, but if the examiner specifies $d_i < p_i$, the student must use deadline-based scheduling (EDF), not RMS. Read the question carefully. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basic Concepts** - **Real-Time System Definition:** Logical correctness + Temporal correctness. Both are mandatory. - **Three Categories:** Hard (catastrophic), Firm (discarded), Soft (degraded). Memorize one example each. - **Three-Layer Reference Model:** Environment $\leftrightarrow$ Computer System $\leftrightarrow$ Software. Bidirectional arrows. - **Nine Characteristics:** Determinism, Responsiveness, Reliability, User Control, Concurrency, Pre-emptability, Maintainability, Safety, Bounded I/O Latency. - **Task Tuple:** $\tau_i = (\phi_i, p_i, e_i, d_i, D_i)$. Know what each symbol represents. - **WCET vs. BCET:** Worst-Case and Best-Case Execution Time — both matter for design. - **RMS Bound:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. Limit is $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$. - **Slack Time:** $Slack = D - WCET$. Indicates scheduling margin. - **Pillars of RTS:** Logical + Temporal + Reliability. - **Reference Numbers:** Hard RT tolerance is $P_{miss} \leq 10^{-9}$ for safety-critical systems. - **Keywords to associate:** Deadline, Determinism, Priority, Preemption, Predictability, Worst-Case. - **Question patterns expected:** - 3-mark: Define and differentiate Hard/Soft/Firm. - 3-mark: List characteristics and explain one in detail. - 7-mark: Three-layer reference model with diagram and differences from conventional computing. - 7-mark: Numerical on RMS utilization bound. - 14-mark: Combined question covering classification + reference model + deadline types.

topics.title: Applications of Real-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of Real-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-Time System (RTS):** A real-time system is any information processing system that must respond to externally generated input stimuli within a finite and specified time interval (the **deadline**) such that the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical result of the computation but also on the **time at which the result is produced**.

According to the KTU 2024 PECST748 syllabus (Module 1), *Applications of Real-Time Systems* establishes the engineering motivation for studying timing-constrained computing. The applications span mission-critical infrastructure where missing a deadline can result in:
- **Catastrophic loss** of human life
- **Significant economic loss**
- **Severe environmental damage**
- **Degradation of service quality** (acceptable to a tolerated degree)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Conventional Systems:** A non-real-time system aims to maximize throughput; a real-time system aims to **guarantee bounded response time** under all specified loading conditions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Controller" Model

Imagine a busy airport tower. The controller receives radar pings (inputs) about aircraft positions every few seconds. When two aircraft are on a collision course, the controller has only **90 seconds** to issue a new heading. Missing that 90-second window is not merely an inefficient response — it is a **disaster**.

| System Component | Air Traffic Analogy | Computing Reality |
|---|---|---|
| External stimulus | Radar ping of conflicting aircraft | Sensor interrupt / network packet |
| Processing logic | Controller computes new heading | Task / thread executes |
| Output action | Voice instruction to pilot | Actuator signal / display update |
| Deadline | 90 seconds before probable collision | Hard or soft timing constraint |
| Failure consequence | Mid-air collision | System failure, injury, financial loss |

**The Takeaway:** A real-time system is one where "**late**" is just as bad as "**wrong**" — and in safety-critical domains, "late" is often **worse** than "wrong".

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Real-Time Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme evaluates applications using these canonical metrics. Memorize each term verbatim.

- **Response Time ($R_i$):** The time elapsed between the arrival of job $J_i$ and its completion. Expressed in **milliseconds (ms)** or **microseconds ($\mu s$)**.
- **Deadline ($D_i$):** The latest time by which job $J_i$ **must** complete. Hard deadline = absolute; soft deadline = preferred.
- **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET):** The maximum possible execution duration of a task under any legal input — denoted **$C_i$**.
- **Release Time ($r_i$):** The instant at which job $J_i$ becomes available for execution.
- **Period ($T_i$):** The interval between consecutive releases of a periodic task.
- **Jitter:** The deviation of actual event timing from the ideal periodic arrival.
- **Schedulability:** A binary property stating whether all tasks can meet their deadlines.

---

### 1.4 Why Study Applications First?

The KTU 2024 Scheme begins Module 1 with applications to:
1. Build **engineering intuition** for the timing constraints that drive all subsequent theory (scheduling, RMS, EDF).
2. Justify the **taxonomy of hard vs soft vs firm** real-time systems.
3. Establish **domain-specific deadlines** that determine the choice of hardware, OS, and scheduling policy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Real-Time System Applications

KTU Module 1 categorizes real-time applications along **two orthogonal axes**:

**Axis A — Criticality (Consequence of deadline miss):**
- **Hard Real-Time (HRT)** — Missing a deadline is a total system failure.
- **Firm Real-Time** — Late results are discarded; occasional misses degrade QoS but do not cause catastrophe.
- **Soft Real-Time (SRT)** — Utility of a result decreases monotonically after the deadline.

**Axis B — Triggering Pattern:**
- **Periodic** — Tasks recur at regular intervals (e.g., 10 ms sensor read).
- **Aperiodic** — Tasks arrive at random, unbounded times (e.g., emergency interrupt).
- **Sporadic** — Aperiodic tasks with a known minimum inter-arrival time.

---

### 2.2 Detailed Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Stimulus Arrival:** The environment (sensors, user, network) produces an event at time $r_i$.
**Step 2 — Task Dispatch:** The real-time OS scheduler selects this task for execution based on priority.
**Step 3 — Bounded Execution:** The task runs for at most its WCET ($C_i$).
**Step 4 — Deadline Check:** At time $r_i + D_i$, the system verifies completion. If $R_i \leq D_i$, the system is **correct** in the temporal sense.
**Step 5 — Actuator Output:** Result is delivered to the physical world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners test whether students can map each application domain to its correct criticality class. A common trap is misclassifying *medical imaging* as hard real-time (it is typically **soft**), or *anti-lock braking* as soft real-time (it is **firm-to-hard**).

---

### 2.3 Canonical Real-Time Application Domains

#### 2.3.1 Aerospace & Avionics
- **Flight Control Systems (FCS):** Execute control laws every 10–20 ms. Hard real-time.
- **Air Traffic Control (ATC):** Soft-to-firm. Displays aircraft positions with bounded latency.
- **Missile Guidance & UAV Autopilots:** Hard real-time. Loop periods often < 1 ms.
- **Engine Control (FADEC):** Hard real-time, deterministic execution.

#### 2.3.2 Automotive
- **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS):** Hard real-time. Must react within ~10 ms.
- **Electronic Stability Control (ESC):** Hard real-time.
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC):** Firm real-time.
- **In-Vehicle Infotainment (IVI):** Soft real-time.
- **Airbag Deployment (SRS):** Hard real-time. Deployment must occur within ~5 ms of crash detection.

#### 2.3.3 Industrial Automation & Robotics
- **SCADA / DCS systems:** Mix of hard and soft.
- **CNC Machine Control:** Hard real-time.
- **Robotic Assembly Arms:** Hard real-time motion control at 1 kHz rates.
- **PLC-based Manufacturing Lines:** Hard real-time cycle times (often 1–100 ms).

#### 2.3.4 Medical Devices
- **Pacemakers & Implantable Defibrillators:** Hard real-time (life-critical).
- **Patient Monitoring (ICU):** Soft-to-firm.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Hard real-time control loops.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT reconstruction):** Soft real-time (interactive).

#### 2.3.5 Telecommunications & Multimedia
- **Voice over IP (VoIP):** Soft real-time, ~150 ms one-way latency budget.
- **Video Conferencing:** Soft real-time, ~200 ms latency.
- **Network Routers / Switches:** Firm real-time QoS guarantees.
- **5G URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency):** Hard real-time, sub-1 ms.

#### 2.3.6 Embedded Consumer & IoT
- **Smart Home Hubs:** Soft real-time.
- **Drones & Consumer Robotics:** Firm-to-hard real-time.
- **Wearable Health Trackers:** Soft real-time.
- **Smart Grid Energy Management:** Firm real-time.

#### 2.3.7 Defense & Strategic Systems
- **RADAR / SONAR signal processing:** Hard real-time, sustained high bandwidth.
- **Nuclear Reactor Control (e.g., I&C of PWR):** Hard real-time, redundant.
- **Submarine Sonar Arrays:** Hard real-time.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme Real-Time Systems. Master each equation below — they appear in both Part A and Part B questions.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notation | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Response Time | $R_i = F_i - r_i$ | $F_i$ = finish time, $r_i$ = release time (ms) | Measures actual timing behavior |
| 2 | Deadline Constraint | $R_i \leq D_i$ | Boolean / ms | Schedulability condition |
| 3 | Relative Deadline | $D_i = d_i - r_i$ | $d_i$ = absolute deadline (ms) | Converts absolute to relative deadline |
| 4 | Worst-Case Utilization (Utilization Bound) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Used by RMS schedulability test |
| 5 | Liu & Layland Bound (RMS) | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Dimensionless | Sufficient (not necessary) schedulability test |
| 6 | Processor Demand Criterion | $h(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{t + T_i - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor \cdot C_i$ | $t$ in time units | Exact schedulability test for arbitrary deadlines |
| 7 | Hyperperiod | $T_{HP} = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ | Time units | Cycle length over which schedule repeats |
| 8 | Worst-Case Response Time (Exact) | $R_i^{n+1} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{n}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Iterative (ms) | Exact response-time analysis |
| 9 | Deadline Monotonic Priority | $P_i \propto 1/D_i$ | Priority index | Optimal fixed-priority assignment |
| 10 | Jitter Bound | $J_i = \max(R_i) - \min(R_i)$ | Time units | Predictability metric |
| 11 | Throughput | $\Theta = \frac{N_{\text{completed}}}{T_{\text{interval}}}$ | Jobs per second | Measures system capacity |
| 12 | Maximum Latency | $L_{\max} = W_{\max} + C_{\max}$ | ms | Upper bound on interrupt-to-action delay |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When the examiner asks "Is the task set schedulable?", always compute **two things**: (i) Utilization $U$ vs Liu-Layland bound, and (ii) Worst-case response time $R_i$ vs deadline $D_i$. Showing only one earns partial credit.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas underpin:
- **DO-178C certification** in avionics (FAA software standard).
- **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** in automotive functional safety.
- **IEC 61508 SIL-4** in industrial process control.
- **FDA Class III** validation for life-sustaining medical devices.

Engineers use the formulas above to produce **certifiable evidence** that a system will meet its deadlines under worst-case load — a legal and ethical necessity in safety-critical engineering.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Response Time for a Fixed-Priority Task

We derive the classic **Joseph & Pandya (1986) / Audsley et al. (1993)** iterative response-time equation — a high-frequency KTU Module 1–3 problem.

**Setup:** Consider task $\tau_i$ with period $T_i$, WCET $C_i$, and relative deadline $D_i$. It shares the processor with a set $hp(i)$ of higher-priority tasks. We seek $R_i$, the worst-case finishing time of any instance of $\tau_i$.

**Step 1 — Identify the worst-case release instant.** The release of $\tau_i$ at time $r_i$ is immediately followed by the maximum possible interference from higher-priority tasks. Hence:

$$R_i^{(0)} = C_i$$

This is the optimistic starting assumption: only $\tau_i$ runs.

**Step 2 — Account for higher-priority interference.** Each higher-priority task $j \in hp(i)$ executes once every $T_j$ time units. Over an interval of length $R_i$, it executes:

$$\left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \text{ times}$$

Therefore, the total higher-priority interference within $R_i$ is:

$$I_{hp}(R_i) = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

**Step 3 — Construct the response-time recurrence.** The new estimate of $R_i$ equals $\tau_i$'s own execution plus the interference:

$$R_i^{(n+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(n)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

**Step 4 — Iterate to fixed point.** Start with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$. Repeatedly apply the equation until either:
- $R_i^{(n+1)} = R_i^{(n)}$ (convergence → $R_i$ found), OR
- $R_i^{(n+1)} > D_i$ (deadline miss → task set not schedulable).

**Step 5 — Feasibility check.** Task $\tau_i$ is schedulable iff $R_i \leq D_i$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Exam-Style

**Problem:** Given three fixed-priority tasks scheduled under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &: \quad T_1 = 5 \text{ ms}, \quad C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}, \quad D_1 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
\tau_2 &: \quad T_2 = 10 \text{ ms}, \quad C_2 = 2 \text{ ms}, \quad D_2 = 10 \text{ ms} \\
\tau_3 &: \quad T_3 = 15 \text{ ms}, \quad C_3 = 3 \text{ ms}, \quad D_3 = 15 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Determine whether the task set is schedulable using **both** (a) Liu & Layland's bound, and (b) the exact response-time analysis.

---

#### (a) Liu & Layland Utilization Bound

**Compute total utilization:**

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{15}$$

Compute each fraction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C_1}{T_1} &= \frac{1}{5} = 0.2000 \\
\frac{C_2}{T_2} &= \frac{2}{10} = 0.2000 \\
\frac{C_3}{T_3} &= \frac{3}{15} = 0.2000
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum:

$$U = 0.2000 + 0.2000 + 0.2000 = 0.6000$$

**Compute the Liu & Layland sufficient bound for $n = 3$:**

$$U_{\text{bound}} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = 3 \cdot \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right)$$

Evaluate $2^{1/3}$:

$$2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599$$

Therefore:

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

**Comparison:**

$$U = 0.6000 \leq U_{\text{bound}} = 0.7798 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Sufficient test PASSES}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Liu-Layland is only **sufficient**. Passing means *probably* schedulable. To confirm, we must run the exact test.

---

#### (b) Exact Worst-Case Response-Time Analysis

Under RMS, priorities are ordered inversely to periods: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$.

**For $\tau_1$ (highest priority, no higher-priority interference):**

$$R_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}$$

Converges immediately: $R_1 = 1 \text{ ms}$.

Check: $R_1 = 1 \leq D_1 = 5$. ✓ Schedulable.

**For $\tau_2$ (interfered by $\tau_1$):**

$$R_2^{(n+1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(n)}}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1$$

Iteration 0: $R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2 \text{ ms}$.

Iteration 1:
$$R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3 \text{ ms}$$

Iteration 2:
$$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3 \text{ ms}$$

Converged: $R_2 = 3 \text{ ms}$.

Check: $R_2 = 3 \leq D_2 = 10$. ✓ Schedulable.

**For $\tau_3$ (interfered by $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$):**

$$R_3^{(n+1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(n)}}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(n)}}{T_2} \right\rceil \cdot C_2$$

Iteration 0: $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3 \text{ ms}$.

Iteration 1:
$$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}$$

Iteration 2:
$$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$

Iteration 3:
$$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$

Converged: $R_3 = 7 \text{ ms}$.

Check: $R_3 = 7 \leq D_3 = 15$. ✓ Schedulable.

**Final verdict:** The task set is **schedulable** under RMS.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Application Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners often award marks for correct classification.** Use this decision rubric.

| If the application has... | And the consequence of a miss is... | Then it is classified as... | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strict periodic deadlines ($D \leq T$) | Catastrophic (loss of life) | **Hard Real-Time** | Airbag controller, FADEC |
| Sporadic or event-driven tasks | Significant economic loss | **Firm Real-Time** | Network packet forwarding with QoS |
| Periodic but tolerant of jitter | Utility drops monotonically | **Soft Real-Time** | Multimedia streaming, VoIP |
| Hard deadlines with redundancy | Mission failure (not death) | **Hard Real-Time, Fail-Operational** | Aircraft FCS (triplex) |
| Mixed criticality on shared HW | Varies per subsystem | **Mixed-Criticality System (MCS)** | Modern automotive ECU |

---

### 3.4 Sensitivity Analysis — What Happens Under Overload?

Suppose utilization $U \to 1^+$ (system overload). Under RMS:
- The **lowest-priority** task misses its deadline first.
- The miss propagates and triggers **domino-effect deadline failure**.
- Under EDF, the same overload causes **all** tasks to miss proportionally — EDF is **domino-effect resilient**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The Liu-Layland bound $U_{\text{bound}} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$. This is why RMS guarantees at most **69.3%** theoretical utilization on saturated systems, whereas EDF can achieve **100%**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Real-Time System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV[Physical Environment] -->|Stimuli| SENSOR[Sensor Array]
    SENSOR -->|Analog/Digital Signal| ADC[ADC and Signal Conditioning]
    ADC -->|Interrupt| RTOS[Real Time Kernel Scheduler]
    RTOS -->|Dispatch| TASK1[Periodic Control Task]
    RTOS -->|Dispatch| TASK2[Sporadic Event Task]
    RTOS -->|Dispatch| TASK3[Hard Deadline Task]
    TASK1 --> COMPUTE[Computation Engine]
    TASK2 --> COMPUTE
    TASK3 --> COMPUTE
    COMPUTE --> DAC[DAC and Actuator Driver]
    DAC -->|Actuation Signal| ACTUATOR[Actuator Hardware]
    ACTUATOR -->|Physical Effect| ENV
    RTOS -->|Timing Telemetry| MON[Deadline Watchdog]
    MON -->|Violation Alert| FAULT[Fault Handler]
    FAULT -->|Safe State| ENV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows indicate data and control flow. The **Deadline Watchdog** is a hardware timer that raises a non-maskable interrupt (NMI) if any task exceeds its WCET — a critical safety feature in hard real-time designs.

---

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Real-Time System Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Real Time System Applications]
    ROOT --> HRT[Hard Real Time]
    ROOT --> FRT[Firm Real Time]
    ROOT --> SRT[Soft Real Time]
    HRT --> AERO[Aerospace FCS]
    HRT --> ABS[Automotive ABS]
    HRT --> PACEMAKER[Pacemaker and ICD]
    HRT --> NUCLEAR[Nuclear Reactor I and C]
    HRT --> MISSILE[Missile Guidance]
    FRT --> ROUTER[Network Routers]
    FRT --> DRONE[Drone Autopilot]
    FRT --> ACC[Adaptive Cruise Control]
    FRT --> GRID[Smart Grid SCADA]
    SRT --> VOIP[VoIP Telephony]
    SRT --> VIDEO[Video Conferencing]
    SRT --> IVI[In Vehicle Infotainment]
    SRT --> MRI[MRI Reconstruction]
    SRT --> IOT[Smart Home IoT Hub]
```

---

### 4.3 Real-Time Task Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant
    Dormant --> Ready : Release at r_i
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preempted by Higher Priority
    Running --> Blocked : Await Resource or I O
    Blocked --> Ready : Resource Granted
    Running --> Completed : Execution Finished
    Completed --> [*] : Within Deadline
    Running --> DeadlineMiss : R_i exceeds D_i
    DeadlineMiss --> FaultHandler : Trigger Recovery
    FaultHandler --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Significance:** State diagrams appear frequently in 14-mark questions on scheduling. Memorize the transitions: *Release → Ready → Running → Completed* and the failure branch to *Deadline Miss → Fault Handler*.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Real-Time Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Input r t] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    SUM -->|Error e t| CTRL[Real Time Controller Task]
    CTRL -->|Control Signal u t| PLANT[Physical Plant]
    PLANT -->|Output y t| FEEDBACK[Sensor Feedback]
    FEEDBACK --> SUM
    CTRL -.->|Deadline D| WD[Watchdog Timer]
    WD -.->|Reset if Late| FAULT[Safe Stop State]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical **digital control loop** used in flight controllers, ABS, and robotic arms. The deadline $D$ must be **strictly less than** the sampling period $T$ to avoid aliasing and instability.

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define a real-time system. Differentiate between hard and soft real-time systems with two examples each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> A real-time system is a system whose correctness depends not only on the logical result of computation but also on the **time** at which the result is delivered. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Hard Real-Time:** Missing a deadline constitutes total system failure. Examples: *Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Aircraft Flight Control, Pacemaker*. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Soft Real-Time:** Missing a deadline degrades quality of service but does not cause system failure. Examples: *Video streaming, VoIP, In-vehicle infotainment*. *(1 mark)*

| Aircraft Flight Control | Avionics | $\leq 10$–$20$ ms |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Module 1 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the classification of real-time systems based on criticality with neat diagrams. Give **five real-world examples** distributed across the classes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A real-time system must poll three sensors at fixed intervals. Given the following periodic task set under Rate Monotonic Scheduling, determine its schedulability using the **exact response-time analysis**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &: \quad T_1 = 4 \text{ ms}, \quad C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}, \quad D_1 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
\tau_2 &: \quad T_2 = 6 \text{ ms}, \quad C_2 = 1 \text{ ms}, \quad D_2 = 6 \text{ ms} \\
\tau_3 &: \quad T_3 = 8 \text{ ms}, \quad C_3 = 2 \text{ ms}, \quad D_3 = 8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

> **Classification based on criticality:** *(3 marks)*
>
> 1. **Hard Real-Time:** Missing deadline = total failure. *Examples: ABS, Pacemaker, Flight Control, Nuclear I&C, Airbag.* *(2 marks)*
> 2. **Firm Real-Time:** Late results discarded; occasional misses tolerated. *Examples: Network routers with QoS, Smart grid, Adaptive cruise control.* *(1 mark)*
> 3. **Soft Real-Time:** Utility decreases after deadline. *Examples: VoIP, Video streaming, IVI, Smart home hub.* *(1 mark)*
>
> *(Include the classification tree diagram from Section 4.2 to earn full marks — 1 mark)*

---

##### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Assign RMS priorities** (shorter period ⇒ higher priority):
$\tau_1$ (priority 3) > $\tau_2$ (priority 2) > $\tau_3$ (priority 1). *(0.5 mark)*

**Step 2 — Analyze $\tau_1$:** No higher-priority interference.

$$R_1 = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms} \leq D_1 = 4 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

*(0.5 mark)*

**Step 3 — Analyze $\tau_2$:** Interfered by $\tau_1$.

$$R_2^{(n+1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(n)}}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1$$

- $R_2^{(0)} = 1$ ms
- $R_2^{(1)} = 1 + \lceil 1/4 \rceil \cdot 1 = 1 + 1 = 2$ ms
- $R_2^{(2)} = 1 + \lceil 2/4 \rceil \cdot 1 = 1 + 1 = 2$ ms ✓ Converged.

$$R_2 = 2 \text{ ms} \leq D_2 = 6 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Analyze $\tau_3$:** Interfered by $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$.

$$R_3^{(n+1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(n)}}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(n)}}{T_2} \right\rceil \cdot C_2$$

- $R_3^{(0)} = 2$ ms
- $R_3^{(1)} = 2 + \lceil 2/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 2/6 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4$ ms
- $R_3^{(2)} = 2 + \lceil 4/4 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 4/6 \rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4$ ms ✓ Converged.

$$R_3 = 4 \text{ ms} \leq D_3 = 8 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Compute utilization for cross-verification:**

$$U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{2}{8} = 0.250 + 0.167 + 0.250 = 0.667$$

Liu-Layland bound for $n=3$ is $0.7798$. Since $0.667 \leq 0.7798$, sufficient test passes. *(1 mark)*

**Final Answer:** Task set is **schedulable** under RMS. All $R_i \leq D_i$. *(0.5 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical real-time system. Describe the role of the **Deadline Watchdog**, **Scheduler**, and **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** in ensuring timely response. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A patient-monitoring ICU system samples four vital signs at different periodic rates. Classify each as **hard, firm, or soft real-time** and justify your answer with reference to the consequence of missing a deadline:

1. Heart rhythm (ECG) sampled every **2 ms**
2. Blood oxygen (SpO$_2$) sampled every **100 ms**
3. Body temperature sampled every **5 s**
4. Network upload of aggregated vitals to the central nurse station every **1 s**

**(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

> **Block Diagram:** *(2 marks)* — Draw the architecture from Section 4.1 (sensors → ADC → RT kernel → tasks → actuators → environment) with feedback.
>
> **Scheduler:** The component of the RTOS kernel that decides *which ready task runs next* on the CPU. In a hard real-time system, the scheduler must be **deterministic** — the same input must always produce the same schedule within bounded time. Examples: Rate Monotonic, Earliest Deadline First. *(2 marks)*
>
> **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR):** A high-priority handler invoked in response to a hardware interrupt. ISRs must have **bounded latency** (entry-to-first-instruction time) and **bounded execution**. They typically perform minimal work and defer longer processing to a deferred procedure call (DPC) or task. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> **Deadline Watchdog:** A hardware timer that triggers a system reset or safe-state transition if any task fails to "kick" (pet) it within its allocated window. This guarantees the system cannot remain in a non-responsive state beyond a known bound — essential for **fail-safe** behavior. *(1.5 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

| # | Signal | Period | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ECG | 2 ms | **Hard Real-Time** | Missing a beat can cause arrhythmia detection failure → patient harm. *(2 marks)* |
| 2 | SpO$_2$ | 100 ms | **Firm Real-Time** | Late value is discarded; subsequent fresh samples recover, but sustained misses degrade monitoring accuracy. *(1.5 marks)* |
| 3 | Body Temp | 5 s | **Soft Real-Time** | Slow-changing signal; delayed reading is still useful with monotonic utility decay. *(1.5 marks)* |
| 4 | Network Upload | 1 s | **Soft Real-Time** | Delayed display at nurse station degrades UX but no patient harm. *(2 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU PECST748 Exams — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to iterate the response-time equation to convergence.** Many students stop at $R_i^{(1)}$ and lose 2–3 marks. Always iterate until $R_i^{(n+1)} = R_i^{(n)}$ or until $R_i > D_i$.
> 2. **Using Liu-Layland as a *necessary* test.** It is only **sufficient**. A task set with $U > U_{\text{bound}}$ may still be schedulable — you must then perform exact analysis to prove it.
> 3. **Misclassifying hard vs soft real-time.** "Medical imaging" is **soft**, not hard. "ABS" is **hard**, not soft. Examiners specifically test this conceptual boundary.
> 4. **Omitting units.** Always write **ms**, $\mu$s, or **%** explicitly. Missing units = -0.5 to -1 mark.
> 5. **Drawing state diagrams without arrow labels.** Each transition must show its triggering event (release, preempt, complete, deadline miss).
> 6. **Confusing period $T$ with deadline $D$.** When $D < T$, you **must** state this explicitly as a constrained-deadline task.
> 7. **Forgetting the watchdog / fault-handler block** in architecture diagrams. Examiners in Module 1 specifically check for safety-net features.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Applications of Real-Time Systems (KTU Module 1)**

- ☐ **Definition:** A real-time system is one where correctness depends on **both** the result *and* the time of delivery.
- ☐ **Three Criticality Classes:**
  - **Hard** — deadline miss = total failure (ABS, pacemaker, FCS, nuclear I&C, airbag).
  - **Firm** — late result discarded; occasional misses tolerable (routers, smart grid, ACC).
  - **Soft** — utility decreases after deadline (VoIP, video, IVI, IoT).
- ☐ **Three Triggering Patterns:** Periodic, Aperiodic, Sporadic (with minimum inter-arrival).
- ☐ **Core Metrics:** Response time $R_i$, deadline $D_i$, WCET $C_i$, period $T_i$, release $r_i$, jitter $J_i$, utilization $U$.
- ☐ **Key Formulas:**
  - $R_i = F_i - r_i$
  - $U = \sum C_i/T_i$
  - $U_{\text{bound}} = n(2^{1/n}-1)$ for RMS
  - Iterative: $R_i^{(n+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(n)}/T_j \rceil C_j$
- ☐ **Domain Examples Map:** Avionics → hard; Automotive → mixed; Medical → depends on class; Telecom → soft-to-firm; Industrial → hard; Consumer IoT → soft.
- ☐ **Architecture Components:** Sensor → ADC → ISR → Scheduler → Task → DAC → Actuator → Plant → Feedback. Plus **Watchdog** for safety.
- ☐ **Liu-Layland Bound:** Sufficient (not necessary); EDF achieves 100% utilization; RMS max ~69.3%.
- ☐ **Schedulability Workflow:** Compute $U$ → compare with $U_{\text{bound}}$ → if fails, run exact response-time iteration → compare each $R_i$ with $D_i$ → declare feasibility.
- ☐ **Watchdog & Fault Handler:** Mandatory in hard real-time systems; provides bounded-recovery guarantee.
- ☐ **Mixed-Criticality Systems:** Modern automotive and avionics integrate hard + soft on shared hardware — governed by standards like ISO 26262 and DO-178C.
- ☐ **State Diagram Transitions:** Dormant → Ready → Running → Completed; failure branch → DeadlineMiss → FaultHandler.

> **End of Notes — Applications of Real-Time Systems | Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme PECST748**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Model of Real-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Model of Real-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Basic Model of Real-Time Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Real-Time System (RTS)** is a computer system whose correctness depends not only on the **logical results** of computation, but also on the **time at which the results are produced**. Failure to meet a timing constraint is considered a **system failure**, often with catastrophic consequences.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (Burns & Wellings Standard Terminology)

A real-time system is an information-processing system that must respond to externally generated input stimuli within a finite and specified time interval (the **deadline**). According to the widely accepted textbook taxonomy used by KTU-affiliated institutions:

- **Hard Real-Time System** — Missing a deadline causes a **catastrophic system failure** (e.g., aircraft flight control, anti-lock braking).
- **Soft Real-Time System** — Missing a deadline degrades performance but does **not** cause system failure (e.g., video streaming, online transaction processing).
- **Firm Real-Time System** — Late results have **zero utility** after the deadline, but no catastrophic damage (e.g., stock-price quoting systems).

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always cite the deadline type in your answer. Examiners award **2 extra marks** when you classify the system correctly under Burns & Wellings terminology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Controller"

Imagine an Air Traffic Controller monitoring multiple aircraft approaching an airport.

- The controller receives **stimuli** (radar pings, radio calls) — these are *inputs* to the system.
- The controller must **process** these within a strict **deadline** (e.g., issue a landing clearance within 8 seconds).
- The controller uses **dedicated tools** (radar display, radio) — these are the *hardware*.
- The controller follows **standard procedures** — these are the *software / RTOS scheduler*.
- If the controller fails to respond in time, **planes may collide** — this is the *catastrophic consequence of a deadline miss*.

In this analogy:
- **Stimulus** = Aircraft telemetry
- **Response** = Landing/Takeoff clearance
- **Deadline** = 8-second window
- **System Failure** = Mid-air collision

### 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Bold

The following industry-standard metrics are **mandatory vocabulary** for KTU examinations:

- **Release Time (r_i)** — Instant when a job becomes ready for execution.
- **Execution Time (e_i or C_i)** — Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) on the given processor.
- **Deadline (d_i)** — Instant by which the job must complete.
- **Period (T_i or p_i)** — Inter-arrival time of consecutive jobs in a periodic task.
- **Response Time (R_i)** — Time from release to completion.
- **Jitter** — Variation in response time across multiple executions.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 question papers almost always test your understanding of **WCET (C_i)** versus **Average Execution Time**. Remember: in real-time theory, we always plan for the **worst case**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Real-Time System Model

A real-time system model consists of three layered components:

1. **Hardware Layer**
   - Processor (CPU), memory (RAM/ROM), I/O devices, timers, interrupt controllers, sensors, actuators.
   - Must be **deterministic** — bounded interrupt latency, bounded instruction execution time.

2. **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) Layer**
   - **Scheduler** — Decides which ready task runs next.
   - **Dispatcher** — Performs context switch.
   - **Timer Manager** — Maintains system clock and triggers periodic tasks.
   - **Interrupt Handler** — Services asynchronous external events.

3. **Application Layer**
   - **Tasks / Jobs** — Units of work scheduled by the RTOS.
   - Each task belongs to a **task class** (periodic, aperiodic, sporadic).

### 2.2 Task Model — Formal Characterization

According to the **Liu & Layland task model (1973)** — the foundational model referenced in KTU syllabus:

- A **task** $\tau_i$ is a sequence of **jobs** $J_{i,1}, J_{i,2}, \dots, J_{i,k}$.
- Each task has a fixed **period** $T_i$ and a fixed **relative deadline** $D_i$.
- Each job $J_{i,j}$ has parameters: release time $r_{i,j}$, execution time $e_i$, and absolute deadline $d_{i,j}$.

### 2.3 Types of Tasks

| Task Type | Release Pattern | KTU Example | Periodic? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Periodic** | Released at regular intervals $T_i$ | Temperature sensor reading every 10 ms | Yes |
| **Aperiodic** | Released at irregular times | Operator button press | No |
| **Sporadic** | Released irregularly with **minimum inter-arrival time** | Fire alarm trigger (at most once every 30 s) | No (bounded) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** Sporadic tasks are aperiodic tasks with a **minimum inter-arrival bound**. Mentioning this distinction can fetch the **full 7 marks** in long-answer questions.

### 2.4 The Stimulus–Response Model

The **Stimulus-Response Model** (Kopetz & Bauer, 2003 — referenced in KTU reference list) defines an RTS as:

$$
\text{RTS} = \{\text{Stimulus}, \text{Response}, \text{Environment}, \text{Temporal Constraint}\}
$$

- **Stimulus** $\rightarrow$ arrives at time $r$ from the environment.
- **Response** $\rightarrow$ must be produced by absolute deadline $d$.
- **Temporal Constraint** $\rightarrow$ $d - r > 0$ and bounded.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table covers every formula that has appeared in KTU ESE for this topic.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Response Time | $R_i = f_i - r_i$ | $f_i$ = finish time, $r_i$ = release time | seconds |
| 2 | Lateness | $L_i = f_i - d_i$ | $f_i$ = finish time, $d_i$ = absolute deadline | seconds |
| 3 | Tardiness | $\max(0, L_i)$ | Non-negative lateness | seconds |
| 4 | Laxity / Slack | $X_i = d_i - f_i$ | Time remaining before deadline | seconds |
| 5 | Utilization (single task) | $U_i = \dfrac{e_i}{T_i}$ | Fraction of CPU used | dimensionless |
| 6 | Total Utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{e_i}{T_i}$ | Sum across $n$ tasks | dimensionless |
| 7 | Liu–Layland Bound (RMS) | $U \le n \cdot \left( 2^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - 1 \right)$ | $n$ = number of tasks | dimensionless |
| 8 | Deadline Constraint (Hard RT) | $d_i \le T_i$ (implicit) or $d_i \le p_i$ | Hard deadline ≤ period | seconds |
| 9 | Hyperperiod (LCM) | $H = \operatorname{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ | Least common multiple of periods | seconds |
| 10 | Throughput | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{completed jobs}}{\text{time window}}$ | Jobs per second | jobs/s |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the **backbone of every scheduling algorithm** — Rate Monotonic, Earliest Deadline First, Least Laxity First. KTU 2024 examiners expect students to combine formulas (e.g., compute $U$, then verify against the Liu–Layland bound).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive**: Engine Control Units (ECU) use the periodic task model; spark plug firing is a periodic task with $T = 10$ ms.
- **Aerospace**: Flight control laws execute at 250 Hz ($T = 4$ ms) with **hard deadlines**.
- **Industrial Robotics**: Conveyor belt sorting uses **sporadic tasks** with minimum inter-arrival bounds.
- **Medical Devices**: Pacemakers operate on **firm deadlines** — late stimuli have no value but no immediate harm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Liu–Layland Schedulability Bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **14-mark favorite** in KTU Module-1 questions. Memorize it as a complete unit.

**Given:**
- $n$ periodic independent tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n$.
- Each task $\tau_i$ has period $T_i$, execution time $e_i$, and utilization $U_i = \dfrac{e_i}{T_i}$.
- Priorities are assigned **inversely proportional to period** (shorter period $\rightarrow$ higher priority).
- All tasks are scheduled on a **single processor** under **preemptive RMS**.

**To Prove:** A sufficient condition for all deadlines to be met is:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{T_i} \;\le\; n \cdot \left( 2^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - 1 \right)
$$

**Derivation Steps:**

#### Step 1: Define the Critical Instant

The **critical instant** for task $\tau_i$ is the instant at which its **worst-case response time** occurs. Liu & Layland proved this happens when:

$$
r_{i,j} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad r_{k,j} = 0 \;\;\forall \; k \le i
$$

That is, all higher-priority tasks release simultaneously with $\tau_i$.

#### Step 2: Compute Worst-Case Interference

In the interval $[0, T_i)$, the number of jobs of higher-priority task $\tau_k$ that can interfere is:

$$
\left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_k} \right\rceil
$$

Therefore, the **worst-case response time** for $\tau_i$ is bounded by:

$$
R_i \;\le\; \sum_{k=1}^{i} e_k \cdot \left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_k} \right\rceil
$$

#### Step 3: Use the Inequality for Floor Function

Since for any real $x$:

$$
\lceil x \rceil \;\le\; x + 1 - \epsilon
$$

we substitute:

$$
R_i \;\le\; \sum_{k=1}^{i} e_k \cdot \left( \frac{T_i}{T_k} + 1 \right)
$$

#### Step 4: Expand the Summation

$$
R_i \;\le\; \sum_{k=1}^{i} \frac{e_k \cdot T_i}{T_k} \;+\; \sum_{k=1}^{i} e_k
$$

$$
R_i \;\le\; T_i \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{i} \frac{e_k}{T_k} \;+\; \sum_{k=1}^{i} e_k
$$

#### Step 5: Apply the Sufficiency Condition

For schedulability, we need $R_i \le T_i$. Using $T_i = \dfrac{e_i}{U_i}$ and bounding the second sum by the maximum possible (all tasks equal period), Liu & Layland derived:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{i} U_k \;\le\; i \cdot \left( 2^{\tfrac{1}{i}} - 1 \right)
$$

#### Step 6: Generalize to All $n$ Tasks

Since the bound $i \cdot (2^{1/i} - 1)$ is **monotonically decreasing** with $i$, the **tightest sufficient bound** occurs at $i = n$:

$$
U \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{e_k}{T_k} \;\le\; n \cdot \left( 2^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - 1 \right)
$$

> **Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

#### Step 7: Special-Case Numerical Sanity Check

For $n = 1$:

$$
1 \cdot (2^{1/1} - 1) = 1 \cdot (2 - 1) = 1
$$

For $n = 2$:

$$
2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) \approx 2 \cdot (1.414 - 1) \approx 0.828
$$

For $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot \left( 2^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - 1 \right) \;=\; \ln 2 \;\approx\; 0.693
$$

This means even with infinite tasks, **RMS can utilize at most ~69.3 %** of the CPU under the sufficient bound. The remaining **30 %** is reserved for aperiodic/sporadic handling.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Task Models

| Property | Periodic | Aperiodic | Sporadic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arrival pattern | Fixed period $T_i$ | Random | Random (bounded) |
| Minimum inter-arrival | $T_i$ | 0 | Bounded by $T_{\min}$ |
| Deadline type | Usually Hard | Usually Soft | Hard/Firm |
| Example | Sensor sampling | User input | Alarm system |
| RTOS handling | Timer-driven | Event-driven | Event-driven + guarded |
| KTU mark weightage | High | Medium | High |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Computing System Utilization

**Problem:** Consider three periodic tasks on a single-processor system:

| Task $\tau_i$ | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution Time $e_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 20 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 50 | 10 |
| $\tau_3$ | 100 | 15 |

**Step 1: Compute per-task utilization**

$$
U_1 = \frac{e_1}{T_1} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20
$$

$$
U_2 = \frac{e_2}{T_2} = \frac{10}{50} = 0.20
$$

$$
U_3 = \frac{e_3}{T_3} = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15
$$

**Step 2: Compute total utilization**

$$
U = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 0.55
$$

**Step 3: Compare with Liu–Layland bound for $n = 3$**

$$
U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \approx 0.7797
$$

**Step 4: Verify schedulability**

Since $0.55 < 0.7797$, the task set is **schedulable under RMS**. $\checkmark$

**Step 5: Compute hyperperiod**

$$
H = \operatorname{lcm}(20, 50, 100) = 100 \text{ ms}
$$

In every 100 ms window, $\tau_1$ releases 5 jobs, $\tau_2$ releases 2 jobs, $\tau_3$ releases 1 job. Total = 8 jobs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Real-Time System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envA[Environment / Sensors] -->|Stimulus| hwA[Hardware Layer CPU Memory Timers]
    hwA -->|Interrupts| rtosA[RTOS Kernel]
    rtosA -->|Dispatch| tA[Task Scheduler RMS EDF]
    tA -->|Context Switch| appA[Application Layer Periodic Aperiodic Sporadic Tasks]
    appA -->|Computation| hwA
    rtosA -->|Response Output| envA
    hwA -->|Actuator Signal| envA
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Environment** generates **stimuli** sensed by hardware.
- The **RTOS Kernel** arbitrates access to the CPU via the **Scheduler** (e.g., RMS or EDF).
- The **Application Layer** runs the user tasks; their outputs are sent back as **actuator signals**.

### 4.2 State Machine — Job Lifecycle in a Real-Time Task

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Released
    Released --> Ready : Admission Control Pass
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Preempted : Higher Priority Arrives
    Preempted --> Ready : Context Restore
    Running --> Completed : Execution Finished
    Ready --> DeadlineMiss : Deadline Expired
    Running --> DeadlineMiss : Deadline Expired
    Completed --> [*]
    DeadlineMiss --> [*]
```

**State Transition Semantics:**

| From State | To State | Trigger / Event |
|---|---|---|
| Released | Ready | RTOS admits the job after checking capacity |
| Ready | Running | Scheduler dispatches the job |
| Running | Preempted | Higher-priority job arrives |
| Preempted | Ready | Lower-priority job is restored |
| Running | Completed | Job finishes within its $e_i$ time |
| Running | DeadlineMiss | Clock crosses $d_i$ before completion |

### 4.3 Stimulus–Response Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stimA[External Stimulus Arrives at time r] --> procA{RTOS Checks Acceptance Test}
    procA -- Accept --> queueA[Insert into Ready Queue]
    procA -- Reject --> dropA[Dropped Overload Handler]
    queueA --> execA[Scheduler Executes at time s]
    execA --> respA[Response Output at time f]
    respA --> checkA{f less than or equal to d}
    checkA -- Yes --> okA[Deadline Met SUCCESS]
    checkA -- No --> failA[Deadline Miss FAILURE]
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Task Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sysA[Real Time System]
    sysA --> taskA[Task Set]
    taskA --> pTask[Periodic Tasks]
    taskA --> aTask[Aperiodic Tasks]
    taskA --> sTask[Sporadic Tasks]
    pTask --> j1P[Job J1]
    pTask --> j2P[Job J2]
    pTask --> jKP[Job Jk]
    aTask --> j1A[Job J1]
    aTask --> j2A[Job J2]
    sTask --> j1S[Job J1]
    sTask --> j2S[Job J2]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:** Each task $\tau_i$ is a **stream of jobs**. KTU question papers often ask you to draw this hierarchy in the **diagrammatic question** (3 marks).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
Define a real-time system. Differentiate between hard and soft real-time systems with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

> A **Real-Time System** is a computer system whose correctness depends on both the **logical correctness** of results and the **time** at which the results are delivered.
>
> **Hard Real-Time System:** Missing a deadline is a **catastrophic system failure**.
> *Example:* Aircraft anti-collision system, pacemaker.
>
> **Soft Real-Time System:** Missing a deadline **degrades quality** but does not cause failure.
> *Example:* Video conferencing, web page rendering.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for hard/soft with example)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
Explain the following temporal parameters of a real-time job:
(a) Release time
(b) Execution time
(c) Absolute deadline

**Model Answer:**

> (a) **Release time $r_i$:** The instant at which a job becomes available to the scheduler. Before $r_i$, the job cannot execute.
>
> (b) **Execution time $e_i$ (or WCET $C_i$):** The maximum amount of CPU time the job requires to complete on the target processor. Real-time analysis always uses the **worst case**.
>
> (c) **Absolute deadline $d_i$:** The instant by which the job **must** finish execution. The condition $f_i \le d_i$ must hold, where $f_i$ is the actual finish time.

*(3 marks: 1 per parameter)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Stimulus–Response Model** of a real-time system. Describe the components of the model in detail. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **Stimulus–Response Model** characterizes a real-time system as a mapping from environmental stimuli to system responses, governed by temporal constraints.

**Components:**

1. **Environment:** The external world that generates stimuli (e.g., physical sensors, user actions).
2. **Stimulus:** An event arriving at time $r_i$ demanding system action.
3. **Real-Time Computer:** The hardware + RTOS + application that processes stimuli.
4. **Response:** The output produced by the system at time $f_i$.
5. **Temporal Constraint:** The requirement $d_i - r_i \ge \text{required processing interval}$.

```
+----------+      stimulus (r)      +----------------+
|          | ---------------------> |                |
| Environ- |                        |  Real-Time     |
|  ment    | <--------------------- |   Computer     |
|          |     response (f)       |                |
+----------+                        +----------------+
        <----- temporal constraint d ----->
```

**Temporal validity condition:**

$$
f_i \;\le\; d_i
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for component definitions, 2 for temporal formula, 1 for example)*

---

**(b)** Consider three periodic tasks with the following parameters. Determine whether the task set is **schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** using the Liu–Layland bound. *(7 marks)*

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution Time $e_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 30 | 5 |
| $\tau_2$ | 60 | 10 |
| $\tau_3$ | 90 | 12 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Assign RMS priorities (shorter period = higher priority)**

Priority order: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$

**Step 2: Compute per-task utilizations**

$$
U_1 = \frac{5}{30} = 0.1667
$$

$$
U_2 = \frac{10}{60} = 0.1667
$$

$$
U_3 = \frac{12}{90} = 0.1333
$$

**Step 3: Compute total utilization**

$$
U = 0.1667 + 0.1667 + 0.1333 = 0.4667
$$

**Step 4: Compute Liu–Layland bound for $n = 3$**

$$
U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7797
$$

**Step 5: Compare**

$$
U = 0.4667 \;<\; 0.7797 = U_{\text{bound}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set **IS schedulable** under RMS. $\checkmark$

*(7 marks: 1 for priority assignment, 2 for utilizations, 2 for bound calculation, 1 for comparison, 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Liu & Layland task model** in detail. With suitable diagrams, describe **periodic, aperiodic, and sporadic tasks**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Liu & Layland Task Model (1973):**

A **task** $\tau_i$ is a stream of jobs characterized by:

$$
\tau_i = (T_i, e_i, D_i, \phi_i)
$$

where $T_i$ = period, $e_i$ = WCET, $D_i$ = relative deadline, $\phi_i$ = phase.

For a task $\tau_i$, the $k^{\text{th}}$ job $J_{i,k}$ has:

$$
r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) \cdot T_i \quad ; \quad d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i
$$

**Task Types:**

| Type | Diagram | Release |
|---|---|---|
| **Periodic** | —○—○—○—○— | At $0, T, 2T, 3T, \dots$ |
| **Aperiodic** | ——○—————○———○—— | Random, unbounded |
| **Sporadic** | ——○———○——————○— | Random, with min gap $T_{\min}$ |

*(7 marks: 2 for model description, 2 for diagram of each type, 2 for distinguishing properties, 1 for examples)*

---

**(b)** A real-time system has four periodic tasks. Compute the **hyperperiod** and the **total processor utilization**. Comment on schedulability. *(7 marks)*

| Task | $T_i$ (ms) | $e_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 25 | 5 |
| $\tau_2$ | 40 | 8 |
| $\tau_3$ | 80 | 12 |
| $\tau_4$ | 100 | 10 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute utilization per task**

$$
U_1 = \frac{5}{25} = 0.20
$$

$$
U_2 = \frac{8}{40} = 0.20
$$

$$
U_3 = \frac{12}{80} = 0.15
$$

$$
U_4 = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10
$$

**Step 2: Total utilization**

$$
U = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 0.65
$$

**Step 3: Hyperperiod**

$$
H = \operatorname{lcm}(25, 40, 80, 100) = 400 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Liu–Layland bound for $n = 4$**

$$
U_{\text{bound}} = 4 \cdot (2^{1/4} - 1) = 4 \cdot (1.1892 - 1) = 0.7568
$$

**Step 5: Verdict**

Since $0.65 < 0.7568$, the task set is **schedulable under RMS**. $\checkmark$

In every 400 ms window, total jobs released:

- $\tau_1$: $400 / 25 = 16$ jobs
- $\tau_2$: $400 / 40 = 10$ jobs
- $\tau_3$: $400 / 80 = 5$ jobs
- $\tau_4$: $400 / 100 = 4$ jobs

**Total = 35 jobs per hyperperiod.**
