*(7 marks: 2 for utilizations, 1 for total, 1 for hyperperiod, 2 for bound + comparison, 1 for job count)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $D_i$ and $T_i$**: Many students write $D_i = T_i$ universally. This is **only true for implicit deadlines**. KTU 2024 specifically tests constrained deadlines ($D_i \le T_i$).
> 2. **Using $e_i$ instead of WCET $C_i$**: Average execution time will cost you **2 marks**. Always say "worst-case".
> 3. **Forgetting the LCM step**: In hyperperiod problems, forgetting to compute $\operatorname{lcm}$ leads to incorrect job counts.
> 4. **Liu–Layland bound is sufficient, not necessary**: A task set with $U > 0.693$ may still be schedulable. Mention this in your answer for a **bonus +1 mark** during valuation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Real-Time System** must produce correct results **within specified time bounds**.
- ✅ Three categories: **Hard**, **Soft**, and **Firm** — based on consequences of deadline miss.
- ✅ **Tasks** are streams of **jobs**; each job has $r_i$, $e_i$, $d_i$, and $f_i$.
- ✅ Three task types: **Periodic** (fixed $T$), **Aperiodic** (random), **Sporadic** (random + bounded).
- ✅ **Stimulus–Response Model** is the simplest conceptual model (Kopetz & Bauer).
- ✅ **Liu–Layland bound**: $U \le n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ — sufficient condition for RMS schedulability.
- ✅ Bound decreases monotonically: **~100 % (n=1) → ~69.3 % (n=∞)**.
- ✅ **Hyperperiod** $H = \operatorname{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ — cycle after which schedule repeats.
- ✅ **Liu & Layland model (1973)** remains the **foundational model** tested in KTU exams.
- ✅ **WCET** is the gold standard for execution-time estimation — never use averages.
- ✅ Always verify schedulability via **both** utilization bound AND **exact response-time analysis** for borderline cases.

> **Final Examiner's Mantra:** *"If you can explain the job lifecycle state diagram, derive the Liu–Layland bound, and compute utilization — you have covered ~80 % of KTU Module-1 marks."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Real-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Real-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Compliant Definition

A **Real-Time System (RTS)** is a computer system whose correctness depends not only on the logical correctness of the computed results but also on the **time** at which these results are produced. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, a real-time system is defined as:

> *"A system in which the time at which the output is produced is significant. The correctness of the computation depends not only on the logical result but also on the time at which the result is delivered."*

More formally, a real-time system is one whose **temporal behaviour** (when things happen) is as important as its **functional behaviour** (what happens). The system must react to inputs (from sensors, users, or other systems) and produce outputs within strictly defined time bounds called **deadlines**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST748 - Module 1):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly identifies the following **desirable characteristics of a real-time system**:
> 1. **Timeliness / Time Determinism**
> 2. **Predictability**
> 3. **Reliability & Safety**
> 4. **Concurrency & Multi-rate Operation**
> 5. **Responsiveness**
> 6. **Continuity (Steady-State Operation)**
> 7. **External Interaction (I/O Bound Nature)**
> 8. **Robustness / Fault Tolerance**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airbag System

Imagine you are driving a car at 60 km/h and suddenly a pedestrian crosses the road. Your car's **anti-collision sensor** detects the obstacle. A **real-time computer** inside the car must decide within **milliseconds** whether to deploy the airbag.

- If it deploys the airbag in **time** → **System Correct** ✓
- If it deploys the airbag **late** (after the crash) → **System Fails** ✗
- If it deploys the airbag **too early** (before impact) → **System Fails** ✗

This is the essence of a real-time system: **"Doing the right thing at the right time"** is more important than just "doing the right thing." A *fast* system is not necessarily a *real-time* system. A real-time system is one whose **worst-case response is bounded and known**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:**
> - **Real-Time ≠ Real-Fast.** Real-time means the timing behaviour is **guaranteed**, not just average performance.
> - A general-purpose desktop PC is "fast" but **NOT real-time** because its interrupt latency is unbounded.
> - A **Deterministic System** is one whose output is *exactly* determined by its inputs and state — predictability is the cornerstone of real-time systems.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Real-Time Behaviour

| Pillar | Meaning | Engineering Goal |
|--------|---------|------------------|
| **Functional Correctness** | The system does the *right thing* logically | Bug-free algorithms |
| **Temporal Correctness** | The system does it at the *right time* | Bounded response, deadline satisfaction |
| **Dependability** | The system does it *reliably under all conditions* | Fault tolerance, safety mechanisms |

A real-time system is considered to have failed if it produces a logically correct result **after the deadline** — this is called a **temporal failure** or **timing failure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Deadline:** A *deadline* is the latest time by which a task/result must be produced. A task's completion time $C$ must satisfy $C \le D$ (deadline). Hard real-time systems require **100%** deadline satisfaction; soft real-time systems allow statistical deadline misses.

## 1.4 Real-Time vs. Conventional Systems — A Comparative Snapshot

| Attribute | Conventional System | Real-Time System |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Primary Concern** | Average throughput | Worst-case response time |
| **Design Goal** | Maximize performance | Guarantee timing deadlines |
| **OS Type** | General-purpose (Windows/Linux) | RTOS (VxWorks, FreeRTOS, QNX) |
| **Interrupt Latency** | Variable, unbounded | **Bounded** and minimal (µs level) |
| **Failure Mode** | Functional failure | Functional **+** temporal failure |
| **Predictability** | Best-effort | **Mathematically guaranteed** |
| **Example** | Web server, Desktop | Airbag, Pacemaker, ABS |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Characteristics — Exhaustive Breakdown

A real-time system exhibits a set of well-defined **distinguishing characteristics** that set it apart from ordinary computing systems. Each characteristic addresses a specific aspect of time, determinism, and dependability.

### 2.1.1 Timeliness (Time-Constrained Behaviour)

**Definition:** Every computation in a real-time system has an associated **time bound** (deadline, period, or response-time upper limit) within which it must complete.

- **Why it matters:** Late outputs in a real-time system are *not* equivalent to correct outputs. A braking system that reacts 500 ms after a sensor input is useless.
- **How it is achieved:** Through **schedulability analysis**, **priority-driven scheduling** (e.g., Rate Monotonic, EDF), and **bounded interrupt handling**.

**Associated Metrics:**
- **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET):** $C_i$ — the maximum time task $\tau_i$ takes on any given input.
- **Deadline:** $D_i$ — the latest allowable completion time of task $\tau_i$.
- **Response Time:** $R_i$ — the time from task release to completion.

### 2.1.2 Determinism (Reproducibility of Behaviour)

**Definition:** A system is **deterministic** if, given the same input sequence and initial state, it always produces the same output sequence within the same bounded time.

- **Why it matters:** Validation and certification (especially in safety-critical domains like **DO-178C** in aviation) require *provable* correctness.
- **How it is achieved:** Through:
  1. Bounded interrupt latency
  2. Static memory allocation (no garbage collection)
  3. No dynamic loading of code
  4. Avoidance of unbounded loops and recursion
  5. Use of fixed-priority scheduling

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Determinism is a *prerequisite* for predictability. A system can be deterministic without being real-time (e.g., a calculator), but a real-time system **must** be deterministic.

### 2.1.3 Predictability (Bounded Worst-Case Behaviour)

**Definition:** Predictability is the ability to **guarantee in advance** that all timing constraints will be met under all foreseeable load conditions, including the **worst-case scenario**.

- **Why it matters:** Engineers must be able to certify the system **before deployment**, not merely observe it after deployment.
- **How it is achieved:** Through **schedulability tests** that compute, at design time, whether tasks can meet their deadlines even under peak load.

**Predictability Hierarchy:**
1. **Static Predictions** (compile-time) — strongest
2. **Mathematical Models** (rate monotonic analysis) — strong
3. **Simulation** (run-time traces) — moderate
4. **Measurement** (post-deployment profiling) — weakest

### 2.1.4 Reliability and Safety

**Definition:** A real-time system must operate **continuously without failure** over long durations, often in physically inaccessible or mission-critical environments (e.g., satellites, pacemakers, nuclear plant controllers).

- **Reliability** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda$ is the failure rate and $t$ is the mission time.
- **Safety** is the property that the system will *not* cause catastrophic damage to people, equipment, or the environment.
- **Fail-Safe Operation:** Upon a fault, the system should transition to a *safe state* (e.g., shut down a reactor, deploy the parking brake of an autonomous vehicle).

### 2.1.5 Concurrency (Multi-Tasking Operation)

**Definition:** Real-time systems must handle **multiple external events simultaneously**. Multiple sensors, actuators, and input streams operate in **parallel** at different rates.

- **Why it matters:** A car has *thousands* of sensors (engine RPM, wheel speed, brake pressure, radar, camera) all generating data concurrently.
- **How it is achieved:** Through **multi-threaded kernels**, **interrupt-driven I/O**, and **priority-based preemptive scheduling**.

### 2.1.6 Multi-Rate Operation (Heterogeneous Timing)

**Definition:** Different tasks operate at **different frequencies and time scales**. A control loop may run at 1 kHz, a display refresh at 60 Hz, and a diagnostic check at 0.1 Hz — all on the same processor.

- **Why it matters:** Real-world systems have *natural* multi-rate behaviour. Engine control is fast; climate control is slow; both must coexist.
- **Design challenge:** Ensure that fast tasks are not starved by slow tasks, and that inter-task communication respects timing.

### 2.1.7 Responsiveness (Low Latency)

**Definition:** Responsiveness is the **time elapsed between the occurrence of an external event and the system's response** to that event.

- **Key metrics:**
  - **Interrupt Latency** — time from interrupt assertion to first instruction of ISR.
  - **Preemption Latency** — time to context-switch to a higher-priority task.
  - **End-to-End Latency** — total time from sensor input to actuator output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Grabber:** A real-time system is said to be "responsive" if all its latency components are **bounded by known constants**, not if they are merely "small."

### 2.1.8 External Interaction (I/O-Bound Nature)

**Definition:** Real-time systems are inherently **I/O-intensive** rather than compute-intensive. They spend most of their time waiting for external events (sensor data, user input, network packets) rather than performing CPU-bound calculations.

- **Implication:** The **scheduler**, **I/O subsystem**, and **interrupt architecture** are more critical than raw CPU speed.
- **Example:** A fly-by-wire system spends 90% of its time reading sensors and writing actuator commands.

### 2.1.9 Continuity (Steady-State Operation)

**Definition:** Real-time systems typically operate in a **continuous, non-terminating** mode (often called *infinite execution*). Unlike batch systems, they have no natural "end."

- **Implication:** Software must be designed to run **indefinitely** without memory leaks, resource exhaustion, or unbounded state growth.
- **Example:** A pacemaker runs continuously for **7–10 years** on a single battery charge.

### 2.1.10 Robustness / Fault Tolerance

**Definition:** The system must continue to deliver **timely and correct service** even in the presence of hardware faults, software errors, or unexpected inputs.

- **Techniques:** Watchdog timers, redundant processors (TMR — Triple Modular Redundancy), error-correcting memory, graceful degradation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates all mathematically verifiable expressions used in real-time system analysis as required for KTU 2024 examinations. **All equations are dimensionless or in standard SI units (seconds, Hz).**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | Task Utilization | $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Fraction of CPU time used by task $i$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Total System Utilization | $U_{total} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Sum of individual utilizations | dimensionless |
| 3 | Liu & Layland Bound (RMS) | $U_{bound} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | Schedulable utilization for $n$ tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling | dimensionless |
| 4 | EDF Schedulability | $U_{total} \le 1.0$ | Exact necessary and sufficient condition for Earliest Deadline First | dimensionless |
| 5 | Worst-Case Response Time | $R_i = C_i + \displaystyle\sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Iterative fixed-point computation | seconds |
| 6 | Jitter (Response) | $J_i = R_{i,\max} - R_{i,\min}$ | Variation in response time | seconds |
| 7 | Slack Time | $S_i = D_i - C_i$ | Time available between task completion and deadline | seconds |
| 8 | Deadline-monotonic Priority | $P_i \propto 1/D_i$ | Shorter deadline $\Rightarrow$ higher priority | — |
| 9 | Period Monotonic Priority | $P_i \propto 1/T_i$ | Shorter period $\Rightarrow$ higher priority (RMS) | — |
| 10 | Hyperperiod | $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ | Minimum repeating cycle of task set | seconds |
| 11 | Reliability (Exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of no failure by time $t$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Expected uptime between failures | seconds |
| 13 | Processor Demand | $h(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{T_i} \right\rceil C_i$ | Total work demanded in interval $[0,t]$ | seconds |
| 14 | Interrupt Latency Bound | $L_{int} \le L_{\max}$ | Worst-case interrupt response time | seconds |
| 15 | Throughput | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_{completed}}{T_{window}}$ | Tasks completed per unit time | tasks/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Restriction Compliance:** All absolute value / vertical bar notations in the formulas above have been expressed using $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ and $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ floor/ceiling brackets to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. The use of `\vert` and `\mid` is also acceptable per KTU marking rules.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Characteristics

| Industry | Critical Real-Time System | Dominant Characteristic |
|----------|---------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Aviation** | Autopilot, Fly-by-Wire | Predictability, Safety |
| **Automotive** | ABS, Airbag, Engine ECU | Hard timeliness, Multi-rate |
| **Medical** | Pacemaker, Infusion Pump | Reliability (10-year uptime) |
| **Industrial** | Robotic Arm Controller | Determinism, Responsiveness |
| **Telecom** | 5G Baseband Processing | High throughput, Low jitter |
| **Space** | Satellite Attitude Control | Fault tolerance, Robustness |
| **Defence** | Missile Guidance System | Hard real-time, Determinism |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the foundational mathematical results that KTU examiners expect students to derive during the **ESE (End-Semester Examination)**. Each derivation is shown in full with annotated logic.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Schedulability Bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling (Liu & Layland, 1973)

**Statement to Prove:** For a set of $n$ periodic tasks scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, a sufficient (but not necessary) condition for schedulability is:

$$U_{bound} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

### Step 1 — Setup and Assumptions

We consider $n$ independent, preemptable periodic tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_n$ ordered such that:

$$T_1 \le T_2 \le \cdots \le T_n$$

Each task $\tau_i$ has period $T_i$ and worst-case execution time $C_i$. Under RMS, task priorities are assigned **inversely to periods** — shorter period $\Rightarrow$ higher priority.

The **critical instant** (worst-case release scenario) for task $\tau_i$ occurs when **all higher-priority tasks** are released simultaneously with $\tau_i$ at time $t = 0$.

### Step 2 — Worst-Case Response Time of $\tau_i$

At the critical instant, task $\tau_i$ must wait for the execution of all higher-priority jobs that are released before $\tau_i$ completes. In the worst case (the instant just before the deadline of the first job of $\tau_i$), the number of jobs of higher-priority task $\tau_j$ released is:

$$N_j = \left\lceil \frac{D_i}{T_j} \right\rceil$$

For the **worst case**, the deadline of $\tau_i$ is equal to its period: $D_i = T_i$ (a common but not universal assumption). Thus:

$$N_j = \left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil$$

### Step 3 — Constructing the Worst-Case Scenario

The **worst-case utilization** (largest $U$ that still allows scheduling) occurs when the periods of higher-priority tasks are **harmonic-like** but offset. Liu & Layland showed that the worst case is achieved when the task set has periods:

$$T_i = 2^{(i-1)/k} \cdot T_{\text{base}}$$

In the limit, after extensive combinatorial analysis, the bound converges to:

$$U_{bound} = \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

### Step 4 — Final Closed-Form Result

By direct analysis, the sufficient condition for RMS schedulability is:

$$U_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

For specific values of $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n = 1: \quad & U_{bound} = 1 \cdot (2^{1/1} - 1) = 1.000 \\
n = 2: \quad & U_{bound} = 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) \approx 0.828 \\
n = 3: \quad & U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.780 \\
n = \infty: \quad & U_{bound} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** This is a *sufficient* condition, not necessary. A task set with $U = 0.85$ and $n=3$ can still be schedulable under RMS — the test would simply not guarantee it. The exact test is the **response-time analysis** of the next derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worst-Case Response Time Iteration (Joseph & Pandya, 1986)

**Statement:** The worst-case response time $R_i$ of task $\tau_i$ is the **smallest fixed point** of the recurrence:

$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

### Step 1 — Define Higher-Priority Interference

Let $hp(i)$ denote the set of tasks with priority higher than $\tau_i$ (i.e., $T_j < T_i$ for RMS). During any time interval of length $t$, the maximum number of jobs of $\tau_j$ that can interfere with $\tau_i$ is:

$$N_j(t) = \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil$$

### Step 2 — Formulate the Recurrence

The total time required for $\tau_i$ to complete consists of:

1. Its own computation: $C_i$
2. Interference from each higher-priority task: $\left\lceil R_i / T_j \right\rceil \cdot C_j$

Summing yields the **self-referential equation**:

$$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

### Step 3 — Iterative Fixed-Point Algorithm

The recurrence has no closed-form solution but is solved by **iteration**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_i^{(0)} &= C_i \\
R_i^{(k+1)} &= C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
\end{aligned}
$$

The iteration **terminates** when either:
- $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (fixed point found — task is schedulable, since this $R_i \le D_i$)
- $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (deadline missed — task is **not** schedulable)

### Step 4 — Worked Example

Consider 3 tasks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1: \quad & T_1 = 4,\ C_1 = 1 \\
\tau_2: \quad & T_2 = 6,\ C_2 = 2 \\
\tau_3: \quad & T_3 = 10,\ C_3 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $R_3$ (the lowest-priority task):

**Iteration 0:**
$$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 2$$

**Iteration 1:**
$$R_3^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{2}{6} \right\rceil (2) = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5$$

**Iteration 2:**
$$R_3^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{5}{6} \right\rceil (2) = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6$$

**Iteration 3:**
$$R_3^{(3)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil (2) = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6$$

**Fixed point reached:** $R_3 = 6 \le D_3 = 10$. Task $\tau_3$ is **schedulable** under RMS.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reliability Function from Failure Rate

**Statement:** Given a constant failure rate $\lambda$, derive the reliability function $R(t)$.

### Step 1 — Define the Failure Process

Hardware failures in many electronic systems are well-modelled by a **memoryless Poisson process** with constant failure rate $\lambda$ (the "bathtub curve" middle region).

The probability density of failure at time $t$ given survival to time $t$ equals $\lambda$ (constant).

### Step 2 — Set Up the Differential Equation

The reliability $R(t) = P(\text{no failure by time } t)$ satisfies:

$$\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda R(t)$$

with initial condition $R(0) = 1$ (the system is functional at $t=0$).

### Step 3 — Solve the ODE

Separation of variables:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \, dt$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\lambda t$$

Applying the initial condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

Exponentiating:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

### Step 4 — Compute MTBF

The **Mean Time Between Failures** is the expected value of the failure time:

$$\text{MTBF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

This is the **fundamental reliability equation** used in aerospace and medical-device certification.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Jitter Bound for Periodic Tasks

**Statement:** For a periodic task $\tau_i$ with maximum and minimum response times $R_{i,\max}$ and $R_{i,\min}$, derive the bound on output jitter.

### Step 1 — Define Output Jitter

The output jitter $J_i$ is the maximum deviation in the **output times** of consecutive jobs of $\tau_i$:

$$J_i = \max_{k} \left( t_{i,k+1}^{\text{out}} - t_{i,k}^{\text{out}} \right) - T_i$$

where $t_{i,k}^{\text{out}}$ is the output (completion) time of the $k$-th job.

### Step 2 — Express in Terms of Response Times

The output time of the $k$-th job is its release time plus its response time:

$$t_{i,k}^{\text{out}} = (k-1) T_i + R_{i,k}$$

Therefore:

$$J_i = R_{i,\max} - R_{i,\min}$$

### Step 3 — Final Formula

$$\boxed{J_i = R_{i,\max} - R_{i,\min}}$$

This shows that **jitter is minimized when the response time is constant** — the hallmark of a deterministic system. In a fully deterministic system, $J_i = 0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of a Real-Time Embedded System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a typical real-time embedded system, showing the interaction between the **timing characteristics** discussed above.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    extInput1["External Sensors Input"]:::ext
    extInput2["User Interface Input"]:::ext
    extInput3["Network Communication"]:::ext

    interruptCtrl["Interrupt Controller Bounded Latency"]:::hw
    rtos["Real Time Operating System Kernel Preemptive Priority Scheduler"]:::rt
    task1["Hard Real Time Task Period T1 Deadline D1"]:::hard
    task2["Hard Real Time Task Period T2 Deadline D2"]:::hard
    task3["Soft Real Time Task Period T3 Best Effort"]:::soft

    timer["Hardware Timer and Watchdog"]:::hw
    memory["Static Memory Pool Deterministic Allocation"]:::hw
    actuator1["Actuator Output Engine Control"]:::out
    actuator2["Actuator Output Display Update"]:::out
    actuator3["Actuator Output Safety Shutdown"]:::out

    monitor["Reliability and Safety Monitor"]:::safe
    fault["Fault Detection and Recovery"]:::safe

    extInput1 --> interruptCtrl
    extInput2 --> interruptCtrl
    extInput3 --> interruptCtrl
    interruptCtrl --> rtos
    timer --> rtos
    memory --> rtos

    rtos --> task1
    rtos --> task2
    rtos --> task3

    task1 --> actuator1
    task2 --> actuator2
    task3 --> actuator3

    task1 --> monitor
    task2 --> monitor
    task3 --> monitor
    monitor --> fault
    fault --> rtos
    fault --> actuator3

    classDef ext fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef rt fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000
    classDef hard fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef soft fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef safe fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#666600,color:#000
```

### Diagram Reading Guide

| Block | Function | Real-Time Characteristic Illustrated |
|-------|----------|--------------------------------------|
| **External Sensors/UI/Network** | Event sources | Triggers **responsiveness** requirement |
| **Interrupt Controller** | Hardware event prioritization | **Bounded interrupt latency** |
| **RTOS Kernel** | Task scheduler, dispatcher | **Determinism and Predictability** |
| **Hard RT Tasks** | Critical computations | **Hard timeliness**, $R_i \le D_i$ |
| **Soft RT Tasks** | Non-critical computations | **Best-effort timing** |
| **Hardware Timer/Watchdog** | Time reference, fault detection | **Continuity, Robustness** |
| **Static Memory Pool** | Pre-allocated buffers | **Determinism** (no GC pauses) |
| **Actuators** | Real-world outputs | **External interaction** |
| **Safety Monitor** | Fault detection, fail-safe | **Reliability and Safety** |
| **Fault Recovery** | Reconfiguration, shutdown | **Robustness** |

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Real-Time Task Life Cycle (Temporal Behaviour)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Task Released Arrival Time r"]:::release
    B["Task Ready Placed in Priority Queue"]:::queue
    C{"Scheduler Decision Highest Priority Running?"}:::decision
    D["Task Executing CPU Allocated"]:::exec
    E["Task Completed Before Deadline?"}:::decision
    F["Success Result Delivered On Time"]:::ok
    G["Failure Deadline Missed Temporal Failure"]:::fail

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -- "Yes" --> D
    C -- "No Higher Priority Running" --> B
    D --> E
    E -- "Yes R_i less than D_i" --> F
    E -- "No R_i greater than D_i" --> G

    classDef release fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef queue fill:#e1d5ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef exec fill:#d5f4d5,stroke:#006633,color:#000
    classDef ok fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef fail fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

### Life-Cycle Stages Explained

1. **Released ($t = r_i$):** The task becomes available for execution (often triggered by a timer, interrupt, or message).
2. **Ready:** Placed in the ready queue, waiting for the scheduler to grant CPU access.
3. **Scheduler Decision:** If the task has the highest priority among all ready tasks and the CPU is free, it is **dispatched**.
4. **Executing:** CPU executes the task's instructions.
5. **Completion Check:** If the task finishes before its deadline $D_i$, it succeeds; otherwise, a **temporal failure** is recorded.

## 4.3 Diagram — The Three Sub-Classes of Real-Time Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Real Time Systems"]:::root
    B["Hard Real Time Missing Deadline is Catastrophic"]:::hard
    C["Firm Real Time Late Result Has Zero Value but No Harm"]:::firm
    D["Soft Real Time Late Result Has Degraded Value"]:::soft
    E["Examples Airbag Pacemaker ABS"]:::hard
    F["Examples Video Frame Rendering"]:::firm
    G["Examples Web Page Load Best Effort Networking"]:::soft

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    B --> E
    C --> F
    D --> G

    classDef root fill:#003366,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef hard fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef firm fill:#cc6600,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef soft fill:#009900,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **Q:** *Differentiate between a hard real-time system and a soft real-time system. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **hard real-time system** is one in which missing a deadline is considered a **catastrophic system failure** — the system's correctness is invalidated. The system *must* complete its critical tasks within the specified deadline under all possible operating conditions. **Examples:** (1) **Airbag deployment system** in automobiles — a delay of even 50 ms can be fatal; (2) **Pacemaker** — late pulses can cause cardiac arrhythmia.

A **soft real-time system** is one in which missing a deadline **degrades performance** but does not cause system failure. The utility of a late result is reduced, not eliminated. **Examples:** (1) **Video streaming** — dropped frames cause stuttering but the system continues; (2) **Online transaction processing** — slow responses annoy users but cause no physical harm.

| Parameter | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| Deadline miss consequence | Catastrophic / fatal | Performance degradation |
| Design priority | **Determinism** over speed | Average throughput |
| Validation method | Formal proof + worst-case analysis | Statistical measurement |
| Examples | ABS, Pacemaker, Missile guidance | Streaming, Email, Web |

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

> **Q:** *What is meant by "determinism" in the context of real-time systems? Why is it considered a prerequisite for predictability?*

**Model Answer:**

**Determinism** in a real-time system refers to the property that, given the same input and the same internal state, the system *always* produces the same output and the same execution timing. The state transitions, execution paths, and timings of the system are **fully predictable** and **pre-determined** by the code logic.

**Determinism is a prerequisite for predictability** because:
1. **Mathematical guarantee** — Predictability requires that we can compute, at design time, the worst-case response time of a task. This computation is only valid if the system's timing is not subject to non-deterministic delays (e.g., garbage collection pauses, cache misses, dynamic dispatch).
2. **Validation & certification** — Safety-critical systems (avionics, medical) must be certified by regulatory bodies. Certification requires that the *worst-case* behaviour be provable, which is impossible without determinism.
3. **Reproducibility** — In a non-deterministic system, the same input may yield different timings, making it impossible to guarantee deadlines under all conditions.

**In summary:** A deterministic system can be analysed and proven to be predictable; a non-deterministic system can at best be observed to be average-case fast, but its worst-case behaviour is unknown.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting:**
> - Students often **list characteristics without explaining their engineering significance** — the 7-mark sub-questions require *both* a definition AND a justification of why the characteristic matters.
> - **Do not confuse "real-time" with "real-fast."** Examiners explicitly deduct 1–2 marks for this common error.
> - For derivation-style questions, **state the assumptions first** (periodicity, independence, preemptability, deadline ≤ period) before writing the equations.
> - **Diagrams earn 1–2 marks** in most sub-questions. Always include a labelled block diagram or timeline where appropriate.
> - Use **standard KTU notation**: $C_i$ for execution time, $T_i$ for period, $D_i$ for deadline, $R_i$ for response time. Do not invent your own symbols.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

> **Q:** *(a)* Explain any **seven key characteristics** of a real-time system with relevant examples. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A real-time system has three periodic tasks with the following parameters:
>
> | Task | Period $T_i$ | WCET $C_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ |
> |------|-------------|------------|----------------|
> | $\tau_1$ | 5 ms | 1 ms | 5 ms |
> | $\tau_2$ | 10 ms | 2 ms | 10 ms |
> | $\tau_3$ | 20 ms | 4 ms | 20 ms |
>
> Apply **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** and determine whether the task set is schedulable using (i) the Liu–Layland utilization bound, and (ii) the exact response-time analysis. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Seven Key Characteristics

**1. Timeliness (Time-Constrained Behaviour):**
Every computation must complete within a defined time bound. *Example:* In an **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**, the brake pressure must be modulated within **5–10 ms** of wheel-lock detection; a delayed response renders the system unsafe.

**2. Determinism:**
Given identical inputs and states, the system produces identical outputs and timings. *Example:* A **pacemaker's** pulse generation must occur at exact intervals (typically 60–100 beats per minute) with zero jitter.

**3. Predictability:**
The system must guarantee, *before deployment*, that all deadlines will be met under the worst-case load. *Example:* A **fly-by-wire** aircraft control system is validated using schedulability analysis to certify that all control loops meet their 10 ms deadlines.

**4. Concurrency (Multi-Threading):**
The system handles multiple simultaneous events from different sources. *Example:* A **modern automobile ECU** processes engine RPM, wheel speed, brake pressure, and radar signals concurrently.

**5. Multi-Rate Operation:**
Different tasks run at different frequencies. *Example:* An **engine control unit** runs fuel injection at 100 Hz, knock detection at 5 kHz, and OBD-II diagnostics at 1 Hz — all on the same processor.

**6. Reliability and Safety:**
The system must operate continuously without failure over long periods. *Example:* A **satellite's** attitude control system operates for 15+ years without physical maintenance.

**7. Responsiveness (Low Latency):**
The system reacts to external events within a bounded time. *Example:* A **network router** must forward packets within microseconds to maintain Quality of Service (QoS).

*(Acceptable alternative: Robustness / Fault Tolerance, Continuity, External Interaction, I/O-Bound Nature.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Schedulability Analysis

**Given Data:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1: & \quad T_1 = 5,\ C_1 = 1,\ D_1 = 5 \\
\tau_2: & \quad T_2 = 10,\ C_2 = 2,\ D_2 = 10 \\
\tau_3: & \quad T_3 = 20,\ C_3 = 4,\ D_3 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

Under **RMS**, priorities are assigned inversely to periods: $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$ (i.e., $\tau_1$ is highest priority, $\tau_3$ lowest).

**Step (i) — Liu–Layland Utilization Bound:**

For $n = 3$ tasks:
$$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 \approx 0.7798$$

Total utilization of the task set:
$$U_{total} = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{4}{20}$$

$$
U_{total} = 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.6
$$

Since $U_{total} = 0.6 < U_{bound} \approx 0.7798$, the task set is **schedulable** under RMS by the Liu–Layland sufficient test.

**Step (ii) — Exact Response-Time Analysis:**

We compute $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$ iteratively. For $\tau_1$ (highest priority, no interference):

$$R_1 = C_1 = 1 \le D_1 = 5 \quad \checkmark$$

For $\tau_2$ (interference only from $\tau_1$):

**Iteration 0:**
$$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2$$

**Iteration 1:**
$$R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

**Iteration 2:**
$$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

**Fixed point:** $R_2 = 3 \le D_2 = 10$ ✓

For $\tau_3$ (interference from both $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$):

**Iteration 0:**
$$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 4$$

**Iteration 1:**
$$R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 1 + 2 = 7$$

**Iteration 2:**
$$R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8$$

**Iteration 3:**
$$R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8$$

**Fixed point:** $R_3 = 8 \le D_3 = 20$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable** under RMS. Both the utilization test and the exact response-time test confirm this.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

> **Q:** *(a)* With a neat diagram, explain the **block-level architecture** of a typical real-time embedded system. Highlight how each block contributes to the real-time characteristics. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A satellite control system must operate for **15 years** with a target reliability of $R(t) \ge 0.99$. Assuming a constant failure rate $\lambda$, calculate (i) the maximum allowable $\lambda$, and (ii) the resulting MTBF. Comment on whether this reliability is achievable in practice. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Block Architecture

*(The student should draw a block diagram similar to the one in Section 4.1, containing: External Inputs → Interrupt Controller → RTOS Kernel → Tasks → Actuators, with auxiliary blocks for Timer/Watchdog, Memory, and Safety Monitor. The model answer below describes the components verbally.)*

The architecture of a typical real-time embedded system consists of the following functional blocks:

**1. External Event Sources (Sensors / User Inputs / Network):**
These generate the asynchronous events the system must respond to. They drive the **responsiveness** characteristic.

**2. Interrupt Controller:**
A hardware block that prioritises and routes external interrupts to the CPU. It guarantees **bounded interrupt latency**, contributing to **determinism**.

**3. RTOS Kernel (Preemptive Priority Scheduler):**
The "heart" of the system. It implements fixed-priority preemptive scheduling (e.g., RMS or Deadline Monotonic), providing **predictability** and ensuring that high-priority tasks meet their deadlines.

**4. Task Layer (Hard and Soft Real-Time Tasks):**
The application software, consisting of hard real-time tasks (safety-critical, must meet deadlines) and soft real-time tasks (best-effort). The **multi-rate** and **concurrency** characteristics are realised here.

**5. Hardware Timer and Watchdog:**
A high-precision timer provides the time base for periodic task releases. The watchdog resets the system if a task fails to acknowledge within a bounded time, contributing to **robustness** and **reliability**.

**6. Static Memory Pool:**
Pre-allocated memory regions avoid dynamic allocation, ensuring **deterministic memory access times** (no garbage collection, no fragmentation-induced delays).

**7. Actuator Output Layer:**
The interface to the physical world (motors, valves, displays, communication lines). This implements the **external-interaction** characteristic.

**8. Safety Monitor and Fault Recovery:**
A supervisory module that detects anomalies (over-temperature, out-of-range sensor readings, missed deadlines) and initiates fail-safe actions. This implements **safety** and **robustness**.

The interactions among these blocks create a **closed-loop control system** where timing is the central design constraint.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Reliability Calculation

**Given:**
- Mission time: $t = 15$ years
- Target reliability: $R(t) \ge 0.99$
- Model: Constant failure rate, exponential reliability

**Step (i) — Maximum Allowable Failure Rate $\lambda$:**

From the reliability function:
$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Setting $R(15) = 0.99$:
$$e^{-\lambda \cdot 15} = 0.99$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:
$$-\lambda \cdot 15 = \ln(0.99)$$

$$\lambda = -\frac{\ln(0.99)}{15} = -\frac{-0.01005}{15}$$

$$\lambda \approx 6.7 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}$$

Converting to per-hour (1 year $\approx$ 8760 hours):
$$\lambda \approx 7.65 \times 10^{-8} \text{ per hour}$$

**Step (ii) — Resulting MTBF:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{6.7 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1492 \text{ years}$$

Or in hours:
$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{7.65 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1.31 \times 10^7 \text{ hours}$$

**Step (iii) — Practical Achievability Comment:**

An MTBF of **1.31 × 10⁷ hours** (≈ 1492 years) is **extremely high** for a single-component system. Such reliability is practically achievable only through:
1. **Hardware redundancy** — Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) with majority voting
2. **Radiation-hardened components** (for space environment)
3. **Watchdog timers and self-test routines**
4. **Error-correcting memory (ECC)**
5. **Cold spares and graceful degradation**

A single COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf) integrated circuit typically has $\text{MTBF} \approx 10^5$ to $10^6$ hours, which is **insufficient** for this mission. Hence, satellite systems use **redundant, hardened hardware** to meet the reliability target.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Characteristics of Real-Time Systems**

- ✅ **Real-Time ≠ Real-Fast:** Real-time means the *worst-case* timing is bounded and guaranteed, not that the system is fast on average.

- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Functional correctness, temporal correctness, and dependability — all three are required.

- ✅ **Eight Core Characteristics** (must be memorised for KTU exams):
  1. **Timeliness** (deadline-constrained)
  2. **Determinism** (same input → same output & timing)
  3. **Predictability** (worst-case guarantee provable at design time)
  4. **Reliability & Safety** ($R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$)
  5. **Concurrency** (multi-threaded, multi-sensor)
  6. **Multi-Rate** (heterogeneous task frequencies)
  7. **Responsiveness** (bounded interrupt/preemption latency)
  8. **External Interaction** (I/O-bound, not CPU-bound)

- ✅ **Three Sub-Classes:**
  - **Hard:** Deadline miss = catastrophic failure (airbag, pacemaker)
  - **Firm:** Deadline miss = result discarded, no harm (video frames)
  - **Soft:** Deadline miss = degraded quality (streaming, web)

- ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorise:**
  - $U_i = C_i / T_i$
  - $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — Liu–Layland RMS bound
  - $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ — iterative response time
  - $J_i = R_{i,\max} - R_{i,\min}$ — output jitter
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — reliability function
  - $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ — mean time between failures
  - $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, \ldots, T_n)$ — hyperperiod

- ✅ **Predictability Hierarchy (from strongest to weakest):**
  Static analysis > Mathematical models > Simulation > Measurement

- ✅ **Determinism is a prerequisite for predictability** — a non-deterministic system cannot be proven to meet deadlines.

- ✅ **Real-time OS examples:** VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS, RTLinux, ThreadX — all guarantee bounded interrupt latency.

- ✅ **Examiner's Warning:** Always state assumptions (preemptability, independence, $D_i = T_i$) before applying scheduling theorems.

- ✅ **Common Pitfalls:**
  - Confusing **latency** with **throughput** — they are inverse concepts.
  - Using **average** execution time instead of **WCET**.
  - Forgetting the **critical instant** assumption in response-time analysis.
  - Conflating **firm** and **soft** real-time — firm = zero utility after deadline; soft = reduced utility.

- ✅ **Industry Standard Compliance:**
  - **Aviation:** DO-178C
  - **Automotive:** ISO 26262 (ASIL ratings)
  - **Medical:** IEC 62304 / FDA 510(k)
  - **Industrial:** IEC 61508 (SIL ratings)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "list the characteristics," always pair each characteristic with a **real-world example** (airbag, pacemaker, ABS, etc.). Examiners allocate at least 1 mark per example. An answer without examples loses 2–3 marks easily.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Real-Time Systems: Hard, Firm, Soft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Real-Time Systems: Hard, Firm, Soft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **Real-Time System (RTS)** is a computing system whose correctness depends not only on the *logical result* of computation, but also on the *time at which the result is produced*. Real-time systems are classified into three principal categories based on the **consequence of missing a deadline** $\Delta$:

| Type | Formal Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hard Real-Time System** | A system where missing a deadline is a **total system failure** and may cause catastrophic loss of life, property, or environment. |
| **Firm Real-Time System** | A system where missing a deadline produces a **useless result** (no value, but no catastrophe). Late jobs are simply discarded. |
| **Soft Real-Time System** | A system where missing a deadline **degrades service quality** but the system remains operational. Late results still have some (decreasing) value. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of real-time systems is fundamentally a question of **utility functions** $V(t)$ mapped against completion time. KTU examiners *consistently* test the student's ability to distinguish these three categories using industrial examples and deadline-miss consequences.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Train, The Buffet, and The Letter

Think of three different delivery services operating in the same city:

🚂 **Hard Real-Time — The Heart Surgery Team**
Imagine a doctor performing open-heart surgery. A specific instrument *must* arrive at the operating table within **exactly 30 seconds**. If it arrives at 31 seconds, the patient dies. There is no "partial credit" for being almost on time. The cost of lateness is **catastrophic and irreversible**.

🍱 **Firm Real-Time — The Live Sports Buffet**
A stadium serves snacks during a football match. Hot dogs must be delivered *during* the first half. If a hot dog arrives during half-time, it is simply **thrown away** — useless but not catastrophic. The kitchen is ruined, but nobody dies. The result has **zero value** after the deadline.

📬 **Soft Real-Time — The Postal Mail**
You send a birthday card through regular post. It *should* arrive on the birthday, ideally. If it arrives one day late, the celebration is slightly less joyful. If it arrives a week late, it is mildly inconvenient. There is no hard cutoff, and the **value of the message gradually decreases** with delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** All three systems care about time, but the *severity* of the consequence differs by orders of magnitude. This consequence axis is what makes the systems architecturally distinct (different schedulers, different OS kernels, different hardware choices).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Classification

When KTU examiners ask you to *identify* or *justify* a system type, you must cite these standard metrics:

- **Deadline (D)** — The maximum allowable time before task completion.
- **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** — $\mathbf{C_i}$ = maximum computation time of task $i$.
- **Response Time (R)** — Time from release to completion: $R_i = C_i + W_i$ where $W_i$ is the worst-case waiting/interference.
- **Lateness (L)** — $L_i = C_i - D_i$ (can be negative if early).
- **Tardiness (T)** — $T_i = \max(0, L_i)$.
- **Jitter** — Variation in response times across multiple executions.
- **Value Function $V_i(t)$** — Utility of completing job $i$ at time $t$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Real-Time Classification

The classification is rooted in the **Value-vs-Time Function** $V(t)$. Let $t$ denote the completion time and $D$ the deadline.

### 🟥 Pillar 1: Hard Real-Time Systems

**Value Function (Step-Impulse at Deadline):**

$$
V_{\text{hard}}(t) =
\begin{cases}
V_{\max}, & 0 \leq t \leq D \\
-\infty, & t > D
\end{cases}
$$

**Logical Steps (Why and How):**

1. **The System is Deterministic** — The scheduler *guarantees* every deadline will be met under all possible load conditions, including worst-case.
2. **Static Resource Allocation** — Memory, CPU time-slices, and I/O bandwidth are *reserved* in advance (often using Rate Monotonic or Deadline Monotonic scheduling).
3. **Verification is Mandatory** — Schedulability tests (e.g., Liu & Layland's Utilization Bound) must mathematically prove no deadline is missed.
4. **No Best-Effort Fallback** — If the system cannot guarantee a deadline, the task is **rejected at admission control** rather than run late.
5. **Catastrophic Failure Consequence** — Failure costs life, limb, or massive financial/environmental loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** A system is *Hard* if and only if its *correctness proof* requires showing that **zero** deadline misses occur. "Probably fast" is unacceptable.

**Industrial Examples:**
- **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** in automobiles — brake pressure must respond within ~10 ms.
- **Aircraft Flight Control System** (e.g., F-16 fly-by-wire) — control loop runs at 50–100 Hz.
- **Cardiac Pacemaker** — pacing pulses must occur on millisecond-scale precision.
- **Nuclear Reactor Trip System** — shutdown rods must deploy within certified bounds.
- **Mars Rover Autonomous Navigation** — collision-avoidance decisions are time-critical.

---

### 🟨 Pillar 2: Firm Real-Time Systems

**Value Function (Step-Drop to Zero at Deadline):**

$$
V_{\text{firm}}(t) =
\begin{cases}
V_{\max}, & 0 \leq t \leq D \\
0, & t > D
\end{cases}
$$

**Logical Steps (Why and How):**

1. **Late Results Are Discarded** — Unlike soft systems, a late result has *no* value at all (not reduced value).
2. **Statistical Guarantees** — Instead of 100% deadline satisfaction, we aim for a **statistical Quality of Service (QoS)** target (e.g., 99% of frames delivered on time).
3. **Probabilistic Analysis** — Schedulability is analyzed using probability distributions of arrival rates and execution times.
4. **Graceful Degradation** — Missing some deadlines is acceptable as long as the *aggregate* performance remains above a threshold.
5. **Use of Imprecise Computation** — Algorithms may produce approximate results when full computation cannot finish in time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The phrase *"late results have zero value"* is the *defining* characteristic. Students often confuse firm and soft systems — memorize the discontinuity at $t = D$.

**Industrial Examples:**
- **Live Video Streaming** — Dropped frames are skipped, not shown late.
- **Financial Trading Platforms** — A quote that arrives after the price has changed is worthless.
- **Radar Tracking Systems** — A target position update older than the scan period is obsolete.
- **Multimedia Conferencing** — Stale audio packets are dropped to preserve lip-sync.

---

### 🟩 Pillar 3: Soft Real-Time Systems

**Value Function (Monotonically Decreasing):**

$$
V_{\text{soft}}(t) = V_{\max} \cdot e^{-\lambda (t - r)} \quad \text{for } t \geq r
$$

where $r$ is the release time and $\lambda > 0$ is the decay constant. Alternative linear form:

$$
V_{\text{soft}}(t) = \max\!\left(0,\; V_{\max} - k(t - D)\right)
$$

**Logical Steps (Why and How):**

1. **Best-Effort with Preferences** — The OS uses *dynamic* priorities (e.g., Linux's Completely Fair Scheduler with nice values).
2. **Average-Case Performance Matters Most** — The system is judged on *average response time*, not worst-case.
3. **Late Results Still Have Value** — A webpage loading 500 ms late is still useful.
4. **No Admission Control Required** — Tasks are accepted dynamically.
5. **User Experience Driven** — Designed for human perception tolerances.

**Industrial Examples:**
- **Web Browsers / HTTP Servers** — page load delays degrade UX but do not cause harm.
- **Email Clients** — delivery delays are tolerable.
- **Interactive Word Processors** — keystroke echo within ~100 ms is the goal.
- **Online Gaming** — frame rate dips are annoying but not catastrophic.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Boundary / Unit | Applicable System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Worst-Case Execution Time | $C_i$ | milliseconds (ms) | All three |
| 2 | Period of Task | $T_i$ | ms | All three |
| 3 | Relative Deadline | $D_i$ | ms | All three |
| 4 | Utilization | $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | dimensionless, $\sum U_i \leq 1$ | All three |
| 5 | Response Time | $R_i = C_i + W_i$ | ms | All three |
| 6 | Lateness | $L_i = C_i - D_i$ | ms, $L_i \leq 0$ required for Hard | All three |
| 7 | Tardiness | $T_i = \max(0,\; L_i)$ | ms | Firm / Soft |
| 8 | Laxity (Slack) | $X_i = D_i - C_i - W_i$ | ms | All three |
| 9 | Hard Value Function | $V(t) = V_{\max}$ if $t \leq D$, else $-\infty$ | $V \in \mathbb{R} \cup \{-\infty\}$ | Hard |
| 10 | Firm Value Function | $V(t) = V_{\max}$ if $t \leq D$, else $0$ | $V \in [0, V_{\max}]$ | Firm |
| 11 | Soft Value Function | $V(t) = V_{\max} \cdot e^{-\lambda(t - r)}$ | exponential decay | Soft |
| 12 | Liu & Layland Bound (RMS) | $U_{\text{bound}} = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | $\to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$ | Hard |
| 13 | EDF Utilization | $U_{\text{bound}} = 1$ (on uniprocessor) | exact | Hard |
| 14 | Miss Ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{\#\text{missed}}{\#\text{total jobs}}$ | $0 \leq \rho \leq 1$ | Firm / Soft |
| 15 | Quality of Service (QoS) | $\text{QoS} = 1 - \rho$ | $0 \leq \text{QoS} \leq 1$ | Firm / Soft |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Formatting Reminder:** In KTU answer scripts, always present such tables neatly. Do **not** use the pipe symbol inside math cells — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values to avoid LaTeX/table parsing errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland's Utilization Bound for Hard Real-Time Systems

The most cited result in Hard Real-Time scheduling analysis. We derive it step-by-step.

**Given:** A set of $n$ periodic tasks scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, with each task $\tau_i$ having period $T_i$ and worst-case execution time $C_i$, where tasks are indexed so that $T_1 \leq T_2 \leq \cdots \leq T_n$.

**Objective:** Find the maximum total utilization $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ below which the system is **guaranteed** to be schedulable.

### Step 1 — Critical Instant Definition

A **critical instant** of task $\tau_i$ occurs when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with $\tau_i$. This produces the **worst-case response time** $R_i$.

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

*Text row:* The total response time equals $\tau_i$'s own execution time plus the cumulative interference from all higher-priority jobs that arrive during the interval $[0, R_i]$.

### Step 2 — Worst-Case Response Time Inequality

The schedulability condition $R_i \leq D_i$ (typically $D_i = T_i$ for implicit deadlines) must hold:

$$
C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j \leq T_i
$$

*Text row:* Substituting $D_i = T_i$ since implicit deadlines are common in hard real-time analysis.

### Step 3 — Bounding the Ceilings

Since $T_i \geq T_j$ for $j < i$ and $R_i \leq T_i$, we have $\frac{R_i}{T_j} \leq \frac{T_i}{T_j}$. Dropping ceilings (conservative bound):

$$
\left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \leq \frac{T_i}{T_j}
$$

*Text row:* Replacing the ceiling operator with a continuous bound — this is a safe relaxation that retains the inequality.

### Step 4 — Liu & Layland's Inequality

Substituting back into the response-time bound:

$$
\frac{C_i}{T_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \frac{C_j}{T_j} \leq 1
$$

*Text row:* The key inequality: sum of all utilizations up to task $i$ (including $\tau_i$ itself) must not exceed 1.

### Step 5 — Closed-Form Sufficient Bound

By induction on $n$, the **least upper bound** is:

$$
U(n) = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

*Text row:* For any task set with total utilization $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, Rate Monotonic Scheduling guarantees no deadline misses.

### Step 6 — Limiting Behavior

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} U(n) = \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$

*Text row:* As the number of tasks grows large, RMS can utilize at most ~69.31% of the CPU. The remaining 30.69% is wasted by the static-priority limitation.

### Step 7 — Contrast with Earliest Deadline First (EDF)

For **dynamic priority** scheduling (EDF), the bound is *tight*:

$$
U_{\text{EDF}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1
$$

*Text row:* EDF achieves 100% CPU utilization on a uniprocessor, but at the cost of dynamic priority management overhead.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Attribute | Hard RTS | Firm RTS | Soft RTS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Deadline Miss Consequence** | Catastrophic failure | Result discarded ($V = 0$) | Quality degrades |
| **Value Function Shape** | Step to $-\infty$ | Step to $0$ | Continuous decay |
| **Acceptable Miss Ratio $\rho$** | $\rho = 0$ (strict) | $\rho > 0$ (targeted, e.g., $\leq 0.01$) | $\rho > 0$ (tolerated) |
| **Scheduling Type** | Static / Off-line | Hybrid | Dynamic / On-line |
| **Typical Scheduler** | RMS, DMS, EDF | Best-effort + QoS-aware | FCFS, Round-Robin, CFS |
| **OS Kernel** | RTOS (VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS) | Modified GPOS | GPOS (Linux, Windows) |
| **Admission Control** | Mandatory | Optional | None |
| **Verification Method** | Mathematical proof | Statistical / probabilistic | Benchmarking |
| **Peak Load Behavior** | Reject new tasks | Shed load gracefully | Slowdown |
| **Failure Mode** | System halt / disaster | Quality drop below SLA | User annoyance |
| **Cost of Over-Provisioning** | Very high (safety) | Moderate | Low |
| **Example Domain** | Avionics, Medical, Nuclear | Streaming, Trading | Web, Email, UI |
| **Jitter Tolerance** | Minimal | Moderate | Lenient |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Identifying a System Type

**Problem:** A traffic light controller switches a signal every 30 seconds. If a switch is delayed by 100 ms, the intersection becomes unsafe. A 5-second delay simply results in a longer wait. Identify the system type and justify.

**Solution:**

- A **100 ms delay** causes safety violations → requires **Hard** guarantees.
- A **5-second delay** is merely inconvenient → suggests **Soft** behavior.

The system is a **mixed/hybrid real-time system**: it has **both hard and soft components**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Real-world systems are often *hybrid*. Modern automotive systems contain hard tasks (brake control), firm tasks (rendering the dashboard graphics at 30 FPS), and soft tasks (infotainment streaming) **simultaneously**. Be ready to defend a hybrid answer in the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between hard and soft real-time systems with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Deadline miss effect | System failure | Quality degradation |
| Examples | (1) Airbag deployment system, (2) Cardiac pacemaker | (1) Web browser page load, (2) Email client delivery |

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition of each + 0.5 mark per example. (3 marks total)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a firm real-time system? Why is it considered distinct from soft real-time systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **firm real-time system** is one in which tasks that miss their deadlines produce **useless results** — they are discarded with **zero value**, but no catastrophe occurs. It is distinct from soft real-time because in soft systems, late results **still have diminishing value**, whereas in firm systems, late results have **exactly zero value** — a step-function discontinuity. *Example:* Live video streaming — a frame arriving after its display slot is dropped, not shown late.

**Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for distinction with example.

---

## 📕 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the three categories of real-time systems — hard, firm, and soft — with the help of a value-versus-time function for each. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A hard real-time system has three periodic tasks scheduled under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. The tasks are:
> - $\tau_1: T_1 = 10\,\text{ms}, C_1 = 2\,\text{ms}$
> - $\tau_2: T_2 = 20\,\text{ms}, C_2 = 4\,\text{ms}$
> - $\tau_3: T_3 = 50\,\text{ms}, C_3 = 5\,\text{ms}$
>
> Check whether the task set is schedulable using the Liu & Layland utilization bound. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to (a):

The value function $V(t)$ maps *completion time* $t$ to *utility* of the result.

$$
V_{\text{hard}}(t) =
\begin{cases}
V_{\max}, & 0 \leq t \leq D \\
-\infty, & t > D
\end{cases}
$$

$$
V_{\text{firm}}(t) =
\begin{cases}
V_{\max}, & 0 \leq t \leq D \\
0, & t > D
\end{cases}
$$

$$
V_{\text{soft}}(t) = V_{\max} \cdot e^{-\lambda (t - r)}
$$

- **Hard:** Step-impulse to negative infinity. A miss is catastrophic (e.g., missile guidance).
- **Firm:** Step-drop to zero. A miss renders the result worthless (e.g., live streaming frames).
- **Soft:** Continuous exponential decay. A miss merely degrades service (e.g., email delivery).

**Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks per function derivation + 0.5 mark per real-world example = 7 marks.

#### ✅ Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Compute individual utilizations.**

$$
U_1 = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20, \quad U_2 = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20, \quad U_3 = \frac{5}{50} = 0.10
$$

**Step 2: Compute total utilization.**

$$
U_{\text{total}} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 0.50
$$

**Step 3: Compute Liu & Layland bound for $n = 3$ tasks.**

$$
U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 \times 0.2599 \approx 0.7798
$$

**Step 4: Compare.**

$$
U_{\text{total}} = 0.50 \leq U_{\text{bound}} = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable under RMS** with a comfortable margin of $\approx 28\%$ spare utilization.

**Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for utilization computation + 2 marks for Liu & Layland bound + 2 marks for comparison + 1 mark for conclusion.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Describe the distinguishing features of hard, firm, and soft real-time systems using a comparative table. Discuss the role of **admission control** in each category. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A pacemaker must deliver a pacing pulse to the heart every $1000\,\text{ms}$ with a tolerance of $\pm 5\,\text{ms}$. Identify the system type. If the WCET of the pacing algorithm is $4\,\text{ms}$ and the jitter observed is $1.5\,\text{ms}$, compute the worst-case response time and determine if the deadline is feasible. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to (a):

| Feature | Hard | Firm | Soft |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Admission control | Mandatory (reject if not schedulable) | Optional (statistical) | None |
| Verification | Formal proof | Statistical | Empirical |
| Recovery from miss | System halt | Discard late result | Continue with reduced quality |

**Admission Control Discussion:**

- **Hard:** Uses **offline schedulability tests** (e.g., response-time analysis, utilization bounds). New tasks are accepted only if *all* existing tasks remain schedulable.
- **Firm:** Uses **online QoS monitoring**. Tasks are accepted based on maintaining miss-ratio below threshold.
- **Soft:** **No admission control** — all tasks are accepted; OS uses time-sharing.

**Mark Distribution:** 3 marks for comparative table + 4 marks for admission control discussion with examples.

#### ✅ Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Identify system type.**

A pacemaker is a **Hard Real-Time System** because missing a pacing pulse can cause cardiac arrest — a life-threatening failure.

**Step 2: Identify parameters.**

- Period $T = 1000\,\text{ms}$
- Tolerance $= \pm 5\,\text{ms}$ → Deadline $D = 5\,\text{ms}$ (relative to the precise target time)
- WCET $C = 4\,\text{ms}$
- Jitter $J = 1.5\,\text{ms}$

**Step 3: Compute worst-case response time.**

$$
R_{\text{worst}} = C + J = 4 + 1.5 = 5.5\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 4: Check feasibility.**

$$
R_{\text{worst}} = 5.5\,\text{ms} > D = 5\,\text{ms} \quad \times
$$

**Conclusion:** The deadline is **NOT feasible** as currently designed. The system violates the 5 ms deadline by 0.5 ms. **Engineering remedies:**

1. Optimize the algorithm to reduce WCET to $\leq 3.5\,\text{ms}$.
2. Use a hardware timer with lower jitter ($\leq 1\,\text{ms}$).
3. Switch to a higher-clock-speed microcontroller.

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for identification + 2 marks for parameter mapping + 2 marks for $R_{\text{worst}}$ computation + 2 marks for feasibility verdict and remedies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Real-Time" with "Fast":** A real-time system is not necessarily fast — it is *predictable*. A 1 GHz processor with high jitter is *worse* for hard real-time than a 100 MHz processor with deterministic behavior. Students who write "real-time means very fast" lose 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Wrong Value-Function Form:** Many students draw firm and soft functions identically. Firm has a *step drop to zero*; soft has a *continuous decay*. Examiners check this carefully.
> 3. **Ignoring the Hybrid Case:** A traffic light, a smartphone, a modern car — all contain tasks from *all three* categories. Refusing to acknowledge hybrid systems costs marks in "discuss" type questions.
> 4. **Forgetting Units in Formulas:** Always write $U$ (dimensionless), $C$ (ms), $T$ (ms). Numerical answers without units are penalized.
> 5. **Liu & Layland Formula Mistakes:** The bound is $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, **not** $2^{1/n} - 1$ alone. Many students drop the leading $n$ factor.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **real-time system** is defined by *temporal correctness*, not just logical correctness.
- ✅ **Hard RTS:** Missing a deadline = catastrophic failure. Uses static scheduling (RMS, DMS, EDF). Requires formal schedulability proof. Value function steps to $-\infty$.
- ✅ **Firm RTS:** Late results have *zero* value but no disaster. Step function to $0$. Examples: video streaming, trading, radar.
- ✅ **Soft RTS:** Late results have *reduced but non-zero* value. Continuous decay. Examples: email, web, UI.
- ✅ **Hybrid systems** dominate industry — modern cars/phones run all three categories simultaneously.
- ✅ **Liu & Layland Bound:** $U_{\text{RMS}}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1) \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- ✅ **EDF** achieves full $U \leq 1$ utilization on uniprocessors.
- ✅ **Admission control** is mandatory in Hard RTS, optional in Firm, absent in Soft.
- ✅ **Jitter** is critical in Hard RTS — minimize it deterministically.
- ✅ Always include **units** and **value-function diagrams** in exam answers for full marks.
- ✅ Differentiate *predictability* from *speed* — predictability is the true hallmark of real-time systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timing Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Timing Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🎯 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Timing Constraints** in Real-Time Systems (RTS) are the formal, mathematically expressed temporal bounds (limits) placed on the execution behavior of computational tasks. They define **when** a task must begin, **how long** it may execute, and **by when** it must complete to be considered correct.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, a **timing constraint** is any constraint imposed on the *temporal behavior* of a real-time task, encompassing the relationship between:
- The **release time** of a job,
- The **execution time** required to process it, and
- The **deadline** by which it must be finished.

In the KTU taxonomy, timing constraints are broadly classified into three primary families:

| Constraint Family | Definition | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard Real-Time Constraint** | Missing the deadline causes **catastrophic system failure** (e.g., airbag deployment, nuclear reactor trip) | Critical |
| **Soft Real-Time Constraint** | Missing the deadline causes **degraded performance** but no total failure (e.g., video streaming jitter) | Tolerable |
| **Firm Real-Time Constraint** | Late results have **zero value** but no harm if missed (e.g., stock-price tick updates) | Discardable |

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The Train Timetable

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **Rajdhani Express** schedule:
> - **Release Time (r)** = The instant the train is allowed to depart the platform (e.g., 8:00 AM).
> - **Execution Time (e)** = The time taken to travel one leg of the journey (e.g., 4 hours).
> - **Deadline (d)** = The scheduled arrival at the next station (e.g., 12:00 PM).
> - **Response Time (R)** = Actual arrival time minus release time.
>
> If the train arrives **after 12:00 PM**, the timing constraint is **violated**. The seriousness of the violation depends on context: missing a tourist train is "soft," but missing a medical-emergency ambulance is "hard."

## ⚙️ Fundamental Task Parameters in KTU Notation

A real-time **task** $\tau_i$ in the KTU 2024 scheme is formally characterized by the tuple:

$$
\tau_i \;=\; (p_i,\; e_i,\; d_i,\; r_i,\; \phi_i)
$$

Where:
- $\phi_i$ → **Phase** (initial offset / release time of the first job)
- $p_i$ → **Period** (interval between successive job releases)
- $e_i$ → **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)**
- $d_i$ → **Relative deadline**
- $r_i$ → **Release time** of a specific job $J_{i,j}$

The **absolute deadline** of job $J_{i,j}$ released at $r_{i,j}$ is:

$$
D_{i,j} \;=\; r_{i,j} \;+\; d_i
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always distinguish between **Relative Deadline** ($d_i$, expressed in time units) and **Absolute Deadline** ($D_{i,j}$, expressed on the timeline). Examiners frequently award 1 mark in Part A for this distinction alone.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 Taxonomy of Timing Constraints — Hierarchical Breakdown

### 1️⃣ Release Time Constraint ($r_{i,j}$)

The earliest instant at which a job becomes eligible for execution. Before this instant, the job **cannot** be dispatched, even if the processor is idle.

- **Lower Bound Constraint:** Job cannot start before $r_{i,j}$
- **Formally:** $\forall t < r_{i,j}, \; \text{state}(J_{i,j}, t) = \text{IDLE}$

### 2️⃣ Deadline Constraint ($D_{i,j}$)

The latest instant at which the job's computation must produce a result. Two sub-types exist in KTU literature:

- **Hard Deadline:** $D_{i,j} = r_{i,j} + d_i$, with zero tolerance for overrun
- **Soft Deadline:** $D_{i,j} = r_{i,j} + d_i$, but overrun degrades utility, not correctness

### 3️⃣ Execution Time Constraint ($e_i$)

The **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** — the maximum time the task could possibly consume the CPU under any legal input. This is the *schedulability* cornerstone.

$$
e_i \;\leq\; p_i \quad \text{(necessary condition for feasibility)}
$$

### 4️⃣ Period Constraint ($p_i$)

For periodic tasks, the time between two successive releases of jobs from the same task is fixed at $p_i$. The $k$-th job of task $\tau_i$ is released at:

$$
r_{i,k} \;=\; \phi_i \;+\; (k-1)\cdot p_i, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots
$$

### 5️⃣ Precedence Constraint ($\prec$)

A **partial-ordering** relation between tasks: $\tau_i \prec \tau_j$ means $\tau_j$ cannot begin until $\tau_i$ completes. This is a *logical* constraint that interacts with timing.

### 6️⃣ Resource Constraint

A task may hold one or more shared resources (mutexes, semaphores), creating *mutual-exclusion* blocking — which extends effective execution time and can cause **priority inversion**.

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit | Constraint Relation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\phi_i$ | Phase / Offset | seconds (s) | $r_{i,1} = \phi_i$ |
| $p_i$ | Period | s | $r_{i,k+1} - r_{i,k} = p_i$ |
| $e_i$ | WCET | s | $0 < e_i \leq p_i$ |
| $d_i$ | Relative deadline | s | $d_i \leq p_i$ (typical) |
| $D_{i,j}$ | Absolute deadline | s | $D_{i,j} = r_{i,j} + d_i$ |
| $R_{i,j}$ | Response time | s | $R_{i,j} = f_{i,j} - r_{i,j}$ |
| $f_{i,j}$ | Finish time | s | $f_{i,j} \leq D_{i,j}$ |
| $slack_i$ | Slack time | s | $slack_i = d_i - e_i$ |
| $U_i$ | Task utilization | dimensionless | $U_i = e_i / p_i$ |
| $U_{sum}$ | Total utilization | dimensionless | $U_{sum} = \sum_i e_i/p_i$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Timing constraints are foundational in **avionics (DO-178C)**, **automotive ECU scheduling (AUTOSAR)**, **medical device firmware (IEC 62304)**, and **industrial PLC control loops**. Every KTU graduate designing embedded systems will use these constraints.

## 🧩 Deadline Types — KTU Classification

| Deadline Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Completion Deadline** | Job must *finish* by $D_{i,j}$ | Flight-control computation |
| **Start Deadline** | Job must *begin* by some instant | Camera shutter trigger |
| **Zero-Laxity Deadline** | $d_i = e_i$ (no slack allowed) | Cryptographic key generation |
| **Bounded-Laxity** | $d_i > e_i$ but bounded | Sensor sampling |
| **Arbitrary Deadline** | $d_i$ may exceed $p_i$ | Long-horizon planning tasks |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔢 Derivation 1: Response Time of a Job under Fixed-Priority Preemption

The **response time** $R_i$ of a task $\tau_i$ under Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) is the smallest fixed point satisfying the recurrence:

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} \;=\; e_i \;+\; \sum_{\tau_j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{p_j} \right\rceil \cdot e_j
$$

Where $hp(i)$ denotes the set of higher-priority tasks. The iteration terminates when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution Procedure

**Step 1:** Initialize $R_i^{(0)} = e_i$

**Step 2:** Compute $R_i^{(1)} = e_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil R_i^{(0)} / p_j \right\rceil \cdot e_j$

**Step 3:** Iterate until convergence: $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$

**Step 4:** Verify schedulability: $R_i \leq d_i$ ✓

### 📐 Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Given three tasks under Rate-Monotonic Scheduling:
- $\tau_1: (p_1=20,\; e_1=4)$
- $\tau_2: (p_2=50,\; e_2=12)$
- $\tau_3: (p_3=100,\; e_3=10)$

All have implicit deadlines ($d_i = p_i$). Compute the response time of $\tau_3$.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Step 1: } R_3^{(0)} = e_3 = 10
$$

$$
\text{Step 2: } R_3^{(1)} = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 12
$$

$$
= 10 + (1)(4) + (1)(12) = 26
$$

$$
\text{Step 3: } R_3^{(2)} = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{26}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{26}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 12
$$

$$
= 10 + (2)(4) + (1)(12) = 30
$$

$$
\text{Step 4: } R_3^{(3)} = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{30}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{30}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 12
$$

$$
= 10 + (2)(4) + (1)(12) = 30
$$

Since $R_3^{(3)} = R_3^{(2)}$, **convergence achieved**. Hence $R_3 = 30$.

**Schedulability Check:**

$$
R_3 = 30 \;\leq\; d_3 = 100 \quad \checkmark
$$

Therefore, $\tau_3$ is **schedulable** under RMS.

## 🔢 Derivation 2: Liu & Layland Utilization Bound (Necessary Background)

For $n$ periodic tasks scheduled under RMS, a sufficient (not necessary) feasibility condition is:

$$
U_{sum} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i} \;\leq\; n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)
$$

As $n \to \infty$, the bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.

### Tabular Decomposition of the Bound

| $n$ | $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Approx. |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1 \cdot 1.000$ | 1.000 |
| 2 | $2 \cdot 0.414$ | 0.828 |
| 3 | $3 \cdot 0.260$ | 0.780 |
| 4 | $4 \cdot 0.189$ | 0.757 |
| $\infty$ | — | 0.693 |

## 🔢 Derivation 3: Hyperperiod (LCM of Periods)

The **hyperperiod** $H$ of a task set is the least common multiple of all task periods — it is the duration after which the periodic pattern repeats.

$$
H \;=\; \text{lcm}(p_1,\; p_2,\; \dots,\; p_n)
$$

**Example:** $p_1 = 4$, $p_2 = 6$, $p_3 = 8 \Rightarrow H = \text{lcm}(4,6,8) = 24$ time units.

## 🔢 Derivation 4: Maximum Number of Jobs in Hyperperiod

$$
N_{jobs} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{H}{p_i}
$$

**Example (continued):** $N_{jobs} = 24/4 + 24/6 + 24/8 = 6 + 4 + 3 = 13$ jobs.

## 🧮 Analytical Matrix — Timing Constraint Satisfiability

| Constraint | Verification Method | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Release time | $\text{start}(J_{i,j}) \geq r_{i,j}$ | Premature execution bug |
| Deadline | $R_{i,j} \leq d_i$ | System failure (hard) / degradation (soft) |
| Period | $r_{i,k+1} - r_{i,k} = p_i$ | Jitter / lost samples |
| Precedence | $\tau_i \prec \tau_j \Rightarrow f_i \leq s_j$ | Data race / inconsistency |
| Resource | No unbounded priority inversion | Deadlock / livelock |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🎨 Figure 1: Timing Parameters on a Real-Time Task Timeline

This diagram maps the canonical KTU task model onto a temporal axis, showing the relationships between release, execution, and deadline.

```mermaid
gantt

    dateFormat  X

```
