<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the following terms with respect to real-time task scheduling:**
**(a)** Release Time
**(b)** Deadline
**(c)** Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Release Time ($r_{i,j}$):** The instant in time at which a job $J_{i,j}$ becomes *available* for execution. Prior to $r_{i,j}$, the job is in a dormant state and cannot be scheduled on the processor, even if the processor is idle. For a periodic task, the release time of the $k$-th job is given by $r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1)\cdot p_i$.

**(b) Deadline ($D_{i,j}$):** The latest instant by which the job $J_{i,j}$ must complete its execution. Formally, $D_{i,j} = r_{i,j} + d_i$, where $d_i$ is the relative deadline. A *hard* deadline demands strict adherence; a *soft* deadline tolerates late completion with graceful degradation.

**(c) WCET ($e_i$):** The maximum possible execution time of task $\tau_i$ across all valid input combinations and execution paths. It is a static, upper-bound estimate used in schedulability analysis. The constraint $e_i \leq p_i$ must hold for any feasible periodic task set.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Hard Real-Time constraints and Soft Real-Time constraints with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Deadline Miss** | System failure / catastrophe | Degraded quality only |
| **Utility Function** | Step function (binary) | Monotonically decreasing |
| **Examples** | (1) Anti-lock braking system (ABS), (2) Pacemaker cardiac pacing | (1) Live video streaming, (2) Online gaming frame rendering |
| **Consequence** | Loss of life / property damage | User dissatisfaction |
| **Design Priority** | Absolute correctness | Average-case performance |

In a hard real-time system, the timing constraint is part of the **correctness specification** — a result produced after $D_{i,j}$ is considered *wrong*. In a soft real-time system, the timing constraint affects **quality of service** but not functional correctness.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **🔷 Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the different types of timing constraints encountered in real-time systems. Provide a detailed classification diagram and describe the role of each constraint in system design.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A real-time system has three periodic tasks with the following parameters:

| Task | Period $p_i$ | WCET $e_i$ | Relative Deadline $d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 20 ms | 4 ms | 20 ms |
| $\tau_2$ | 50 ms | 12 ms | 50 ms |
| $\tau_3$ | 100 ms | 10 ms | 100 ms |

Using **Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, determine whether the task set is schedulable. Justify using both the **Liu & Layland utilization bound** and the **response-time analysis** method.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of Timing Constraints — Detailed Explanation:**

The major timing constraints are:
1. **Release Time Constraint** — earliest admissible start.
2. **Deadline Constraint** — latest admissible finish (hard / soft / firm).
3. **Execution Time Constraint (WCET)** — maximum CPU consumption.
4. **Period Constraint** — fixed inter-release interval for periodic tasks.
5. **Precedence Constraint** — logical ordering (e.g., $\tau_i \prec \tau_j$).
6. **Resource Constraint** — bounded blocking on shared resources.
7. **Latency Constraint** — end-to-end response bound across a chain.

Each constraint interacts with the scheduler: a violation of any of them causes *temporal incorrectness*. In safety-critical systems (DO-178C Level A), all constraints become hard.

**(b) Schedulability Analysis:**

**Step 1 — Priority Assignment (RMS):** Priorities are inversely proportional to periods. Hence:
- $\tau_1$ (p=20) → highest priority
- $\tau_2$ (p=50) → middle priority
- $\tau_3$ (p=100) → lowest priority

**Step 2 — Liu & Layland Utilization Test:**

$$
U_{sum} = \frac{4}{20} + \frac{12}{50} + \frac{10}{100} = 0.200 + 0.240 + 0.100 = 0.540
$$

For $n=3$, the bound is $U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$.

Since $U_{sum} = 0.540 \leq 0.7798$, the **sufficient condition is satisfied**. The task set *may* be schedulable.

**Step 3 — Response-Time Analysis (Exact Test):**

For $\tau_1$ (highest priority, no higher-priority preemption):
$$
R_1 = e_1 = 4 \;\text{ms} \;\leq\; d_1 = 20 \;\text{ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

For $\tau_2$:
$$
R_2^{(0)} = 12
$$
$$
R_2^{(1)} = 12 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 12 + 4 = 16
$$
$$
R_2^{(2)} = 12 + \left\lceil \frac{16}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 12 + 4 = 16
$$

Converged: $R_2 = 16 \;\text{ms} \leq d_2 = 50 \;\text{ms} \quad \checkmark$

For $\tau_3$ (already computed in Section 3): $R_3 = 30 \;\text{ms} \leq d_3 = 100 \;\text{ms} \quad \checkmark$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable** under RMS. The response-time analysis confirms the Liu & Layland sufficient test, and provides exact timing-constraint satisfaction verification.

---

### **🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat timeline diagram, explain the following timing parameters of a real-time task: release time, execution time, deadline, response time, and slack time. Derive the relationship between them.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a task set with the following parameters, compute the **hyperperiod**, the **total number of jobs** in the hyperperiod, and the **average system utilization**:

- $\tau_1: p_1 = 5$ ms, $e_1 = 1$ ms
- $\tau_2: p_2 = 10$ ms, $e_2 = 2$ ms
- $\tau_3: p_3 = 20$ ms, $e_3 = 3$ ms

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Timing Parameters — Timeline Explanation:**

Consider a single job $J_{i,j}$ on a temporal axis (units in ms):

```
Time:    0     r_ij            (r_ij + e_i) = f_ij              D_ij
         |------|=================|--------------------------------|
                ^Release          ^Finish                          ^Deadline
                |---e_i (exec)----|                                |
                |--------R_ij (response time)---------------------|
                |                              |-----Slack--------|
                                            f_ij                 D_ij
```

**Mathematical Relationships:**

- Release time: $r_{i,j}$ (given)
- Finish time: $f_{i,j} = r_{i,j} + \text{actual\_exec}$
- Response time: $R_{i,j} = f_{i,j} - r_{i,j}$
- Worst-case response time: $R_{i,j}^{wc} = e_i + \text{interference}$
- Slack time: $slack_{i,j} = D_{i,j} - f_{i,j} = (r_{i,j} + d_i) - f_{i,j}$
- In the best case: $slack_{i,j} = d_i - e_i$

**Schedulability condition:** $R_{i,j} \leq d_i$ (or equivalently, $f_{i,j} \leq D_{i,j}$).

**(b) Hyperperiod, Job Count, and Utilization:**

**Step 1 — Hyperperiod:**

$$
H = \text{lcm}(p_1, p_2, p_3) = \text{lcm}(5, 10, 20) = 20 \;\text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Jobs in Hyperperiod:**

$$
N_{jobs} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{H}{p_i} = \frac{20}{5} + \frac{20}{10} + \frac{20}{20} = 4 + 2 + 1 = 7 \;\text{jobs}
$$

**Step 3 — Total CPU Utilization:**

$$
U_{sum} = \frac{e_1}{p_1} + \frac{e_2}{p_2} + \frac{e_3}{p_3} = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{20}
$$

$$
= 0.200 + 0.200 + 0.150 = 0.550
$$

**Conclusion:** The system operates at **55% utilization**, leaving 45% slack for handling aperiodic jobs, interrupts, and system overhead. The hyperperiod is 20 ms, containing 7 jobs total.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps Identified by KTU Board Examiners:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Relative and Absolute Deadline** — A relative deadline $d_i$ is a duration; an absolute deadline $D_{i,j}$ is an instant on the timeline. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting the Ceiling Function** — In response-time analysis, $\lceil R_i^{(k)} / p_j \rceil$ must be used, not floor or simple division. This costs **2 marks** in Part B.
> 3. **Not Showing Iteration Convergence** — Response-time analysis must explicitly state when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$. Without this, the answer is incomplete (-2 marks).
> 4. **Treating Liu & Layland Bound as Necessary** — The bound $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ is **sufficient, not necessary**. A task set exceeding it may still be schedulable. Use response-time analysis for exact verification.
> 5. **Ignoring the First Job's Phase** — If $\phi_i \neq 0$, the first job's release time differs from subsequent ones. Marks are lost for assuming $\phi_i = 0$ without justification.
> 6. **Mixing Hard and Soft Deadline Justifications** — In design questions, always state the assumption (hard/soft) explicitly. The KTU valuation key is unforgiving on ambiguity.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Timing constraints** define the temporal correctness of real-time tasks — they are as critical as logical correctness.
- ✅ A real-time task $\tau_i$ is modeled as the tuple $(p_i, e_i, d_i, r_i, \phi_i)$ in KTU notation.
- ✅ **Release time** = earliest start; **Deadline** = latest finish; **WCET** = upper-bound CPU consumption.
- ✅ **Hard** deadlines must be met absolutely; **Soft** deadlines tolerate misses; **Firm** deadlines cause discarded late results.
- ✅ **Periodic tasks** release jobs at fixed intervals $p_i$; **aperiodic/sporadic** tasks have irregular arrivals.
- ✅ The **absolute deadline** is $D_{i,j} = r_{i,j} + d_i$, not equal to $d_i$ alone.
- ✅ **Response time analysis** uses iterative fixed-point computation: $R_i^{(k+1)} = e_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / p_j \rceil \cdot e_j$.
- ✅ **Liu & Layland bound** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is sufficient but not necessary — it gives a *quick test*; response-time analysis gives the *exact test*.
- ✅ **Hyperperiod** $H = \text{lcm}(p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n)$ is the cycle length for periodic task analysis.
- ✅ **Precedence constraints** impose partial ordering: $\tau_i \prec \tau_j \Rightarrow f_i \leq s_j$.
- ✅ **Slack time** $slack_{i,j} = d_i - e_i$ quantifies timing margin; zero slack = critical path.
- ✅ **Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is optimal for fixed-priority periodic tasks; **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** is optimal for dynamic-priority.
- ✅ Always verify **$e_i \leq p_i$** as a sanity check before deeper analysis.
- ✅ KTU board expects **timeline diagrams** in 7-mark questions — draw them; they earn easy marks.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Topic: Timing Constraints | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling Timing Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modelling Timing Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Timing Constraints?

In the context of **Real-Time Systems (RTS)**, a *timing constraint* is a temporal (time-based) requirement that dictates **when** a computational task must begin or complete its execution. Unlike general-purpose computing, where *correctness* depends only on the logical output, an RTS demands that results be produced within a stipulated **time window**, failing which the system is deemed to have failed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A *timing constraint* is a formal specification binding the execution of a job (instance of a task) to a specific interval of real time, characterized primarily by its **Release Time (r)**, **Deadline (d)**, and **Period (p)**. Violation of these constraints constitutes a *temporal fault*, which for hard real-time systems may result in catastrophic system failure.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Timing

1. **Release Time (r)** – The instant at which a job becomes *ready* for execution.
2. **Deadline (d)** – The latest instant by which the job *must* complete.
3. **Period (p)** – The fixed time interval between successive activations of a periodic task.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A job is said to have a *relative deadline* of `D` if `d = r + D`. Absolute deadlines are written with reference to system start time.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍕

Imagine you are a **pizza delivery executive** in a busy city:

- The **order arrives** at 7:00 PM → This is your *Release Time (r)*.
- The customer expects pizza within **30 minutes** → This is your *Deadline (d)*.
- You receive **recurring orders** every day at 7:00 PM → This is your *Period (p)*.

If you deliver at 7:45 PM (45 mins), the *logical correctness* of the order is fine (pizza is hot, order is correct), but the *timing correctness* is violated — the customer is unhappy. In hard real-time systems (e.g., **airbag deployment**), such a delay could be *fatal*.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Timing Constraints

| **Constraint Type**        | **Description**                                                                  | **Example System**                |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------- |
| **Hard Timing Constraint** | Violation causes **catastrophic** system failure. Zero tolerance.                | Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)    |
| **Soft Timing Constraint** | Violation degrades performance but system remains functional.                    | Video streaming frame drop       |
| **Firm Timing Constraint** | Late results are *useless* (discarded) but not damaging.                         | Weather forecasting data         |
| **Hard Real-Time Task**     | Computation deadlines `D` are mandatory.                                         | Flight control system            |
| **Soft Real-Time Task**     | Deadlines `D` are desirable, not mandatory.                                      | Online transaction processing    |
| **Non-Real-Time Task**      | No timing constraints.                                                           | Email server                     |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Problems:** Worst-Case Execution Time (**WCET**) — denoted as `e` or `C`. The unit is in **milliseconds (ms)** or **microseconds (µs)**.

---

## 1.5 Task Models in Timing Specification

Real-time systems categorize jobs/tasks based on their arrival patterns:

1. **Periodic Task (Tₚ):** Activated regularly at fixed intervals of period `p`.
   - Example: Sensor sampling every 10 ms.
2. **Aperiodic Task (Tₐ):** Arrives at irregular, unpredictable times.
   - Example: User keystroke.
3. **Sporadic Task (Tₛ):** Aperiodic task with a *minimum inter-arrival time* constraint.
   - Example: Emergency alarm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Real-Time Task Model

A real-time task `τᵢ` is formally represented as a 6-tuple:

$$\tau_i = (\phi_i, p_i, e_i, d_i, D_i, \text{type})$$

Where each symbol maps to a specific physical/temporal property:

| **Symbol**  | **Parameter**              | **Meaning**                                                            | **Unit** |
| ----------- | -------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------- |
| $\phi_i$    | Phase                      | The first release (offset) time of the task                            | **ms**   |
| $p_i$       | Period                     | Time between successive job releases                                   | **ms**   |
| $e_i$       | WCET                       | Worst-Case Execution Time on the given hardware                        | **ms**   |
| $d_i$       | Absolute Deadline          | Latest completion time referenced to system start                      | **ms**   |
| $D_i$       | Relative Deadline          | Time allowed from release to completion: $D_i = d_i - \phi_i$          | **ms**   |
| type        | Preemptive/Non-Preemptive  | Whether a job can be interrupted mid-execution                         | —        |

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How Timing Constraints Are Modeled

The modelling process follows a deterministic flow:

1. **Identify External Events:** Determine what triggers the real-time task (interrupts, timers, sensors).
2. **Assign Release Times:** For each event, compute the earliest possible activation time `r`.
3. **Determine Deadlines:** Derive the deadline `d` from the application's physical requirements.
4. **Compute WCET:** Profile or measure the worst-case execution time `e` on the target CPU.
5. **Classify the Task:** Assign Hard/Soft/Firm classification based on failure impact.
6. **Validate Schedulability:** Use *Rate Monotonic Analysis* or *Response Time Analysis* to verify feasibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Model:**
> The 6-tuple model is *deterministic* — given a task's specification, its future behavior can be predicted exactly, which is the cornerstone of verifying real-time correctness.

---

## 2.3 Precedence and Resource Constraints

Beyond timing, tasks can have:

- **Precedence Constraints:** Task τⱼ cannot begin until task τᵢ completes (e.g., `τᵢ → τⱼ`).
- **Resource Constraints:** Tasks may share mutually exclusive resources (semaphores, memory).
- **Communication Constraints:** Inter-task message passing introduces additional latency.

---

## 2.4 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula**                                                  | **Description**                                              |
| ----- | ------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| 1     | $D_i = d_i - \phi_i$                                         | Relative deadline from absolute deadline                     |
| 2     | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i}$                         | Total CPU Utilization (must be $\leq 1$ for feasibility)     |
| 3     | $U_{RM} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$                             | Rate Monotonic Upper Bound for `n` tasks                     |
| 4     | $R_i = e_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{p_j} \right\rceil e_j$ | Response Time (iterative solution)              |
| 5     | $r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) p_i$                               | Release time of the k-th job                                 |
| 6     | $d_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) p_i + D_i$                         | Absolute deadline of the k-th job                            |
| 7     | $f_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + C_i$                                    | Finish time assuming no preemption (ideal case)              |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Note:** For KTU board exams, write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ — *never* use vertical bars `|x|` in markdown table cells.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Modelling timing constraints is foundational in:

- **Automotive:** Engine Control Units (ECU) operate on tasks with deadlines of 1–5 ms.
- **Aerospace:** Flight control loops demand deterministic ≤ 10 ms response.
- **Medical:** Pacemaker firmware must sample heart signals every 250 ms with strict deadlines.
- **Industrial Automation:** PLC scan times are bounded by 1–10 ms for safety interlocks.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Sensor fusion timing models guide hardware-software co-design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Release Time & Absolute Deadline for the k-th Job

Let us derive the absolute release and deadline times for a periodic task τᵢ that releases its first job at time φᵢ with period pᵢ and relative deadline Dᵢ.

### Step 1 — Define the Release Time Sequence

A periodic task releases jobs at uniform intervals. The first release happens at the phase φᵢ. Subsequent releases occur exactly one period `pᵢ` apart.

$$
r_{i,1} = \phi_i
$$

$$
r_{i,2} = \phi_i + p_i
$$

$$
r_{i,3} = \phi_i + 2p_i
$$

### Step 2 — Generalize the Pattern

Observing the pattern, the k-th job's release time is:

$$
r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) \cdot p_i
$$

This is an arithmetic progression with common difference $p_i$.

### Step 3 — Derive the Absolute Deadline

The deadline is defined as the release time plus the relative deadline $D_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{i,k} &= r_{i,k} + D_i \\
        &= \phi_i + (k-1) p_i + D_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify for k = 1

For the first job, k = 1:

$$
d_{i,1} = \phi_i + (1-1) p_i + D_i = \phi_i + D_i
$$

This correctly states that the deadline of the first job is its phase plus the relative deadline, matching the formal definition $D_i = d_i - \phi_i$. ✔️

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound

For `n` independent periodic tasks scheduled under **Rate Monotonic (RM)** priority (shorter period = higher priority), Liu & Layland (1973) derived a sufficient schedulability test.

### Step 1 — Define Utilization

For a task set, the CPU utilization is the fraction of time the processor is busy:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i}
$$

Where $e_i$ is the WCET and $p_i$ is the period.

### Step 2 — Critical Instant

The worst-case response time for any task under RM occurs at the **critical instant** — when the task is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks.

### Step 3 — Liu & Layland Bound

For the task set to be schedulable, a sufficient (but not necessary) condition is:

$$
U \leq n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Limit Analysis

As $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693
$$

So for a large number of tasks, utilization must remain below **69.3%** to guarantee RM schedulability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Mini-Example:** For n = 3 tasks, the RM bound is $3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$, meaning a system using only 77.98% of CPU is provably schedulable.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Task Models

| **Property**            | **Periodic Task**                 | **Aperiodic Task**               | **Sporadic Task**                |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Arrival Pattern         | Regular at fixed intervals        | Random, no minimum gap           | Random, with min inter-arrival   |
| Predictability          | **High**                          | **Low**                          | **Medium**                       |
| Typical Use             | Sensor polling                    | User input                       | Emergency interrupt              |
| Bounded Jitter?         | Yes                               | No                               | Yes (bounded)                    |
| KTU Classification      | Hard / Soft Real-Time             | Often Soft                       | Hard (if safety-critical)        |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timing Diagram — Periodic Task Release & Deadlines

The following mermaid diagram depicts the temporal evolution of a periodic task τᵢ with release times r, deadlines d, and execution windows.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Periodic Task Timing Model
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Task 1
    Job 1 (Release r1)      :a1, 0, 3
    Idle                     :idle1, 3, 5
    Job 2 (Release r2)      :a2, 5, 3
    Idle                     :idle2, 8, 5
    Job 3 (Release r3)      :a3, 13, 3
    section Deadlines
    Deadline d1              :crit, 0, 0
    Deadline d2              :crit, 8, 0
    Deadline d3              :crit, 16, 0
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Modelling Timing Constraints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Real World Event] --> B[Assign Release Time r]
    B --> C[Compute Relative Deadline D]
    C --> D[Measure WCET e via Profiling]
    D --> E{Classify Task Type}
    E -->|Periodic| F[Set Period p]
    E -->|Aperiodic| G[No Fixed Period]
    E -->|Sporadic| H[Set Min Inter-Arrival Time]
    F --> I[Construct Task Tuple tau]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Schedulability Test]
    J -->|Pass| K[System Schedulable]
    J -->|Fail| L[Re-engineer Constraints]
    L --> B
```

---

## 4.3 Architecture: Event-Triggered vs Time-Triggered Systems

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "Event-Triggered Model"
        E1[External Event] --> E2[Interrupt Handler]
        E2 --> E3[Task Activated]
        E3 --> E4[Execute on Demand]
    end
    subgraph "Time-Triggered Model"
        T1[System Tick] --> T2[Schedule Table Lookup]
        T2 --> T3[Task Activated]
        T3 --> T4[Execute at Slot]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** In *event-triggered* systems, timing is *reactive* and may have jitter. In *time-triggered* systems, timing is *deterministic* with predictable latency, preferred for hard real-time safety systems (e.g., AUTOSAR in vehicles).

---

## 4.4 Precedence Constraint DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1 --> T2 --> T4
    T1 --> T3 --> T4
    T4 --> T5
```

The graph above represents: Task 1 precedes both Task 2 and Task 3; both must complete before Task 4 begins; Task 4 precedes Task 5.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Hard Real-Time System. How does it differ from a Soft Real-Time System?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A *Hard Real-Time System* is one in which the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical result of computation but also on the time at which the result is produced. Missing a deadline in such a system is considered a **total system failure**, often with catastrophic consequences. Example: Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Aircraft flight controller.

In contrast, a *Soft Real-Time System* allows occasional deadline misses; performance degrades gracefully but the system remains operational. Example: Video conferencing application, audio playback buffer.

| **Parameter**          | **Hard RTS** | **Soft RTS**            |
| ---------------------- | ------------ | ----------------------- |
| Deadline Miss Impact   | Catastrophic | Reduced QoS             |
| Average Performance    | Not enough   | Acceptable metric       |
| Example                | Pacemaker    | Online video streaming  |

*(Marks: 1 – Definition of HRTS, 1 – Definition of SRTS, 1 – Tabular comparison)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**Distinguish between Periodic, Aperiodic, and Sporadic tasks with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Periodic Task:** Activated at regular, fixed time intervals. Example: A temperature sensor sampled every 100 ms.
- **Aperiodic Task:** Activated at random, unpredictable times with no minimum inter-arrival time. Example: User keyboard input on a workstation.
- **Sporadic Task:** Aperiodic task but with a *guaranteed minimum inter-arrival time*, bounding the arrival rate. Example: An emergency fire alarm (minimum 5 minutes between activations).

*(Marks: 1 – Each definition with example)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE-INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain in detail the components of a real-time task model using the 6-tuple notation. List the types of timing constraints that can be imposed on real-time tasks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A periodic task τ₁ has phase φ₁ = 0, period p₁ = 20 ms, and WCET e₁ = 5 ms. Compute the release times and absolute deadlines of the first three jobs, assuming relative deadline D₁ = 20 ms. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Components of a Real-Time Task Model (7 Marks):**

A real-time task τᵢ is described by the 6-tuple:

$$
\tau_i = (\phi_i, p_i, e_i, d_i, D_i, \text{type})
$$

| **Parameter** | **Meaning**                                                                                  |
| ------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| $\phi_i$      | Phase: offset of first release from system start                                             |
| $p_i$         | Period: inter-arrival interval for periodic tasks                                            |
| $e_i$         | Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)                                                             |
| $d_i$         | Absolute deadline with respect to system start                                               |
| $D_i$         | Relative deadline: $D_i = d_i - \phi_i$                                                      |
| type          | Preemptive or Non-preemptive                                                                 |

**Types of Timing Constraints:**

1. *Release Time Constraint* – Specifies when a job becomes ready.
2. *Deadline Constraint* – Specifies when a job must complete.
3. *Periodic Constraint* – Inter-arrival interval between consecutive jobs.
4. *Precedence Constraint* – Ordering between dependent tasks.
5. *Resource Constraint* – Mutually exclusive access to shared resources.
6. *Latency Constraint* – Maximum end-to-end response time.

*(Marks: 2 – Tuple definition, 2 – Component meanings, 2 – Constraint types, 1 – Example/diagram)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** φ₁ = 0, p₁ = 20 ms, e₁ = 5 ms, D₁ = 20 ms.

**Step 1 — Release Times using $r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) p_i$:**

$$
r_{1,1} = 0 + (1-1) \times 20 = 0 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
r_{1,2} = 0 + (2-1) \times 20 = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
r_{1,3} = 0 + (3-1) \times 20 = 40 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Absolute Deadlines using $d_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) p_i + D_i$:**

$$
d_{1,1} = 0 + 0 + 20 = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
d_{1,2} = 0 + 20 + 20 = 40 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
d_{1,3} = 0 + 40 + 20 = 60 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Tabulated Result:**

| **Job (k)** | **Release r (ms)** | **Deadline d (ms)** | **WCET e (ms)** |
| ----------- | ------------------ | ------------------- | --------------- |
| 1           | 0                  | 20                  | 5               |
| 2           | 20                 | 40                  | 5               |
| 3           | 40                 | 60                  | 5               |

*(Marks: 2 – Formula, 2 – Calculation, 2 – Tabulation, 1 – Final conclusion)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2022]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the differences between **Event-Triggered** and **Time-Triggered** real-time systems. Discuss their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two periodic tasks are to be scheduled on a single processor using Rate Monotonic Scheduling.
- Task τ₁: e₁ = 1 ms, p₁ = 4 ms
- Task τ₂: e₂ = 2 ms, p₂ = 6 ms

Check whether the task set is schedulable using the **Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound** test. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Event vs Time-Triggered (7 Marks):**

**Event-Triggered Systems:**
- Tasks are activated in response to external or internal *events* (interrupts, signals).
- Timing is *reactive* and asynchronous.
- *Advantages:* Low overhead, fast response to events, efficient CPU usage.
- *Disadvantages:* Hard to predict, high jitter, complex to verify deterministically.

**Time-Triggered Systems:**
- Tasks are activated at predetermined *time slots* based on a global clock tick.
- Timing is *deterministic* and synchronous.
- *Advantages:* High predictability, easier verification, low jitter.
- *Disadvantages:* Idle CPU time, rigid scheduling, less flexible.

**Diagram (Verbal representation in answer):**

- *Event-Triggered:* Event → Interrupt → ISR → Task
- *Time-Triggered:* Clock Tick → Scheduler → Slot Lookup → Task Execution

*(Marks: 2 – Definitions, 2 – Differences, 2 – Pros/Cons, 1 – Diagram)*

---

**(b) RM Schedulability Test (7 Marks):**

**Given:** τ₁: (e₁ = 1, p₁ = 4), τ₂: (e₂ = 2, p₂ = 6)

**Step 1 — Compute Total Utilization:**

$$
U = \frac{e_1}{p_1} + \frac{e_2}{p_2} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6}
$$

$$
U = 0.25 + 0.3333 = 0.5833
$$

**Step 2 — Compute RM Bound for n = 2:**

$$
U_{RM} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1) = 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) = 2 \cdot (1.4142 - 1) = 0.8284
$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$
U = 0.5833 \leq U_{RM} = 0.8284
$$

✅ **Result:** The task set is **schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

*(Marks: 2 – Utilization formula, 2 – RM bound formula, 2 – Numerical substitution, 1 – Conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Period with Deadline:** Many students assume `D = p`, but the *relative deadline* is an *independent* parameter in the KTU model.
> 2. **Forgetting the Phase:** In phased task sets, always include φᵢ in release time calculations. Omitting it loses **1–2 marks** consistently.
> 3. **RM Bound Misuse:** The Liu-Layland bound is *sufficient*, not *necessary*. A system with `U > U_RM` may still be schedulable, but the bound cannot prove it — use Response Time Analysis instead.
> 4. **Unit Mismatch:** Mixing seconds and milliseconds in the same problem is a **1-mark penalty** issue.
> 5. **Absolute vs Relative Deadline:** Always show both — KTU expects the relationship `D = d − φ` explicitly stated.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⏱️ A *timing constraint* binds task execution to a time window, not just a logical outcome.
- 📐 The 6-tuple task model is the **canonical KTU representation**: $\tau_i = (\phi_i, p_i, e_i, d_i, D_i, \text{type})$.
- 🔢 **Release time formula:** $r_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) p_i$
- 🎯 **Absolute deadline formula:** $d_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) p_i + D_i$
- ⚙️ **Utilization formula:** $U = \sum e_i / p_i$
- 📊 **RM bound:** $U_{RM} = n (2^{1/n} - 1)$, with limit $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$.
- 🟥 **Hard RTS:** Zero deadline tolerance; **Soft RTS:** Graceful degradation.
- 🔁 **Periodic / Aperiodic / Sporadic** — three task classes to remember.
- 🧩 **Event-Triggered** = reactive, jitter-prone; **Time-Triggered** = deterministic, predictable.
- 📈 **Precedence constraints** are represented as **DAGs** in KTU.
- 🚫 **No vertical pipe `|` in markdown tables** — use `\lvert` or `\mid` for LaTeX absolute value.
- ✍️ **Always state assumptions** (e.g., preemptive kernel, independent tasks) before solving schedulability problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Real-Time Task

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Real-Time Task** is a computation unit (program, subroutine, or process) that is activated in response to, or in anticipation of, a real-world event, and whose correctness depends not only on the **logical result** of the computation but also on the **time at which the result is produced**.

A *Job* is a single instance or invocation of a task. A *Task* is therefore formally defined as a *set of related jobs* that collectively perform a specific function. Each job is a unit of work scheduled and executed by the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2, "Basic Concepts"**
> The cornerstone of every scheduling theorem, feasibility test, and response-time analysis you will encounter in this module rests on understanding the *parameters of a real-time task and its jobs*. You cannot solve scheduling problems without mastering these parameters first.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Train Dispatcher

Imagine a busy railway junction with multiple trains (jobs) needing to use a single track section (the processor). The dispatcher (the scheduler) must:
- Know **when each train is ready** to enter the track (release time).
- Know **how long the track is needed** (execution time).
- Know **the latest the train can leave** to not delay connecting passengers (deadline).

A late train (missed deadline) may cause a major accident (catastrophic system failure) — this is *hard* real-time. A late bus that just causes a small passenger wait is *soft* real-time. Real-time systems engineering is exactly this dispatcher problem, formalized mathematically.

### 1.3 Core Terminology — Quick Reference

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Task** | A set of related jobs performing a specific function |
| **Job** | A single unit of work (instance) of a task |
| **Release Time ($r_i$)** | Instant when a job becomes available for execution |
| **Execution Time ($e_i$)** | CPU time required by the job |
| **Deadline ($d_i$)** | Instant by which the job must finish |
| **Response Time** | Time from release to completion |
| **Processor Utilization ($U$)** | Fraction of processor time consumed by the workload |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention Used Throughout KTU 2024 Scheme**
> Subscript $i$ always refers to the $i^{th}$ job. Subscript $k$ refers to the $k^{th}$ task. A bold symbol like $\mathbf{T}_k$ refers to a *task set*, while $T_k$ refers to a scalar parameter of task $k$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Task Parameters (Task-Level Characteristics)

A task $\tau_k$ is characterized by the following *intrinsic* parameters that remain constant for every job of that task:

**Step 1 — Phase ($\phi_k$):**
The phase of a task is the release time of its first job. A task with phase $\phi_k = 0$ begins its life the moment the system starts at time $t = 0$. A task with $\phi_k > 0$ is said to be *phase-delayed*.

**Step 2 — Period ($p_k$):**
The period is the fixed time interval between the release times of two successive jobs of the same task. For a periodic task, every $p_k$ time units, a new job is released.

**Step 3 — Relative Deadline ($D_k$):**
The relative deadline is the maximum allowed time from a job's release until its completion. If the relative deadline equals the period ($D_k = p_k$), the task is said to have an *implicit deadline*. If $D_k < p_k$, it has a *constrained deadline*. If $D_k$ can be arbitrary, it has an *arbitrary deadline*.

**Step 4 — Worst-Case Execution Time ($e_k$ or WCET):**
This is the upper bound on the CPU time required to complete any job of the task, given the worst-case input data and the worst-case hardware/software state.

**The "Why" behind these parameters:** Together, $(p_k, e_k, D_k, \phi_k)$ completely characterize a periodic task. Any scheduling algorithm (Rate Monotonic, Earliest Deadline First, etc.) is built to work with exactly these four numbers.

### 2.2 Job Parameters (Job-Level Characteristics)

A job $J_i$ of task $\tau_k$ has the following *instance-specific* parameters:

**Step 1 — Release Time ($r_i$):**
For the very first job, $r_i = \phi_k$. For subsequent jobs, $r_{i+1} = r_i + p_k$.

**Step 2 — Absolute Deadline ($d_i$):**
Computed as $d_i = r_i + D_k$. This is the wall-clock instant by which the job must complete.

**Step 3 — Start Time ($s_i$):**
The wall-clock instant at which the scheduler actually begins executing the job on the processor.

**Step 4 — Finish Time / Completion Time ($f_i$):**
The wall-clock instant at which the job completes its execution.

**Step 5 — Response Time ($R_i$):**
The elapsed time from release to completion: $R_i = f_i - r_i$. A job *meets* its deadline iff $R_i \le D_k$, which equivalently means $f_i \le d_i$.

**Step 6 — Slack Time / Laxity ($X_i$):**
The amount of time a job can be delayed and still meet its deadline. Computed as $X_i = d_i - f_i$ (dynamic) or $X_i = D_k - e_i$ (static, at release).

### 2.3 Classification of Real-Time Tasks

**By Temporal Trigger:**
- **Periodic Task:** Jobs are released at regular intervals, with $r_{i+1} = r_i + p_k$. Examples: sensor sampling, control loops.
- **Aperiodic Task:** Jobs are released at irregular, unpredictable times with no minimum inter-arrival separation. Examples: operator keystrokes, alarm triggers.
- **Sporadic Task:** Aperiodic tasks that carry a *minimum inter-arrival time* constraint $p_k$ (the smallest gap between any two jobs). They behave like periodic tasks for analysis purposes.

**By Criticality of Deadline:**
- **Hard Real-Time Task:** Missing a deadline causes *catastrophic* system failure (loss of life, equipment damage). Example: airbag controller.
- **Soft Real-Time Task:** Missing a deadline degrades *quality of service* but does not cause system failure. Example: video frame decoder.
- **Firm Real-Time Task:** Late results have *zero value* but no catastrophic harm. Example: stock-tick display.

**By Preemption:**
- **Preemptive Task:** The scheduler may pause the job at any instant to run a higher-priority job.
- **Non-Preemptive Task:** Once started, the job runs to completion on the processor.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r_{i+1} = r_i + p_k$ | Successive job release time | Periodic tasks only |
| 2 | $d_i = r_i + D_k$ | Absolute deadline computation | Valid for all tasks |
| 3 | $R_i = f_i - r_i$ | Response time of job $i$ | $R_i \ge e_i$ always |
| 4 | Deadline met iff $R_i \le D_k$ | Feasibility condition per job | Equivalent to $f_i \le d_i$ |
| 5 | $X_i = d_i - a_i$ | Dynamic laxity at age $a_i$ | $a_i = t - r_i$ is current job age |
| 6 | $U = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{e_k}{p_k}$ | Total processor utilization | For $n$ independent periodic tasks |
| 7 | $U_{\text{bound}} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Liu & Layland utilization bound | RM-schedulability for implicit deadlines |
| 8 | $U_{\text{bound}} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | Asymptotic RM bound | As $n \to \infty$ |
| 9 | $H_k = \text{lcm}(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k)$ | Hyperperiod of first $k$ tasks | Used to bound schedule length |
| 10 | $W_k(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} e_j \cdot \lceil t/p_j \rceil$ | Worst-case workload of $k$ tasks in $[0,t]$ | For response-time analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> The processor utilization $U$ is the most-used metric in the automotive (AUTOSAR), avionics (ARINC 653), and industrial control (IEC 61131) industries. When a designer says "this ECU has 60% CPU headroom," they have computed $U$ and compared it against the bound for the chosen scheduling policy. A miscalculation here is how a satellite gets lost in space.

### 2.5 Processor Utilization — The "How Loaded Is the CPU?" Metric

The processor utilization $U$ is the fraction of processor time consumed by a real-time workload in the limit as the observation window grows. For $n$ independent periodic tasks, it is the simple sum:

$$U = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{e_k}{p_k}$$

Each term $\frac{e_k}{p_k}$ represents the fraction of *one period* spent in execution. The sum is the *average* fraction of processor time consumed. A necessary condition for any feasible schedule is $U \le 1$. A *sufficient* condition for Rate Monotonic schedulability (for implicit deadlines) is $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Processor Utilization of a Single Periodic Task

**Given:** A single periodic task $\tau_1$ with period $p_1$ and worst-case execution time $e_1$.

**Step 1 — Number of jobs in time $T$:**
In any observation window of length $T$, the number of jobs released by $\tau_1$ is given by the ceiling function:

$$N_1(T) = \left\lceil \frac{T}{p_1} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Total execution demand in time $T$:**
Each job demands at most $e_1$ time units, so the total demand is:

$$E_1(T) = N_1(T) \cdot e_1 = e_1 \left\lceil \frac{T}{p_1} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3 — Define the *average* utilization as the limit:**

$$U_1 = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{E_1(T)}{T} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{e_1 \left\lceil T / p_1 \right\rceil}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the squeeze theorem on the ceiling function.**
Recall that for any real $x$, the inequality $x \le \lceil x \rceil < x+1$ holds. Substituting $x = T/p_1$:

$$\frac{T}{p_1} \le \left\lceil \frac{T}{p_1} \right\rceil < \frac{T}{p_1} + 1$$

**Step 5 — Multiply through by $e_1 / T$:**

$$\frac{e_1}{p_1} \le \frac{e_1 \left\lceil T/p_1 \right\rceil}{T} < \frac{e_1}{p_1} + \frac{e_1}{T}$$

**Step 6 — Take the limit $T \to \infty$.**
The middle term $e_1 / T \to 0$, and the left and right bounds both converge to $e_1/p_1$:

$$\boxed{U_1 = \frac{e_1}{p_1}}$$

This proves that the utilization of a single periodic task is simply the ratio of its worst-case execution time to its period — a beautifully intuitive result.

### 3.2 Derivation — Liu and Layland's Rate Monotonic Bound

**Given:** $n$ independent periodic tasks, each with period $p_k$ equal to its relative deadline $D_k$, executing on a uniprocessor under fixed-priority preemption.

**Step 1 — The hardest schedulable case.**
Liu and Layland proved that the *hardest* utilization configuration to schedule is when each task's utilization $u_k = e_k/p_k$ is set to the largest possible value that still allows all higher-priority tasks to exactly meet their deadlines. Let this critical value be $u_k = \lambda_k$.

**Step 2 — Critical instant argument.**
The longest response time of task $\tau_k$ occurs when it is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks. At this critical instant, the total demand placed on the processor in $[0, t]$ is:

$$W_k(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} e_j \cdot \left\lceil \frac{t}{p_j} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3 — Feasibility equation.**
For task $\tau_k$ to just meet its deadline $D_k = p_k$, the workload must satisfy $W_k(p_k) = p_k$. Setting all higher-priority tasks to the critical utilization $\lambda$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda \cdot p_j \cdot \left\lceil \frac{p_k}{p_j} \right\rceil = p_k$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ceiling bound.**
Using $\lceil x \rceil \le x + 1$:

$$\lambda \cdot \left( \sum_{j=1}^{k} p_j + (k-1) \cdot p_k \right) \le p_k \cdot k$$

This yields a recursive bound on $\lambda_k$ that, when computed exactly for $n$ tasks with periods $p_j = 2^{j-1}$ (worst-case harmonic relationship), simplifies to:

$$\lambda_n = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$

**Step 5 — Final bound:**

$$\boxed{U_{\text{RM bound}} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)}$$

**Step 6 — Asymptotic limit.**
Applying $\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ and using L'Hôpital's rule (or the known limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} n(x^{1/n} - 1) = \ln x$ for $x > 0$):

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

**Interpretation:** Even in the absolute worst case, a Rate Monotonic scheduler can always feasibly schedule any task set whose total utilization does not exceed $\ln 2 \approx 69.31\%$. For small $n$, the bound is *higher* (e.g., $n=2$ gives 82.8%, $n=3$ gives 77.9%).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A real-time system has three periodic tasks with the following parameters:

| Task | $\phi_k$ | $p_k$ | $e_k$ | $D_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 0 | 4 | 1 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 0 | 6 | 2 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 0 | 8 | 3 | 8 |

**Find:** (a) Total processor utilization. (b) Hyperperiod. (c) Is the system RM-schedulable by the Liu-Layland bound?

**Solution:**

**(a) Total processor utilization:**

$$U = \frac{e_1}{p_1} + \frac{e_2}{p_2} + \frac{e_3}{p_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{8}$$

$$U = 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.3750 = 0.9583$$

**(b) Hyperperiod:**

$$H = \text{lcm}(4, 6, 8) = 24 \text{ time units}$$

**(c) Liu-Layland bound check:**
For $n = 3$:

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

Since $U = 0.9583 > 0.7798$, the Liu-Layland bound is **violated**, but the system *might* still be feasible (the bound is sufficient, not necessary). Time-demand analysis would be required to confirm.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Job Parameter Computation

| Job Index $i$ | Task $k$ | $r_i$ | $d_i$ | $e_i$ | $f_i$ (hypothetical) | $R_i$ | Deadline Met? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 4 | 1 | 1 | 1 | ✓ |
| 2 | 1 | 4 | 8 | 1 | 5 | 1 | ✓ |
| 3 | 2 | 0 | 6 | 2 | 7 | 7 | ✗ (missed) |
| 4 | 3 | 0 | 8 | 3 | 10 | 10 | ✗ (missed) |

This kind of table is exactly what KTU examiners expect you to construct when given a task set and a scheduling algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Task Lifecycle — From Release to Termination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Defined phase k period pk execution ek] --> B[System Time t=0]
    B --> C[First Job J1 Released at t=phi k]
    C --> D{Scheduler Decision}
    D -->|Highest Priority| E[Job Starts Execution at s sub i]
    D -->|Lower Priority| F[Job Waits in Ready Queue]
    F --> D
    E --> G[Job Executes for e sub i units]
    G --> H{Execution Complete at f sub i}
    H --> I[Check Deadline Condition f sub i le d sub i]
    I -->|Yes| J[Deadline Met Job Valid]
    I -->|No| K[Deadline Missed Failure]
    J --> L[Compute Response Time R sub i equals f sub i minus r sub i]
    L --> M[Next Job Released r sub i plus 1 equals r sub i plus p sub k]
    M --> D
    K --> N[System Reports Timing Fault]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Real-Time Scheduling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT[External World Events Sensors Actuators] -->|Triggers| ARR[Job Arrivals r sub i]
    ARR --> RT[Ready Queue Ordered by Priority]
    RT --> SCH[Scheduler Reads Current Job Parameters]
    SCH --> DSP[Dispatcher Grants Processor to Job]
    DSP --> CPU[Processor Executes Job for e sub i]
    CPU --> FIN[Completion at f sub i]
    FIN --> CHK{Deadline Check f sub i le d sub i}
    CHK -->|Met| OK[Valid Output to Actuator]
    CHK -->|Missed| FLT[Timing Fault Handler]
    FIN --> CLK[Clock Tick Advance t equals t plus 1]
    CLK --> ARR
    OK --> EXT
    FLT --> LOG[Fault Log and Recovery]
```

### 4.3 Classification Hierarchy of Real-Time Tasks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RT[Real Time Task] --> T1[By Temporal Trigger]
    RT --> T2[By Deadline Criticality]
    RT --> T3[By Preemption Behaviour]
    T1 --> PER[Periodic p k constant]
    T1 --> APE[Aperiodic No fixed pattern]
    T1 --> SPO[Sporadic Min inter arrival p k]
    T2 --> HR[Hard Deadline Miss is catastrophic]
    T2 --> SR[Soft Deadline Miss degrades QoS]
    T2 --> FR[Firm Deadline Miss loses value only]
    T3 --> PRM[Preemptive Scheduler may pause job]
    T3 --> NPM[Non Preemptive Job runs to completion]
```

### 4.4 Timeline of a Periodic Task Set

```mermaid
gantt
    title Gantt Chart of Three Periodic Tasks over Hyperperiod H=24
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Task tau1 period 4
    tau1 Job1 (0 to 1)    :a1, 0, 1
    tau1 Job2 (4 to 5)    :a2, 4, 1
    tau1 Job3 (8 to 9)    :a3, 8, 1
    tau1 Job4 (12 to 13)  :a4, 12, 1
    tau1 Job5 (16 to 17)  :a5, 16, 1
    tau1 Job6 (20 to 21)  :a6, 20, 1
    section Task tau2 period 6
    tau2 Job1 (1 to 3)    :b1, 1, 2
    tau2 Job2 (7 to 9)    :b2, 7, 2
    tau2 Job3 (13 to 15)  :b3, 13, 2
    tau2 Job4 (19 to 21)  :b4, 19, 2
    section Task tau3 period 8
    tau3 Job1 (3 to 6)    :c1, 3, 3
    tau3 Job2 (11 to 14)  :c2, 11, 3
    tau3 Job3 (19 to 22)  :c3, 19, 3
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the following terms with respect to real-time task scheduling: (i) Release Time, (ii) Relative Deadline, (iii) Response Time. Give one example of a real-time system where each parameter is critical.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 per term):**

**(i) Release Time ($r_i$):** The instant at which a job becomes available for execution on the processor. Before $r_i$, the job is dormant and cannot be scheduled even if the processor is idle. *Example:* A temperature sensor sampling at fixed intervals — its data acquisition routine cannot begin until the hardware releases the next sample.

**(ii) Relative Deadline ($D_k$):** The maximum time interval, measured from the release of a job, within which the job must complete. It is a property of the *task*, not any individual job. *Example:* In an anti-lock braking system, the wheel-speed calculation must complete within 5 ms of the sensor interrupt to maintain braking force.

**(iii) Response Time ($R_i$):** The total time elapsed from a job's release to its completion, computed as $R_i = f_i - r_i$. *Example:* The response time of a packet scheduling job in a real-time network must be bounded to ensure Quality of Service guarantees.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate clearly between periodic, aperiodic, and sporadic tasks. For each type, give one real-world application.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Trigger Pattern | Min. Inter-Arrival | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Periodic** | Exactly periodic, period $p_k$ | $p_k$ (exact) | Vehicle engine control loop reading crankshaft position every 10 ms |
| **Aperiodic** | Unpredictable, no pattern | None (zero) | Operator pressing the emergency-stop button in an industrial robot |
| **Sporadic** | Unpredictable, but bounded | $p_k$ (minimum) | Arrival of a new print job at a network printer — must be processed within 2 seconds, but no two jobs arrive within 1 second of each other |

The key distinction is that sporadic tasks have a *minimum inter-arrival time* (a contract that the environment will not overload the system), which is what makes them analyzable like periodic tasks despite their irregular arrival.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE Module 2]

**(a)** Define the term **Processor Utilization** for a set of $n$ independent periodic tasks. Derive the expression for total processor utilization. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a real-time system with three tasks having the following parameters: $\tau_1 = (p_1=5, e_1=1)$, $\tau_2 = (p_2=10, e_2=3)$, $\tau_3 = (p_3=20, e_3=4)$. Compute (i) total processor utilization, (ii) the hyperperiod, and (iii) verify schedulability using the Liu-Layland bound for Rate Monotonic scheduling. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Processor Utilization $U$ is defined as the fraction of processor time consumed by a real-time workload in the steady state, i.e., in the limit as the observation interval grows to infinity.

$$U = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\text{Total execution demand in } [0, T]}{T}$$

**Derivation (5 marks):**

For a single periodic task $\tau_k$ with period $p_k$ and execution time $e_k$:

*Step 1:* In time interval $[0, T]$, the number of jobs released is $\lceil T/p_k \rceil$.

*Step 2:* Total execution demand is $E_k(T) = e_k \cdot \lceil T/p_k \rceil$.

*Step 3:* Apply the ceiling inequality $T/p_k \le \lceil T/p_k \rceil < T/p_k + 1$:

$$e_k \cdot \frac{T}{p_k} \le E_k(T) < e_k \left( \frac{T}{p_k} + 1 \right)$$

*Step 4:* Divide by $T$ and take the limit $T \to \infty$:

$$\frac{e_k}{p_k} \le U_k < \frac{e_k}{p_k} + \frac{e_k}{T}$$

*Step 5:* As $T \to \infty$, the rightmost term vanishes, giving:

$$U_k = \frac{e_k}{p_k}$$

*Step 6:* For $n$ independent tasks, by linearity of limits:

$$U = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{e_k}{p_k}$$

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Total processor utilization (2 marks):**

$$U = \frac{e_1}{p_1} + \frac{e_2}{p_2} + \frac{e_3}{p_3} = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{3}{10} + \frac{4}{20}$$

$$U = 0.2000 + 0.3000 + 0.2000 = 0.7000$$

**(ii) Hyperperiod (2 marks):**

$$H = \text{lcm}(5, 10, 20) = 20 \text{ time units}$$

**(iii) Liu-Layland bound check (3 marks):**
For $n = 3$ tasks:

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1)$$

Computing $2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599$:

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

**Comparison:** $U = 0.7000 < U_{\text{bound}} = 0.7798$.

**Conclusion:** The sufficient condition is satisfied. The task set is **schedulable under Rate Monotonic scheduling** on a uniprocessor.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the classification of real-time tasks based on (i) temporal trigger and (ii) deadline criticality. For each category, give a real-world industrial example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A periodic task $\tau_1$ has phase $\phi_1 = 0$, period $p_1 = 6$ ms, worst-case execution time $e_1 = 2$ ms, and relative deadline $D_1 = 6$ ms. Compute the release time, absolute deadline, and absolute release instant for the first *four* jobs of $\tau_1$. State whether each job can meet its deadline if it starts at $r_i$ and runs to completion. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Classification by temporal trigger (3.5 marks):**

- **Periodic tasks** release jobs at regular intervals $p_k$. The next job is released exactly $p_k$ time units after the previous. *Industrial example:* In an automotive Electronic Control Unit (ECU), the engine control task samples crankshaft position every 6 ms to compute fuel injection timing.

- **Aperiodic tasks** release jobs at irregular, unpredictable instants with no minimum separation. *Industrial example:* The interrupt service routine triggered by a fault condition in a nuclear reactor's pressure sensor — it may never fire for years, or it may fire multiple times within a second.

- **Sporadic tasks** release jobs at irregular instants but are *contractually guaranteed* by the environment to have a minimum inter-arrival time $p_k$. *Industrial example:* Network packets arriving at an in-vehicle Ethernet switch — they are bursty but the network protocol guarantees no more than 1000 packets per second on a given port.

**(ii) Classification by deadline criticality (3.5 marks):**

- **Hard real-time tasks** must meet *every* deadline; a miss causes catastrophic failure. *Industrial example:* The airbag deployment controller must fire the inflator within 10 ms of crash detection — a late inflation causes injury.

- **Soft real-time tasks** may occasionally miss deadlines with graceful quality degradation. *Industrial example:* A digital audio decoder skipping a frame produces a brief audible pop but the song continues.

- **Firm real-time tasks** produce zero-value output if late, but no harm results. *Industrial example:* A weather-data display showing the *latest* sensor reading — a 2-minute-old reading is discarded, but no one is endangered.

**(b) — 7 Marks:**

Given: $\phi_1 = 0$, $p_1 = 6$ ms, $e_1 = 2$ ms, $D_1 = 6$ ms.

For periodic tasks, the release time of the $i^{th}$ job is $r_i = \phi_1 + (i-1) \cdot p_1 = (i-1) \cdot 6$ ms. The absolute deadline is $d_i = r_i + D_1 = r_i + 6$ ms.

| Job $i$ | $r_i$ (ms) | $d_i$ (ms) | $e_i$ (ms) | $f_i$ (ms) if starts at $r_i$ | $R_i = f_i - r_i$ | $R_i \le D_1$? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 6 | 2 | 2 | 2 | ✓ Met |
| 2 | 6 | 12 | 2 | 8 | 2 | ✓ Met |
| 3 | 12 | 18 | 2 | 14 | 2 | ✓ Met |
| 4 | 18 | 24 | 2 | 20 | 2 | ✓ Met |

**Computation walkthrough — Job 1:** $r_1 = 0 + (1-1) \cdot 6 = 0$ ms. $d_1 = 0 + 6 = 6$ ms. Job runs from 0 to 2 ms, so $f_1 = 2$ ms, $R_1 = 2 - 0 = 2$ ms. Since $2 \le 6$, deadline met.

**Computation walkthrough — Job 2:** $r_2 = 0 + (2-1) \cdot 6 = 6$ ms. $d_2 = 6 + 6 = 12$ ms. Job runs from 6 to 8 ms, so $f_2 = 8$ ms, $R_2 = 8 - 6 = 2$ ms. Since $2 \le 6$, deadline met.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** All four jobs meet their deadlines. The single task has a worst-case response time of 2 ms, well within the 6 ms relative deadline.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse absolute deadline $d_i$ with relative deadline $D_k$.** A common mistake is to write $d_i = D_k$ (omitting the release time). This will cost 1 full mark in Part A and 2 marks in Part B derivations.
> 2. **Always show the ceiling function explicitly** when computing the number of jobs in an interval. Writing $T/p_k$ instead of $\lceil T/p_k \rceil$ is a frequent error that leads to incorrect response-time computations.
> 3. **For the Liu-Layland bound**, students often compute $2^{1/n}$ incorrectly. The most reliable method is to use the identity $2^{1/n} = e^{\ln 2 / n}$ and a calculator — do not attempt mental arithmetic for $n \ge 4$.
> 4. **Hyperperiod is lcm, not gcd.** Writing $H = \gcd(p_1, p_2, \ldots)$ is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. Remember: lcm$\times$gcd $= p_1 \cdot p_2$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **task** is a *set* of jobs; a **job** is *one* unit of work.
- [x] Task parameters: $(\phi_k, p_k, e_k, D_k)$ — phase, period, WCET, relative deadline.
- [x] Job parameters: $(r_i, s_i, f_i, d_i, R_i, X_i)$ — release, start, finish, absolute deadline, response, laxity.
- [x] Key identity: $d_i = r_i + D_k$.
- [x] Key identity: $R_i = f_i - r_i$.
- [x] Deadline met $\iff R_i \le D_k \iff f_i \le d_i$.
- [x] Periodic jobs obey $r_{i+1} = r_i + p_k$.
- [x] Sporadic tasks have a *minimum* inter-arrival time $p_k$.
- [x] Hard vs Soft vs Firm — failure mode distinguishes them.
- [x] Preemptive vs Non-preemptive — schedulability is easier under preemption.
- [x] Processor utilization: $U = \sum_{k=1}^{n} e_k / p_k$.
- [x] Necessary feasibility condition: $U \le 1$.
- [x] RM sufficient condition: $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- [x] Asymptotic RM bound: $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- [x] Hyperperiod: $H = \text{lcm}(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$.
- [x] Workload in $[0,t]$: $W(t) = \sum e_j \lceil t/p_j \rceil$.
- [x] **Mnemonic for job lifecycle:** *Re-lease → Start → Finish → Check-deadline → next Re-lease.* Remember the chain **R-S-F-C**.
- [x] **Mnemonic for utilization bound:** *For $n$ tasks, RM guarantees feasibility if $U$ stays below the "Liu-Layland S-curve" $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ that flattens at $\ln 2$.*
- [x] **Watch out:** The Liu-Layland bound is *sufficient* but NOT *necessary*. A task set with $U = 0.95$ may still be RM-schedulable — the bound is just a quick test.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clock Driven Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clock Driven Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Clock Driven Scheduling** (also known as **Time-Driven Scheduling** or **Static Table-Driven Scheduling**) is a non-preemptive or preemptive scheduling paradigm used predominantly in **hard real-time systems** where all scheduling decisions are predetermined and executed at specific **clock-tick instants**. The complete schedule of all jobs (tasks) is computed **offline** (at design time / a priori) and stored as a static dispatch table. At runtime, the scheduler is a trivial *dispatcher* that consults this table and releases jobs precisely when the system clock reaches the prescribed release times.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, this is classified as a **static scheduling approach** belonging to the family of *offline-scheduled, online-dispatched* algorithms, and is the cornerstone of the classical **Cyclic Executive** architecture used in safety-critical embedded systems (e.g., automotive ECUs, avionics, industrial controllers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition for KTU Board Exams:**
> *"Clock Driven Scheduling is a static, table-driven scheduling approach in which the schedule of periodic and sporadic jobs is pre-computed off-line and dispatched at run-time by a scheduler driven by a high-precision clock interrupt. It is optimal for systems where the set of tasks is known a priori and tasks are predominantly periodic."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **grandfather clock in a railway station** controlling a complex set of switches. Every minute, a pre-printed paper tape (the *schedule table*) tells the station master (the *dispatcher*) exactly which train platform switch to flip, which signal to turn green, and which announcement to play. The station master does **not** decide anything on the fly — he simply follows the rigid paper tape that the chief engineer (the *offline scheduler*) designed the previous night based on the **24-hour train timetable**.

The **clock** is the grandfather clock. The **paper tape** is the static schedule table. The **station master** is the lightweight runtime dispatcher. The **24-hour timetable** is the major cycle (LCM of all task periods).

- **Offline work** → building the paper tape once.
- **Online work** → just *reading* the tape when the clock ticks.

This analogy captures the essence: **all hard work is done ahead of time**, leaving only trivial decisions at runtime — which is exactly why clock-driven scheduling is prized for certification-critical systems (DO-178C, ISO 26262, IEC 61508).

## 1.3 Fundamental Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlights — Must Memorize for KTU:**
> - Tasks are **periodic** with known periods $T_i$ and worst-case execution times $C_i$.
> - The schedule is generated **offline** and stored in a dispatch table.
> - A **timer interrupt** (typically at every *frame boundary*) awakens the dispatcher.
> - The scheduling **decision cost at runtime is $O(1)$** — table lookup only.
> - The system is **deterministic** and **fully predictable**.
> - Predominantly used in **hard real-time embedded control systems** where the task set is **closed** (no dynamic task arrivals).

## 1.4 When is Clock-Driven Scheduling the Right Choice?

| System Property | Suitability |
|-----------------|-------------|
| Task set is **static and known** | ✅ Ideal |
| Tasks are **strictly periodic** | ✅ Ideal |
| Sporadic jobs (rare, aperiodic) | ⚠️ Possible with *sporadic servers* |
| Highly dynamic / unknown workloads | ❌ Not suitable |
| **Hard real-time** deadlines | ✅ Excellent (provable) |
| System requires **simplicity & certifiability** | ✅ Excellent |
| Frequent mode changes / reconfiguration | ⚠️ Requires multiple tables |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cyclic Executive Model — The Heart of Clock-Driven Scheduling

The classical implementation of clock-driven scheduling is the **Cyclic Executive**. The major elements are:

### 2.1.1 Major Cycle (Hyper-period)
The **Major Cycle** $T_{mc}$ is the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of the periods of all periodic tasks in the system. After one major cycle, the entire schedule **repeats** periodically.

$$T_{mc} = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_n)$$

### 2.1.2 Frame
The major cycle is partitioned into a sequence of equal-duration **frames**. Each frame $F_k$ has a fixed length $f$ (the **frame size**). The number of frames in the major cycle is:

$$N_f = \frac{T_{mc}}{f}$$

### 2.1.3 Slot
Within each frame, individual jobs are assigned fixed **slots** of execution time.

## 2.2 The Four Mandatory Constraints on Frame Size

Choosing the frame size $f$ is **not arbitrary**. It must satisfy the following four constraints (these are **board-exam favourites** for KTU):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Frame-Size Constraints (Liu & Layland / KTU standard):**
> 1. **Constraint C1 (Frame Capacity):** $\displaystyle f \geq \max_{1 \leq i \leq n}(C_i)$ — a frame must be long enough to accommodate the longest individual job.
> 2. **Constraint C2 (Frame Granularity):** $\displaystyle f \leq \min_{1 \leq i \leq n}(T_i)$ — a frame must be shorter than the shortest period, so every task gets at least one release opportunity per period.
> 3. **Constraint C3 (Task-Partitionability):** Every job of every task must complete **within a single frame** (i.e., $C_i \leq f$ for all $i$). A task's execution time $C_i$ must fit entirely in one frame.
> 4. **Constraint C4 (Harmonic Frame Decomposability):** $\displaystyle \frac{T_{mc}}{f}$ must be an **integer** — the major cycle must contain a whole number of frames.

## 2.3 Building the Cyclic Schedule — Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Task Set Enumeration:** Identify all periodic tasks $\tau_i = (T_i, C_i, D_i)$ where $D_i = T_i$ (typical for periodic tasks).

**Step 2 — Compute Major Cycle:** Calculate $T_{mc} = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$.

**Step 3 — Choose Frame Size $f$:** Select $f$ satisfying all four constraints. A common heuristic is the **largest $f$ that satisfies C1 and C2 simultaneously** (to reduce overhead).

**Step 4 — Compute Number of Frames:** $N_f = T_{mc} / f$.

**Step 5 — Task Partitioning (Slicing):** If a task's execution time $C_i$ cannot fit within the frame capacity, it must be **sliced** (partitioned) into sub-jobs. Each sub-job must be of size $\leq f$ and the total sub-jobs must equal $C_i$.

**Step 6 — Place Jobs into Frames:** Assign each job (or sub-job) to a specific frame. The number of jobs of task $\tau_i$ in the major cycle is $T_{mc} / T_i$.

**Step 7 — Validate:** Verify that in every interval of length $T_i$, the cumulative execution time allotted to $\tau_i$ equals exactly $C_i$ and the phasing preserves deadlines.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|--------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | Major Cycle | $T_{mc} = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ | Hyper-period; entire schedule repeats here. Used in CPU utilization analysis. |
| 2 | Number of Frames | $N_f = T_{mc} / f$ | Count of frames in one major cycle. |
| 3 | Number of Jobs of $\tau_i$ per Major Cycle | $J_i = T_{mc} / T_i$ | Used to compute total CPU load. |
| 4 | Frame Size Lower Bound | $f \geq \max(C_i)$ | C1 constraint — capacity. |
| 5 | Frame Size Upper Bound | $f \leq \min(T_i)$ | C2 constraint — granularity. |
| 6 | CPU Utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Total load; must be $\leq 1$ for feasibility. |
| 7 | Total Frame Overhead | $\text{OH} = N_f \cdot f_{oh}$ | Where $f_{oh}$ is dispatcher + interrupt overhead per frame. |
| 8 | Effective Utilization | $U_{eff} = U + \frac{\text{OH}}{T_{mc}}$ | Must be $\leq 1$. |
| 9 | Slack Time per Frame | $S_f = f - \sum \text{(job time in frame)}$ | For aperiodic job servicing. |
| 10 | Maximum Phasing Jitter | $J_i = f - C_i$ | Worst-case release-time variation of $\tau_i$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **automotive AUTOSAR OS**, **avionics ARINC 653** time-partitioned schedulers, and **industrial PLCs** for offline schedulability proofs required by safety standards (ISO 26262 ASIL-D, DO-178C DAL-A).

## 2.5 Advantages and Disadvantages (High-Weightage for KTU 2-Mark Questions)

**Advantages:**
- **Provably deterministic** — schedule is mathematically known in advance.
- **Zero runtime scheduling overhead** — only a table lookup.
- **Easier certification** — required for safety-critical domains.
- **No priority inversion** issues (no dynamic priorities).
- **Simple kernel** — minimal memory footprint.
- **Easy to verify timing behavior** via static analysis.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Brittle to task-set changes** — any new task requires regenerating the entire table.
- **Hard to handle aperiodic/sporadic tasks** — requires *polling server* or *deferrable server* extensions.
- **Release-time jitter** can occur due to frame quantization.
- **Difficult to design** for large, complex task sets.
- **No graceful degradation** — the entire table is static.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a Cyclic Schedule

This is a **classic KTU Part-B style problem**. Let us construct a complete clock-driven schedule.

### Given Task Set

| Task $\tau_i$ | Period $T_i$ | Execution Time $C_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $\tau_1$ | **4** | 1 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | **5** | 2 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | **20** | 5 | 20 |

### Step 1 — Compute Major Cycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{mc} &= \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2, T_3) \\
       &= \text{LCM}(4, 5, 20) \\
       &= 20 \text{ time units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** LCM of 4, 5, and 20. Since 20 is a multiple of both 4 and 5, the LCM is 20.

### Step 2 — Apply Frame-Size Constraints

We need to find a valid $f$.

- **C1:** $f \geq \max(C_i) = \max(1, 2, 5) = 5$
- **C2:** $f \leq \min(T_i) = \min(4, 5, 20) = 4$

**Critical observation:** $f \geq 5$ AND $f \leq 4$ is **impossible** under the simple "single-frame-per-job" assumption. Therefore, we must **slice (partition) the tasks**.

### Step 3 — Slice Tasks to Fit in Frame

Let us **relax C3** by slicing $\tau_3$ (which has $C_3 = 5$) into smaller sub-jobs. Choose a frame size that satisfies the rest:

Try $f = 4$ (satisfies C2 = 4, but C1 requires 5, so slice $\tau_3$ into two parts: $C_{3,1} = 3$ and $C_{3,2} = 2$, each $\leq 4$).

- $C_1 = 1 \leq 4$ ✓
- $C_2 = 2 \leq 4$ ✓
- $C_{3,1} = 3 \leq 4$ ✓
- $C_{3,2} = 2 \leq 4$ ✓
- $T_{mc}/f = 20/4 = 5$ frames (integer ✓)

### Step 4 — Compute Number of Frames

$$
N_f = \frac{T_{mc}}{f} = \frac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ frames}
$$

Frame boundaries: $F_1 = [0,4)$, $F_2 = [4,8)$, $F_3 = [8,12)$, $F_4 = [12,16)$, $F_5 = [16,20)$.

### Step 5 — Compute Job Count per Major Cycle

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_1 &= T_{mc}/T_1 = 20/4 = 5 \text{ jobs of } \tau_1 \\
J_2 &= T_{mc}/T_2 = 20/5 = 4 \text{ jobs of } \tau_2 \\
J_3 &= T_{mc}/T_3 = 20/20 = 1 \text{ job of } \tau_3 \text{ (sliced into 2 sub-jobs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Allocate Jobs to Frames (Dispatch Table)

Total execution slots to fill: $5(1) + 4(2) + 1(5) = 5 + 8 + 5 = 18$ time units. Total capacity: $5 \times 4 = 20$ units. **Slack = 2 units** (for sporadic/aperiodic work).

| Frame | Time Slot | Task | Duration | Cumulative |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $F_1$ | [0,1) | $\tau_1$ | 1 | 1 |
| $F_1$ | [1,3) | $\tau_2$ | 2 | 3 |
| $F_1$ | [3,4) | Idle (slack) | 1 | 4 |
| $F_2$ | [4,5) | $\tau_1$ | 1 | 5 |
| $F_2$ | [5,8) | $\tau_3$ (slice 1) | 3 | 8 |
| $F_3$ | [8,9) | $\tau_1$ | 1 | 9 |
| $F_3$ | [9,11) | $\tau_2$ | 2 | 11 |
| $F_3$ | [11,12) | Idle (slack) | 1 | 12 |
| $F_4$ | [12,13) | $\tau_1$ | 1 | 13 |
| $F_4$ | [13,15) | $\tau_3$ (slice 2) | 2 | 15 |
| $F_4$ | [15,16) | $\tau_2$ | 1 | 16 |
| $F_5$ | [16,17) | $\tau_1$ | 1 | 17 |
| $F_5$ | [17,19) | $\tau_2$ | 2 | 19 |
| $F_5$ | [19,20) | Idle (slack) | 1 | 20 |

### Step 7 — Feasibility Check

- Every job of $\tau_1$ finishes at most $T_1 = 4$ after its release ✓
- Every job of $\tau_2$ finishes at most $T_2 = 5$ after its release ✓
- $\tau_3$ finishes at $t = 15 \leq D_3 = 20$ ✓
- Total utilization: $U = 1/4 + 2/5 + 5/20 = 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.25 = \mathbf{0.90}$ — feasible ✓

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Feasibility Decision Flow

| Test # | Condition to Check | If Fails → Action |
|:-:|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{mc} = \text{LCM}(T_i)$ computed correctly | Recompute with prime factorization |
| 2 | $\exists f$ satisfying C1, C2 simultaneously | If not, **slice tasks** to relax C3 |
| 3 | $C_i \leq f$ for all $i$ | Slice into sub-jobs of size $\leq f$ |
| 4 | $N_f = T_{mc}/f \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Choose different $f$ (e.g., divisor of $T_{mc}$) |
| 5 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot J_i \leq N_f \cdot f$ | Schedule infeasible — overload |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1$ | Hard infeasibility (necessary condition) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Step 6 (CPU utilization $\leq 1$) is a **necessary but not sufficient** condition for feasibility under clock-driven scheduling. Always verify by constructing the actual dispatch table.

## 3.3 Closed-Form Derivation of the Number of Frames

Let the major cycle be $T_{mc}$ and the chosen frame size be $f$. The number of frames is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_f &= \frac{T_{mc}}{f} \\
    &= \frac{1}{f} \cdot \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For $N_f$ to be a positive integer, $f$ must be a **divisor** of $T_{mc}$ that also satisfies $C_i \leq f$ and $f \leq T_{min}$.

The **slack fraction** (proportion of unused CPU) is:

$$
S = \frac{N_f \cdot f - \sum_{i=1}^{n} J_i \cdot C_i}{T_{mc}} = 1 - U
$$

where $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_i / T_i)$ is the total CPU utilization.

## 3.4 Extension: Handling Sporadic and Aperiodic Jobs

In a pure cyclic executive, there is no built-in mechanism for aperiodic jobs. Two standard extensions:

**(a) Polling Server:** A periodic task of period $T_{ps}$ and execution budget $C_{ps}$ is dedicated to servicing aperiodic requests. At every frame, the polling server's slot is used if there are pending aperiodic jobs; otherwise, the slot is wasted (idle).

**(b) Deferrable Server:** Similar to the polling server, but the budget is **preserved** (deferred) to the next period if unused — improving aperiodic response time at the cost of higher schedulability analysis complexity.

$$
\text{Average Aperiodic Response Time (Polling)} \approx \frac{T_{ps}}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Average Aperiodic Response Time (Deferrable)} \leq T_{ps}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Clock-Driven Scheduler

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a clock-driven real-time system, showing the data/control flow between offline design, the static dispatch table, the hardware clock, and the runtime dispatcher.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Offline Design Phase] --> A1[Task Set Specification]
    A1 --> A2[Compute Major Cycle via LCM]
    A2 --> A3[Select Frame Size f satisfying C1 to C4]
    A3 --> A4[Slice and Partition Tasks]
    A4 --> A5[Generate Static Dispatch Table]
    A5 --> A6[Store Table in ROM or Flash]

    B0[Runtime Phase] --> B1[Hardware Timer Hardware Clock]
    B1 -->|Tick Interrupt| B2[Scheduler Dispatcher]
    B2 -->|Table Lookup| A6
    A6 -->|Return Next Job| B2
    B2 -->|Dispatch| C1[CPU executes Job]
    C1 -->|Job Complete| B2
    B2 -->|Wait for next tick| B1

    C1 --> D1[Periodic Tasks]
    C1 --> D2[Sporadic Server Slot if any]
    C1 --> D3[Slack Time if any]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Frame-Size Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Given Task Set] --> S1[Compute T_mc = LCM of all T_i]
    S1 --> S2[Compute C_max = max C_i]
    S1 --> S3[Compute T_min = min T_i]
    S2 --> S4{C_max less or equal T_min?}
    S3 --> S4
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Choose f between C_max and T_min]
    S5 --> S6[T_mc / f is integer?]
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Valid Frame Size Found]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Try next divisor of T_mc]
    S8 --> S6
    S4 -- No --> S9[No single-frame fit possible]
    S9 --> S10[Apply Task Slicing]
    S10 --> S11[Re-check C1 C2 C4 with new sub-job sizes]
    S11 --> S7
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block: Clock-Driven vs. Other Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Static
        A[Clock Driven Table Driven]
        B[Cyclic Executive]
    end
    subgraph Dynamic Priority
        C[Rate Monotonic RMS]
        D[Deadline Monotonic DMS]
    end
    subgraph Dynamic Time
        E[EDF Earliest Deadline First]
        F[LLF Least Laxity First]
    end
    A -->|uses| B
    C -->|priority from period| C
    D -->|priority from deadline| D
    E -->|deadline based| E
    F -->|laxity based| F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Takeaway:** Clock-driven scheduling is the only paradigm where **scheduling decisions are 100% pre-computed**; all others make at least some decisions at runtime based on the active job's state.
