# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]
**Define clock-driven scheduling. List its two main characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**
Clock-driven scheduling is a static, table-driven scheduling approach used in hard real-time systems where the schedule of all jobs is computed offline and stored in a dispatch table. At runtime, a hardware clock interrupt triggers the dispatcher, which consults the table and releases the next job.

**Two main characteristics:**
1. **Offline schedule generation** — the entire job sequence is pre-computed.
2. **Online dispatch is trivial** — only an $O(1)$ table lookup at each tick.

*(Any two valid characteristics such as determinism, periodicity, low runtime overhead, suitability for hard real-time may also be accepted.)*

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between a *frame* and a *major cycle* in clock-driven scheduling.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Frame | Major Cycle |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| Definition | Fixed-duration time slice into which the major cycle is partitioned | The LCM of all task periods — the hyper-period after which the schedule repeats |
| Symbol | $f$ | $T_{mc}$ |
| Count | $N_f = T_{mc} / f$ frames per major cycle | Only **one** major cycle in the system schedule |
| Duration | Typically $f \leq T_{min}$ and $f \geq C_{max}$ | Much longer — equals LCM of all periods |
| Role | Unit of dispatcher activation | Unit of schedule repetition |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four constraints** that must be satisfied while choosing the frame size in a clock-driven (cyclic executive) scheduler. Illustrate with one example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a real-time system with three periodic tasks:

| Task | $T_i$ (ms) | $C_i$ (ms) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $\tau_1$ | 10 | 2 |
| $\tau_2$ | 15 | 3 |
| $\tau_3$ | 30 | 5 |

Determine a valid **frame size $f$** and construct the **dispatch table** for one major cycle.

---

### **Question B.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **architecture of a cyclic executive**. With the help of a block diagram, explain the offline scheduling and online dispatch phases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the task set:

| Task | $T_i$ | $C_i$ |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $\tau_1$ | 6 | 2 |
| $\tau_2$ | 10 | 3 |

(i) Compute the major cycle.
(ii) Find a valid frame size.
(iii) Construct the dispatch table and compute CPU utilization.

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to **Question A(a)** — Four Frame-Size Constraints

The four mandatory constraints are:

> **C1 — Frame Capacity:** $f \geq \max_{i}(C_i)$
> *Example:* If $C_{max} = 5$ ms, then $f \geq 5$ ms; otherwise a job cannot fit.

> **C2 — Frame Granularity:** $f \leq \min_{i}(T_i)$
> *Example:* If $T_{min} = 4$ ms, then $f \leq 4$ ms; otherwise the shortest-period task may not get a release opportunity in time.

> **C3 — Job-Partitionability:** $C_i \leq f$ for all $i$, OR the job is sliced into sub-jobs each of size $\leq f$.
> *Example:* A task with $C = 7$ ms and $f = 4$ ms must be split into two sub-jobs (e.g., 4 ms + 3 ms).

> **C4 — Integer Frame Count:** $T_{mc} / f \in \mathbb{Z}^+$
> *Example:* $T_{mc} = 30$ and $f = 4$ gives $7.5$ — invalid; try $f = 5$ giving $6$ frames — valid.

---

### Solution to **Question A(b)** — Dispatch Table Construction

**Step 1: Compute Major Cycle**

$$
T_{mc} = \text{LCM}(10, 15, 30) = 30 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Bounds**
- $C_{max} = 5$ ms → $f \geq 5$
- $T_{min} = 10$ ms → $f \leq 10$

Choose $f = 5$ ms. Check: $30/5 = 6$ frames (integer ✓).

**Step 3: Number of Frames**

$$
N_f = \frac{30}{5} = 6
$$

**Step 4: Number of Jobs per Major Cycle**
- $J_1 = 30/10 = 3$ jobs of $\tau_1$
- $J_2 = 30/15 = 2$ jobs of $\tau_2$
- $J_3 = 30/30 = 1$ job of $\tau_3$

**Step 5: Allocation**

Total slots: $3(2) + 2(3) + 1(5) = 6 + 6 + 5 = 17$ ms. Total capacity: $6 \times 5 = 30$ ms. **Slack = 13 ms.**

| Frame | Time | Task(s) | Notes |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|---|
| $F_1$ | [0,2) | $\tau_1$ | 2 ms |
| $F_1$ | [2,5) | $\tau_2$ | 3 ms |
| $F_2$ | [5,7) | $\tau_1$ | 2 ms |
| $F_2$ | [7,10) | $\tau_3$ | 5 ms (consumes 3 ms) — but $C_3 = 5$ > slot left. **Replan:** |
| $F_2$ | [7,10) | $\tau_3$ (partial 3 ms) | Need 2 more ms next frame |

**Revised Allocation (slicing $\tau_3$):**

| Frame | Time | Job |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $F_1$ | [0,2) | $\tau_1$ (2) |
| $F_1$ | [2,5) | $\tau_2$ (3) |
| $F_2$ | [5,7) | $\tau_1$ (2) |
| $F_2$ | [7,10) | $\tau_3$-slice-A (3) |
| $F_3$ | [10,12) | $\tau_1$ (2) |
| $F_3$ | [12,14) | $\tau_3$-slice-B (2) |
| $F_3$ | [14,15) | $\tau_2$ (1) |
| $F_4$ | [15,20) | Idle (slack — usable for aperiodic) |
| $F_5$ | [20,25) | Idle (slack) |
| $F_6$ | [25,30) | Idle (slack) |

**CPU Utilization Check:**

$$
U = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{15} + \frac{5}{30} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.167 = \mathbf{0.567} = 56.7\%
$$

Feasible ✓.

---

### Solution to **Question B(a)** — Cyclic Executive Architecture

The **cyclic executive** has two distinct phases:

**Phase 1 — Offline (Design Time):**
- Task set is enumerated: $\tau_i = (T_i, C_i, D_i)$.
- Major cycle $T_{mc}$ is computed.
- Frame size $f$ is selected using the four constraints.
- Static **dispatch table** is generated and stored in non-volatile memory (ROM/Flash).

**Phase 2 — Online (Run Time):**
- A **periodic timer interrupt** (e.g., every $f$ ms) awakens the dispatcher.
- The dispatcher indexes into the dispatch table and releases the next pre-scheduled job.
- The job runs to completion (or preemption if the kernel supports it).
- The dispatcher goes back to sleep until the next tick.

*(Block diagram: see Section 4.1 above for the complete architecture flow.)*

---

### Solution to **Question B(b)** — Alternative Task Set

**Step 1: Major Cycle**

$$
T_{mc} = \text{LCM}(6, 10) = 30
$$

**Step 2: Frame Size**
- $C_{max} = 3$ → $f \geq 3$
- $T_{min} = 6$ → $f \leq 6$

Valid choices: $f = 3$ or $f = 6$. Choose $f = 3$ for finer granularity.

**Step 3: Number of Frames**

$$
N_f = \frac{30}{3} = 10 \text{ frames}
$$

**Step 4: Number of Jobs**
- $J_1 = 30/6 = 5$ jobs of $\tau_1$
- $J_2 = 30/10 = 3$ jobs of $\tau_2$

**Step 5: Dispatch Table**

| Frame | Time | Job | Duration |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| $F_1$ | [0,2) | $\tau_1$ | 2 |
| $F_1$ | [2,3) | $\tau_2$ (partial 1) | 1 |
| $F_2$ | [3,5) | $\tau_2$ (cont. 2) | 2 |
| $F_2$ | [5,6) | $\tau_1$ | 1 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |

(Complete table can be written out; pattern alternates per period.)

**Step 6: CPU Utilization**

$$
U = \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.333 + 0.300 = \mathbf{0.633} = 63.3\%
$$

Feasible ✓.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Clock-Driven Scheduling Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify all four constraints** — Students often check only C1 and C2, missing C3 and C4. KTU examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for stating and verifying C1–C4.
> 2. **Ignoring the integer constraint on $N_f$** — Always confirm $T_{mc}/f \in \mathbb{Z}^+$; otherwise the chosen $f$ is invalid.
> 3. **Confusing frame size with task period** — A common blunder. Frame size $f$ is a *design parameter*, not a task period.
> 4. **Failing to slice long jobs** — When $C_i > T_{min}$, the task MUST be sliced; this is worth **2–3 marks** in KTU Part B.
> 5. **CPU utilization > 1** — If $\sum C_i/T_i > 1$, the schedule is **infeasible by definition**; no clever framing can save it.
> 6. **Missing slack time** — Always show how unused time slots (slack) are utilized for sporadic/aperiodic jobs.
> 7. **Not mentioning jitter** — KTU examiners often award a mark for stating that clock-driven scheduling suffers from *release-time jitter* up to $f - C_i$.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (1-liner):** Clock-driven scheduling is a *static, table-driven* real-time scheduling approach where all job releases are pre-decided and triggered by clock ticks.
- **Cyclic Executive** is the canonical implementation.
- **Major cycle** = $\text{LCM}$ of all task periods. It is the schedule repetition period.
- **Frame** = fixed-duration slice of the major cycle. A dispatcher wakes up at every frame boundary.
- **Four constraints (MEMORIZE):** $f \geq C_{max}$, $f \leq T_{min}$, $C_i \leq f$ (or sliced), $T_{mc}/f \in \mathbb{Z}^+$.
- **Task slicing** is required when $C_i > T_{min}$.
- **CPU utilization** $U = \sum C_i/T_i \leq 1$ is necessary but not sufficient.
- **Slack time** in frames can service **aperiodic jobs** (polling/deferrable server).
- **Advantages:** deterministic, $O(1)$ runtime, certifiable, no priority inversion.
- **Disadvantages:** rigid, brittle to changes, hard to handle aperiodics, jitter.
- **Used in:** AUTOSAR OS, ARINC 653, VxWorks time-partitioned mode, safety-critical ECUs.
- **Comparison keywords:** "static vs dynamic", "offline vs online", "table-driven vs priority-driven".
- **Pitfalls to avoid:** forgetting C3 or C4, not slicing, mixing frame and period symbols, ignoring jitter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** For 14-mark Part-B questions on clock-driven scheduling, structure your answer as **(i) Definition → (ii) Architecture/Phases → (iii) Four Constraints → (iv) Worked Example with Dispatch Table → (v) Feasibility Check + CPU Utilization → (vi) Pros/Cons**. This structure is exactly what KTU board examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Table Driven Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Table Driven Scheduling ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Table Driven Scheduling** is a *deterministic, clock-driven* scheduling paradigm in which the complete sequence of job executions is **pre-computed off-line** at design time and stored as a static **scheduling table** (also called a *dispatch table* or *cyclic executive schedule*). At run time, the dispatcher performs a single, bounded table-lookup operation to decide which job must execute in the current scheduling tick. The schedule repeats periodically over the **hyperperiod** of the task set. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"In table-driven scheduling, the schedule of jobs is pre-determined and stored in a table that is consulted by the dispatcher at run time to decide which job to execute next."* > — (PECST748, Module 2, Real-Time Systems) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Train Timetable Imagine the **Indian Railways reservation chart** printed for a specific platform on a specific day. Every train arrival, departure, platform number, and engine type has been *decided weeks in advance* by the control room. The station master on duty does **not** make complex decisions; he simply **glances at the chart and announces the next train**. | Concept in Scheduling | Real-World Analogy | |---|---| | Pre-computed schedule | The printed railway timetable | | Dispatcher (OS kernel) | The station master on duty | | Current time tick | The wall clock at the platform | | Lookup operation | Glancing at the chart | | Job release | Train arriving at the platform | | Schedule table (Array) | The big notice-board chart | The station master (dispatcher) is *not* solving a complex algorithm. The hard work (deciding which train goes where) was already done by the planning office (off-line scheduler) and frozen in ink (read-only table). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Table Driven?** > - **Bounded run-time overhead** — O(1) table lookup instead of O(n) priority computations. > - **Absolute predictability** — schedule is provable at design time. > - **Certified for safety-critical** systems (DO-178C, IEC 61508, ISO 26262). > - **Zero on-line scheduling decisions** — eliminates a major class of timing faults. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Used in the KTU 2024 Scheme - **Schedule Table** — A 1-D or 2-D array mapping each *time tick* → *job to execute*. - **Major Cycle (Hyperperiod)** — The smallest interval after which the entire schedule repeats. Numerically: $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$. - **Minor Cycle (Frame)** — A subdivision of the major cycle, an indivisible unit of time used as the *table resolution*. - **Cyclic Executive** — A tiny run-time kernel that walks the schedule table on every tick. - **Slot Time** — One row of the schedule table; assigns a single job to a time interval. ### 1.4 Physical & System Constants | Symbol | Standard Value / Meaning | |---|---| | **Frame size $f$** | 1 ms – 10 ms (typical for avionics) | | **Hyperperiod $H$** | $\text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ | | **Table size $N$** | $H / f$ entries | | **Tick resolution** | Usually 1 µs – 1 ms | | **Context switch overhead** | Must be **≪ $f$** for table-driven guarantees | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Phases of Table Driven Scheduling Table driven scheduling is decomposed into **two distinct, non-overlapping phases** that KTU examiners love to test: #### Phase A — Off-Line (Design-Time, Pre-Deployment) 1. Collect the task set $\{\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_n\}$ with periods $\{T_i\}$ and worst-case execution times $\{e_i\}$. 2. Compute the **hyperperiod** $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, \ldots, T_n)$. 3. Generate the **list of all job releases** within $[0, H)$. For each $\tau_i$: jobs are released at $t = k \cdot T_i$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, \frac{H}{T_i} - 1$. 4. Apply a deterministic, off-line scheduling algorithm (e.g., *Earliest Deadline First on jobs*, *Rate Monotonic on jobs*, or a heuristic) to produce a *valid static schedule* $\sigma(t)$ for $t \in [0, H)$. 5. Validate the schedule using **schedulability analysis** — every job must complete before its deadline, and no two jobs overlap. 6. Encode $\sigma(t)$ into a **read-only schedule table** and burn it into ROM/Flash of the target ECU. #### Phase B — On-Line (Run-Time, In-The-Loop) 1. The **clock interrupt** (driven by a hardware timer with period = frame size $f$) fires. 2. The **cyclic executive** reads the current time tick $k$. 3. Indexes the schedule table: `JobID = ScheduleTable[k]`. 4. Saves the context of the previously running job (if any). 5. Restores the context of `JobID` and transfers control to it. 6. The job runs to completion (or until the next tick). 7. Repeat indefinitely — the table wraps around at index $H/f$. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why off-line?** Eliminates the cost and uncertainty of priority computation at run time. Real-time guarantees become *mathematical proofs* rather than *probabilistic estimates*. - **Why use a hyperperiod?** Periodic tasks by definition repeat forever, so once we have a valid schedule for one hyperperiod, it is valid *for all time*. - **Why divide the major cycle into minor cycles (frames)?** Frames impose a **regular release pattern**, bound the *maximum blocking time* to one frame, and make the table compact. - **Why store in ROM?** Guarantees the schedule cannot be corrupted by runaway tasks or memory faults (radiation, EMI, software bugs). > [!TIP] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Table driven scheduling is the *only* scheduling paradigm whose worst-case response time equals the *exact* value derivable from the table — there is no scheduling jitter caused by priority inversion, preemption, or on-line decisions. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The complete list of exam-ready formulas for Table Driven Scheduling.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hyperperiod | $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ | Time units (ms, µs) | | 2 | Number of jobs of $\tau_i$ in one hyperperiod | $N_i = H / T_i$ | Dimensionless integer | | 3 | Total job releases in $H$ | $N_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | Number of table entries (slots) | $S = H / f$ | $f$ = frame size | | 5 | Processor utilization (feasibility) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (e_i / T_i)$ | Must be $\leq 1$ | | 6 | Required table memory | $M = S \times \log_2(n)$ bits | $n$ = number of jobs/task IDs | | 7 | Run-time dispatcher cost | $C_{\text{dispatch}} = O(1)$ lookup | **Constant**, independent of $n$ | | 8 | Maximum frame constraint | $2f - \gcd(T_i, f) \geq e_j$ | Liu & Layland frame constraint | | 9 | Response time (job $J_{i,k}$) | $R_{i,k} = f_{end}(J_{i,k}) - r_{i,k}$ | Read directly from table | | 10 | Idle time within hyperperiod | $I = H - \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \cdot e_i$ | If $I \geq 0$, schedule exists | > [!NOTE] > **Note on table syntax:** Vertical bars in formulas (e.g. absolute value, set cardinality) are written as `\vert` or `\mid` inside markdown tables to prevent column breaking. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Avionics** — Boeing 777, Airbus A320 use cyclic executives (ARINC 653 + time-partitioned dispatch tables). - **Automotive ECUs** — Engine control units (ECU) on every modern car; AUTOSAR OS supports table-driven dispatch. - **Industrial PLCs** — Ladder-logic PLCs run on table-driven cyclic scans. - **Medical devices** — Pacemakers, infusion pumps — *all* table-driven for certification. - **Satellite on-board computers** — Radiation-hardened, table-driven software dominates. - **Mars Rovers** — NASA's VxWorks-based systems use cyclic executives for the highest-criticality tasks. ### 2.5 Pre-Conditions & Assumptions For table driven scheduling to be **valid**, the following must hold (often asked as a 3-mark question): 1. All tasks must be **periodic** and **independent** (no shared resources with unbounded blocking). 2. All jobs of a task are **known at design time** (release times, execution times, deadlines). 3. The **worst-case execution time** must be bounded and measurable. 4. There is **no inter-task communication during execution** (or it is fully bounded). 5. System must be **closed** — no aperiodic/sporadic jobs in the hard-real-time portion (they go to a background slack-stealing slot). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often say *"Table driven scheduling supports aperiodic tasks."* This is **partially true** — aperiodic tasks can only be placed in *idle* or *spare* slots of the table. The hard-real-time part of the table is strictly periodic. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Building a Schedule Table from Scratch **Problem (KTU-Style):** Consider three periodic tasks to be scheduled on a single processor using the **Table Driven** approach. | Task | Period $T_i$ | Worst-case execution time $e_i$ | Relative deadline $D_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | $\tau_1$ | 4 ms | 1 ms | 4 ms | | $\tau_2$ | 5 ms | 2 ms | 5 ms | | $\tau_3$ | 20 ms | 2 ms | 20 ms | Assume a **frame size $f = 1$ ms**. Build the schedule table for one hyperperiod and verify feasibility. --- #### Step 1 — Compute the Hyperperiod $$ \begin{aligned} H &= \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, T_3) \\ &= \text{lcm}(4, 5, 20) \\ &= \text{lcm}(4, 5) = 20 \text{ (since } 20 \text{ is a multiple of } 4 \text{ and } 5\text{)} \\ &= 20 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** The lcm of 4 and 5 is 20. Since $T_3 = 20$ already divides 20, the final lcm remains 20 ms. --- #### Step 2 — Enumerate All Job Releases within $[0, 20)$ ms $$ \begin{aligned} \tau_1 &: \text{jobs at } t = 0,\ 4,\ 8,\ 12,\ 16 \quad \Rightarrow N_1 = H / T_1 = 20/4 = 5 \text{ jobs} \\ \tau_2 &: \text{jobs at } t = 0,\ 5,\ 10,\ 15 \quad \Rightarrow N_2 = H / T_2 = 20/5 = 4 \text{ jobs} \\ \tau_3 &: \text{jobs at } t = 0 \quad \Rightarrow N_3 = H / T_3 = 20/20 = 1 \text{ job} \end{aligned} $$ **Total job releases** $N_{\text{total}} = 5 + 4 + 1 = 10$ jobs. **Logic:** Each task $\tau_i$ releases one job at every integer multiple of its period, and stops at the hyperperiod boundary. --- #### Step 3 — Check Processor Utilization Bound $$ \begin{aligned} U &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{e_i}{T_i} \\ &= \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{5} + \frac{2}{20} \\ &= 0.250 + 0.400 + 0.100 \\ &= 0.750 \end{aligned} $$ Since $U = 0.750 \leq 1.0$, the **necessary condition** for feasibility is satisfied. **Logic:** Utilization must be ≤ 100% for *any* valid schedule to exist. --- #### Step 4 — Determine the Number of Table Entries (Slots) $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \frac{H}{f} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 20 \text{ entries} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** With a 1 ms frame and a 20 ms major cycle, the table has 20 rows. --- #### Step 5 — Construct the Schedule Table using EDF on Jobs We list all 10 job releases in chronological order, breaking ties by job number. Then assign them to the 20 available 1-ms slots, choosing the job with the **earliest absolute deadline** at every slot. | Job | Release $r$ | Exec $e$ | Abs. Deadline $d$ | |---|---|---|---| | $J_{1,1}$ | 0 | 1 | 4 | | $J_{2,1}$ | 0 | 2 | 5 | | $J_{3,1}$ | 0 | 2 | 20 | | $J_{1,2}$ | 4 | 1 | 8 | | $J_{2,2}$ | 5 | 2 | 10 | | $J_{1,3}$ | 8 | 1 | 12 | | $J_{2,3}$ | 10 | 2 | 15 | | $J_{1,4}$ | 12 | 1 | 16 | | $J_{2,4}$ | 15 | 2 | 20 | | $J_{1,5}$ | 16 | 1 | 20 | **Logic:** A job with release $r$, execution $e$, and period $T$ has absolute deadline $d = r + T$ (because $D_i = T_i$). --- #### Step 6 — Fill the Table Slot-by-Slot We process slot $k$ from $k = 0$ to $k = 19$. At each slot, pick the ready job with the **earliest absolute deadline**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Slot } 0 &: \text{Ready} = \{J_{1,1}(d=4), J_{2,1}(d=5), J_{3,1}(d=20)\} \Rightarrow J_{1,1} \\ \text{Slot } 1 &: \text{Ready} = \{J_{2,1}(d=5), J_{3,1}(d=20)\} \Rightarrow J_{2,1} \text{ (1/2)} \\ \text{Slot } 2 &: \text{Ready} = \{J_{2,1}(d=5), J_{3,1}(d=20)\} \Rightarrow J_{2,1} \text{ (2/2)} \\ \text{Slot } 3 &: \text{Ready} = \{J_{3,1}(d=20)\} \Rightarrow J_{3,1} \text{ (1/2)} \\ \text{Slot } 4 &: \text{Release } J_{1,2} \Rightarrow \text{Ready} = \{J_{1,2}(d=8), J_{3,1}(d=20)\} \Rightarrow J_{1,2} \\ \text{Slot } 5 &: \text{Release } J_{2,2} \Rightarrow \text{Ready} = \{J_{2,2}(d=10), J_{3,1}(d=20)\} \Rightarrow J_{2,2} \text{ (1/2)} \\ \text{Slot } 6 &: \text{Ready} = \{J_{2,2}(d=10), J_{3,1}(d=20)\} \Rightarrow J_{2,2} \text{ (2/2)} \\ \text{Slot } 7 &: \text{Ready} = \{J_{3,1}(d=20)\} \Rightarrow J_{3,1} \text{ (2/2)} \\ \text{Slot } 8 &: \text{Release } J_{1,3} \Rightarrow J_{1,3} \\ \text{Slot } 9 &: \text{IDLE} \\ \text{Slot } 10 &: \text{Release } J_{2,3} \Rightarrow J_{2,3} \text{ (1/2)} \\ \text{Slot } 11 &: \text{Ready} = \{J_{2,3}(d=15)\} \Rightarrow J_{2,3} \text{ (2/2)} \\ \text{Slot } 12 &: \text{Release } J_{1,4} \Rightarrow J_{1,4} \\ \text{Slot } 13, 14 &: \text{IDLE} \\ \text{Slot } 15 &: \text{Release } J_{2,4} \Rightarrow J_{2,4} \text{ (1/2)} \\ \text{Slot } 16 &: \text{Release } J_{1,5}; \text{Ready} = \{J_{1,5}(d=20), J_{2,4}(d=20)\}; \text{tie, } \tau_1 \text{ wins} \Rightarrow J_{1,5} \\ \text{Slot } 17 &: J_{2,4} \text{ (2/2)} \\ \text{Slot } 18, 19 &: \text{IDLE} \end{aligned} $$ --- #### Step 7 — Final Schedule Table (The Burned-In Artifact) | Slot $k$ | $t$ (ms) | Job | Deadline | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0–1 | $J_{1,1}$ | 4 | | 1 | 1–2 | $J_{2,1}$ | 5 | | 2 | 2–3 | $J_{2,1}$ | 5 | | 3 | 3–4 | $J_{3,1}$ | 20 | | 4 | 4–5 | $J_{1,2}$ | 8 | | 5 | 5–6 | $J_{2,2}$ | 10 | | 6 | 6–7 | $J_{2,2}$ | 10 | | 7 | 7–8 | $J_{3,1}$ | 20 | | 8 | 8–9 | $J_{1,3}$ | 12 | | 9 | 9–10 | IDLE | — | | 10 | 10–11 | $J_{2,3}$ | 15 | | 11 | 11–12 | $J_{2,3}$ | 15 | | 12 | 12–13 | $J_{1,4}$ | 16 | | 13 | 13–14 | IDLE | — | | 14 | 14–15 | IDLE | — | | 15 | 15–16 | $J_{2,4}$ | 20 | | 16 | 16–17 | $J_{1,5}$ | 20 | | 17 | 17–18 | $J_{2,4}$ | 20 | | 18 | 18–19 | IDLE | — | | 19 | 19–20 | IDLE | — | **Logic verification:** Every job finishes at or before its deadline. ✓ --- #### Step 8 — Compute Idle Time $$ \begin{aligned} I &= H - \sum_{i=1}^{3} N_i \cdot e_i \\ &= 20 - (5 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 2) \\ &= 20 - (5 + 8 + 2) \\ &= 20 - 15 = 5 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ This matches the **5 IDLE slots** observed in the table. ✓ --- #### Step 9 — Read Response Times Directly from the Table $$ \begin{aligned} R_{1,1} &= 1 - 0 = 1 \text{ ms} \\ R_{2,1} &= 3 - 0 = 3 \text{ ms} \\ R_{3,1} &= 8 - 0 = 8 \text{ ms} \\ R_{1,5} &= 17 - 16 = 1 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic:** In a table-driven system, response times are *not estimated* — they are *read directly* off the table, which is the entire selling point of the approach. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Table Driven Scheduler ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef offline fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#88c0d0,color:#eceff4,stroke-width:2px classDef rom fill:#5e3a1f,stroke:#ebcb8b,color:#eceff4,stroke-width:2px classDef kernel fill:#1f4a2f,stroke:#a3be8c,color:#eceff4,stroke-width:2px classDef task fill:#3a1f4a,stroke:#b48ead,color:#eceff4,stroke-width:2px A[Task Set Specification
Period, WCET, Deadline]:::offline B[Off-Line Scheduler
Design-Time Tool]:::offline C[Schedulability Analyzer
Mathematical Proof]:::offline D[Schedule Table Generator
Compile to ROM Image]:::offline E[ROM / Flash Memory
Burned ScheduleTable[0..H] ]:::rom F[Hardware Timer
Periodic Interrupt f kHz]:::kernel G[Cyclic Executive
On-Line Dispatcher]:::kernel H[Context Save
Prev Job State]:::kernel I[Table Lookup
JobID equals Table tick]:::kernel J[Context Restore
Load JobID State]:::kernel K[Job Execution
Run to Completion of Frame]:::task L[Idle Slot Handler
Background / Aperiodic]:::task A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> I F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J --> K I -.->|JobID equals IDLE| L K --> G L --> G ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow — How a Single Tick is Dispatched ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef state fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#88c0d0,color:#eceff4 classDef action fill:#1f4a2f,stroke:#a3be8c,color:#eceff4 classDef decision fill:#5e3a1f,stroke:#ebcb8b,color:#eceff4 S1((System Boot)):::state --> A1[Initialize Hardware Timer]:::action A1 --> A2[Load Schedule Table Pointer]:::action A2 --> A3[Set tick counter k equals 0]:::action A3 --> A4[Tick Interrupt k plus 1]:::action A4 --> A5[Save current job context]:::action A5 --> A6[Read JobID equals Table k]:::action A6 --> D1{Is JobID IDLE?}:::decision D1 -- No --> A7[Restore JobID context]:::action A7 --> A8[Dispatch to JobID]:::action A8 --> A9[Wait for next tick]:::action A9 --> A4 D1 -- Yes --> A10[Run Idle Handler
Aperiodic / Background]:::action A10 --> A9 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Off-Line + On-Line Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef block fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#88c0d0,color:#eceff4 classDef store fill:#5e3a1f,stroke:#ebcb8b,color:#eceff4 classDef live fill:#1f4a2f,stroke:#a3be8c,color:#eceff4 subgraph OFFLINE["OFF-LINE PHASE (Design-Time, Host PC)"] direction TB T1[Input Task Set]:::block T2[Hyperperiod Calculator]:::block T3[Job Release Enumerator]:::block T4[Off-Line Job Scheduler
EDF or Heuristic]:::block T5[Validation and Proof]:::block T6[ROM Image Generator]:::block end subgraph STORAGE["PERSISTENT STORAGE (Target Hardware)"] direction TB R1[ROM Image of
Schedule Table]:::store end subgraph ONLINE["ON-LINE PHASE (Target ECU, Real-Time)"] direction TB L1[Clock Interrupt Service]:::live L2[Table Indexer k mod S]:::live L3[Context Switch Logic]:::live L4[Task Execution Layer]:::live end T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 --> T6 --> R1 R1 --> L2 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 --> L4 L4 -.feedback.-> L1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a logical function. The **ROM Image** is the *only* bridge between off-line and on-line — once deployed, the schedule is frozen and the on-line kernel performs *no* scheduling decisions. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Define Table Driven Scheduling. List any two of its key advantages.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Definition (2 marks):* Table Driven Scheduling is a *clock-driven, deterministic* scheduling technique in which the schedule of jobs is **pre-computed off-line at design time** and stored in a static **schedule table**. At run time, the dispatcher simply indexes the table using the current time tick to determine which job to execute, achieving $O(1)$ scheduling overhead. *Any two advantages (1 mark):* 1. **Bounded and minimal run-time overhead** — single table lookup, independent of task count. 2. **Provable worst-case timing** — every job's response time is *known exactly* from the table, enabling formal certification (DO-178C, IEC 61508). 3. *Bonus:* Determinism — no on-line priority inversions possible. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]** **What is a hyperperiod? Why is it central to Table Driven Scheduling?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Hyperperiod definition (1 mark):* The **hyperperiod** $H$ of a periodic task set $\{\tau_1, \ldots, \tau_n\}$ is the **least common multiple** of all task periods: $$H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$$ *Why it is central (2 marks):* 1. The schedule is computed once for the interval $[0, H)$ and **repeats identically** for all $t \geq H$ because every task's release pattern also repeats every $H$ time units. 2. The schedule table needs only $H / f$ entries, where $f$ is the frame size. The hyperperiod directly determines **memory footprint** of the on-line table and the **off-line computation time**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **off-line and on-line phases** of Table Driven Scheduling with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Consider the task set: $\tau_1 = (T_1 = 4, e_1 = 1)$, $\tau_2 = (T_2 = 6, e_2 = 2)$, $\tau_3 = (T_3 = 12, e_3 = 2)$. Using a frame size $f = 2$ ms, build the complete **schedule table** for one hyperperiod and verify feasibility. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Off-line & On-line phases (7 marks):** **Off-line phase (design-time, runs on host PC) — 4 marks:** 1. Collect the periodic task set with periods $T_i$ and WCETs $e_i$. 2. Compute hyperperiod $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, \ldots, T_n)$. 3. Enumerate all job releases within $[0, H)$: each $\tau_i$ contributes $H/T_i$ jobs. 4. Run an off-line scheduling algorithm (e.g., EDF on the job set) to produce $\sigma(t)$ for $t \in [0, H)$. 5. Validate the schedule — every job must complete before its absolute deadline. 6. Encode $\sigma(t)$ as a ROM image of the schedule table. **On-line phase (run-time, runs on target ECU) — 3 marks:** 1. Hardware timer issues a periodic interrupt every $f$ time units. 2. Cyclic executive reads `JobID = Table[tick]`. 3. Context switch to `JobID` and execute for one frame. 4. Repeat indefinitely; table wraps at index $H/f$. *(Block diagram — refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.)* --- **(b) Building the schedule table (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Hyperperiod:** $$H = \text{lcm}(4, 6, 12) = 12 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 2 — Frame count:** $$S = H / f = 12 / 2 = 6 \text{ entries}$$ **Step 3 — Job releases in $[0, 12)$:** - $\tau_1$: jobs at $t = 0, 4, 8$ → **3 jobs**, each $e_1 = 1$ ms - $\tau_2$: jobs at $t = 0, 6$ → **2 jobs**, each $e_2 = 2$ ms - $\tau_3$: job at $t = 0$ → **1 job**, $e_3 = 2$ ms **Step 4 — Utilization check:** $$U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{2}{12} = 0.25 + 0.333 + 0.167 = 0.750 \leq 1 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Note the frame constraint:** A job of $e = 2$ ms can fit inside one frame $f = 2$ ms. $\checkmark$ **Step 6 — Build the table (using EDF on jobs):** | Slot | $t$ (ms) | Job | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0–2 | $J_{2,1}$ | Earliest deadline 6 | | 1 | 2–4 | $J_{3,1}$ | d = 12 | | 2 | 4–6 | $J_{1,2}$ | d = 8; release at 4 | | 3 | 6–8 | $J_{2,2}$ | d = 12; release at 6 | | 4 | 8–10 | $J_{1,3}$ | d = 12; release at 8 | | 5 | 10–12 | IDLE | No jobs remain | **Step 7 — Verification:** - $J_{1,1}$ must be scheduled — but wait, it is released at $t=0$ and has deadline 4. The table above does not include it. **Correction** — apply EDF strictly at $t=0$: ready jobs are $J_{1,1}(d=4), J_{2,1}(d=6), J_{3,1}(d=12)$. Earliest deadline is $J_{1,1}$. | Slot | $t$ (ms) | Job | |---|---|---| | 0 | 0–2 | $J_{1,1}$ (d=4) — first half of $J_{1,1}$ needs only 1 ms, but frame = 2 ms | | 1 | 2–4 | $J_{2,1}$ (d=6) | | 2 | 4–6 | $J_{1,2}$ (d=8) | | 3 | 6–8 | $J_{2,2}$ (d=12) | | 4 | 8–10 | $J_{1,3}$ (d=12) | | 5 | 10–12 | $J_{3,1}$ (d=12) | All jobs complete by their deadlines. ✓ **Feasibility verified.** --- #### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Compare **Table Driven Scheduling** with **Priority Driven Scheduling**. Highlight at least four points of comparison. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A real-time system has the following task set to be scheduled using Table Driven Scheduling: $\tau_1 = (T_1 = 3, e_1 = 1)$, $\tau_2 = (T_2 = 5, e_2 = 1)$. Choose an appropriate frame size $f$, compute the hyperperiod, and design the schedule table. Also compute the **response time of the second job of $\tau_1$**. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):** | # | Parameter | Table Driven | Priority Driven | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Decision time | Off-line (design) | On-line (run-time) | | 2 | Run-time overhead | $O(1)$ table lookup | $O(n)$ priority recomputation | | 3 | Predictability | Exact, provable | Bounded but estimated | | 4 | Flexibility to aperiodic jobs | Limited (idle slots only) | High (background/server tasks) | | 5 | Memory footprint | Requires $H/f$ table entries | Requires priority queue | | 6 | Certification suitability | Excellent (DO-178C) | Conditional | | 7 | Best suited for | Highly periodic, safety-critical | Mixed workload systems | *(Any four of the above earn full marks.)* --- **(b) Schedule design (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Choose $f$.** The frame must satisfy $f \geq \max(e_i) = 1$ and $f$ must divide $H$. A natural choice is $f = 1$ ms. **Step 2 — Hyperperiod:** $$H = \text{lcm}(3, 5) = 15 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3 — Job releases in $[0, 15)$:** - $\tau_1$: jobs at $t = 0, 3, 6, 9, 12$ → **5 jobs** - $\tau_2$: jobs at $t = 0, 5, 10$ → **3 jobs** **Step 4 — Utilization check:** $$U = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} = \frac{5+3}{15} = \frac{8}{15} \approx 0.533 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Schedule table (15 entries, EDF):** | Slot | $t$ | Job | Deadline | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0–1 | $J_{1,1}$ | 3 | | 1 | 1–2 | $J_{2,1}$ | 5 | | 2 | 2–3 | IDLE | — | | 3 | 3–4 | $J_{1,2}$ | 6 | | 4 | 4–5 | IDLE | — | | 5 | 5–6 | $J_{2,2}$ | 10 | | 6 | 6–7 | $J_{1,3}$ | 9 | | 7 | 7–8 | IDLE | — | | 8 | 8–9 | IDLE | — | | 9 | 9–10 | $J_{1,4}$ | 12 | | 10 | 10–11 | $J_{2,3}$ | 15 | | 11 | 11–12 | IDLE | — | | 12 | 12–13 | $J_{1,5}$ | 15 | | 13 | 13–14 | IDLE | — | | 14 | 14–15 | IDLE | — | **Step 6 — Response time of $J_{1,2}$:** - Release time: $r_{1,2} = 3$ ms - Completion time: $f_{end} = 4$ ms (read from table) - **Response time**: $$R_{1,2} = 4 - 3 = 1 \text{ ms}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (3-Mark & 14-Mark Questions)** > > 1. **Confusing hyperperiod with frame size.** Hyperperiod $H$ is the *whole schedule*; frame $f$ is the *table granularity*. Students often write $H = f$, losing 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to verify $U \leq 1$.** Even a "correct-looking" table may be infeasible. Always state the utilization. > 3. **Off-by-one in slot indexing.** Slot $k$ covers $[kf, (k+1)f)$. Writing the table with mismatched intervals is a classic 1-mark deduction. > 4. **Ignoring tie-breaking in EDF.** When two jobs share a deadline, the examiner expects you to state the tie-break rule (e.g., lowest task index wins). > 5. **Stating "table driven scheduling is dynamic."** It is **static / off-line**. This single-word mistake can cost a full 3-mark question. > 6. **Forgetting aperiodic handling.** If the question mentions "sporadic events," explicitly state they go to the *idle slot handler* with slack stealing. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2, Table Driven Scheduling** - ☐ **Definition** — Pre-computed schedule stored in a table; on-line dispatcher does a single lookup. - ☐ **Two phases** — Off-line (design) + On-line (dispatch). They are **strictly separated**. - ☐ **Hyperperiod formula** — $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$. - ☐ **Number of jobs of $\tau_i$** — $N_i = H / T_i$. - ☐ **Number of table entries** — $S = H / f$, where $f$ = frame size. - ☐ **Feasibility check** — $U = \sum e_i / T_i \leq 1$ (necessary, not sufficient). - ☐ **Run-time cost** — $O(1)$, **independent** of $n$. - ☐ **Response time** — read directly from table; no estimation. - ☐ **Memory cost** — table grows linearly with $H/f$. - ☐ **Limitations** — poor support for aperiodic jobs; static; large hyperperiods ⇒ large tables. - ☐ **Best for** — highly periodic, safety-critical, certifiable systems (avionics, automotive, medical). - ☐ **Frame constraints** — $f$ must be ≥ max $e_i$; $f$ must divide $H$ for clean wrap-around. - ☐ **Tie-breaking** in EDF — state the rule explicitly in your answer. - ☐ **Comparison with priority driven** — off-line vs. on-line; $O(1)$ vs. $O(n)$; certifiable vs. flexible. - ☐ **Cyclic executive** — the tiny kernel that walks the table; *not* a scheduler, just a dispatcher.

topics.title: Cyclic Schedulers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cyclic Schedulers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cyclic Scheduler** (also called a **Cyclic Executive**) is a table-driven, non-priority-based scheduling mechanism used in hard real-time systems where a set of periodic tasks is dispatched according to a **precomputed static schedule** that repeats at a fixed interval known as the **major cycle** (or **hyper-period**).

In this approach, the system designer **offline** constructs a schedule by partitioning time into equal-length **frames** (minor cycles). Each frame is a contiguous block of time during which a specific subset of jobs executes to completion without preemption. The scheduler simply steps through a lookup table — known as the **dispatch table** — at every clock interrupt (typically the frame boundary) to determine which task to launch.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Cyclic Schedulers use a static, pre-computed schedule table that is executed cyclically. The schedule repeats every major cycle, and tasks are allocated to frames based on their periods and execution times. No runtime priority arbitration is required.*

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

- **Major Cycle (Hyper-period):** The smallest interval of time after which the entire periodic task set pattern repeats. Equivalent to the **LCM (Least Common Multiple)** of all task periods.
- **Minor Cycle (Frame):** A fixed-duration time slice that subdivides the major cycle. Each frame begins with a clock interrupt and dispatches one (or a small batch of) job(s).
- **Cyclic Executive:** The runtime dispatcher that walks through the dispatch table at every frame boundary.
- **Job:** A single invocation/instance of a task.
- **Frame Size (f):** Duration of one minor cycle, usually a power-of-two multiple of the clock tick.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Train Timetable

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Think of a **suburban railway timetable**. The railways don't decide at the moment of arrival *which* train to dispatch next based on crowding or priority. Instead, a fixed timetable is published years in advance (the *dispatch table*). Every **hour boundary** (the *frame*), the controller looks up the table and dispatches the next scheduled train. If a train is delayed, the whole schedule falls apart — exactly like a cyclic executive in real-time systems.
>
> **Key insight:** Just as a train timetable must be perfectly constructed **beforehand** with sufficient **slack** to absorb minor delays, the cyclic executive requires the offline schedule to be **provably feasible** under all conditions, with sufficient idle time within each frame.

### 1.4 Why Use a Cyclic Scheduler?

| Feature | Benefit in Hard Real-Time Systems |
|---|---|
| **No runtime priority computation** | Deterministic, predictable behavior |
| **No priority inversion** | Eliminates complex synchronization protocols |
| **Minimal runtime overhead** | Suitable for resource-constrained embedded MCUs |
| **Offline feasibility proof** | Schedule can be validated before deployment |
| **Simpler certification** | Preferred in avionics, automotive (ISO 26262), medical devices |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Cyclic schedulers were the *de facto* standard in early real-time systems (e.g., the **Mars Pathfinder** precursor designs, **Boeing 777** Primary Flight Control) before dynamic priority schedulers like RMS became mainstream. They are still preferred in **safety-critical systems** where certification simplicity outweighs flexibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Components of a Cyclic Schedule

A cyclic schedule is built by **partitioning** the major cycle into consecutive, non-overlapping **frames** $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_N$. Every frame $f_i$ has the same fixed duration $f$ (frame size). At the start of each frame, a clock interrupt fires, and the executive consults the dispatch table to launch the designated job(s).

The construction proceeds through these logical stages:

- **Step 1 — Enumerate the task set:** Identify all periodic tasks $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ with their periods $p_i$ and worst-case execution times (WCET) $e_i$.
- **Step 2 — Compute the major cycle:** $P = \text{LCM}(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$.
- **Step 3 — Determine frame size:** Choose $f$ such that it satisfies the **frame constraints** (see §2.2).
- **Step 4 — Slice the major cycle:** Divide $P$ into $N = P/f$ consecutive frames.
- **Step 5 — Allocate jobs:** Within each frame, schedule jobs using a **non-preemptive** list, ensuring each job completes **before the frame ends**.
- **Step 6 — Validate:** Check that every job instance (across the hyper-period) is scheduled within a frame that lies *within* its deadline window.

### 2.2 Frame Size Selection — The Four Mandatory Constraints

The frame size $f$ is **not arbitrary**. KTU examiners specifically test the four constraints below (often as a 7-mark short-answer or sub-part):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint 1 — Integer Division:**
> The frame size must divide the major cycle evenly:
> $$P \mod f = 0$$
> This guarantees the schedule *tiles* perfectly without leftover time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint 2 — Frame Size ≤ Minimum Period:**
> $$f \leq \min_i(p_i)$$
> A frame must be **shorter than the shortest task period**, otherwise a high-frequency task could miss its next release while a long frame is still executing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint 3 — Sufficient Capacity per Frame:**
> No single frame may demand more computation time than its duration:
> $$\sum_{j \in f_i} e_j \leq f$$
> where the sum is over all jobs assigned to frame $f_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint 4 — Job Completion within Period (Job-to-Deadline Constraint):**
> Every job must complete **within one period** of its release. If a task $T_i$ releases a job at time $t$, that job must finish before time $t + p_i$. Formally, a job of $T_i$ placed in frame $f_k$ must satisfy:
> $$\text{start}(f_k) + e_i \leq \text{release\_time} + p_i$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \text{LCM}(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$ | **Major cycle / hyper-period** — schedule repeats every $P$ time units. Used to bound offline analysis. |
| 2 | $N = P / f$ | **Number of frames** in one major cycle. |
| 3 | $f \leq \min_i(p_i)$ | **Maximum frame size** — ensures no high-frequency task is starved. |
| 4 | $f \geq \max_i(e_i)$ | **Minimum frame size** — each frame must accommodate the largest single job. |
| 5 | $\sum_{j \in f_i} e_j \leq f$ | **Per-frame capacity constraint** — total work in any frame $\leq$ frame size. |
| 6 | $2f - \text{GCET} \leq p_i \leq P$ | **Job-to-Deadline constraint** — worst-case job finishes within its period. |
| 7 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i} \leq 1$ | **Processor utilization** — necessary condition for any feasible schedule. |
| 8 | $\text{Slack} = f - \sum_{j \in f_i} e_j$ | **Idle time per frame** — buffer for overruns; cyclic schedulers require non-zero slack for robustness. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Cyclic schedulers underpin **avionics software (ARINC 653 partition scheduling)**, **automotive ECU body controllers**, and **medical infusion pumps**. Their **dispatch table** is essentially a compiled `switch` statement in firmware, making them trivially auditable during DO-178C / IEC 62304 certification audits.

### 2.4 The Cyclic Executive Algorithm (Offline Construction)

The classical algorithm to construct a cyclic schedule proceeds as follows:

1. **Compute the major cycle** $P = \text{LCM}(p_1, \ldots, p_n)$.
2. **Select the frame size** $f$ satisfying Constraints 1–4.
3. **Divide** $P$ into $N = P/f$ equal frames, numbered $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_N$.
4. **For each task** $T_i$, place **one instance** of $T_i$ into every $p_i / f$-th frame, ensuring the placement respects the job-to-deadline window.
5. **Combine** multiple small jobs into a single frame if their total execution time fits.
6. **Resolve conflicts:** If a frame is overloaded, increase $f$ (subject to constraints) and rebuild.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing a Cyclic Schedule

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Consider three periodic tasks with the following parameters:

| Task | Period $p_i$ | WCET $e_i$ |
|------|------|------|
| $T_1$ | 4 | 1 |
| $T_2$ | 5 | 2 |
| $T_3$ | 20 | 2 |

Construct a cyclic schedule. Identify the major cycle, frame size, and frame allocations.

---

**Step 1 — Compute the Major Cycle**

The major cycle is the LCM of all task periods.

$$P = \text{LCM}(4, 5, 20)$$

Prime factorizations:

$$4 = 2^2, \quad 5 = 5^1, \quad 20 = 2^2 \cdot 5$$

$$\text{LCM} = 2^2 \cdot 5^1 = 20$$

So $P = 20$ time units.

---

**Step 2 — Select the Frame Size**

Apply Constraints 2 and 4:

- Constraint 2: $f \leq \min(p_i) = \min(4, 5, 20) = 4$
- Constraint 4: $f \geq \max(e_i) = \max(1, 2, 2) = 2$
- Constraint 1: $P \mod f = 0$, i.e., $20 \mod f = 0$

Choosing $f = 4$ satisfies all constraints (since $2 \leq 4 \leq 4$ and $20/4 = 5$).

> [!NOTE]
> Choosing $f = 4$ gives the **finest granularity** and is most flexible. Frame size $f = 2$ would also work but increases dispatch overhead.

---

**Step 3 — Slice the Major Cycle into Frames**

$$N = P / f = 20 / 4 = 5 \text{ frames}$$

The frame boundaries occur at times: $\{0, 4, 8, 12, 16\}$.

---

**Step 4 — Allocate Jobs to Frames**

Enumerate all job releases across $[0, 20)$:

- $T_1$ releases at times $\{0, 4, 8, 12, 16\}$ — total of $20/4 = 5$ jobs.
- $T_2$ releases at times $\{0, 5, 10, 15\}$ — total of $20/5 = 4$ jobs.
- $T_3$ releases at time $\{0\}$ — total of $1$ job.

Total execution demand:

$$U = \frac{e_1}{p_1} + \frac{e_2}{p_2} + \frac{e_3}{p_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{5} + \frac{2}{20}$$

$$\boxed{U = 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.10 = 0.75}$$

Since $U \leq 1$, the task set is **feasible** in principle. Now construct the frame allocations:

| Frame | Time Window | Allocated Jobs | Total WCET | Slack |
|-------|-------------|----------------|------------|-------|
| $f_1$ | $[0, 4)$ | $T_1$(0–1), $T_2$(1–3), $T_3$(3–4)? — conflict | — | — |

**Conflict detected:** $T_1$ releases at $t=0$, $T_2$ releases at $t=0$ and $t=5$ (not in frame 1), $T_3$ releases at $t=0$. Frame 1 has $1+2+2 = 5 > 4$. Overload!

**Resolution:** $T_3$ cannot finish in frame 1 (it needs $e_3 = 2$, but frame 1 must also serve $T_1$ and the $t=0$ release of $T_2$). Shift $T_3$ to a later frame.

Recall Constraint 6 (job-to-deadline):

$$\text{start}(f_k) + e_3 \leq 0 + p_3 = 20$$

So $T_3$ may finish by $t=20$. Place $T_3$ in frame $f_5 = [16, 20)$.

Revised allocation:

| Frame | Time Window | Allocated Jobs | Total WCET | Slack |
|-------|-------------|----------------|------------|-------|
| $f_1$ | $[0, 4)$ | $T_1$, $T_2$ | $1+2 = 3$ | 1 |
| $f_2$ | $[4, 8)$ | $T_1$, $T_2$ (released at $t=5$) | $1+2 = 3$ | 1 |
| $f_3$ | $[8, 12)$ | $T_1$, $T_2$ (released at $t=10$) | $1+2 = 3$ | 1 |
| $f_4$ | $[12, 16)$ | $T_1$, $T_2$ (released at $t=15$) | $1+2 = 3$ | 1 |
| $f_5$ | $[16, 20)$ | $T_1$, $T_3$ | $1+2 = 3$ | 1 |

**Validation:** All frames have $\text{WCET} \leq f = 4$. ✓

All job-to-deadline constraints satisfied:
- $T_1$ at $t=16$ finishes at $t=17 \leq 16 + 4 = 20$. ✓
- $T_2$ at $t=15$ finishes at $t=18 \leq 15 + 5 = 20$. ✓
- $T_3$ at $t=16$ finishes at $t=18 \leq 0 + 20 = 20$. ✓

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cyclic vs. Priority-Driven Schedulers

| Property | Cyclic Scheduler | Rate Monotonic (RMS) | Earliest Deadline First (EDF) |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Scheduling decision** | Offline lookup table | Online priority by period | Online priority by deadline |
| **Runtime overhead** | **Very low** | Moderate | High |
| **Predictability** | **Excellent** | Good | Excellent |
| **Handles aperiodic tasks** | Poor (slices only) | Background / polling server | Slack stealing |
| **Implementation complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **CPU utilization bound** | ~100% (if schedulable) | ~69% (Liu & Layland) | 100% |
| **Certification friendliness** | **Highest** | Moderate | Low (runtime overhead) |
| **Adaptability to change** | **Poor** (rebuild schedule) | Good (add task + analysis) | Good |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Cyclic Executive

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Boot] --> B[Load Dispatch Table from ROM]
    B --> C[Initialize Clock Tick Interrupt at Frame Boundaries]
    C --> D[Start Major Cycle Counter = 0]
    D --> E[Read Current Frame Index]
    E --> F[Look Up Dispatch Table entry for current frame]
    F --> G[Launch Pre-Allocated Job]
    G --> H{Job Complete?}
    H -->|No| H
    H -->|Yes| I{Frame Time Elapsed?}
    I -->|No| J[Idle / Slack Time]
    J --> I
    I -->|Yes| K[Increment Frame Index]
    K --> L{End of Major Cycle?}
    L -->|No| E
    L -->|Yes| M[Reset Frame Index to 0]
    M --> E
```

### 4.2 Frame Partitioning & Job Allocation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MC[Major Cycle P equals 20]
        F1[Frame 1: 0 to 4]
        F2[Frame 2: 4 to 8]
        F3[Frame 3: 8 to 12]
        F4[Frame 4: 12 to 16]
        F5[Frame 5: 16 to 20]
    end
    F1 --- J1[T1 and T2]
    F2 --- J2[T1 and T2]
    F3 --- J3[T1 and T2]
    F4 --- J4[T1 and T2]
    F5 --- J5[T1 and T3]
```

### 4.3 Cyclic Schedule Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input: Task Set T1..Tn with pi and ei] --> S2[Compute Major Cycle P equals LCM of periods]
    S2 --> S3[Choose Frame Size f satisfying all 4 constraints]
    S3 --> S4[Divide P into N equals P over f frames]
    S4 --> S5[Allocate jobs to frames using non-preemptive list]
    S5 --> S6{Frame overloaded?}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Increase f or shift job to later frame]
    S7 --> S5
    S6 -->|No| S8{Job-to-deadline OK?}
    S8 -->|No| S7
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Generate Dispatch Table]
    S9 --> S10[Output: Validated Cyclic Schedule]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Embedded Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLK[Hardware Clock / SysTick] --> ISR[Frame Tick ISR]
    ROM[(Dispatch Table in ROM)] --> EXEC[Cyclic Executive Loop]
    ISR --> EXEC
    EXEC -->|Launch| TSK1[Task T1]
    EXEC -->|Launch| TSK2[Task T2]
    EXEC -->|Launch| TSK3[Task Tn]
    TSK1 --> EXEC
    TSK2 --> EXEC
    TSK3 --> EXEC
    EXEC -->|Idle / Slack| SLK[Slack Time Slot for Aperiodic]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define a **cyclic scheduler**. State any two advantages of using a cyclic scheduler in hard real-time systems.

**Model Answer:**
A **cyclic scheduler** (or cyclic executive) is a non-priority-based, table-driven scheduling mechanism in which a static, pre-computed schedule of periodic tasks repeats at fixed intervals called the **major cycle**. The major cycle is divided into equal **frames**, and at the start of each frame, a clock interrupt fires and the executive consults the dispatch table to launch the designated job(s).

**Advantages:**
1. **Minimal runtime overhead** — only a table lookup and jump are required at each frame boundary; no priority comparison or arbitration logic.
2. **High predictability and certifiability** — the schedule is provably feasible offline, making it ideal for safety-critical systems (avionics, automotive, medical) under standards like DO-178C and ISO 26262.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is a **major cycle** in the context of cyclic scheduling? How is it computed for a given task set?

**Model Answer:**
The **major cycle** (also called the **hyper-period**) is the smallest time interval after which the entire periodic scheduling pattern repeats exactly. It bounds the offline analysis — once a feasible schedule is constructed for one major cycle, the same schedule repeats indefinitely.

**Computation:**
For a task set $\{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$ with periods $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$, the major cycle is the **Least Common Multiple (LCM)** of all task periods:

$$P = \text{LCM}(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$$

For example, if $p_1 = 4, p_2 = 6, p_3 = 8$, then $P = \text{LCM}(4,6,8) = 24$ time units.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

**Q3 (a) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Choice A]**
With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a cyclic executive**. Discuss the **four constraints** that must be satisfied while selecting the frame size $f$.

**Model Answer:**

*(7 marks — to be answered in three parts: diagram, architecture, and constraints)*

**Architecture (4 marks):**

A cyclic executive consists of:

- **Hardware clock** generating periodic interrupts at every frame boundary.
- **Dispatch table** stored in ROM, indexed by the current frame number, holding pointers to job entry functions.
- **Frame tick ISR** that increments the frame counter, looks up the table, and launches the next job(s).
- **Idle / slack handler** that runs during unused CPU time within a frame.

The major cycle is partitioned into $N = P/f$ consecutive frames of equal duration $f$. Each frame executes a statically allocated subset of jobs non-preemptively. After the last frame of the major cycle, the counter wraps to 0 and the cycle repeats.

**Four Constraints on Frame Size $f$ (3 marks):**

1. **Integer division:** $f$ must divide the major cycle $P$ evenly, i.e., $P \mod f = 0$, ensuring the schedule tiles perfectly.
2. **Upper bound:** $f \leq \min_i(p_i)$ — the frame size must not exceed the smallest period, else a high-frequency task may miss its next release.
3. **Capacity:** $\sum_{j \in f_i} e_j \leq f$ — no frame may be assigned work exceeding its duration.
4. **Job-to-deadline:** Every job must finish within its period: $\text{finish\_time} \leq \text{release\_time} + p_i$.

---

**Q3 (b) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Choice A continued]**
Consider the following task set:

| Task | Period $p_i$ | WCET $e_i$ |
|------|------|------|
| $T_1$ | 6 | 2 |
| $T_2$ | 10 | 3 |
| $T_3$ | 15 | 3 |

**(i)** Compute the **major cycle** $P$.
**(ii)** Choose a suitable **frame size** $f$, justifying your choice against the constraints.
**(iii)** Construct the **cyclic schedule** (dispatch table) with frame allocations.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Major Cycle (2 marks):**

$$P = \text{LCM}(6, 10, 15)$$

$$6 = 2 \cdot 3, \quad 10 = 2 \cdot 5, \quad 15 = 3 \cdot 5$$

$$P = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 = 30 \text{ time units}$$

**(ii) Frame Size Selection (2 marks):**

- Constraint 2 (upper bound): $f \leq \min(6, 10, 15) = 6$.
- Constraint 4 (lower bound): $f \geq \max(2, 3, 3) = 3$.
- Constraint 1 (integer division): $f$ must divide 30. Valid candidates: $f \in \{3, 5, 6\}$.

Choose $f = 5$ to balance granularity and dispatch overhead.

**(iii) Schedule Construction (3 marks):**

$N = P/f = 30/5 = 6$ frames.

Total utilization:

$$U = \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} + \frac{3}{15} = 0.333 + 0.300 + 0.200 = 0.833 \leq 1 \checkmark$$

Job releases per task across $[0, 30)$:
- $T_1$: at $\{0, 6, 12, 18, 24\}$ — 5 jobs.
- $T_2$: at $\{0, 10, 20\}$ — 3 jobs.
- $T_3$: at $\{0, 15\}$ — 2 jobs.

**Dispatch Table:**

| Frame | Time | Allocated Jobs | WCET | Slack |
|-------|------|----------------|------|-------|
| $f_1$ | $[0,5)$ | $T_1$ | 2 | 3 |
| $f_2$ | $[5,10)$ | $T_1$ (at $t=6$), $T_2$ | $2+3 = 5$ | 0 |
| $f_3$ | $[10,15)$ | $T_1$ (at $t=12$), $T_3$ | $2+3 = 5$ | 0 |
| $f_4$ | $[15,20)$ | $T_1$ (at $t=18$) | 2 | 3 |
| $f_5$ | $[20,25)$ | $T_1$ (at $t=24$), $T_2$ (at $t=20$) | $2+3 = 5$ | 0 |
| $f_6$ | $[25,30)$ | $T_2$ (at $t=20$? — violates), $T_3$ (at $t=15$) | — | — |

**Refinement:** $T_2$ at $t=20$ must finish by $t=25$ — it does (occupies $[20,23)$). $T_3$ at $t=15$ must finish by $t=30$ — slot it in $f_6$ as $[27,30)$ with $T_2$ at $[25,28)$. Adjust $f_6$: $T_2$ (continued, $[25,28)$), $T_3$ at $[28,30)$ — but $e_3 = 3$ needs 3 units, only 2 left. **Conflict.** Shift $T_3$ to a slack slot.

> This iterative refinement illustrates the **non-triviality of cyclic schedule construction** — a frequent source of marks lost in KTU exams if students do not validate every frame.

---

**Alternative Choice B — Q4 (a) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**
Explain the **limitations of cyclic schedulers**. Compare cyclic scheduling with **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** in terms of flexibility, runtime overhead, and utilization bound.

**Model Answer Outline (7 marks):**

- Cyclic schedulers cannot easily handle **aperiodic tasks** (only via polling or background slots).
- Adding a new task requires **complete schedule rebuild** — poor maintainability.
- Cannot efficiently accommodate tasks with **harmonic periods** mismatches leading to large hyper-periods.
- All jobs must be **non-preemptive** within a frame, leading to potentially long response times for high-frequency tasks.
- Comparison table with RMS on the three axes (flexibility, overhead, utilization bound ~100% cyclic vs. ~69% RMS).

**Q4 (b)**
A real-time system has 4 tasks with periods $\{2, 5, 10, 20\}$ and identical WCET of $1$ time unit. Construct a cyclic schedule. What is the smallest feasible frame size? Justify.

**Model Answer Skeleton:**
- $P = \text{LCM}(2,5,10,20) = 20$.
- Constraints: $f \leq 2$, $f \geq 1$, and $20 \mod f = 0$. So $f = 1$ or $f = 2$.
- Smallest feasible frame: $f = 1$ (since $f \geq 1$).
- Frames = 20, each handles 1 unit of work.
- $T_1$ (period 2) runs every 2nd frame; $T_2$ every 5th; etc.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Cyclic Scheduler Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting Constraint 2** ($f \leq \min p_i$): Examiners frequently give partial credit only if all 4 constraints are explicitly listed.
> 2. **Ignoring the job-to-deadline constraint:** A job may fit within a frame *in isolation*, but if the frame boundary is too close to its deadline, it still violates feasibility.
> 3. **Computing major cycle incorrectly:** Confusing LCM with GCD or product. Always show **prime factorization** in the answer.
> 4. **Assuming all frames are fully loaded:** A correct schedule **must** include slack — zero slack is a red flag for infeasibility under overruns.
> 5. **Mixing up aperiodic and sporadic handling:** Cyclic schedulers use **polling servers / slack stealing**, not priority queues. Examiners deduct heavily for describing aperiodic servicing in RMS terms.
> 6. **Skipping the dispatch table:** Always tabulate the frame allocations explicitly — a verbal description alone is insufficient for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Cyclic Schedulers**

- ☐ **Cyclic Scheduler** = static, table-driven, non-priority-based dispatcher for hard real-time systems.
- ☐ **Major Cycle** $P = \text{LCM}(p_1, \ldots, p_n)$ — schedule repeats every $P$.
- ☐ **Frame (Minor Cycle)** = equal-duration time slice; frame size is $f$.
- ☐ **Four Frame Constraints:**
  1. $P \mod f = 0$
  2. $f \leq \min_i(p_i)$
  3. $\sum_{j \in f_i} e_j \leq f$
  4. Job-to-deadline: $\text{finish} \leq \text{release} + p_i$
- ☐ Utilization bound: $\sum e_i / p_i \leq 1$ (necessary, not sufficient).
- ☐ Total frames: $N = P / f$.
- ☐ **Slack** = $f - \sum e_j$ per frame (must be $\geq 0$).
- ☐ **No priority inversion** in cyclic schedulers (jobs are non-preemptive within a frame).
- ☐ Aperiodic tasks handled via **polling server** or **slack stealing**.
- ☐ Preferred in **safety-critical, certified systems** (avionics, automotive, medical).
- ☐ Major **drawback:** inflexible — adding/removing tasks requires schedule rebuild.
- ☐ Cyclic schedulers provide **100% utilization** *if* schedule is constructible; RMS is limited to ~69% (Liu & Layland bound).
- ☐ **Dispatch table** is the runtime artifact — stored in ROM for certifiability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Schedulers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Schedulers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hybrid Scheduler** in real-time systems is a scheduling architecture that intentionally **fuses two or more distinct scheduling paradigms** (e.g., fixed-priority, dynamic-priority, time-driven, event-driven, or partitioned + global) into a single coherent system. The objective is to inherit the *guarantees of one scheme* (e.g., predictability of fixed-priority) while exploiting the *flexibility of another* (e.g., higher utilization under dynamic-priority).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, hybrid schedulers are classified under the broad taxonomy of **advanced scheduling** that addresses the inherent limitations of pure-scheme schedulers (Rate Monotonic, Earliest Deadline First, etc.) when dealing with modern real-time workloads that include a mix of *hard*, *firm*, and *soft* tasks, possibly on **multiprocessor/multicore platforms** with **shared resources** and **compositional subsystems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> A *Hybrid Scheduler* is a multi-level or multi-policy scheduler in which tasks (or task groups) are assigned to sub-schedulers operating under different algorithms, while a *master/executive* policy arbitrates between them. Typical realizations include **Hierarchical Schedulers**, **Two-Level Schedulers (e.g., RTLinux)**, **Reservation-Based Servers (CBS, H-CBS)**, **Proportionate-Fair (PFair) Schedulers**, and **Semi-Partitioned Schedulers**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Control Tower

Imagine a busy international airport:

- **Domestic flights** are managed by a **time-table** (time-driven): they take off at fixed slots.
- **Medical emergency flights** are dispatched **on-demand by priority** (event-driven, fixed-priority).
- A **central air traffic controller** decides *which policy* is invoked for *which flight* at *which moment*.

The control tower is a **Hybrid Scheduler**: it does not commit to one rule. It *interleaves* or *hierarchically nests* rules so that critical services get immediate attention while routine services remain predictable. Likewise, hybrid RT schedulers *compose* policies to simultaneously support **hard real-time tasks** (which must never miss a deadline), **soft real-time tasks** (whose tardiness degrades quality), and **non-real-time best-effort tasks** (like Linux background daemons).

### 1.3 Why Pure Schedulers Are Not Enough

| Pure Scheduler | Strength | Critical Weakness | Hybrid Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rate Monotonic (RM) | Simple, predictable on uniprocessors | Degrades badly on multiprocessors; low utilization bound ($U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$) | Multiprocessor hybrid (partitioned RM + global EDF) |
| Earliest Deadline First (EDF) | Optimal on uniprocessor ($\leq 100\%$ load) | Pathological behavior on multiprocessors (Dhall effect) | Semi-partitioned EDF (Baruah’s algorithm) |
| Time-Slice (Round-Robin) | Fairness for best-effort | No deadline support | Hierarchical RR over EDF/CBS core |
| Strict Priority | Predictable, low overhead | No bandwidth isolation → priority inversion | Resource Reservations (CBS) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024, Module 2):** The discussion of hybrid schedulers typically follows the classic theme of *“classical single-policy schedulers are insufficient for compositional, multicore, or mixed-criticality real-time systems.”* Hybrid schemes are therefore introduced as the **engineering solution** to bridge theory and practice.

### 1.4 Classification of Hybrid Schedulers (KTU view)

Hybrid schedulers can be classified along three orthogonal axes:

1. **Compositional axis** — *Hierarchical / Two-Level* (a parent scheduler dispatches child schedulers)
2. **Resource-isolation axis** — *Reservation-Based* (CBS, H-CBS, sporadic server, deferrable server, polling server)
3. **Multi-processor axis** — *Partitioned + Global* (semi-partitioned, pfair, PD²)

Each axis can be combined; for instance, **H-CBS** is both *hierarchical* and *reservation-based*, and **PD²** is a *pfair* scheduler that can be deployed in a *two-level hierarchy*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical (Two-Level) Schedulers

A **Hierarchical Scheduler** arranges schedulers in a tree. The **root** is the *executive* or *master* scheduler; leaves are *subsystem schedulers* (each running its own tasks under its own policy). The master’s job is to allocate **CPU budget** to each child server, in turn.

#### 2.1.1 Operational Logic

1. Each subsystem $S_i$ declares a **contract** $(\Pi_i, \Theta_i)$ — a **period** $\Pi_i$ and a **budget** $\Theta_i$ (CPU time per period).
2. The master scheduler (e.g., a Constant Bandwidth Server, or a TDMA wheel) **dispatches** $S_i$ for at most $\Theta_i$ time units every $\Pi_i$.
3. Inside $S_i$, the local scheduler (e.g., EDF, RM, RR) decides *which task of $S_i$* runs first.
4. **Temporal isolation** is achieved: a misbehaving subsystem cannot starve its siblings because its budget is hard-capped.

#### 2.1.2 Server Replenishment Rules

- **Polling Server (PS):** Budget replenishes to $\Theta$ at the start of every period $\Pi$. If not used, it is *discarded* (server becomes idle until next period). *Low overhead, but bursty miss patterns.*
- **Deferrable Server (DS):** Budget replenishes to $\Theta$ at the start of every period, but **preserved** if unused until the period ends. *Better for bursty tasks, but suffers from capacity loss due to “double replenishment” coupling.*
- **Sporadic Server (SS):** A capacity-preserving server introduced by Sprunt, Sha, and Lehoczky. Replenishment is **triggered by consumption**, not by the period boundary — it explicitly emulates a sporadic task. *Cost: 2× state per server.*
- **Constant Bandwidth Server (CBS):** Bandwidth $\alpha = \Theta/\Pi$ is preserved continuously. *See §2.3.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CBS** is the canonical KTU 2024 example of a *reservation-based* hybrid scheduler because it is a **soft-server** (no hard guarantee) that provides **temporal isolation** and **bandwidth enforcement** — perfect for hierarchical compositions.

### 2.2 Two-Level Scheduler (RTLinux Model)

The **RTLinux / RTAI** paradigm is a textbook KTU example.

- **Level 0 (Real-time core):** A small, fixed-priority preemptive scheduler running hard real-time tasks.
- **Level 1 (Non-real-time guest):** Standard Linux kernel runs as the *idle task* of Level 0; it is preempted whenever a real-time task is ready.

**Why it is “hybrid”:** Level 0 is *time-driven + event-driven* (periodic timer + interrupt), while Level 1 is the standard *fair-share* Linux scheduler. The two are *composed* by simple **preemption**, not by budget accounting.

### 2.3 Constant Bandwidth Server (CBS) — Detailed

The CBS, introduced by Abeni and Buttazzo (1998), is a *soft real-time server* that:

- Guarantees a **task running inside it** experiences an **effective service curve** equivalent to a periodic task of period $P_s$ and capacity $Q_s$.
- **Replenishment** occurs *lazily* on consumption.

#### 2.3.1 CBS Algorithm

Server state: $(Q_s, P_s, c_d)$ where $c_d$ = current *server deadline*.

For each job arrival $J_k$ at time $a_k$:

- If $J_k$ is **earlier** than the current server deadline (i.e., $a_k < c_d$): use the *current* deadline and *current* budget.
- Else: **replenish** budget to $Q_s$ and **push** deadline forward: $c_d \leftarrow a_k + P_s$.

Whenever the server executes the job for a duration $\delta$:

- $Q_s \leftarrow Q_s - \delta$
- If $Q_s \leq 0$: **suspend** the server (mark it idle) until the *next replenishment time* $c_d$.

#### 2.3.2 Bandwidth Property

The server **reserves at most** a fraction $\alpha = Q_s / P_s$ of the CPU. Under any load, the *aggregate* CBS bandwidth is preserved, so a CBS cannot accidentally use more than its declared share.

### 2.4 Hierarchical CBS (H-CBS)

When a parent task is itself scheduled by a CBS, the *child* CBS must respect the *parent’s budget*. The **Hierarchical CBS** modifies replenishment:

- A child CBS *replenishment* is allowed only if the parent CBS still has budget.
- This yields **nested bandwidth isolation** — failure of a low-criticality subsystem cannot propagate upward.

### 2.5 Proportionate Fair (PFair / PD²) Schedulers

PFair schedulers (Baruah, Cohen, Plaxton, Varvel, 1996) target **multiprocessor** systems and pursue the goal:

> In *any* time interval $[t_1, t_2]$, task $\tau_i$ must receive time *proportional to its weight* $w_i = C_i / T_i$ within a *bounded lag* $\epsilon < 1$.

#### 2.5.1 PD Algorithm (Proportionate-Derived)

- Time is divided into **quantum slots** of length $T_{slot} = \gcd$ of all task periods divided by 2 (or scaled to ensure integer slot count).
- At each slot boundary, every task has a *release time* $r_i$ and *deadline* $d_i$.
- Tasks are scheduled using a **pinwheel** rule: at each slot, the task with the *earliest* $d_i - r_i$ weighted urgency is selected.

#### 2.5.2 PD² Algorithm (PFair, k=2)

A specialized PFair scheme where each task $\tau_i$ is decomposed into **sub-tasks** at levels $k$ corresponding to the binary representation of the slot index. **PD²** is *optimal* for $m$ identical processors and supports $O(m)$ scheduling decisions per slot.

### 2.6 Semi-Partitioned Schedulers

- Some tasks are *pinned* to a specific processor (partitioned); one or more *migrating* tasks are *split* across processors.
- Example: **B-UND** (Baruah’s Unified Non-migratory Deferrable) splits a long task $\tau_k$ into fragments scheduled on different processors at different times.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-yield formulas** for hybrid schedulers (use $\vert$ for absolute value inside equations to avoid breaking tables).

| # | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Server utilization | $\alpha = Q_s \,/\, P_s$ | Reserved CPU fraction by a server |
| 2 | CBS deadline update | $c_d \leftarrow a_k + P_s$ | Replenishment rule on new arrival |
| 3 | CBS budget update | $Q_s \leftarrow Q_s - \delta$ | Per-execution decrement |
| 4 | Sporadic-server replenishment | $R \leftarrow R + \delta$ at consumption | Capacity-preserving |
| 5 | PFair weight | $w_i = C_i \,/\, T_i$ | Proportion of CPU for $\tau_i$ |
| 6 | PFair lag bound | $\vert \text{received}(i,t) - w_i \cdot t \vert < 1$ | PFairness constraint |
| 7 | Slot length (PD²) | $T_{slot} = T_{LCM} \,/\, 2^k$ | Quantum duration |
| 8 | Hierarchical utilization | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \leq 1$ | Bandwidth must sum to $\leq 100\%$ |
| 9 | Deferrable capacity loss | $L = (n-1) \cdot \Theta \cdot \Delta t$ | Loss vs ideal periodic server |
| 10 | Polling server worst-case response | $R = 2\Pi - \Theta$ | For single bursty task |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, a *common mistake* is to write CBS budget as *additive* on replenishment. The actual rule is **replenishment only when $Q_s$ is exhausted** AND the *scheduled time* has reached the *next period boundary* $c_d$. Do not lump CBS with deferrable server.

### 2.8 Engineering Utility

- **Avionics (IMA / ARINC 653):** ARINC 653 partitions are exactly *two-level hierarchical schedulers* — a major time-wheel at Level 0, EDF/RM inside each partition.
- **Industrial automation (PROFINET IRT):** Hybrid time-triggered + event-driven scheduling on the same Ethernet fabric.
- **Smartphones:** Linux CFS (fair-share, non-RT) for UI + RT tasks (audio, modem) on a real-time core.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Mixed-criticality OSes compose fixed-priority + time-triggered servers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Bandwidth Isolation of CBS

We want to prove that a CBS of parameters $(Q_s, P_s)$ never demands more than fraction $\alpha = Q_s / P_s$ of the CPU, regardless of the incoming job stream.

#### Step 1: Define the Server’s Service Function

Let $W(t_1, t_2)$ be the total time the server has executed during interval $[t_1, t_2]$. The server starts with budget $Q_s$ and deadline $c_d$.

#### Step 2: Bound Service per Replenishment Cycle

In any *replenishment cycle* of length $P_s$, the server can execute for **at most** $Q_s$ time units. This is because:

- $Q_s$ is reset to $Q_s$ at the start of a new cycle, AND
- $Q_s$ is decremented by exactly the execution time at every run.

Therefore:

$$
W(t, t+P_s) \leq Q_s
$$

#### Step 3: Convert Time Bound to Bandwidth

Dividing both sides by $P_s$:

$$
\frac{W(t, t+P_s)}{P_s} \leq \frac{Q_s}{P_s} = \alpha
$$

#### Step 4: Conclude the Long-Run Average

For any interval of length $L = k \cdot P_s$ (integer $k$):

$$
W(t, t+L) \leq k \cdot Q_s
$$

Hence the long-run utilization is at most $\alpha$. Even if the workload is *bursty* and the server is *suspended* for several cycles, when it eventually runs, the budget is still bounded per cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** CBS guarantees **bounded bandwidth** but **not** a hard real-time guarantee. A job inside a CBS may miss its absolute deadline if the bandwidth $\alpha$ is set too low. This is the formal reason CBS is called a *soft* server and is *hybrid* — it bridges best-effort and hard-RT worlds.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — PD² Slot Length

We need to ensure all deadlines fall on slot boundaries for *integer* scheduling.

#### Step 1: List Task Parameters

Let tasks $\tau_i$ have $(C_i, T_i)$ with weights $w_i = C_i / T_i$.

#### Step 2: LCM of Periods

Let $T_{LCM} = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$. The hyperperiod must be divided into slots.

#### Step 3: Find Slot Count

For PD², choose $k$ such that $T_{LCM} / 2^k$ is the **slot length**. The standard choice is $2^k$ where $2^k$ is the largest power of 2 not exceeding $m$ (the number of processors) and the slot count per period is integral.

#### Step 4: Decompose Each Task into Sub-tasks

Each $\tau_i$ at period $T_i$ becomes a sequence of sub-tasks at multiples of $T_i / 2^k$:

$$
S_{i,j} \text{ released at } t = j \cdot \frac{T_i}{2^k}, \quad \text{deadline at } t = (j+1) \cdot \frac{T_i}{2^k}
$$

#### Step 5: Verify PFairness

At any time $t$, the number of slots scheduled for task $\tau_i$ up to $t$ is at least $\lfloor w_i \cdot t \rfloor$ and at most $\lceil w_i \cdot t \rceil$, hence the lag is strictly less than 1. This satisfies PFairness by definition.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is *exactly* the kind of comparison question KTU examiners use to test *understanding* of hybrid schemes.

| Property | Polling Server | Deferrable Server | Sporadic Server | CBS | PD² |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hard RT guarantee | No (polling miss) | No (deferrable miss) | Yes (SS) | No (soft) | Yes (PFair) |
| Replenishment | Period boundary | Period boundary | On consumption | On consumption | Slot-based |
| Capacity preserved | No | Yes | Yes | Yes (effective) | N/A (fair) |
| Bandwidth isolation | Weak | Weak | Medium | **Strong** | N/A |
| Multiprocessor friendly | No | No | Limited | Limited | **Yes** |
| Implementation overhead | Low | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| Hierarchical nesting | Poor | Poor | Good | **Excellent (H-CBS)** | Good |
| KTU syllabus coverage | Introductory | Introductory | Common | **Core** | Advanced |

### 3.4 Worked Example — CBS Execution Trace

**Setup:** A CBS with $(Q_s = 4, P_s = 10)$ runs a task with job arrivals at $t = 0, 6, 13, 25$ ms, each needing 2 ms.

| Time (ms) | Event | $Q_s$ | $c_d$ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Arrival $J_1$ | 4 | 10 | Schedule $J_1$ (deadline = $0+10=10$) |
| 0–2 | $J_1$ runs | 2 | 10 | Budget decreases by 2 |
| 2–6 | Idle | 2 | 10 | Wait |
| 6 | Arrival $J_2$ | 2 | 10 | $a_k=6 < c_d=10$, keep deadline |
| 6–8 | $J_2$ runs | 0 | 10 | Budget exhausted → suspend |
| 8–10 | Idle (suspended) | 0 | 10 | Server idle until $c_d$ |
| 10 | Replenishment | **4** | **20** | New deadline = $10+10=20$ |
| 13 | Arrival $J_3$ | 4 | 20 | $a_k=13 < c_d=20$, keep deadline |
| 13–15 | $J_3$ runs | 2 | 20 | Budget decreases |
| 15–20 | Idle | 2 | 20 | Wait |
| 20 | Replenishment | **4** | **30** | New deadline |
| 25 | Arrival $J_4$ | 4 | 30 | $a_k=25 < c_d=30$, keep deadline |
| 25–27 | $J_4$ runs | 2 | 30 | Done |

**Total CPU used = 8 ms** over 27 ms ⇒ effective utilization $\approx 0.296 < \alpha = 0.4$. ✔ Bandwidth respected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This trace is the **single most useful artifact** for a KTU exam answer on CBS — examiners award full marks when the trace shows the replenishment rules correctly.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Scheduler Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HW[CPU Hardware Timer]
    MS[Master Executive Scheduler]
    S1[Subsystem S1 Server Q1 P1]
    S2[Subsystem S2 Server Q2 P2]
    S3[Subsystem S3 Server Q3 P3]
    L1A[Local EDF in S1]
    L1B[Local RM in S1]
    L2A[Local RR in S2]
    L3A[Local TDMA in S3]

    HW --> MS
    MS -->|budget Q1 per P1| S1
    MS -->|budget Q2 per P2| S2
    MS -->|budget Q3 per P3| S3
    S1 --> L1A
    S1 --> L1B
    S2 --> L2A
    S3 --> L3A
```

**Interpretation:** The master executive (typically CBS-based or TDMA) arbitrates three *servers*. Each server hosts its own local scheduler, forming a *two-level hierarchy*. The local schedulers can be **heterogeneous** (EDF in S1, RR in S2, TDMA in S3), which is the defining strength of hierarchical composition.

---

### 4.2 CBS State Machine

