```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Active
    Active --> Active: Job arrives a_k less than c_d, schedule, Qs decreases
    Active --> Suspended: Qs reaches zero
    Suspended --> Active: Replenishment at time c_d, Qs reset to Q0, c_d updated to a_k + Ps
    Active --> Active: New arrival a_k greater than c_d, Qs reset to Q0, c_d set to a_k + Ps
    Suspended --> Active: Replenishment event
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- *Active* = server has budget > 0 and is dispatching.
- *Suspended* = budget exhausted; server halts.
- *Replenishment* on either (a) the scheduled time $c_d$ arriving, or (b) a new job arriving *after* $c_d$ — both cases reset budget *and* deadline together.

---

### 4.3 Two-Level RTLinux Composition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hardware Interrupt]
    B[Real-Time Core L0 Fixed Priority Preemptive]
    C[RT Task 1]
    D[RT Task 2]
    E[Linux Kernel L1 Fair Share Non RT]
    F[User Applications]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    B --> D
    B -->|Linux is the idle task| E
    E --> F
```

**Interpretation:** Hardware interrupts fire into the *real-time core* (Level 0). Linux runs as the **lowest-priority** real-time task; whenever an RT task wakes up, Linux is preempted instantly. This is *hybrid* in the strictest sense: Level 0 = fixed-priority, Level 1 = CFS / RR, and the composition is by **preemption priority**, not by **budget accounting** (unlike CBS hierarchies).

---

### 4.4 PFair / PD² Slot Scheduling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST[Slot Boundary Tick]
    CT[Compute Subtask Deadlines]
    UR[Compute Urgency d minus r over weight]
    SL[Select m tasks with lowest urgency]
    EX[Execute one slot each]
    RE[Repeat for next slot]

    ST --> CT --> UR --> SL --> EX --> RE
    RE --> ST
```

**Interpretation:** Every slot boundary, PD² recomputes *urgency* = (deadline − now)/weight, picks the $m$ most urgent tasks (where $m$ = # processors), and runs them for one slot. This *self-correcting* property is why PD² is called *proportionate fair*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Polling Server, Deferrable Server, and Sporadic Server. Why are these classified as hybrid schedulers?**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
A **Polling Server** replenishes its full budget $\Theta$ at the start of every period $\Pi$, and *discards* the unused budget if no job arrived. A **Deferrable Server** also replenishes at the period boundary but *preserves* the unused budget until the period ends, allowing it to serve backlogs. A **Sporadic Server** is *capacity-preserving* and replenishes *only when* the server actually consumes budget, emulating a sporadic task. They are classified as *hybrid* because they combine a **time-triggered replenishment mechanism** (characteristic of time-driven schedulers) with an **event-driven dispatch** (characteristic of fixed-priority servers). *(3 marks: 1 each for distinguishing + 0.5 for hybrid reasoning.)*

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of a Constant Bandwidth Server (CBS) in a hierarchical real-time system? State its bandwidth formula.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
The CBS is a **reservation-based soft server** that reserves a fixed fraction of the CPU for a *group* of tasks, providing **temporal isolation** between subsystems. Each CBS is parameterized by $(Q_s, P_s)$, and the **bandwidth** reserved is:

$$
\alpha = \frac{Q_s}{P_s}
$$

A CBS *replenishes* lazily on consumption and *defer* deadline when the previous deadline is exceeded. It is the *de-facto* scheduler of Linux SCHED_DEADLINE. *(3 marks: 2 for role, 1 for formula.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Two-Level (Hierarchical) Scheduler**. Discuss how a CBS at Level 0 can arbitrate among EDF-based subsystems at Level 1. Derive the bandwidth condition that must hold for schedulability. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

A **Two-Level Hierarchical Scheduler** consists of:
- **Level 0 (Master):** One or more servers (e.g., CBS instances) that each reserve a fraction $\alpha_i$ of the CPU.
- **Level 1 (Local):** Inside each server, a *local scheduler* (e.g., EDF, RM, RR) schedules the subsystem’s tasks.

The block flow (already shown in §4.1) has:
1. Hardware timer → Master CBS wheel.
2. Master CBS wheel dispatches one of $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n$ for its current budget slot.
3. The selected server hands control to its local scheduler, which picks a task.
4. When the server’s budget is exhausted, control returns to the master, which selects the next non-empty server.

**CBS at Level 0:** Each subsystem $S_i$ is wrapped in a CBS with parameters $(Q_i, P_i)$. The CBS at level 0 is responsible for:
- Tracking $Q_i$ (remaining budget) and $c_{d,i}$ (server deadline).
- Suspending $S_i$ when $Q_i = 0$ and $c_{d,i}$ is the next replenishment.
- Replenishing on $c_{d,i}$ expiry.

**EDF at Level 1:** Inside each CBS, the local scheduler can be EDF. The local EDF treats its tasks’ deadlines as if they ran on a dedicated slower processor of speed equal to $\alpha_i$.

**Bandwidth Condition (Schedulability Derivation):**

The total CPU fraction available is 1 (or $m$ on $m$ processors). For a set of $n$ servers to be **mutually schedulable**, the *sum of their bandwidths* must not exceed 1 (or $m$ on $m$ processors):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{Q_i}{P_i} \;\leq\; 1
$$

**Derivation outline:**
- Each server $S_i$ requests $Q_i$ time per $P_i$ ⇒ utilization $\alpha_i$.
- CBS guarantees *no more* than $\alpha_i$ of CPU is consumed by $S_i$ (proved in §3.1).
- For the master not to be over-subscribed, $\sum_i \alpha_i \leq 1$ (uniprocessor) or $\sum_i \alpha_i \leq m$ (multiprocessor).
- Conversely, EDF inside $S_i$ can schedule any task set with utilization $\leq \alpha_i$ (on a *virtual* processor of speed $\alpha_i$).

Thus the **schedulability test** decomposes into:
- $\sum_i \alpha_i \leq 1$ (server-level).
- For each $S_i$, the local EDF test: $\sum_{j \in S_i} (C_j/T_j) \leq \alpha_i$.

**(b)** *A real-time system contains three subsystems $S_1$, $S_2$, $S_3$ with the following CBS parameters: $(Q_1, P_1) = (4, 20)$, $(Q_2, P_2) = (3, 12)$, $(Q_3, P_3) = (5, 25)$. Check whether the system is hierarchically schedulable. If not, suggest a corrective design. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the bandwidth of each CBS:**

$$
\alpha_1 = \frac{Q_1}{P_1} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20
$$

$$
\alpha_2 = \frac{Q_2}{P_2} = \frac{3}{12} = 0.25
$$

$$
\alpha_3 = \frac{Q_3}{P_3} = \frac{5}{25} = 0.20
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the bandwidths:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{3} \alpha_i = 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 0.65
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the schedulability condition:**

For a **uniprocessor** system, hierarchical schedulability requires:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{3} \alpha_i \leq 1
$$

Since $0.65 \leq 1$, the system **is** hierarchically schedulable. ✔

**Step 4 — Idle CPU fraction:**

$$
\text{Idle fraction} = 1 - 0.65 = 0.35 \;(35\%)
$$

**Step 5 — Re-design discussion (in case the condition had failed):**

If the sum had been $> 1$, the corrective options would be:
- **Option A — Reduce server budget:** e.g., shrink $Q_2$ to $2$ (so $\alpha_2 = 0.167$).
- **Option B — Lengthen server period:** e.g., increase $P_2$ to $15$ (so $\alpha_2 = 0.20$).
- **Option C — Move $S_2$ to a separate processor** (multiprocessor), with the new check $\sum \alpha_i \leq 2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner’s Pitfall:** Students often forget to *sum* the bandwidths or mistakenly check the **individual** bandwidth against 1 (which is always true). Always sum the *fractions*. Also, do not confuse $\alpha_i = Q_i/P_i$ with a task’s own $C/T$ ratio — they live in *different conceptual spaces* (server vs task).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** *Explain the **PFair** and **PD²** scheduling algorithms. Show that the slot length in PD² is $T_{slot} = T_{LCM}/2^k$ where $k$ is the number of bits needed to represent the slot count. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**PFair (Proportionate Fair):** A scheduling discipline for multiprocessors that aims to allocate CPU time to each task in *exact proportion* to its weight $w_i = C_i/T_i$ over *any* time interval. The defining condition is:

$$
\forall i, \forall t : \quad \big\vert \, \text{received}(i, t) - w_i \cdot t \, \big\vert \;<\; 1
$$

where $\text{received}(i, t)$ is the cumulative time scheduled for $\tau_i$ up to time $t$. PFair is *optimal* in the sense that any task set with $\sum w_i \leq m$ (on $m$ processors) is schedulable.

**PD² Algorithm:** A *practical* implementation of PFair:

- Time is divided into *uniform slots* of length $T_{slot}$.
- Each task $\tau_i$ is decomposed into a sequence of *sub-tasks*, each of unit length $T_{slot}$.
- A sub-task $S_{i,j}$ has *release time* $r_{i,j} = j \cdot T_{slot}$ and *deadline* $d_{i,j} = (j+1) \cdot T_{slot}$.
- A *group deadline* is also assigned: $D_{i,j} = (j + 2^k) \cdot T_{slot}$ for tasks at level $k$.
- At each slot boundary, the algorithm selects the $m$ sub-tasks with the *earliest* group deadlines.

**Derivation of $T_{slot}$:**

Let $T_{LCM} = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$. For PD² to be *integral* — i.e., for every sub-task to have integer-valued release and deadline — we need $T_{LCM}/T_{slot}$ to be a *power of 2*. Define $2^k$ as the smallest power of 2 such that:

$$
T_{slot} = \frac{T_{LCM}}{2^k} \quad \text{with} \quad 2^k \geq \text{(number of slots per shortest period)}
$$

Hence $k = \lceil \log_2(T_{LCM}/T_{min}) \rceil$ where $T_{min}$ is the smallest period. Equivalently, $k$ is the number of *bits* in the binary representation of the slot count.

**Optimality:** PD² is *optimal* for $m$ identical processors. It achieves $\sum w_i = m$ utilization, which is the multiprocessor analogue of EDF on a uniprocessor.

**(b)** *Consider three tasks with $(C_i, T_i) = (2, 6), (1, 4), (2, 8)$ on a 2-processor system. Compute the weights and verify PFairness for the first hyperperiod. Find a feasible PD² schedule for $T_{LCM} = 24$ ms with $T_{slot} = 3$ ms. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute weights:**

$$
w_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

$$
w_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

$$
w_3 = \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of weights:**

$$
W = w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 0.333 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.833
$$

Since $W = 0.833 \leq m = 2$, the system is *potentially* PFair-schedulable. ✔

**Step 3 — Required time per hyperperiod (24 ms):**

$$
\text{Time for } \tau_1 = w_1 \cdot 24 = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\text{Time for } \tau_2 = w_2 \cdot 24 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\text{Time for } \tau_3 = w_3 \cdot 24 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

Total = 20 ms. The remaining 4 ms is **idle** (since $W < m$).

**Step 4 — Slot decomposition with $T_{slot} = 3$ ms:**

$T_{LCM}/T_{slot} = 24/3 = 8$ slots per hyperperiod.

Number of slots per period:
- $\tau_1$: $6/3 = 2$ slots per period → 4 periods in hyperperiod → 8 slots
- $\tau_2$: $4/3$ is not integer! ✗ — *slot length is invalid.*

**Adjustment:** Choose $T_{slot}$ that divides all periods. $T_{slot} = 1$ ms is the safe choice: $T_{LCM}/1 = 24$ slots.

**Step 5 — Recompute with $T_{slot} = 1$ ms:**

- $\tau_1$ needs $C_1 = 2$ slots per period (of 6 slots): **2 out of 6 slots.**
- $\tau_2$ needs $C_2 = 1$ slot per period (of 4 slots): **1 out of 4 slots.**
- $\tau_3$ needs $C_3 = 2$ slots per period (of 8 slots): **2 out of 8 slots.**

**Step 6 — A feasible PD² schedule (one of many):**

| Slot (ms) | Proc 1 | Proc 2 |
|---|---|---|
| 0–1 | $\tau_1$ | $\tau_2$ |
| 1–2 | $\tau_1$ | $\tau_3$ |
| 2–3 | idle | $\tau_3$ |
| 3–4 | idle | $\tau_2$ |
| 4–5 | idle | idle |
| 5–6 | $\tau_1$ | idle |
| 6–7 | $\tau_1$ | $\tau_2$ |
| 7–8 | idle | $\tau_3$ |
| 8–9 | idle | $\tau_3$ |
| 9–10 | idle | $\tau_2$ |
| 10–11 | idle | idle |
| 11–12 | $\tau_1$ | idle |
| ... (repeats with period 6, 4, 8) | | |

**Step 7 — PFairness check at $t = 12$ ms:**

- $\tau_1$ has received 4 ms; required $w_1 \cdot 12 = 4$ ms → lag 0 ✔
- $\tau_2$ has received 3 ms; required $w_2 \cdot 12 = 3$ ms → lag 0 ✔
- $\tau_3$ has received 3 ms; required $w_3 \cdot 12 = 3$ ms → lag 0 ✔

PFairness is satisfied.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner’s Pitfall (PD²):** A very common mistake is choosing a slot length that does *not* divide all task periods integrally. Always check $T_i / T_{slot} \in \mathbb{Z}$ for all $i$ before constructing the schedule. Another frequent error is to confuse **PFair lag** with **deadline tardiness** — they are *different* concepts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hybrid Schedulers**

- [x] A **hybrid scheduler** combines ≥ 2 paradigms (priority + time, fixed + dynamic, etc.).
- [x] **Hierarchical / Two-level** = a master scheduler arbitrates among local sub-schedulers; *temporal isolation* is the key benefit.
- [x] **Reservation servers** allocate a *bandwidth* $\alpha = Q_s/P_s$ to a group of tasks.
  - Polling Server: replenish at period start, **discard** unused.
  - Deferrable Server: replenish at period start, **preserve** unused.
  - Sporadic Server: replenish *on consumption* (capacity-preserving, emulates sporadic task).
  - CBS: replenish on **deadline expiry** or **new arrival past deadline**.
- [x] **CBS deadline update** rule: $c_d \leftarrow a_k + P_s$ when $a_k \geq c_d$.
- [x] **CBS budget** decreases by execution time, suspends at zero.
- [x] **Hierarchical CBS (H-CBS)** = nested CBS providing *bandwidth isolation* up and down the tree.
- [x] **RTLinux / RTAI** = classical two-level with Linux as the idle task of the RT core.
- [x] **PFair schedulers** maintain *proportional* time allocation; PD² is the practical optimum for $m$ processors.
- [x] PD² slot length: $T_{slot} = T_{LCM}/2^k$, must divide all $T_i$ integrally.
- [x] **Semi-partitioned** = hybrid of partitioned + global; migrates *some* tasks across processors.
- [x] KTU schedulability test for hybrid: $\sum \alpha_i \leq m$ (bandwidth sum) AND $\sum_{j \in S_i} C_j/T_j \leq \alpha_i$ (local test).
- [x] PFair lag: $\vert \text{received}(i,t) - w_i \cdot t \vert < 1$.
- [x] Practical use cases: **ARINC 653 (avionics), AUTOSAR (automotive), Linux SCHED_DEADLINE (CBS), Linux SCHED_FIFO (RTLinux), industrial PROFINET IRT**.

> **Last-Mile Tip for KTU 2024:** In any hybrid-scheduler answer, **draw a labelled block diagram first**, *then* state the formulas, *then* show a *worked trace* or *numerical verification*. Examiners reward structured answers; a diagram without a derivation will only get partial marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Driven Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Event Driven Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Event-Driven Scheduling** in real-time systems is a non-periodic task scheduling paradigm in which the activation (release) of a job is triggered by the occurrence of an *external or internal event* rather than by the regular tick of a clock. The scheduler responds to these events — typically signaled via **interrupts**, **flags**, or **semaphores** — and dispatches the corresponding handler at the highest priority among the currently ready tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Event-driven (or *interrupt-driven*) scheduling is the classical mechanism used in real-time kernels to handle **aperiodic** and **sporadic** tasks whose arrival times cannot be predicted. The Ready Queue is ordered by **static or dynamic priority**, and the Highest-Priority Ready (HPR) task is always executed.

The two principal task classes serviced by event-driven scheduling are:

| Task Class | Activation Pattern | Deadline Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Aperiodic** | Random arrivals, soft deadlines | Not strict |
| **Sporadic** | Random arrivals, **minimum inter-arrival time** guaranteed | Hard |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room (ER):**
> - A hospital does **not** follow a strict timetable for emergencies. Instead, an *event* (a patient arriving in critical condition) triggers the on-duty doctor to immediately suspend whatever elective procedure they were doing and attend to the urgent case.
> - The **triage nurse** acts as the *event dispatcher* (interrupt controller) and **prioritizes** cases (heart attack > fracture > cold).
> - The **ICU monitor's alarm** is analogous to a **hardware interrupt** — it preempts the doctor instantly.
> - Just as the ER keeps a **priority list** of incoming patients, the real-time kernel keeps a **Ready Queue** of pending event-handlers.
> - This is the essence of **Event-Driven Scheduling**: the system *reacts* to occurrences in its environment rather than running on a rigid pre-planned timeline.

The word "**event**" in KTU parlance is a *one-shot occurrence* — it has **no period** in general, only a guaranteed **minimum gap** between consecutive occurrences (in the sporadic case).

## 1.3 Core Metrics (Bold-Highlighted Constants)

The following KTU-relevant parameters characterize event-driven workloads:

- **$\lambda$ (Arrival Rate):** Expected number of event arrivals per unit time, measured in **events/second**.
- **$T_i$ (Minimum Inter-Arrival Time):** Smallest legal gap between two consecutive instances of event $i$.
- **$C_i$ (Worst-Case Execution Time, WCET):** Maximum computation time of handler $i$.
- **$D_i$ (Relative Deadline):** Time by which handler $i$ must finish after release.
- **$U_i = C_i / T_i$ (Utilization):** Fraction of CPU consumed by handler $i$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always write the units of every metric in your answer script. Examiners reward unit-awareness as a mark-bearing skill.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Event-Driven Scheduling

**Why is it needed?**
Most real-world systems (automotive ECUs, pacemakers, aircraft flight controllers) face tasks that are **not periodic** — they occur *irregularly*. A pure Time-Triggered Architecture (TTA) like Rate Monotonic (RMS) cannot efficiently service these. Event-driven scheduling complements RMS by handling *asynchronous* arrivals.

**How does it work?**
1. The CPU runs the **current task**.
2. An **event source** (sensor, timer, communication packet) generates an *interrupt request* (IRQ).
3. The **Interrupt Controller** (e.g., PIC, NVIC in ARM Cortex-M) prioritizes the IRQ.
4. The kernel saves the **context** of the current task on its stack.
5. The corresponding **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** / event handler is invoked.
6. On completion, the kernel performs a **priority-based dispatch** and may or may not return to the preempted task depending on priority.

## 2.2 Operational Flow — Stepwise Bulleted Logic

- **Step 1 — Idle/Waiting State:** The system executes the highest-priority periodic task in the TTA portion, or remains idle.
- **Step 2 — Event Detection:** A hardware interrupt line is asserted by an external device (e.g., a GPIO edge on a microcontroller).
- **Step 3 — Interrupt Arbitration:** The interrupt controller identifies the **highest-priority pending IRQ** if multiple lines are active.
- **Step 4 — Context Save:** The CPU pushes PC, status register, and general-purpose registers onto the stack (latency: $C_{save}$).
- **Step 5 — Handler Execution:** The handler $H_i$ runs to completion (in *non-nested* design) or until blocked (in *nested* design).
- **Step 6 — Context Restore:** The kernel restores the prior context and resumes the preempted task.
- **Step 7 — Priority Re-evaluation:** If new events were queued during the ISR, a new HPR is computed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Philosophy:** KTU examiners love the *priority inversion problem*. Always mention that event-driven scheduling is **vulnerable to priority inversion** unless guarded by a **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** or **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**.

## 2.3 Polled Execution vs. Interrupt-Driven Execution

Two architectural sub-categories exist within event-driven scheduling:

| Feature | **Polled Execution** | **Interrupt-Driven Execution** |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Software checks flag in a loop | Hardware signal |
| Latency | Bounded by polling period | Bounded by interrupt latency |
| CPU Overhead | Higher (continuous polling) | Lower (event-only) |
| Use Case | Low-power MCUs, simple sensors | High-performance RTOS |
| Predictability | Deterministic if period is fixed | Depends on interrupt nesting |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Total CPU utilization for $n$ periodic tasks | Dimensionless, must be $\leq 1$ |
| 2 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | **Response-Time Analysis (RTA)** — least fixed point | Iterative; solution exists iff $R_i \leq D_i$ |
| 3 | $\text{Interrupt Latency} = T_{detect} + C_{save}$ | Time from event to first ISR instruction | $\mu s$ / clock cycles |
| 4 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{C_{ISR} + C_{restore}}$ | Maximum sustainable event frequency | Hz |
| 5 | $D_i = T_i$ for **sporadic** tasks | Implicit deadline assumption | KTU default |
| 6 | $U_{bound}^{RM} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | Liu & Layland bound for Rate Monotonic | Sufficient (not necessary) |
| 7 | **Schedulability:** $U \leq 1$ for EDF | Necessary & sufficient (preemptive) | Liu & Layland, 1973 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Rule:** In every KTU answer, whenever you write $C_i$ and $T_i$, define them *in the same sentence*. Marks are deducted for "orphan" symbols.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Event-driven scheduling is the *backbone* of:
- **AUTOSAR** automotive ECUs (event-triggered + time-triggered hybrid).
- **FreeRTOS** interrupt priorities (configMAX_SYSCALL_INTERRUPT_PRIORITY).
- **VxWorks** wind kernel for aerospace.
- **Medical implants** (pacemaker event detection from cardiac signals).
- **Industrial PLCs** responding to sensor edges on conveyor lines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Response-Time Analysis (RTA) for Sporadic Tasks

We derive the worst-case response time $R_i$ of a sporadic task $\tau_i$ under **fixed-priority preemptive scheduling** (the canonical event-driven scheme).

### Assumptions
- Task set $\Gamma = \{\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_n\}$ with $\tau_1$ being the highest priority.
- Each $\tau_i$ has WCET $C_i$, minimum inter-arrival time $T_i$, relative deadline $D_i$.
- A *critical instant* occurs when $\tau_i$ arrives simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks.

### Step 1 — Define the Worst-Case Response Time

The response time $R_i$ of $\tau_i$ is the duration from its release to its completion. Under preemption, it must satisfy:

$$
R_i = C_i + I_i
$$

where $I_i$ is the **interference** from higher-priority tasks during the interval $[0, R_i]$.

### Step 2 — Express Interference from Higher-Priority Tasks

A higher-priority task $\tau_j$ can release a new job every $T_j$ time units. Within a window of length $R_i$, the number of jobs of $\tau_j$ is:

$$
N_j(R_i) = \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil
$$

Each such job consumes $C_j$ of CPU time. Therefore:

$$
I_i = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

where $hp(i)$ is the set of tasks with priority *higher* than $\tau_i$.

### Step 3 — Combine to Get the RTA Equation

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j
$$

### Step 4 — Iterative Fixed-Point Solution

Initialize $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$. Iterate until either $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (**converged**) or $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (**unschedulable**).

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

Consider three sporadic tasks released by interrupts:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 5 | 5 | Highest |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 10 | 10 | Middle |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 20 | 20 | Lowest |

**Goal:** Check schedulability of $\tau_3$.

**Step 4.1** — Compute $R_1$:

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 \leq D_1 = 5 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 4.2** — Compute $R_2$ (interference from $\tau_1$):

$$
R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2
$$

$$
R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
$$

$$
R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \;\;\text{(converged)}
$$

$R_2 = 3 \leq D_2 = 10 \;\;\checkmark$

**Step 4.3** — Compute $R_3$ (interference from $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$):

$$
R_3^{(0)} = 3
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \;\;\text{(converged)}
$$

$R_3 = 7 \leq D_3 = 20 \;\;\checkmark$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Show the **full iteration table** in your exam answer. Even if convergence is fast, showing two or three steps is mandatory to score full marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Event-Driven vs. Time-Driven Scheduling

| Criterion | Event-Driven | Time-Driven (RMS / TTA) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Interrupt | Timer tick |
| Overhead | Per-event | Per-tick |
| Flexibility | High (handles aperiodic) | Low (rigid schedule) |
| Jitter | Higher (interrupt jitter) | Lower (deterministic) |
| Hard-RT Use | Yes (with bounded latency) | Preferred (aerospace) |
| Complexity | Moderate | High (offline schedule table) |
| Testability | Harder (race conditions) | Easier (deterministic) |
| Typical OS | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RT-Linux | OSEKtime, ARINC 653 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Event-Driven Scheduling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Event Source: Sensor / Network / User Input] --> B{Interrupt Controller: NVIC / PIC}
    B --> C[IRQ Line Asserted]
    C --> D[CPU: Vector Table Lookup]
    D --> E[Context Save: Push PC, PSW, GPRs]
    E --> F[Invoke ISR / Event Handler]
    F --> G{New Higher-Priority Event?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Service Nested Interrupt]
    H --> F
    G -- No --> I[Context Restore: Pop Registers]
    I --> J[Return from ISR: IRET]
    J --> K[Resume Preempted Task]
    K --> L{Priority Re-evaluation}
    L -- Higher task ready --> M[Preempt and Dispatch HPR]
    L -- No change --> K
    M --> N[Update Ready Queue: Sorted by Priority]
    N --> K
```

## 4.2 Priority Queue State Machine (Event Handler Lifecycle)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant
    Dormant --> Waiting: Event Occurs
    Waiting --> Ready: ISR Completes / Unblocks
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatches
    Running --> Blocked: Awaits Resource
    Blocked --> Ready: Resource Available
    Running --> Completed: Execution Finished
    Completed --> Dormant
    Running --> Waiting: Higher-Priority Interrupt
```

## 4.3 Scheduling Decision Flow for Sporadic vs. Aperiodic Tasks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Event Arrives] --> B{Is it Sporadic?}
    B -- Yes, with minimum gap --> C[Sporadic Handler: Hard Deadline Check via RTA]
    B -- No, fully random --> D[Aperiodic Handler: Best-Effort Service]
    C --> E{U <= Utilization Bound?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Admit Task]
    E -- No --> G[Reject / Use Sporadic Server]
    D --> H[Queue by Priority]
    F --> H
    H --> I[CPU Dispatch]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Mermaid flowcharts above map the *control flow* of an event-driven RTOS kernel. In the KTU exam, drawing a similar labeled block diagram (even hand-drawn) carries **2 to 3 marks** in 14-mark descriptive questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1. Differentiate between aperiodic and sporadic tasks with an example each.**

**Model Answer:**
Aperiodic tasks are those that arrive at *random* instants with no minimum inter-arrival guarantee. Example: a user pressing a key on an embedded keypad — there is no law governing how often keys may be pressed.
Sporadic tasks also arrive randomly, but the system *guarantees* a minimum inter-arrival time $T_i$. Example: an anti-lock braking system (ABS) sensor — wheel-speed events may not occur more frequently than once every 5 ms. Sporadic tasks typically have **hard deadlines**; aperiodic tasks have **soft deadlines**. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is interrupt latency? List two factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**
Interrupt latency is the time elapsed between the assertion of an interrupt request line and the execution of the **first instruction** of the corresponding Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). It equals:

$$
L = T_{detect} + C_{save}
$$

**Two factors affecting it:**
1. The longest instruction in the currently executing task (pipelined CPUs can have bounded latency).
2. The time spent with interrupts disabled in critical sections.
3. Interrupt controller arbitration time.
**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of event-driven scheduling in a real-time system. Describe the role of the interrupt controller, context save/restore, and the priority-based dispatcher. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A real-time system handles three interrupt-driven sporadic tasks with the following parameters:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 12 |

Assume fixed-priority preemptive scheduling with $\tau_1$ having the highest priority. Using **Response-Time Analysis (RTA)**, determine whether the task set is schedulable. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

The event-driven architecture consists of four cooperating components:

1. **Event Source** — A hardware device (timer, sensor, communication port) that generates an electrical signal on a dedicated interrupt line.
2. **Interrupt Controller** — Hardware (e.g., ARM NVIC) that arbitrates simultaneous interrupts and supplies a *vector address* to the CPU.
3. **CPU + Context Manager** — Saves the current task's Program Counter (PC), Program Status Word (PSW), and general-purpose registers, then transfers control to the ISR entry point.
4. **Priority Dispatcher** — On ISR completion, examines the **Ready Queue** (ordered by static/dynamic priority) and preempts the current task if a higher-priority job is now ready.

The flow is: **Event → IRQ → Save Context → Execute Handler → Restore Context → Dispatch Next**.

Event-driven scheduling must handle **nested interrupts** (an ISR can itself be interrupted by a higher-priority event) and **shared resources** (guarded by mutexes with Priority Inheritance). **(7 marks — award 2 marks for diagram, 3 for component explanation, 2 for nesting/protocol mention.)**

#### Model Solution to (b)

Using the RTA equation:

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**For $\tau_1$:** $R_1 = 1$ ms $\leq 4$ ms $\;\;\checkmark$

**For $\tau_2$:** Iterating from $R_2^{(0)} = 2$:

$$
R_2^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 3
$$

$$
R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 3 \;\;\text{(converged)}
$$

$R_2 = 3 \leq 6 \;\;\checkmark$

**For $\tau_3$:** Iterating from $R_3^{(0)} = 3$:

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 4 = 9
$$

$$
R_3^{(4)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10
$$

$$
R_3^{(5)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \;\;\text{(converged)}
$$

$R_3 = 10 \leq D_3 = 12 \;\;\checkmark$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable** under fixed-priority preemptive scheduling. **(7 marks — 3 for RTA equation, 3 for iterations, 1 for final conclusion.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare **polled execution** and **interrupt-driven execution** as two event-handling strategies. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **priority inversion problem** in event-driven real-time systems. Explain how the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves it with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

| Aspect | Polled Execution | Interrupt-Driven Execution |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Software loops checking a flag | Hardware signals the CPU |
| Latency | Bounded by polling period | Bounded by interrupt latency |
| CPU Overhead | Continuous (high) | Event-only (low) |
| Power Consumption | High (CPU always active) | Low (CPU sleeps) |
| Determinism | Predictable | Depends on nesting |

**Polled — Advantage:** Deterministic and simple; no race conditions. **Disadvantage:** Wastes CPU cycles even when no event has occurred.
**Interrupt — Advantage:** Energy-efficient and responsive. **Disadvantage:** Complex to debug; vulnerable to priority inversion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Priority Inversion** occurs when a high-priority task $\tau_H$ is forced to wait for a low-priority task $\tau_L$ to release a shared resource, while medium-priority tasks $\tau_M$ preempt $\tau_L$, *further* delaying $\tau_H$. The high-priority task is effectively *inverted* below medium-priority ones — a potentially unbounded blocking.

**Example:** Task $\tau_L$ acquires a mutex, then $\tau_H$ arrives and blocks on it. Now $\tau_M$ preempts $\tau_L$, and $\tau_H$ waits indefinitely until both $\tau_M$ and $\tau_L$ complete — violating $\tau_H$'s hard deadline.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** When a high-priority task blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task, the lower-priority task **temporarily inherits** the priority of the highest-priority waiting task. Thus $\tau_L$ runs at the priority of $\tau_H$, preventing $\tau_M$ from preempting it. The original priority is restored when $\tau_L$ releases the resource.

This bounds the blocking time to the duration of **at most one critical section** of any lower-priority task, making the system analytically tractable. **(7 marks — 3 for problem statement, 2 for PIP mechanism, 2 for bounded blocking property.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse *interrupt latency* with *context switch time* — they are distinct. Latency ends when the first ISR instruction runs; context switch ends when the new task's first instruction runs.
> 2. **Do NOT** forget to **iterate** the RTA equation until convergence. Showing only one iteration loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Do NOT** claim EDF is event-driven — EDF is *deadline-driven* (a *time* property, not an event property). Event-driven means *trigger* by external event.
> 4. **Do NOT** mix up $C_i$ (WCET) with average execution time. Always use the *worst* case in real-time analysis.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Event-Driven Scheduling** activates tasks on external/interrupt events — it is the canonical way to handle **aperiodic** and **sporadic** workloads.
- **Sporadic tasks** have a guaranteed minimum inter-arrival $T_i$; **aperiodic tasks** do not.
- **Fixed-priority preemptive scheduling** is the dominant event-driven scheme (e.g., FreeRTOS).
- **Response-Time Analysis (RTA)** is the *exact* schedulability test; iterate:

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

- Schedulability holds iff $R_i \leq D_i$ for all $i$.
- **Interrupt latency** = detection time + context save time.
- **Priority inversion** must be mitigated using **PIP** or **PCP**.
- **Polled vs. Interrupt:** Polled is deterministic but wasteful; interrupt is efficient but needs careful design.
- Always **define symbols** before using them; always include **units**; always show **iteration tables** for RTA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EDF Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** EDF Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** is a *dynamic-priority*, *preemptive* scheduling algorithm used in **uniprocessor real-time systems**, in which the task (or job) whose **absolute deadline is closest to the current instant** is assigned the **highest priority** and is selected for execution. The priority of a job is therefore *not* fixed at design time — it is *recomputed dynamically* whenever a new job arrives or whenever a job completes, making EDF fundamentally different from static-priority schemes such as Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framing, EDF belongs to the class of *job-level dynamic* algorithms and is taught alongside Fixed Priority scheduling as the canonical dynamic counterpart. A periodic task $\tau_i$ in this context is represented by the canonical tuple $(C_i, D_i, T_i, \Phi_i)$, where $C_i$ is the worst-case execution time, $D_i$ is the relative deadline, $T_i$ is the period, and $\Phi_i$ is the phase. Under EDF, the *j-th* job of $\tau_i$, released at $r_{i,j} = \Phi_i + (j-1)T_i$, has an absolute deadline $d_{i,j} = r_{i,j} + D_i$, and at every scheduling decision instant the scheduler picks the ready job with the minimum $d_{i,j}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-style):** "EDF is a dynamic-priority algorithm that at every instant executes the ready task whose absolute deadline is nearest in the future. On a uniprocessor with preemptable, independent, periodic tasks having $D_i = T_i$, EDF is *optimal* in the sense that *any feasible schedule can be produced by EDF*."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are at the **airport check-in counter** with a single counter and a queue of passengers. Each passenger has a flight that departs at a specific time. The airline agent, being extremely smart, does **not** serve passengers in *first-come-first-served* order (FCFS), nor does she remember who arrived first. Instead, she constantly scans the queue and serves the passenger whose **flight is departing soonest** — if a passenger's flight leaves in 30 minutes and another in 2 hours, the 30-minute passenger is served first, even if the 2-hour passenger has been waiting longer.

That airport agent **is EDF**.

- The *flights* are **absolute deadlines**.
- The *service time* is the **computation time** $C_i$.
- The *passengers arriving repeatedly* are **periodic jobs**.
- A *new passenger arriving* (new job release) causes the agent to **re-evaluate the queue** — that is the *dynamic priority recomputation*.

If the agent is so fast that the total check-in workload never exceeds her single-counter capacity (utilization $\le 1$), she will never miss a flight. This is the celebrated **optimality of EDF** on a uniprocessor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** EDF is the only algorithm that achieves the *theoretical maximum* schedulability bound — 100% CPU utilization — on a uniprocessor. RMS, by contrast, is bounded at $\ln 2 \approx 69.3\%$ for harmonic-free task sets (Liu & Layland bound). Every extra percentage point of processor headroom matters in safety-critical systems.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Conditions

The following standard metrics are central to every EDF problem in KTU examinations:

- **Processor Utilization:** $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$, dimensionless, **must satisfy $U \le 1$** for EDF feasibility on a uniprocessor (sufficient only — see Processor Demand Criterion).
- **Hyperperiod (LCM):** $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$, the interval over which the periodic schedule repeats.
- **Absolute Deadline:** $d_{i,j} = \Phi_i + (j-1)T_i + D_i$.
- **Scheduling Point:** An instant at which the scheduler makes a decision (job arrival, job completion, or both).
- **Critical Instant:** The instant that produces the largest response time for a task; for EDF with $D_i = T_i$ it is typically when all higher-utilization tasks release simultaneously with $\tau_i$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Point:** The phrase *"EDF is optimal"* appears in nearly every End Semester paper. Be ready to state the precise conditions of optimality: *preemptive, independent, periodic tasks on a single processor with deadlines equal to periods.* If any of these four conditions is violated, the optimality guarantee may break.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How EDF Works Step-by-Step

The EDF scheduling logic is best understood as an *event-driven* loop. Below is the precise sequence the OS scheduler follows:

1. **Maintain a ready queue** of all jobs that have been released (arrival time $\le$ current time) but have not yet completed.
2. **Tag each ready job** with its absolute deadline $d_{i,j}$.
3. **On every scheduling event** (job arrival OR job completion OR timer tick), scan the ready queue and select the job with the **minimum** $d_{i,j}$ — this is the *earliest deadline* job.
4. **Dispatch** the selected job to the processor. If the running job is preempted by a newly arrived job with an earlier deadline, the preempted job is *re-inserted* into the ready queue with its original deadline unchanged.
5. **Repeat** indefinitely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Implementation cost:** A naive implementation requires $O(n)$ scan per event. Heaps (priority queues ordered by deadline) reduce this to $O(\log n)$ insert/extract, which is why EDF is practical in modern RTOS kernels such as **FreeRTOS** (configurable), **RTEMS**, and **VxWorks** (in some profiles).

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind EDF's Optimality

The intuition is rooted in the principle of *deadline-urgency exchange*:

- A job that finishes *strictly before* its deadline is "wasting" slack time that could have been used to begin another, more urgent job earlier.
- By always running the job whose deadline is nearest, EDF *minimizes the maximum lateness* $L_{\max} = \max_j (f_j - d_j)$ for any job set.
- This minimization of $L_{\max}$ to *zero* (when feasible) is exactly the meaning of "optimal for meeting all deadlines."

The classical *optimality theorem* (Dertouzos, 1974) is informally: *if any algorithm can produce a feasible schedule for a job set, then EDF can also produce a feasible schedule for that job set, on a preemptable uniprocessor.*

## 2.3 Comparison with Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)

| Aspect | RMS (Static Priority) | EDF (Dynamic Priority) |
|---|---|---|
| Priority assignment | Fixed by period (shorter $T_i$ → higher) | Reassigned at runtime by deadline |
| Optimality ceiling | $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1) \le \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | $U_{bound} = 1.0$ (full utilization) |
| Run-time overhead | Lower (priority table) | Higher (heap / red-black tree) |
| Predictability under overload | Predictable (low-priority tasks miss) | *Domino effect* possible |
| KTU 2024 relevance | Module 2 (also) | This topic |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examinable** in KTU 2024 ESE for PECST748. Master these four.

| # | Formula / Condition | Symbol Meaning | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Processor utilization | dimensionless | Quick feasibility check |
| 2 | $U \le 1$ | Sufficient condition for EDF | dimensionless | Necessary and sufficient for *implicit-deadline* ($D_i = T_i$) task sets |
| 3 | $h(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{t + T_i - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor \cdot C_i$ | Processor demand over $[0, t]$ | time units | Processor Demand Criterion |
| 4 | $\forall\, t > 0: \; h(t) \le t$ | Feasibility condition (Baruah et al.) | time units | Exact feasibility test for arbitrary $D_i$ |
| 5 | $d_{i,j} = \Phi_i + (j-1)T_i + D_i$ | Absolute deadline of $j$-th job | time units | Schedule construction |
| 6 | $r_{i,j} = \Phi_i + (j-1)T_i$ | Release time of $j$-th job | time units | Schedule construction |
| 7 | $R_i = f_i - r_i$ | Response time of a job | time units | Tardiness analysis |
| 8 | $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, \dots, T_n)$ | Hyperperiod | time units | Bounded simulation horizon |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Notation Trap:** KTU papers frequently use both $D_i$ (relative deadline) and $d_{i,j}$ (absolute deadline of a job). Mixing them is a guaranteed mark-deduction. Always carry units (typically **ms** in exam questions) and never let the symbol leak.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics & Spacecraft:** NASA's **VxWorks**-based CFS (Core Flight System) and ESA's RTEMS deployments use EDF variants for soft-real-time housekeeping alongside hard-RMS for control loops.
- **Industrial Control:** PROFINET IRT (Isochronous Real-Time) class 3 uses deadline-based scheduling conceptually close to EDF.
- **Linux Real-Time:** `SCHED_DEADLINE` kernel patch (merged in Linux 3.14) is a **direct implementation of CBS + EDF** — tasks are admitted via a *server-based* admission control, then scheduled by EDF. This is by far the most production-relevant EDF deployment in 2024.
- **Multimedia & Networking:** IEEE 802.16 WiMAX MAC, LTE QoS schedulers, and many VoIP systems approximate EDF under the name *Earliest-Due-Date*.

## 2.6 Limitations & When EDF is *Not* a Good Choice

- **Overload behavior is catastrophic:** When $U > 1$ and no feasible schedule exists, EDF can cause a *domino effect* in which missing one deadline cascades into a cascade of misses for many tasks simultaneously. RMS, in contrast, fails in a *predictable* manner (lowest-priority tasks miss first).
- **Multiprocessor / partitioned scheduling:** EDF is *not* optimal for multiprocessors; utilization is bounded at $\frac{m+1}{2}$ (for $m$ processors) under partitioned EDF, motivating *P-fair* algorithms such as $\text{PD}^2$.
- **High context-switch cost:** In deeply embedded systems with limited RAM, the priority-queue overhead can dominate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Processor Demand Criterion $h(t) \le t$ Characterizes Feasibility

We want a necessary and sufficient condition under which a *constrained-deadline* periodic task set $\Gamma = \{\tau_1, \dots, \tau_n\}$ is schedulable by preemptive EDF on a uniprocessor. The derivation is reproduced below in full.

### Step 1 — Define the processor demand over an interval

For any non-negative interval $[0, t]$, the total processing time demanded by all jobs of $\Gamma$ whose deadlines fall *within* $[0, t]$ is denoted $h(t)$. A schedule is feasible **iff** for every interval of length $t$, the supplied processing time (which is exactly $t$ on a non-idling uniprocessor) is at least the demanded processing time $h(t)$.

$$
h(t) \;\triangleq\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{demand}_i(t)
$$

where $\text{demand}_i(t)$ is the demand of task $\tau_i$ in $[0, t]$.

### Step 2 — Express $\text{demand}_i(t)$ for periodic tasks

A job of $\tau_i$ has deadline $d_{i,j} = (j-1)T_i + D_i$ (taking $\Phi_i = 0$ for simplicity). We need the count of jobs with $d_{i,j} \le t$:

$$
n_i(t) \;=\; \max\!\left\{ j \,\middle|\, (j-1)T_i + D_i \le t \right\}
$$

This is equivalent to:

$$
j - 1 \;\le\; \frac{t - D_i}{T_i}
$$

Since $j$ is an integer, the number of *such* jobs is the floor:

$$
n_i(t) \;=\; \max\!\left(0,\; \left\lfloor \frac{t - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor + 1\right) \;=\; \max\!\left(0,\; \left\lfloor \frac{t + T_i - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor\right)
$$

The last equality uses the identity $\lfloor x \rfloor + 1 = \lfloor x + 1 \rfloor$ for non-integer $x$, and $\lfloor (t - D_i)/T_i \rfloor + 1 = \lfloor (t - D_i + T_i)/T_i \rfloor = \lfloor (t + T_i - D_i)/T_i \rfloor$.

### Step 3 — Multiply by per-job execution cost

Each job costs $C_i$ time units, so the demand of $\tau_i$ is:

$$
\text{demand}_i(t) \;=\; n_i(t) \cdot C_i \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{t + T_i - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor \cdot C_i
$$

Summing over all tasks:

$$
h(t) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{t + T_i - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor \cdot C_i
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the supply = demand inequality

On a non-idling uniprocessor, the supply over $[0, t]$ is exactly $t$ (assuming no idle time can be postponed without harming feasibility — this is a *classical* lemma: in an EDF schedule, you can shift idle time earlier without hurting deadlines). Hence feasibility requires:

$$
\forall\, t > 0 : \quad h(t) \le t
$$

This is the **Processor Demand Criterion (PDC)** of Baruah, Mok, and Rosier (1990). $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> The check $h(t) \le t$ need only be evaluated at *deadline instants* $t \in \mathcal{D} = \{d_{i,j}\}$ within one hyperperiod, giving a finite (though possibly exponential) test set.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem.** Consider three periodic tasks on a uniprocessor under EDF:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 8 | 8 |

Check feasibility using the Processor Demand Criterion over the hyperperiod.

### Step 5 — Compute the hyperperiod

$$
H = \text{lcm}(4, 6, 8) = 24 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 6 — Enumerate deadlines in $[0, 24]$

$$
\mathcal{D} = \{4, 6, 8, 8, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 22, 24\}
$$

Distinct set: $\{4, 6, 8, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 22, 24\}$.

### Step 7 — Evaluate $h(t)$ at each candidate

$$
h(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \left\lfloor \frac{t + T_i - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor C_i
$$

Since $D_i = T_i$ (implicit-deadline), the formula simplifies to $\lfloor t / T_i \rfloor C_i$:

$$
h(t) = \left\lfloor \frac{t}{4} \right\rfloor\!\cdot 1 \;+\; \left\lfloor \frac{t}{6} \right\rfloor\!\cdot 2 \;+\; \left\lfloor \frac{t}{8} \right\rfloor\!\cdot 3
$$

Tabulating:

| $t$ (ms) | $\lfloor t/4 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor t/6 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor t/8 \rfloor$ | $h(t)$ | $h(t) \le t$ ? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | ✓ |
| 6 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 3 | ✓ |
| 8 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 7 | ✓ |
| 12 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 10 | ✓ |
| 14 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 10 | ✓ |
| 16 | 4 | 2 | 2 | 14 | ✓ |
| 18 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 16 | ✓ |
| 20 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 17 | ✓ |
| 22 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 17 | ✓ |
| 24 | 6 | 4 | 3 | 23 | ✓ |

### Step 8 — Conclusion

Since $h(t) \le t$ at every candidate deadline, the task set is **schedulable under EDF**. Total utilization $U = 1/4 + 2/6 + 3/8 = 0.25 + 0.333 + 0.375 = 0.958 < 1$, consistent with feasibility.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Hint:** When you write the table, *also* write the explicit floor expressions — KTU examiners reward showing intermediate arithmetic, not just the final number.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Feasibility Test

| Task-Set Class | Test | Formula | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Implicit-deadline ($D_i = T_i$) | Utilization bound | $U \le 1$ | $O(n)$ |
| Constrained-deadline ($D_i \le T_i$) | Processor Demand Criterion | $\forall t \in \mathcal{D}: h(t) \le t$ | Pseudo-polynomial |
| Arbitrary deadlines | Processor Demand Criterion + Response-Time Check | $R_i \le D_i$ for all $i$ | Pseudo-polynomial |
| Sporadic / Aperiodic | Feasibility interval $L^*$ | $L^* = \min\{t > 0 : W(t) = t\}$ | Iterative |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EDF Scheduler Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hardware Timer Tick] --> B[Scheduler Event Detector]
    C[New Job Arrival Interrupt] --> B
    D[Job Completion Interrupt] --> B
    B --> E[Refresh Ready Queue]
    E --> F[Scan: find min absolute deadline d_ij]
    F --> G{Is current running job earlier-deadlined than the candidate?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Continue current job]
    G -- No --> I[Preempt current job]
    I --> J[Push preempted job back to ready queue]
    J --> K[Dispatch earliest-deadline job to CPU]
    H --> K
    K --> L[CPU executes selected job]
    L --> M[Update job state: RUNNING]
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 EDF Gantt Chart — Three-Task Example (Conceptual)

The diagram below visualises the execution pattern produced by EDF for the worked example in §3.2. Each row is a task, time flows left to right, and the *labels above each block* show the job index $j$ of $\tau_i$ being executed.

```mermaid
gantt
    title EDF Gantt Chart for tau1 C=1 T=4, tau2 C=2 T=6, tau3 C=3 T=8
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section tau1 period 4
    tau1_j1 (C=1)        :a1, 0, 1
    tau1_j2 (C=1)        :a2, 4, 1
    tau1_j3 (C=1)        :a3, 8, 1
    tau1_j4 (C=1)        :a4, 12, 1
    tau1_j5 (C=1)        :a5, 16, 1
    tau1_j6 (C=1)        :a6, 20, 1
    section tau2 period 6
    tau2_j1 (C=2)        :b1, 1, 2
    tau2_j2 (C=2)        :b2, 7, 2
    tau2_j3 (C=2)        :b3, 13, 2
    tau2_j4 (C=2)        :b4, 19, 2
    section tau3 period 8
    tau3_j1 (C=3)        :c1, 3, 3
    tau3_j2 (C=3)        :c2, 11, 3
    tau3_j3 (C=3)        :c3, 15, 3
```

> [!NOTE]
> Reading the Gantt chart: at $t=0$, only $\tau_1$ job 1 is ready, so it runs from 0–1. At $t=1$, $\tau_2$'s first job (deadline 6) preempts — wait, $\tau_1$ job 2 is *not yet released* (it arrives at $t=4$). The scheduler picks the earliest deadline among $\{\tau_2(j=1, d=6)\}$ and $\{\tau_3(j=1, d=8)\}$, which is $\tau_2$ — so $\tau_2$ runs from 1–3. At $t=3$, $\tau_3$ has the earliest deadline, so it runs 3–6, etc. The dynamic reordering is the heart of EDF.

## 4.3 Feasibility Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Task Set Gamma] --> B{All D_i = T_i?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute U = sum Ci/Ti]
    C --> D{U le 1?}
    D -- Yes --> E[FEASIBLE by Utilization Test]
    D -- No --> F[Run PDC at all candidate t in D]
    B -- No --> G{All D_i le T_i?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Run PDC: check h t le t for all t in D]
    G -- No --> I[General: combined PDC + response time]
    H --> J{h t le t for all t?}
    J -- Yes --> E
    J -- No --> K[NOT FEASIBLE under EDF]
    I --> J
    F --> J
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Earliest Deadline First (EDF) scheduling. State its optimality property.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** EDF is a *dynamic-priority, preemptive* scheduling algorithm in which the ready job with the *earliest absolute deadline* is selected for execution. Priorities are recomputed at every scheduling event (arrival or completion).
- **Optimality property (1.5 marks):** On a *single processor*, with *preemptable, independent* tasks having deadlines equal to periods, *if any scheduling algorithm can feasibly schedule a task set, then EDF can also feasibly schedule it.* Equivalently, the schedulability bound is $U = 1.0$ (full utilization).

---

### Q2. Why does RMS fail to schedule certain task sets that EDF can schedule? Give one numerical example.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 3 marks]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Reason (2 marks):** RMS assigns *fixed* priorities based on periods; its worst-case schedulability bound is $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1) \le \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ for $n$ tasks. EDF, being *dynamic*, can re-prioritize at runtime and achieve $U \le 1.0$.
- **Numerical example (1 mark):** Let $\tau_1 = (1, 3)$, $\tau_2 = (1, 3)$, $\tau_3 = (1, 3)$ — three identical tasks. Utilization $U = 3 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1.0$. EDF schedules it (all have equal deadlines, no issue). But Liu–Layland bound for $n=3$ is $3(2^{1/3}-1) \approx 0.779 < 1.0$, so the *bound* does not certify RMS, and indeed RMS can be shown to fail on certain 3-task sets with $U = 1.0$ depending on phase offsets.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B Module 2 questions carry **14 marks** and feature an *internal choice* — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both versions below are self-contained.

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the Earliest Deadline First (EDF) scheduling algorithm with a neat diagram. Discuss the conditions under which EDF is optimal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following three periodic real-time tasks to be scheduled by preemptive EDF on a single processor:

| Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | $D_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 2 | 5 | 5 |
| $\tau_2$ | 1 | 8 | 8 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 | 10 |

Determine whether the task set is schedulable using the Processor Demand Criterion. Draw the Gantt chart for one hyperperiod. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**
- Definition of EDF — at every scheduling instant, the ready job with the *earliest absolute deadline* is dispatched. *(1 mark)*
- *Block diagram / flowchart* of the scheduler (similar to §4.1 above). *(2 marks)*
- *Optimality conditions*: (i) preemptive scheduling, (ii) uniprocessor, (iii) independent jobs, (iv) deadlines equal to or greater than periods? No — *implicit-deadline* is the simplest; constrained-deadline also yields optimality. State Dertouzos' theorem. *(2 marks)*
- *Comparison with RMS*: utilization bounds ($0.693$ vs $1.0$), run-time overhead, overload behaviour. *(1 mark)*
- Real-world example: `SCHED_DEADLINE` in Linux. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hyperperiod:**
$$
H = \text{lcm}(5, 8, 10) = 40
$$

**Step 2 — Compute utilization:**
$$
U = \frac{2}{5} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.4 + 0.125 + 0.3 = 0.825
$$
Since $U < 1$, *necessary* condition holds, but we need PDC for sufficiency. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Since $D_i = T_i$, formula simplifies to $h(t) = \sum \lfloor t/T_i \rfloor C_i$:**
$$
h(t) = 2 \left\lfloor \frac{t}{5} \right\rfloor + 1 \left\lfloor \frac{t}{8} \right\rfloor + 3 \left\lfloor \frac{t}{10} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4 — Candidate deadlines** (multiples of $T_i$ within $[0, 40]$):
$$
\mathcal{D} = \{5, 8, 10, 15, 16, 20, 24, 25, 30, 32, 35, 40\}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate $h(t)$ at each candidate:**

| $t$ | $\lfloor t/5 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor t/8 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor t/10 \rfloor$ | $h(t)$ | $h(t) \le t$ ? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5  | 1 | 0 | 0 | 2  | ✓ |
| 8  | 1 | 1 | 0 | 3  | ✓ |
| 10 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 8  | ✓ |
| 15 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 10 | ✓ |
| 16 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 11 | ✓ |
| 20 | 4 | 2 | 2 | 16 | ✓ |
| 24 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 17 | ✓ |
| 25 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 19 | ✓ |
| 30 | 6 | 3 | 3 | 24 | ✓ |
| 32 | 6 | 4 | 3 | 25 | ✓ |
| 35 | 7 | 4 | 3 | 26 | ✓ |
| 40 | 8 | 5 | 4 | 30 | ✓ |

**Step 6 — Conclusion (1 mark):** All deadlines satisfied, so the task set is **schedulable by EDF**. Margin slack at $t = 40$ is $40 - 30 = 10$ ms.

**Step 7 — Gantt chart (2 marks):** Draw using the same logic as §4.2. Key transitions: at $t=0$ run $\tau_1$ (d=5); at $t=2$, $\tau_3$ arrives (d=10) — earliest deadline among $\{\tau_3(d=10)\}$ is $\tau_3$ (since $\tau_1$ job 2 not yet released). At $t=5$, $\tau_1$ job 2 arrives (d=10), tied with $\tau_3$ — break ties arbitrarily, etc.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the Earliest Deadline First (EDF) algorithm. How is it different from Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)? Compare their performance under overload. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the task set:

| Task | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | $D_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 3 | 3 |
| $\tau_2$ | 1 | 5 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | 2 | 7 | 7 |

Verify schedulability using the Processor Demand Criterion. Compute the maximum slack at $t = 35$ and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**
- Block diagram of EDF scheduler (cf. §4.1). *(2 marks)*
- *Difference from RMS*: dynamic vs static priority; recomputation at every event; utilization bound $1.0$ vs $0.693$. *(2 marks)*
- *Overload behavior* (this is the key KTU 2024 favorite): Under overload, RMS fails *predictably* (lowest-priority tasks miss deadlines first), whereas EDF can suffer the **domino effect** — missing one deadline may cause a cascade of subsequent misses because the late job occupies CPU that *should* have gone to a more urgent job. *(2 marks)*
- Mitigation: use **Deadline-Monotonic** or **admission control** with EDF in production systems. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hyperperiod:**
$$
H = \text{lcm}(3, 5, 7) = 105
$$

**Step 2 — Utilization:**
$$
U = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{7} = 0.333 + 0.200 + 0.286 = 0.819
$$
$U < 1$, *necessary* condition holds. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — PDC formula** (implicit deadline, $D_i = T_i$):
$$
h(t) = 1 \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{t}{3} \right\rfloor + 1 \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{t}{5} \right\rfloor + 2 \cdot \left\lfloor \frac{t}{7} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4 — Candidate deadlines in $[0, 35]$:**
$$
\mathcal{D} = \{3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 10, 12, 14, 15, 18, 20, 21, 24, 25, 27, 28, 30, 33, 35\}
$$

**Step 5 — Critical evaluation points** (focus on the most stringent deadlines):

| $t$ | $\lfloor t/3 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor t/5 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor t/7 \rfloor$ | $h(t)$ | $h(t) \le t$ ? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3  | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1  | ✓ |
| 5  | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2  | ✓ |
| 6  | 2 | 1 | 0 | 3  | ✓ |
| 7  | 2 | 1 | 1 | 5  | ✓ |
| 10 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 7  | ✓ |
| 14 | 4 | 2 | 2 | 10 | ✓ |
| 15 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 12 | ✓ |
| 20 | 6 | 4 | 2 | 14 | ✓ |
| 21 | 7 | 4 | 3 | 17 | ✓ |
| 25 | 8 | 5 | 3 | 19 | ✓ |
| 28 | 9 | 5 | 4 | 22 | ✓ |
| 30 | 10 | 6 | 4 | 24 | ✓ |
| 35 | 11 | 7 | 5 | 28 | ✓ |

**Step 6 — Slack at $t=35$:** *(1 mark)*
$$
\text{slack}(35) = 35 - h(35) = 35 - 28 = 7 \text{ ms}
$$
The system has 7 ms of unused CPU capacity in the interval $[0, 35]$, indicating the task set is comfortably schedulable with significant headroom.

**Step 7 — Comment (1 mark):** Since the minimum slack $\min_t\{t - h(t)\} = 0$ is *not* reached (every $h(t) \le t - 1$ in fact), EDF can absorb small transient overruns. This is the engineering value of *slack* — it quantifies the safety margin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (Critical):**
> 1. **Mixing $D_i$ and $d_{i,j}$:** Students often write "$D_{i,j}$" — a notation that does not exist. Use $d_{i,j}$ for the *absolute* deadline of job $j$ and $D_i$ for the *relative* deadline of task $i$. Penalty: 0.5–1 mark per instance.
> 2. **Forgetting the floor function:** The processor demand formula contains a $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$. Writing $\frac{t + T_i - D_i}{T_i}$ *without* the floor is a textbook-style error that loses at least **2 marks** in KTU 2024 valuations.
> 3. **Stopping at $h(t) \le t$ check only at $t = H$:** This is *insufficient*. The PDC must be checked at *every* candidate deadline $t \in \mathcal{D}$. Failure to do so = up to **3 marks** lost.
> 4. **Confusing feasibility conditions:** "Necessary" vs "sufficient" — $U \le 1$ is *necessary* (and sufficient only for implicit-deadline). For $D_i < T_i$ you *must* use PDC. Examiners will not award full marks for the utilization test on a constrained-deadline problem.
> 5. **Forgetting the hyperperiod bound:** PDC checks need only be done for $t \le H$ in implicit-deadline case, and for $t \le L^*$ in the constrained case, where $L^*$ is a known finite bound. Writing "we check up to $t = \infty$" loses a mark.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ EDF = **dynamic-priority**, **preemptive**, **deadline-driven** scheduler for **uniprocessors**.
- ✅ Optimality holds on a **single processor** with **independent**, **preemptable** jobs. Dertouzos (1974).
- ✅ Schedulability bound: $U = \sum C_i / T_i \le 1$ (**sufficient and necessary** for implicit-deadline task sets).
- ✅ For constrained deadlines, use the **Processor Demand Criterion**:
  $$h(t) = \sum_i \left\lfloor \frac{t + T_i - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor C_i \le t \quad \forall t \in \mathcal{D}$$
- ✅ Check PDC only at **deadline instants** $t \in \mathcal{D}$ within one **hyperperiod** $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, \dots, T_n)$.
- ✅ Absolute deadline: $d_{i,j} = \Phi_i + (j-1)T_i + D_i$.
- ✅ **Slack at $t$:** $t - h(t)$; positive slack = safety margin.
- ✅ EDF can be implemented with a **min-heap** keyed on absolute deadline — $O(\log n)$ per event.
- ✅ **Linux `SCHED_DEADLINE`** is the canonical modern EDF implementation (CBS + EDF).
- ✅ EDF is **NOT optimal** for multiprocessors; use $\text{PD}^2$ or similar *fair* algorithms.
- ✅ Overload behavior: EDF may exhibit a *domino effect* — a single missed deadline can cascade.
- ✅ RMS vs EDF: RMS is simpler and predictable on overload; EDF achieves full utilization.
- ✅ When asked "is this task set schedulable?", show (1) utilization, (2) PDC table, (3) Gantt chart, (4) final yes/no.
- ✅ Common KTU notation: $\tau_i(C_i, D_i, T_i)$ — always define every symbol the first time you use it.
- ✅ For *sporadic* tasks, replace $T_i$ with the *minimum inter-arrival time* in all formulas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resource Sharing Among RT Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resource Sharing Among RT Tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a "Resource" in Real-Time Systems?

A **resource** in a real-time system is any software or hardware entity that a task requires to make progress and complete its execution. Resources can be:

- **Hardware resources**: Sensors, actuators, memory-mapped I/O registers, communication buses (CAN, SPI, I2C), timers, ADCs, DACs.
- **Software resources**: Shared variables, data structures, buffers, queues, files, kernel services, semaphores, mutex locks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **resource** is any passive entity (data, object, variable, hardware) whose acquisition and release is under task control. A resource is **serially reusable** if only one task can use it at a time (mutual exclusion). A task is said to be in its **critical section** when it is accessing a shared resource.

### 1.2 What is Resource Sharing?

**Resource sharing** is the mechanism by which multiple real-time tasks access the **same physical or logical resource** during their execution. Because most embedded systems (automotive ECUs, avionics, robotics controllers) run multiple concurrent tasks on a single processor, sharing is unavoidable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mutual Exclusion Principle**: When two or more tasks need the same non-shareable resource, the system must enforce that only **one** task uses the resource at any given instant, while the others wait. This is achieved using synchronization primitives like **mutexes**, **binary semaphores**, or **priority ceiling protocols**.

### 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy: The Single-Desk Office

Imagine a small engineering office with only **one printer** (the shared resource). Three engineers (tasks) — Aarav (Junior, low priority), Bhavya (Manager, medium priority), and Dr. Chitra (Director, high priority) — all occasionally need the printer.

- **Without any protocol**: Aarav begins printing a 100-page draft. Before he finishes, Dr. Chitra (the most urgent) needs a 1-page report urgently. But she must wait because the printer is busy. Bhavya arrives and wants a 2-page memo — but the printer is still held by Aarav. Now even though Bhavya is junior to Dr. Chitra, Bhavya is ahead in queue.

- **Result**: The highest-priority Director is blocked indefinitely. The "shared resource" (printer) has created a scheduling disaster.

This is precisely the **priority inversion problem** that plagues real-time systems — and the rest of this module is about formal protocols to prevent it.

### 1.4 Why is Resource Sharing a Hard Problem in RT Systems?

In a hard real-time system, every task must meet its **deadline**. When tasks share resources, three pathological phenomena emerge:

1. **Priority Inversion** — A high-priority task is forced to wait for a lower-priority task.
2. **Unbounded Priority Inversion** — The waiting time of a high-priority task becomes unpredictable (only bounded by lower-priority tasks' execution), violating real-time guarantees.
3. **Deadlock** — Two or more tasks each hold one resource and wait for the resource held by the other, causing permanent blocking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — The Mars Pathfinder Incident (1997):**
> The NASA Mars Pathfinder spacecraft experienced total system resets on Mars due to **unbounded priority inversion** in its bus management task. The fix was a *priority inheritance* implementation in the VxWorks RTOS. This is a canonical KTU exam example.

### 1.5 Classification of Resources

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Active Resource** | A resource that can change its own state (a process, server) | Network service, I/O controller |
| **Passive Resource** | A resource that cannot change its own state; tasks manipulate it | Shared variable, file, mutex, I/O register |
| **Serially Reusable Resource** | Can be used by one task at a time, then released for reuse | All hardware + most software resources |
| **Consumable Resource** | Created and consumed; not returned to a pool | Memory blocks, messages, tokens |

### 1.6 Resource Access Control — The Big Picture

Four broad families of solutions exist in real-time systems:

- **Mutual Exclusion without Blocking Avoidance** — simple locks (NOT recommended for hard RT).
- **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** — well-known, simple, used in VxWorks, POSIX.
- **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** — stronger guarantees, prevents deadlocks.
- **Stack Resource Policy (SRP)** — most general, allows shared run-time stacks.
- **Lock-Free / Wait-Free Synchronization** — atomic primitives (used cautiously in RT).

These are detailed in subsequent sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of a Critical Section

When a task accesses a shared resource, it goes through a structured sequence:

1. **Entry Protocol** — Request / lock the resource (acquire mutex).
2. **Critical Section (CS)** — Execute code that touches the shared resource.
3. **Exit Protocol** — Release the resource (unlock mutex).
4. **Remainder Section** — Normal task code, no shared resources.

The objective of resource sharing protocols is to **bound the blocking time** a task can suffer due to lower-priority tasks' critical sections.

### 2.2 Priority Inversion (Detailed)

**Definition**: *Priority inversion occurs when a higher-priority task is blocked because a lower-priority task holds a shared resource, and a medium-priority task (which does not need the resource) preempts the lower-priority task, thereby indirectly blocking the high-priority task.*

#### 2.2.1 The Three Tasks of the Canonical Example

Consider three tasks (Liu & Kay classic example):
- **Task H** (high priority) — needs resource R
- **Task M** (medium priority) — does NOT need R
- **Task L** (low priority) — needs resource R

**Sequence of events (Unbounded Priority Inversion):**

1. **L** starts, locks resource R, enters critical section.
2. **H** arrives, preempts L, tries to lock R, but R is held by L. **H blocks**.
3. **L** resumes (because H has blocked itself), continues critical section.
4. **M** arrives (medium priority), preempts L (because L is the only running task and M > L). **M runs while H waits.**
5. **M** finishes. **L** resumes, finishes critical section, releases R.
6. **H** finally acquires R and runs.

**Critical Observation**: Task H was blocked for the duration of L's critical section **PLUS the entire execution time of M** — a medium-priority task that had nothing to do with R. The blocking time depends on the durations of unrelated tasks — this is **unbounded** and unacceptable for hard real-time systems.

### 2.3 Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Sha, Rajkumar, Lehoczky, 1990):**
> Under **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**, if a higher-priority task is blocked by a lower-priority task that holds a shared resource, the lower-priority task **temporarily inherits the priority of the highest-priority task waiting** for that resource. The inheritance is transitive.

#### 2.3.1 PIP Rules

1. **Scheduling Rule**: Always dispatch the highest-priority runnable task.
2. **Priority Inheritance Rule**: When task $T_i$ blocks task $T_k$ on a resource $R$, $T_i$ executes at the **priority of $T_k$** (the highest priority among all tasks blocked waiting for $R$).
3. **Priority Restoration Rule**: When $T_i$ releases $R$, $T_i$'s priority reverts to its **original static priority**.

#### 2.3.2 Effect of PIP on the Three-Task Scenario

- L locks R, then H arrives. H blocks on R.
- **L inherits H's priority**. Now L > M.
- M arrives but **cannot preempt L** (L is now at H's priority).
- L finishes critical section, releases R, reverts to its original low priority.
- H acquires R, runs to completion.
- M now runs.

**Result**: H is blocked only for L's critical section, NOT for M's execution. **Bounded priority inversion.**

#### 2.3.3 PIP Limitations

- PIP does **not prevent deadlocks** (consider circular wait chains).
- PIP can cause **chained blocking** (a task may be transitively blocked through multiple inheritance).
- PIP does **not avoid unnecessary blocking** in all cases.

### 2.4 Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> Under **PCP**, every resource $R_k$ is assigned a **priority ceiling** $\lceil R_k \rceil$, defined as the **maximum static priority of any task that may lock $R_k$**. A task $T_i$ may lock $R_k$ only if its priority is **strictly greater** than the priority ceilings of all resources currently locked by other tasks.

#### 2.4.1 PCP Rules

1. **Scheduling Rule**: Same as PIP.
2. **Priority Inheritance Rule**: Same as PIP (L inherits H if blocked).
3. **Priority Ceiling Rule**: A task $T_i$ can lock resource $R_k$ only if $\pi_i > \lceil R_j \rceil$ for every resource $R_j$ currently locked by **any** other task $T_j$. Otherwise, $T_i$ blocks, and the task $T_j$ holding $R_j$ inherits the priority of $T_i$.

#### 2.4.2 Why PCP Prevents Deadlocks

PCP prevents deadlocks by the **ceiling rule**: a task that holds a resource will never be blocked trying to lock another resource, because its priority exceeds all other resource ceilings.

#### 2.4.3 Properties of PCP

- **Deadlock free**.
- **Transitive blocking** is limited: a task can be blocked at most **once** by another task.
- **Worst-case blocking for task $T_i$**: the duration of the **longest critical section** of any lower-priority task $T_j$ that accesses a resource whose ceiling is $\geq \pi_i$.

### 2.5 Stack Resource Policy (SRP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SRP** is the most general protocol. It uses a concept of **system ceiling** and a **preemption level** assigned to each task. Resources have ceilings equal to the maximum preemption level of any task that may lock them.

#### 2.5.1 SRP Properties

- Allows **stack sharing** between tasks (each task starts with a fresh stack frame).
- A task is allowed to start a critical section only if its **preemption level is strictly greater than the system ceiling**.
- A task can suffer blocking from at most **one lower-priority task's critical section**.

### 2.6 Comparison of Protocols

| Property | No Protocol | PIP | PCP | SRP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bounded priority inversion | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Deadlock free | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Bounded blocking | ❌ | Multiple chains | At most one CS | At most one CS |
| Stack sharing | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | Higher | Higher |
| Used in | — | VxWorks, POSIX | Ada, RTSJ | RTSJ, LITMUS^RT |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following blocking-time bounds are essential for solving KTU scheduling analysis problems. Notation: $\pi_i$ = priority of task $T_i$, $C_i$ = worst-case execution time, $B_i$ = worst-case blocking time, $\zeta_{i,k}$ = duration of the $k$-th critical section of $T_i$.

| Protocol | Worst-Case Blocking Time $B_i$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **No Protocol** | $B_i = \sum \text{(all unrelated tasks)}$ | Unbounded; do not use in hard RT |
| **PIP** | $B_i = \sum_{j < i} \zeta_{j}^{\max}$ over all CSs of $T_j$ that access a resource also accessed by $T_i$ | Includes **transitive (chained)** blocking |
| **PCP** | $B_i = \max \{\zeta_{j,k} \mid \pi_j < \pi_i \text{ and } \lceil R_k \rceil \geq \pi_i\}$ | At most one CS duration |
| **SRP** | $B_i = \max \{ \zeta_{j,k} \mid \text{preemption level}(T_j) < \text{preemption level}(T_i) \}$ | Single CS bound |
| **General Response Time** (RTA) | $R_i = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j < i} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Recurrence; iterate until fixed point |
| **Utilization Bound** (with blocking, EDF) | $U \leq 1$ for EDF | EDF schedulable iff $U \leq 1$ under SRP |

> **Symbols used**: $\pi_i$ = priority; $T_i$ = period of task $i$; $C_i$ = WCET; $B_i$ = worst-case blocking; $R_i$ = response time; $\zeta_{i,k}$ = length of $k$-th critical section of $T_i$.

### 2.8 Engineering Real-World Utility

Resource-sharing protocols underpin safety-critical systems:

- **Avionics (DO-178C)**: ARINC 653 partitioned scheduling uses ceiling-based locking.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR OS)**: Implements OSEK/VDX standard with priority ceiling.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304)**: Real-time pacemakers and infusion pumps use PIP.
- **Spacecraft (NASA CFS)**: Use POSIX mutexes with priority inheritance in cFS.
- **Industrial Robotics**: SRP enables multi-task stack sharing for memory-constrained controllers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Bounded Blocking Time Under PCP

**Problem Setup** (KTU-style 14-mark problem):
A real-time system has four tasks. Their priorities, periods, WCETs, and critical sections are given.

| Task $T_i$ | Priority $\pi_i$ | Period $T_i$ (ms) | $C_i$ (ms) | Critical Section $\zeta_i$ (ms) | Resource(s) Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 1 (Highest) | 10 | 3 | $\zeta_{1,1}=1$ | $R_1$ |
