| $T_2$ | 2 | 15 | 4 | $\zeta_{2,1}=1$ | $R_2$ |
| $T_3$ | 3 | 20 | 5 | $\zeta_{3,1}=2$ | $R_1, R_2$ |
| $T_4$ | 4 (Lowest) | 25 | 6 | $\zeta_{4,1}=3$ | $R_1$ |

> (Lower numerical value of $\pi_i$ = HIGHER priority; $\pi_1 > \pi_2 > \pi_3 > \pi_4$.)

#### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Compute Resource Priority Ceilings

$$
\lceil R_1 \rceil = \max(\pi_1, \pi_3, \pi_4) = \max(1, 3, 4) = 1
$$

$$
\lceil R_2 \rceil = \max(\pi_2, \pi_3) = \max(2, 3) = 2
$$

**Result**: $\lceil R_1 \rceil = 1$, $\lceil R_2 \rceil = 2$.

#### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Compute Worst-Case Blocking Time $B_i$ for Each Task

The general PCP blocking formula is:

$$
B_i = \max \left\{ \zeta_{j,k} \;\middle|\; \pi_j < \pi_i \text{ and } \lceil R_k \rceil \geq \pi_i \right\}
$$

**For $T_1$ (highest priority $\pi_1 = 1$)**:
Find all lower-priority tasks $T_j$ (i.e., $\pi_j > 1$) whose critical section accesses a resource $R_k$ with $\lceil R_k \rceil \geq 1$.

- $T_2$ uses $R_2$, $\lceil R_2 \rceil = 2 \geq 1$ ✓, $\zeta_{2,1}=1$
- $T_3$ uses $R_1, R_2$, both ceilings $\geq 1$ ✓, $\zeta_{3,1}=2$
- $T_4$ uses $R_1$, ceiling $= 1 \geq 1$ ✓, $\zeta_{4,1}=3$

$$
B_1 = \max(1, 2, 3) = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

**For $T_2$ ($\pi_2 = 2$)**:
Lower priority tasks with $\lceil R_k \rceil \geq 2$:

- $T_3$ uses $R_2$, $\lceil R_2 \rceil = 2 \geq 2$ ✓, $\zeta_{3,1}=2$
- $T_4$ uses $R_1$, $\lceil R_1 \rceil = 1 < 2$ ✗

$$
B_2 = \max(2) = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

**For $T_3$ ($\pi_3 = 3$)**:
Lower priority tasks with $\lceil R_k \rceil \geq 3$:

- $T_4$ uses $R_1$, ceiling $= 1 < 3$ ✗
- No qualifying task.

$$
B_3 = 0 \text{ ms}
$$

**For $T_4$ (lowest priority)**:
No lower-priority task exists, so $B_4 = 0$ ms.

#### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Response Time Analysis (RTA)

The standard recurrence is:

$$
R_i^{(n+1)} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j < i} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(n)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

> (Convention used here: task $T_j$ with $\pi_j < \pi_i$ "preempts" $T_i$.)

**For $T_1$** ($\pi_1=1$ is highest, so no higher-priority tasks preempt it):
$$
R_1 = C_1 + B_1 = 3 + 3 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$
$\Rightarrow R_1 = 6 \text{ ms} \leq T_1 = 10$ ms ✅

**For $T_2$** (preempted by $T_1$):
$$
R_2^{(0)} = C_2 + B_2 = 4 + 2 = 6
$$
$$
R_2^{(1)} = 4 + 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9
$$
$$
R_2^{(2)} = 4 + 2 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 + 3 = 9
$$
Fixed point reached: $R_2 = 9 \text{ ms} \leq 15$ ms ✅

**For $T_3$** (preempted by $T_1, T_2$):
$$
R_3^{(0)} = 5 + 0 = 5
$$
$$
R_3^{(1)} = 5 + 0 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 5 + 0 + 3 + 4 = 12
$$
$$
R_3^{(2)} = 5 + 0 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 5 + 0 + 6 + 4 = 15
$$
$$
R_3^{(3)} = 5 + 0 + \left\lceil \frac{15}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{15}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 5 + 0 + 6 + 4 = 15
$$
Fixed point: $R_3 = 15 \text{ ms} \leq 20$ ms ✅

**For $T_4$** (preempted by $T_1, T_2, T_3$):
$$
R_4^{(0)} = 6 + 0 = 6
$$
$$
R_4^{(1)} = 6 + 0 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 6 + 0 + 3 + 4 + 5 = 18
$$
$$
R_4^{(2)} = 6 + 0 + \left\lceil \frac{18}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{18}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{18}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 6 + 6 + 8 + 5 = 25
$$
$$
R_4^{(3)} = 6 + 0 + \left\lceil \frac{25}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{25}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{25}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 6 + 9 + 8 + 10 = 33
$$
$$
R_4^{(4)} = 6 + 0 + \left\lceil \frac{33}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{33}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{33}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 6 + 9 + 12 + 10 = 37
$$
$$
R_4^{(5)} = 6 + 0 + \left\lceil \frac{37}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{37}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{37}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 6 + 12 + 12 + 10 = 40
$$
Fixed point: $R_4 = 40 \text{ ms} > T_4 = 25$ ms ❌ **DEADLINE MISS!**

#### 3.1.4 Analytical Matrix — Verification Table

| Task $T_i$ | $C_i$ | $B_i$ | $R_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 3 | 3 | 6 | 10 | ✅ |
| $T_2$ | 4 | 2 | 9 | 15 | ✅ |
| $T_3$ | 5 | 0 | 15 | 20 | ✅ |
| $T_4$ | 6 | 0 | 40 | 25 | ❌ |

**Conclusion**: The system is **NOT schedulable** under PCP. To make it schedulable, one would reduce $C_4$ to $4$ ms (which gives $R_4 \approx 28$, still fails), or fundamentally redesign the system.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Priority Inversion Timing (PIP)

Given: $T_H = 4$ ms, $T_M = 6$ ms, $T_L = 8$ ms (WCETs, no resource sharing except $L$ and $H$ share $R$). $\zeta_{L} = 3$ ms, $\zeta_{H} = 1$ ms.

**Without Protocol (Unbounded Inversion):**
Blocking of $H$ = $\zeta_L + C_M = 3 + 6 = 9$ ms.

**With PIP:**
Blocking of $H$ = $\zeta_L = 3$ ms (only one CS).

**Reduction Factor**:
$$
\frac{\text{Without}}{\text{With PIP}} = \frac{9}{3} = 3
$$

This 3× reduction in blocking time is a **KTU classic exam computation**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Resource Sharing in an RTOS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1["TASK H High Priority"]
    app2["TASK M Medium Priority"]
    app3["TASK L Low Priority"]
    sched["SCHEDULER DISPATCHER"]
    pip["PIP PCP SRP PROTOCOL ENGINE"]
    resmgr["RESOURCE MANAGER CEILINGS TABLE"]
    mutex1["MUTEX R1"]
    mutex2["MUTEX R2"]
    shared["SHARED RESOURCE POOL"]
    invlog["INVERSION LOGGER"]

    app1 --> sched
    app2 --> sched
    app3 --> sched
    sched --> pip
    pip --> resmgr
    pip --> mutex1
    pip --> mutex2
    mutex1 --> shared
    mutex2 --> shared
    pip --> invlog
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Unbounded Priority Inversion

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TL as Task L (Low)
    participant TH as Task H (High)
    participant TM as Task M (Medium)
    participant R as Resource R

    TL->>R: Lock R (enter CS)
    Note over TL: L holds R, executes CS
    TH-->>TL: Arrives, preempts L
    TH->>R: Lock R
    R-->>TH: BLOCKED (R held by L)
    Note over TH: H blocked on R
    TL->>TL: L resumes (H blocked)
    Note over TL: L still in CS
    TM-->>TL: M arrives, preempts L (M > L)
    Note over TM: M runs unrelated work
    TM->>TM: M completes
    TL->>TL: L resumes, finishes CS
    TL->>R: Unlock R
    TH->>R: Lock R (succeeds)
    Note over TH: H finally runs
    TH->>R: Unlock R
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Rescue

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TL as Task L (Low)
    participant TH as Task H (High)
    participant TM as Task M (Medium)
    participant R as Resource R

    TL->>R: Lock R (enter CS)
    Note over TL: L holds R, executes CS
    TH-->>TL: Arrives, preempts L
    TH->>R: Lock R
    R-->>TH: BLOCKED
    Note over TL: L inherits H priority
    TL->>TL: L runs at HIGH priority
    TM-->>TL: M arrives
    Note over TM: M CANNOT preempt L (L is now HIGH)
    TL->>TL: L finishes CS quickly
    TL->>R: Unlock R
    Note over TL: L reverts to LOW priority
    TH->>R: Lock R (succeeds)
    TH->>R: Unlock R
    TM->>TM: M now runs
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow: Choosing a Resource-Sharing Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["START: Choose RT Resource Protocol"]
    q1{"Hard deadlines?"}
    q2{"Single shared resource?"}
    q3{"Stack sharing needed?"}
    q4{"Multiple chained resources?"}

    pip["Use PIP Priority Inheritance"]
    pcp["Use PCP Priority Ceiling"]
    srp["Use SRP Stack Resource Policy"]
    no["Avoid mutexes or use lock-free atomic ops"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|No| no
    q1 -->|Yes| q2
    q2 -->|Yes simple| pip
    q2 -->|No multiple| q4
    q4 -->|Deadlock risk| pcp
    q4 -->|Stack sharing needed| srp
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set B]**
*Define priority inversion. How does it differ from bounded and unbounded priority inversion?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is forced to wait for the execution of a lower-priority task that holds a shared resource, allowing medium-priority tasks (that do not need the resource) to preempt the lower-priority task, thereby indirectly blocking the high-priority task.
- **Bounded Inversion (1 mark):** When the blocking of the high-priority task is limited to the duration of the lower-priority task's critical section (achievable via PIP/PCP).
- **Unbounded Inversion (1 mark):** When the blocking includes the execution time of unrelated medium-priority tasks, leading to unpredictable, potentially very long delays (e.g., the Mars Pathfinder bug).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the three rules of the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Scheduling Rule (1 mark):** If multiple tasks are ready, dispatch the task with the highest (possibly inherited) priority.
2. **Priority Inheritance Rule (1 mark):** When task $T_i$ is blocked by a lower-priority task $T_k$ holding a resource $R$, $T_k$ temporarily inherits the priority of the highest-priority task waiting on $R$.
3. **Priority Restoration Rule (1 mark):** When $T_k$ releases $R$, $T_k$'s priority reverts to its original static priority. Inherited priorities are transitive.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - July 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat sequence/timing diagram how **unbounded priority inversion** occurs in a real-time system with three tasks $T_H$ (high), $T_M$ (medium), $T_L$ (low) sharing a common resource $R$. Assume $T_L$ enters the critical section first, then $T_H$ arrives, then $T_M$ arrives. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** How does the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** resolve this problem? Show the modified sequence diagram and compute the reduction in blocking time if $C_H=4$ ms, $C_M=6$ ms, $C_L=8$ ms, and the critical section of $T_L$ is $\zeta_L = 3$ ms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Unbounded Priority Inversion (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** Define priority inversion clearly.
- **(2 marks)** Draw/explain the timing sequence: $T_L$ locks $R$ → $T_H$ arrives and blocks → $T_M$ preempts $T_L$ → $T_M$ runs while $T_H$ waits → $T_L$ finishes CS → $T_H$ finally runs.
- **(2 marks)** Show that $T_H$'s blocking time $= \zeta_L + C_M$ (which is unbounded with respect to medium-priority task durations).
- **(1 mark)** Mention the Mars Pathfinder example as a real-world consequence.

Blocking without protocol:
$$
B_H = \zeta_L + C_M = 3 + 6 = 9 \text{ ms}
$$

**(b) PIP Resolution (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** State the PIP rules.
- **(2 marks)** Show the modified timing: $T_L$ locks $R$, $T_H$ blocks, $T_L$ **inherits $T_H$'s priority**, $T_M$ arrives but **cannot preempt $T_L$** (since $T_L$ is now at $T_H$'s priority), $T_L$ finishes CS quickly, releases $R$, reverts to low priority, $T_H$ runs.
- **(2 marks)** Compute blocking time with PIP:
$$
B_H^{\text{PIP}} = \zeta_L = 3 \text{ ms}
$$
- **(1 mark)** Compute reduction factor:
$$
\frac{9}{3} = 3\times \text{ reduction in blocking time}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]* — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** in detail. How does it differ from PIP? Show that PCP prevents deadlocks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the system described below. Compute the **resource priority ceilings** and the **worst-case blocking time $B_i$** for each task using PCP. Verify schedulability. **(7 marks)**

| Task $T_i$ | Priority $\pi_i$ | Period $T_i$ (ms) | $C_i$ (ms) | Critical Section $\zeta_i$ (ms) | Resources |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 1 (High) | 10 | 3 | 1 | $R_1$ |
| $T_2$ | 2 | 15 | 4 | 1 | $R_2$ |
| $T_3$ | 3 | 20 | 5 | 2 | $R_1, R_2$ |
| $T_4$ | 4 (Low) | 25 | 6 | 3 | $R_1$ |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PCP Explanation (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** Define priority ceiling of a resource.
- **(2 marks)** State the three PCP rules (Scheduling, Inheritance, Ceiling).
- **(2 marks)** Show that PCP prevents deadlocks: under the ceiling rule, a task holding a resource has a priority higher than all ceilings of resources held by other tasks, so it cannot be blocked when requesting another resource — hence no circular wait.
- **(1 mark)** State the key difference from PIP: PIP bounds priority inversion but allows deadlocks; PCP bounds both.

**(b) Computations (7 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Ceilings:
$$
\lceil R_1 \rceil = \max(1, 3, 4) = 1, \quad \lceil R_2 \rceil = \max(2, 3) = 2
$$
- **(3 marks)** Blocking times:
$$
B_1 = 3, \quad B_2 = 2, \quad B_3 = 0, \quad B_4 = 0 \text{ ms}
$$
- **(3 marks)** RTA iteration (full iteration as shown in Section 3.1.3) — conclude $R_4 = 40 \text{ ms} > 25$ ms → **Not schedulable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting transitive blocking in PIP (–2 marks):** Many students compute $B_i$ as a single critical section duration even for PIP. PIP allows **chained** blocking — sum all relevant CSs.
> 2. **Wrong ceiling definition (–1 mark):** The ceiling of a resource is the **maximum priority of tasks that LOCK it**, not the maximum priority in the system. Writing $\lceil R \rceil = 1$ for all resources is a common error.
> 3. **Iterating RTA without a fixed-point check (–2 marks):** You must iterate the recurrence $R_i^{(n+1)} = C_i + B_i + \sum \lceil R_i^{(n)} / T_j \rceil C_j$ until the value stops growing. Stopping at one iteration may give an incorrect (overly optimistic) answer.
> 4. **Ignoring the convention for $\pi_i$ (–1 mark):** Some texts use higher numerical value = higher priority. Always state the convention you are using at the start of the answer.
> 5. **PIP vs PCP confusion on deadlock prevention (–1 mark):** PIP does **NOT** prevent deadlocks. Only PCP and SRP do.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Resource**: a passive entity a task needs (shared variables, I/O devices, mutexes).
- **Mutual exclusion**: only one task at a time may access a non-shareable resource.
- **Priority inversion** = high-priority task is blocked by lower-priority tasks. Unbounded inversion is **fatal** in hard RT.
- **PIP (Priority Inheritance Protocol)**: blocked lower-priority task **temporarily inherits** the higher-priority task's priority; bounded inversion; does **NOT prevent deadlocks**; allows **transitive/chained blocking**.
- **PCP (Priority Ceiling Protocol)**: each resource has a **ceiling = max priority of any task that may lock it**; a task locks a resource only if its priority is strictly higher than all current ceilings; **deadlock-free**; a task can be blocked **at most once**.
- **SRP (Stack Resource Policy)**: most general, enables **stack sharing**; uses preemption levels and system ceiling; similar blocking bound to PCP.
- **Key formulas**:
  * PIP blocking: $B_i = \sum \zeta_j^{\max}$ (sum over all CSs of $T_j$ that share resources with $T_i$, $\pi_j < \pi_i$).
  * PCP blocking: $B_i = \max \zeta_{j,k}$ (one CS, single ceiling-gated critical section).
  * RTA recurrence: $R_i^{(n+1)} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j < i} \lceil R_i^{(n)} / T_j \rceil C_j$.
- **Classic Examples to remember**: Mars Pathfinder (PIP rescue), ARINC 653 / AUTOSAR OS (PCP), LITMUS^RT research (SRP), Dijkstra's dining philosophers.
- **Comparison mantra for exams**: "**PIP bounds inversion; PCP prevents deadlock; SRP shares stacks.**"
- **Blocking-time ordering**: $B_i^{\text{PIP}} \geq B_i^{\text{PCP}} \geq B_i^{\text{SRP}}$ (PCP is tighter than PIP).
- **Exam mantra**: always state the **ceiling definition**, **priority convention**, and **fixed-point** convergence for RTA.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priority Inversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Priority Inversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly in real-time systems where a higher-priority task is **indirectly preempted** by a lower-priority task, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities of two competing tasks. Formally, it occurs when a *Higher Priority Task (H)* is blocked on a **shared resource** held by a *Lower Priority Task (L)*, and one or more *Medium Priority Tasks (M)* — unrelated to the critical section — preempt *L*, thereby causing *H* to wait indefinitely for *M* to complete.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, priority inversion is classified into two canonical variants:

| Variant | Property |
|---|---|
| **Unbounded Priority Inversion** | The blocking duration is **unbounded** — theoretically infinite if *M* tasks keep arriving. |
| **Bounded Priority Inversion** | The blocking duration is **bounded** by known protocol properties (e.g., one critical section length). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 emphasizes that priority inversion is a **semantic violation of fixed-priority preemption** — the *highest-priority ready task is not executing*, which directly violates the Rate Monotonic / Deadline Monotonic assumption used in schedulability analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Traffic Jam on a Single-Lane Bridge

Imagine a narrow, single-lane bridge where only one vehicle can pass at a time:

1. **Truck (Low Priority)** enters the bridge first — it is slow and long.
2. **Ambulance (High Priority)** arrives behind the truck. The ambulance *cannot* pass because the bridge is occupied.
3. **Sports cars (Medium Priority)** now zoom past on the **opposite lane** and continue driving, completely unrelated to the bridge.
4. The **ambulance is effectively stuck** behind the truck, but the *real culprit* delaying it is the **continuous stream of medium-priority cars** that keep arriving, while the truck is "distracted."

> **Mapping:** Truck = Low-priority task holding the lock; Ambulance = High-priority task blocked on the mutex; Sports cars = Medium-priority tasks running unimpeded. The **ambulance's urgency is "inverted"** — it behaves as if it were lower than the sports cars.

### 1.3 Standard KTU Metrics to Remember

- **Critical Section (CS):** Region of code accessing a shared resource under mutual exclusion.
- **Blocking Time (B_i):** Maximum time task *i* can be blocked due to lower-priority tasks.
- **Priority Inheritance:** A dynamic priority-boosting mechanism.
- **Priority Ceiling:** A static priority reservation mechanism.
- **Standard OS metric:** $C_i$ (worst-case execution time), $B_i$ (blocking factor), $R_i$ (response time).

> [!TIP]
> The KTU textbook reference is **"Real-Time Systems" by Jane W. S. Liu (PHI/Pearson, 2000)** — Chapter on Resource Sharing — which is the canonical source for these definitions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Task Canonical Scenario

The minimal configuration required to demonstrate priority inversion consists of **three tasks** with distinct priorities:

- Task **H** (High Priority) — accesses shared resource *R*.
- Task **M** (Medium Priority) — does *not* access *R*.
- Task **L** (Low Priority) — accesses shared resource *R*.

### 2.2 Operational Timeline (Without Any Protocol)

**Why it happens — Step-by-step logic:**

1. At time $t_0$, task **L** starts executing and enters its **critical section**, acquiring resource *R* via `lock(R)`.
2. At time $t_1$, task **H** arrives (preempts L) and tries to `lock(R)`. Since R is held by L, **H blocks** and is placed on the resource's wait queue.
3. At time $t_2$, task **M** arrives. **M preempts L** because L is now ready (not executing) and M has higher priority than L.
4. Task **M runs to completion**, arbitrarily long.
5. After M finishes, **L resumes**, finishes its critical section, and releases R.
6. **H is unblocked** and finally runs.

**The anomaly:** H waited not just for L's critical section, but for the **entire duration of M** — even though H has higher priority than both. This is **unbounded priority inversion**.

### 2.3 Solutions — KTU-Mandated Protocols

#### 2.3.1 Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** When a task *L* blocks a higher-priority task *H*, *L* temporarily **inherits H's priority** until it releases the resource. This prevents medium-priority tasks from preempting L.

- **Effect:** M cannot preempt L (since L now runs at H's priority).
- **Bounding:** H is blocked for **at most one critical section** of L.
- **Drawback:** Can still suffer from **chained blocking** and **deadlock** (transitively, not eliminated).

#### 2.3.2 Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** Each resource/mutex is assigned a **priority ceiling** equal to the highest priority of any task that may lock it. A task may enter a critical section **only if** its priority is **strictly higher** than the priority ceilings of all resources currently locked by other tasks.

- **Effect:** Prevents deadlocks and **transitive blocking**.
- **Bounding:** H is blocked for **at most one critical section**.
- **Variants:** Original Ceiling Protocol (OCP) and Immediate Ceiling Protocol (ICP / Immediate Priority Ceiling).

#### 2.3.3 Highest Locker Priority (HLP) Protocol

A hybrid scheme where the resource's ceiling equals the highest locker priority; lock acquisition temporarily boosts the requesting task's priority.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready summary** for solving priority-inversion problems. **Do not use vertical pipes `|` inside cells** — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid markdown breakage.

| Concept | Formula / Property | Units / Domain | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Worst-case Blocking Time (PIP/PCP)** | $B_i \le \sum_{k \in \text{locks}(i)} \max_{j \,:\, \text{lock } k \text{ used by } j, \, P_j < P_i} CS_j$ | Time units (ticks / µs) | Schedulability test |
| **Response Time** | $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i$ | Time units | RTA equation |
| **Interference** | $I_i = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$ | Time units | Sum of higher-priority work |
| **Rate Monotonic Bound (with blocking)** | $U_{bound} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ only if $B_i = 0$; else use RTA | Dimensionless | Quick feasibility check |
| **PIP Block Bound** | $B_i \le \sum \text{length of one CS per shared lock}$ | Time units | Direct bound |
| **PCP Block Bound** | $B_i \le \text{length of one CS of any lower-priority task}$ | Time units | Direct bound |
| **Unbounded Inv. Duration** | $B_i^{\text{unbounded}} \le \sum_{k=1}^{n_M} C_k$ (worst case: all medium tasks) | Time units | Baseline (no protocol) |
| **Inversion Duration (bounded)** | $D_{\text{inv}} \le \max_{j : P_j < P_i, \, j \text{ locks } R} \text{CS}_j$ | Time units | With PIP/PCP |
| **Effective Utilization** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Dimensionless | Schedulability input |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** NASA's **Mars Pathfinder** (1997) experienced system resets due to priority inversion on the VxWorks bus mutex — solved remotely by enabling priority inheritance.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR / OSEK):** PCP variants are used in engine control units (ECUs) to bound worst-case blocking.
- **Robotics (ROS 2 / Xenomai):** PIP is the default mutex protocol in POSIX real-time threads.
- **Medical devices:** Insulin pumps and pacemakers use priority ceiling to guarantee life-critical task deadlines.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exams, **always state which protocol you are using** before computing $B_i$. Markers deduct 1–2 marks for an unspecified protocol.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Timing of the Three-Task Scenario (No Protocol)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every transition is shown explicitly. We define:
> - $T_H = 8$ (period of H)
> - $T_M = 12$ (period of M)
> - $T_L = 20$ (period of L)
> - $C_H = 2$, $C_M = 4$, $C_L = 5$ (worst-case execution)
> - $CS_L = 3$ (critical section length of L)
> - All times in **ms**.

**Step 1 — Initial execution of L (Low Priority).**

At $t = 0$, only L is ready. L starts and acquires resource R, enters its critical section.

**Step 2 — Arrival of H (High Priority) at t = 2 ms.**

H preempts L. H tries to lock R, but R is held by L. **H blocks.** L is moved back to the ready queue at *priority* equal to H (under PIP) — but **without a protocol**, L remains at original low priority.

**Step 3 — Arrival of M (Medium Priority) at t = 4 ms.**

M preempts L (since L is the only running ready task and M > L). M runs for its full $C_M = 4$ ms.

**Step 4 — M finishes at t = 8 ms.** L resumes.

**Step 5 — L completes critical section at t = 9 ms**, releases R, finishes remaining work at t = 9 ms (since CS started at t=0, 3 ms long, L ran 2 ms before being preempted → 2+1=3 ms used in CS; remaining 2 ms non-CS).

**Step 6 — H unblocks at t = 9 ms** and runs for $C_H = 2$ ms, finishing at $t = 11$ ms.

Now let us formalize the inversion duration algebraically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{inv}} &= \underbrace{\text{(remaining CS of L)}}_{= CS_L - t_{\text{elapsed in CS}}} + \underbrace{\text{(work of all M-tasks that preempt L)}}_{= \sum C_{M_k}} \\[6pt]
&= (3 - 2) + 4 \\[4pt]
&= 1 + 4 = 5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Response time of H: } R_H &= t_{\text{finish}_H} - t_{\text{arrival}_H} \\[4pt]
&= 11 - 2 = 9 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compare with **H's intrinsic execution** $C_H = 2$ ms. H waited **9 − 2 = 7 ms** beyond its own computation, of which **4 ms was due to M** (the inversion portion) and **1 ms was the leftover CS of L**.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Same Scenario WITH Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)

**Step 1:** At $t=0$, L starts and enters CS, locking R.

**Step 2:** At $t=2$, H arrives, preempts L, attempts `lock(R)`, **blocks**. **PIP activates**: L's dynamic priority is boosted to $P_H$ (highest waiting task's priority).

**Step 3:** At $t=4$, M arrives. M attempts to preempt L, but L's current dynamic priority is now $P_H > P_M$. **M cannot preempt L**. M is placed in the ready queue.

**Step 4:** L continues its critical section from $t=4$ to $t=5$ (1 ms remaining). Releases R at $t=5$. L's priority reverts to original $P_L$.

**Step 5:** At $t=5$, H is unblocked (priority is now highest ready), runs from $t=5$ to $t=7$.

**Step 6:** At $t=7$, H finishes. M (still pending) now runs from $t=7$ to $t=11$.

**Step 7:** L resumes its remaining non-CS work (1 ms) from $t=11$ to $t=12$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_H^{\text{PIP}} &= t_{\text{finish}_H} - t_{\text{arrival}_H} = 7 - 2 = 5 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
B_H^{\text{PIP}} &= R_H^{\text{PIP}} - C_H = 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ ms} \\[4pt]
\text{vs. } B_H^{\text{no protocol}} &= 9 - 2 = 7 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The **blocking time under PIP is exactly the length of L's critical section** that remains when H blocks — i.e., $B_H \le CS_L = 3$ ms. This is the **bounded** property.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Metric | No Protocol | PIP | PCP / ICP |
|---|---|---|---|
| $B_H$ (blocking on H) | **Unbounded** (≤ $\sum C_{M_k}$) | ≤ $CS_L$ | ≤ $CS_{\max, \text{lower}}$ |
| Deadlock possible? | Yes (if multiple locks) | Yes (transitive) | **No** |
| Chained blocking? | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Runtime overhead | **None** | Medium (priority changes) | High (ceiling checks on each lock) |
| Implementation cost | 0 | O(1) per lock | O(1) per lock + static analysis |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Response Time Analysis (RTA)

Consider three tasks with shared resource *R* under **PCP**:

- $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 1$, $T_1 = 4$, $P_1 = \text{High}$, uses $R$ with $CS_1 = 0.5$.
- $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 2$, $T_2 = 6$, $P_2 = \text{Medium}$, does not use $R$.
- $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 3$, $T_3 = 10$, $P_3 = \text{Low}$, uses $R$ with $CS_3 = 1$.

Under PCP, ceiling of $R$ is $\max(P_1, P_3) = P_1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= 0 \quad \text{(no lower-priority task can block H — H is highest)} \\
B_2 &= CS_3 = 1 \quad \text{(M blocked by L's CS if L enters first)} \\
B_3 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**RTA for $\tau_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= C_2 + B_2 = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
I_2^{(0)} &= \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 1 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= C_2 + B_2 + I_2^{(0)} = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \\
I_2^{(1)} &= \left\lceil \frac{4}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 1 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \quad \text{(fixed point)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_2 = 4 \le T_2 = 6$, **$\tau_2$ is schedulable**.

> [!WARNING]
> The iterative RTA must **start with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$** (not $C_i + B_i$); the $B_i$ term is sometimes mistakenly added to the initial guess. Board examiners **deduct marks** for this.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define priority inversion. Differentiate between bounded and unbounded priority inversion.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Priority Inversion (1 mark):** A scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is *indirectly* blocked by a lower-priority task while an intermediate-priority task preempts the lower-priority task, causing the higher-priority task to wait indefinitely.
>
> **Unbounded Priority Inversion (1 mark):** The blocking duration depends on the *number and execution times* of medium-priority tasks; it has no static upper bound. Example: the original Three-Task scenario without a protocol.
>
> **Bounded Priority Inversion (1 mark):** The blocking duration is **statically bounded** by known protocol properties, e.g., under PIP/PCP, $B_i \le$ (length of one critical section of any lower-priority task).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Retest Model]**
*State the rules of the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP). Why does it bound priority inversion?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Rule 1 (1 mark):** When a higher-priority task *H* blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task *L*, *L* temporarily **inherits H's priority** until *L* releases the resource.
>
> **Rule 2 (1 mark):** The inheritance is **transitive**: if *L* is itself blocked waiting for another task, the inheritance propagates up the chain.
>
> **Bounding Justification (1 mark):** Medium-priority tasks *M* cannot preempt *L* (since *L* now runs at *H*'s priority > *P_M*). Thus *H* is blocked for **at most the remaining critical section of *L***, which is bounded.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Module question offers **internal choice between two questions** (OR option). Both must be answered at full depth. Each 14-mark question typically splits as 7 + 7 (or 5 + 9).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Model, Module 2 Internal Choice A]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider three periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling:
- $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 1$, $T_1 = 4$, accesses resource $R$ (CS length = 0.5).
- $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 2$, $T_2 = 6$, no shared resource.
- $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 3$, $T_3 = 10$, accesses resource $R$ (CS length = 1).

The system uses the **Priority Inheritance Protocol**. Draw the schedule for time $t = 0$ to $t = 10$ ms and compute the worst-case blocking time $B_2$ for $\tau_2$. Is $\tau_2$ schedulable? Justify using RTA.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**. How does it differ from PIP in handling deadlocks and chained blocking? Provide one real-world scenario where PCP is preferred over PIP.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

*Schedule construction (3 marks):*
- Priorities (RM): $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$.
- $\tau_3$ arrives at $t=0$, runs from $t=0$ to $t=1$, enters CS, locks $R$.
- $\tau_1$ arrives at $t=1$, preempts $\tau_3$, but $\tau_1$ requests $R$ → blocks. **PIP activates**: $\tau_3$'s priority boosted to $P_1$.
- $\tau_1$ blocked; $\tau_3$ resumes at boosted priority, runs CS until $t=2$ (1 ms CS done), releases $R$ at $t=2$, priority restored to $P_3$.
- $\tau_1$ unblocked at $t=2$, runs from $t=2$ to $t=3$ ($C_1 = 1$).
- $\tau_2$ arrives at $t=2$, but $\tau_1$ is running (higher prio), so $\tau_2$ waits.
- $\tau_3$ resumes at $t=3$, runs non-CS remainder (1 ms), finishes at $t=4$.
- $\tau_2$ runs from $t=4$ to $t=6$ ($C_2 = 2$).

*Blocking time computation (2 marks):*
$$
B_2 = CS_{\tau_3} = 1 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(under PIP)}
$$

*RTA for $\tau_2$ (2 marks):*
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= C_2 = 2 \\
I_2^{(0)} &= \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 1 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 \\
I_2^{(1)} &= \left\lceil \frac{4}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 1 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 4 \quad \text{(fixed point)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_2 = 4 \le T_2 = 6$, **$\tau_2$ is schedulable**. ✅

**(b) — 7 marks**

*PCP Definition (2 marks):* Each mutex/resource is assigned a **priority ceiling** = highest priority of any task that may lock it. A task $T$ may lock a resource only if its **dynamic priority is strictly higher than the ceilings of all resources currently locked by other tasks**. This preemptively prevents cyclic-wait deadlocks.

*PCP vs PIP (4 marks):*

| Property | PIP | PCP |
|---|---|---|
| Blocking bound | ≤ sum of CS lengths of one lower-prio task per shared lock | ≤ **one** CS length of **any** lower-prio task |
| Deadlock prevention | **No** (only inheritance) | **Yes** (ceiling check enforces total ordering) |
| Chained blocking | Possible (transitive inheritance) | **Impossible** (single-shot block) |
| Overhead | Dynamic priority updates on each block | Static ceiling table + check per lock |

*Real-world scenario (1 mark):* **AUTOSAR-OS in automotive ECUs** uses Immediate Ceiling Protocol (variant of PCP) because deterministic, deadlock-free behavior is mandatory for safety-critical functions (ABS, airbag deployment).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Sample Model, Module 2 Internal Choice B]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Mars Pathfinder incident** (1997) as a classic case of priority inversion. Identify which task was high-priority, which was low-priority, and explain how the system was fixed remotely by NASA engineers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A system has tasks $\tau_1$ (highest), $\tau_2$, $\tau_3$ (lowest) sharing a common mutex $M$ under the **Immediate Ceiling Protocol (ICP)**. The critical section lengths are $CS_1 = 0.5$, $CS_2 = 0.8$, $CS_3 = 1.2$ ms respectively. Compute the maximum blocking time for each task and state which task experiences zero blocking.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

*Background (2 marks):* The Mars Pathfinder spacecraft (lander deployed July 4, 1997) experienced **spontaneous system resets** approximately every few hours, threatening the entire mission. VxWorks RTOS was used.

*Tasks involved (2 marks):*
- **High-priority bus management task** (communications with Earth).
- **Medium-priority meteorological task** (weather data collection).
- **Low-priority bus distribution task** (data transfer to instrumentation bus).
- A shared **bus semaphore** protected the ASI bus.

*Inversion mechanism (2 marks):* The low-priority task acquired the bus semaphore. The high-priority task tried to acquire it and blocked. The medium-priority meteorological task preempted the low-priority task. The high-priority task was now blocked for an **unbounded duration**, causing a **watchdog timeout** → system reset. This is the canonical **unbounded priority inversion** failure.

*Remote fix (1 mark):* Engineers at JPL uploaded a **VxWorks patch from Earth** that enabled the **Priority Inheritance Protocol** on the bus semaphore. After the patch, inversions were bounded to one critical section and the resets ceased. This is one of the most cited real-world priority-inversion case studies.

**(b) — 7 marks**

*ICP rule recap (2 marks):* Under ICP, the ceiling of mutex $M$ is $C(M) = \max(P_1, P_2, P_3) = P_1$. A task acquiring $M$ immediately has its priority raised to $C(M) = P_1$.

*Blocking computation (3 marks):*
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= 0 \quad \text{(no higher prio, cannot be blocked by definition)} \\
B_2 &= CS_3 = 1.2 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(blocked at most by one CS of } \tau_3) \\
B_3 &= 0 \quad \text{(no lower-priority task to block on)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Zero-blocking task (1 mark):* **$\tau_1$ and $\tau_3$ experience zero blocking** under ICP, because $\tau_1$ is the highest-priority task (no higher waiter), and $\tau_3$ is the lowest-priority task (no lower task to be blocked by).

*Final answer (1 mark):* $\tau_2$ suffers the only blocking of $B_2 = 1.2$ ms. This bound is **single-shot** and **deadlock-free** under ICP.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in priority-inversion questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the protocol** before computing $B_i$. Always write "Under PIP/PCP, …" as a prefix. (**−1 mark**)
> 2. **Confusing PIP with PCP.** PIP uses *dynamic* inheritance; PCP uses *static* ceilings. Examiners **deduct 2 marks** for interchange.
> 3. **Starting RTA iteration with $C_i + B_i$** instead of $C_i$. The fixed-point iteration is sensitive to the initial guess.
> 4. **Omitting the Gantt chart** when the question says "draw the schedule." A textual description without a chart loses **2–3 marks** out of 7.
> 5. **Claiming PIP prevents deadlocks.** It does **not** — this is a frequent factual error worth **2 marks**.
> 6. **Mis-computing ceiling** in PCP. The ceiling is the **maximum priority of any task that may lock the resource**, not the priority of the *current* locker.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density bullet recap for last-minute revision:**

- ✅ **Priority Inversion** = H blocked on resource held by L, with M preempting L.
- ✅ **Unbounded** inversion = blocking time depends on M's arrival pattern (no protocol).
- ✅ **Bounded** inversion = blocking time ≤ one critical section (PIP/PCP).
- ✅ **PIP rule:** Lower-priority task **inherits** the highest waiting task's priority on the resource.
- ✅ **PCP rule:** Resource has a static **ceiling** = max(priority of all lockers); lock acquisition requires strict priority dominance.
- ✅ **ICP (Immediate Ceiling Protocol):** A simpler variant — priority is raised to ceiling **immediately** upon lock acquisition.
- ✅ **PIP does NOT prevent deadlock**; PCP DOES.
- ✅ **PCP prevents chained blocking**; PIP allows transitive inheritance chains.
- ✅ **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** is the canonical real-world PIP-enabled-fix case.
- ✅ **Blocking term** $B_i$ is **added** in the RTA equation: $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i$.
- ✅ **Initial RTA guess:** $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ (NOT $C_i + B_i$).
- ✅ **RTA fixed point:** Iterate until $R_i^{(n+1)} = R_i^{(n)}$; schedulable iff $R_i \le D_i \le T_i$.
- ✅ **Examiner trick:** "Compute $B_i$" → always specify protocol first; default answer for "no protocol" is **unbounded**.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** "**P**IP = **P**riority **I**nherits on **P**reempt"; "**P**CP = **P**riority **C**eiling **P**rotects."
- ✅ **Standard reference:** Jane W. S. Liu, *Real-Time Systems*, Chapter on Resource Sharing.
- ✅ **POSIX thread mutex attribute:** `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT` enables PIP; some systems support `PTHREAD_PRIO_PROTECT` (ceiling).

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** is a synchronization protocol for shared resources in real-time systems, formally proposed by **Sha, Rajkumar, and Lehoczky (1990)**. It is a *resource allocation protocol* designed to **eliminate unbounded priority inversion** and prevent **deadlock** in fixed-priority preemptive systems.

In PIP, when a high-priority task is **blocked** by a lower-priority task that has locked a shared resource, the lower-priority task **temporarily inherits the priority of the highest-priority task waiting** for that resource. This "priority inheritance" continues until the resource is released, at which point the task reverts to its original (assigned) priority.

The protocol is governed by three fundamental rules:

1. **Rule 1 — Acquisition:** A task $J$ enters its critical section and locks the shared resource at its **assigned (original) priority**.
2. **Rule 2 — Inheritance (Promotion):** If a higher-priority task $H$ attempts to lock the same resource and is blocked, the executing task $J$ **dynamically inherits the priority** of $H$ (i.e., its effective priority is raised to $\pi_H$, where $\pi_H > \pi_J$).
3. **Rule 3 — Restoration (Demotion):** When $J$ exits the critical section and releases the resource, its priority is **restored to the original assigned value** $\pi_J$ (which may be the *highest* priority it inherited during the chain — transitively).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** PIP is a *static* (run-time overhead is low) protocol. It eliminates **unbounded** priority inversion but **does NOT prevent deadlock** when tasks acquire multiple resources. The extended version, **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**, was introduced precisely to address this limitation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boss and the Intern" Story

Imagine a corporate office with three employees:

- **Alice (Senior Manager)** — Assigned Priority: **HIGH (5)**
- **Bob (Team Lead)** — Assigned Priority: **MEDIUM (3)**
- **Charlie (Intern)** — Assigned Priority: **LOW (1)**

All three need to use the **single company printer** (a shared resource).

**Without any protocol (Chaos):**
Charlie starts printing a 500-page report. Alice urgently needs a 1-page document, but the printer is locked. Even though Alice is more important, she must wait because Charlie's job is in progress. Meanwhile, Bob (medium priority) preempts Charlie to do his own (non-printer) work. Alice is stuck waiting for *both* Charlie AND Bob — even though neither of them has anything to do with her document! This is **unbounded priority inversion**.

**With Priority Inheritance Protocol:**
Charlie is printing. Alice arrives, tries to print, gets **blocked**. Suddenly, Charlie's badge lights up and flashes **"PRIORITY 5"** — he has **temporarily inherited Alice's senior-manager priority**. Now Bob cannot preempt Charlie, because Charlie (acting as Alice's proxy) outranks Bob. Charlie finishes the 500 pages, prints Alice's 1 page, releases the printer, and his badge reverts to **"PRIORITY 1"**. 

> [!TIP]
> **The "Boss and Intern" mental model:** The lower-priority task "borrows" the boss's authority **only** to finish the critical work quickly, then returns it. The boss doesn't get promoted; the intern gets temporarily upgraded.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Assigned Priority | $\pi_i$ | The static, original priority of task $J_i$ |
| Effective Priority | $\hat{\pi}_i$ | The *runtime* priority (may be boosted) |
| Critical Section | $Z_i$ | Code segment accessing shared resource |
| Resource | $R_k$ | A shared, mutually-exclusive entity |
| Blocking | $B_i$ | Time task $J_i$ waits due to lower-priority task |
| Priority Inversion | — | A higher-priority task waiting for a lower-priority one |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Throughout Module 2, priorities are denoted such that a *larger numerical value* means *higher priority* (e.g., $\pi_H = 7, \pi_M = 4, \pi_L = 2$). Some texts invert this — always verify the convention used in your problem statement.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Priority Inversion Problem (Background)

Priority Inversion occurs when a higher-priority task is **forced to wait** for a lower-priority task to release a shared resource. There are three categories:

1. **No Inversion** — Tasks do not interact on shared resources.
2. **Bounded Priority Inversion** — A high-priority task waits only for the duration of the *interfering* lower-priority task's critical section.
3. **Unbounded Priority Inversion** — A high-priority task waits for an **arbitrarily long** time due to *preemption* of the resource-holding task by medium-priority tasks. This is the **pathological case** (famously, the **Mars Pathfinder bug, 1997**).

The maximum blocking time under PIP for a task $J_i$ that accesses $n_i$ shared resources is given by the **minimax formula**:

$$
B_i = \min_{k=1}^{n_i} \left( \text{duration of the longest critical section of any lower-priority task on } R_k \right)
$$

In the worst case (single shared resource scenario):

$$
B_i^{PIP} = \max_{k : \pi_{J_k} < \pi_{J_i}} \left( C_k^{crit} \right)
$$

where $C_k^{crit}$ is the execution time of the critical section of task $J_k$ on the contended resource.

## 2.2 Operational Walkthrough — How PIP Works

Consider three tasks accessing a single shared resource $R_1$:

- $J_H$ (High priority, $\pi_H = 7$)
- $J_M$ (Medium priority, $\pi_M = 4$)
- $J_L$ (Low priority, $\pi_L = 2$)

**Timeline Analysis (PIP Behavior):**

| Time | Event | Effective Priorities | Inversion? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t_0$ | $J_L$ starts; locks $R_1$; enters critical section | $\hat{\pi}_L = 2$ | No |
| $t_1$ | $J_H$ arrives; preempts $J_L$ | $\hat{\pi}_H = 7$ | — |
| $t_2$ | $J_H$ requests $R_1$; **BLOCKED** (held by $J_L$) | — | Inversion begins |
| $t_3$ | $J_L$ **inherits** $\pi_H$ | $\hat{\pi}_L = 7, \hat{\pi}_H = 7$ | Bounded |
| $t_4$ | $J_M$ arrives; **CANNOT preempt** $J_L$ (both at 7) | $\hat{\pi}_L = 7$ | Bounded only |
| $t_5$ | $J_L$ exits critical section; releases $R_1$ | $\hat{\pi}_L = 2$ | — |
| $t_6$ | $J_H$ acquires $R_1$; preempts $J_L$ | $\hat{\pi}_H = 7$ | — |
| $t_7$ | $J_H$ exits critical section; resumes $J_L$ | $\hat{\pi}_L = 2$ | — |

**Key Observation:** Without PIP, $J_M$ at $t_4$ would have preempted $J_L$, causing **unbounded** inversion. With PIP, $J_L$ runs at priority 7 until it releases $R_1$.

## 2.3 Transitive Inheritance (Chain Blocking)

PIP supports **transitive priority inheritance**, where the inheritance can cascade through multiple blocked tasks:

$$
J_A \rightarrow J_B \rightarrow J_C
$$

- $J_C$ holds $R_2$, blocks on $R_1$ held by $J_B$.
- $J_B$ holds $R_1$, blocks on $R_3$ held by $J_A$.
- $J_C$ inherits $J_B$'s (boosted) priority, which itself inherited $J_A$'s priority.

The system maintains a **priority inheritance chain** (or "wait-for graph") to track this.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete, exam-ready formula reference** for PIP and related blocking analysis in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2.

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\hat{\pi}_i \geq \pi_i$ | Effective priority is **at least** the assigned priority (boosted) | $\hat{\pi}_i, \pi_i$: priority indices (unitless) |
| 2 | $\hat{\pi}_i = \max(\pi_i, \max_{J_k \in W_i} \pi_k)$ | Effective priority = max of own and all blocked-on-me tasks | $W_i$: set of tasks blocked by $J_i$ |
| 3 | $B_i^{PIP} = \sum_{k=1}^{n_i} \text{usage}_k(i) \cdot C_{k, \max}^{crit}$ | Blocking time = sum of max critical section durations for accessed resources | $n_i$: number of resources accessed; $C_{k,\max}^{crit}$: seconds |
| 4 | $C_{k,\max}^{crit} = \max_{J_j : \pi_j < \pi_i, R_k \in \mathcal{R}_j} Z_j$ | Maximum critical section length on $R_k$ by any lower-priority task | $Z_j$: critical section execution time (seconds) |
| 5 | $R_i^{PIP}(k) = \max_{j : \pi_j \leq \pi_i, j \neq i} \left( C_j - 1 \right)$ | **Response time recursion** under PIP | $C_j$: worst-case execution time (seconds) |
| 6 | $\text{PIP is deadlock-prone}$ | Multiple nested resource requests can deadlock | Qualitative |
| 7 | $B_i \leq (n-1) \cdot C_{max}^{crit}$ | **Theoretical upper bound** on blocking (Liu & Layland bound) | $n$: number of tasks |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Rendering Note for Tables:** In KTU exam scripts written by hand, write the absolute value / norm as $\mid \cdot \mid$ or with explicit text "magnitude of". Never use the pipe character `|` inside KTU answer sheets — it is often misread.

## 2.5 Why PIP Matters in Real Engineering

- **Aerospace & Avionics:** The **Mars Pathfinder** spacecraft suffered system resets due to unbounded priority inversion (1997). Engineers at JPL implemented PIP via a software patch uploaded remotely — a textbook real-world PIP case study.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR / OSEK):** Modern automotive RTOSes (e.g., VxWorks, QNX, AUTOSAR OS) implement **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** — a direct evolution of PIP — to guarantee bounded blocking.
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemakers and infusion pumps use PIP-based scheduling to ensure life-critical tasks never experience unbounded delay.
- **Industrial Control (IEC 61131-3, PLCs):** Hard real-time PLC runtimes use resource ceiling emulation rooted in PIP principles.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Blocking Time Computation (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:**

Three real-time tasks $J_1, J_2, J_3$ run on a uniprocessor under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**. They access a shared resource $R_1$ protected by mutex (using PIP).

| Task | Priority $\pi_i$ | Period $T_i$ | WCET $C_i$ | Critical Section on $R_1$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $J_1$ | HIGH (3) | 10 ms | 4 ms | $Z_1 = 1$ ms |
| $J_2$ | MEDIUM (2) | 20 ms | 5 ms | $Z_2 = 2$ ms |
| $J_3$ | LOW (1) | 40 ms | 8 ms | $Z_3 = 3$ ms |

**Question:** Compute the **maximum blocking time** $B_1$ for $J_1$ under PIP, and determine if the task set is **schedulable** using the **Priority Ceiling / PIP-blocking-augmented utilization test**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify which tasks can block $J_1$.**

PIP blocking occurs only when a **lower-priority** task holds a resource that $J_1$ requests. Here, $J_1$ accesses $R_1$, so any task that also accesses $R_1$ AND has lower priority can block $J_1$.

Lower-priority tasks accessing $R_1$: $J_2$ and $J_3$ (both have priority $< \pi_1 = 3$).

**Step 2 — For each blocking candidate, take the critical section length.**

$$
Z_2 = 2 \text{ ms}, \quad Z_3 = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the PIP blocking formula.**

$$
B_1 = \max_{J_k : \pi_k < \pi_1, R_1 \in \mathcal{R}_k} (Z_k)
$$

$$
B_1 = \max(Z_2, Z_3) = \max(2, 3) = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why maximum, not sum?** Under PIP, $J_1$ is blocked by *only one* lower-priority task at a time on a given resource (the one currently holding the lock). The blocking time is bounded by the **longest single critical section**, not the sum.

**Step 4 — Apply the schedulability test.**

The PIP-augmented utilization bound (Liu & Layland with blocking) is:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq U_{RMS}(n) - \frac{B_i}{T_i}
$$

Compute the utilization:

$$
U = \frac{4}{10} + \frac{5}{20} + \frac{8}{40}
$$

$$
U = 0.4 + 0.25 + 0.2 = 0.85
$$

For $n = 3$ tasks under RMS, the Liu & Layland bound is:

$$
U_{RMS}(3) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) \approx 0.779
$$

However, with blocking allowance, the **sufficient condition** is:

$$
\forall i: \quad \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{B_1}{T_1} + \sum_{j=2}^{i} \frac{C_j}{T_j} \leq i \left( 2^{1/i} - 1 \right)
$$

**For $J_1$ ($i = 1$):**

$$
\frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{B_1}{T_1} = \frac{4}{10} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.7
$$

$$
0.7 \leq 1 \cdot (2^{1} - 1) = 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**For $J_2$ ($i = 2$):**

$$
\frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{B_2}{T_2} = 0.4 + 0.25 + \frac{B_2}{20}
$$

$B_2$ = max critical section of lower-priority tasks on $R_1$ = $Z_3 = 3$ ms

$$
0.4 + 0.25 + \frac{3}{20} = 0.4 + 0.25 + 0.15 = 0.8
$$

$$
0.8 \leq 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) \approx 0.828 \quad \checkmark
$$

**For $J_3$ ($i = 3$):** No lower-priority task, so $B_3 = 0$:

$$
0.4 + 0.25 + 0.2 = 0.85 \leq 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.779 \quad \times \text{FAILS}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> $J_3$ fails the sufficient test. However, the **sufficient test is conservative** — we must run the **exact response-time analysis** to confirm.

**Step 5 — Exact Response-Time Analysis (Iteration).**

The response time of $J_i$ under PIP is:

$$
R_i^{k+1} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j : \pi_j > \pi_i} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^k}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is the ceiling function.

**For $J_1$:** $B_1 = 3$ ms. Higher-priority tasks: none. So:

$$
R_1 = C_1 + B_1 = 4 + 3 = 7 \text{ ms} \leq T_1 = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**For $J_2$:** $B_2 = 3$ ms. Higher-priority task: $J_1$. So:

$$
R_2^0 = C_2 + B_2 = 5 + 3 = 8
$$

$$
R_2^1 = 5 + 3 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 8 + 4 = 12
$$

$$
R_2^2 = 5 + 3 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 8 + 8 = 16
$$

$$
R_2^3 = 5 + 3 + \left\lceil \frac{16}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 8 + 8 = 16 \quad \text{(converged)}
$$

$$
R_2 = 16 \leq T_2 = 20 \quad \checkmark
$$

**For $J_3$:** $B_3 = 0$ ms. Higher-priority tasks: $J_1, J_2$. So:

$$
R_3^0 = C_3 = 8
$$

$$
R_3^1 = 8 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 8 + 4 + 5 = 17
$$

$$
R_3^2 = 8 + \left\lceil \frac{17}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{17}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 8 + 8 + 5 = 21
$$

$$
R_3^3 = 8 + \left\lceil \frac{21}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{21}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 8 + 12 + 5 = 25
$$

$$
R_3^4 = 8 + \left\lceil \frac{25}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{25}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5 = 8 + 12 + 5 = 25 \quad \text{(converged)}
$$

$$
R_3 = 25 \leq T_3 = 40 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{All three tasks are schedulable under PIP}, \quad B_1 = 3 \text{ ms}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — PIP Behavior with Transitive Inheritance

**Problem Statement:**

Three tasks $J_H, J_M, J_L$ with priorities $\pi_H > \pi_M > \pi_L$ access **two** shared resources $R_1$ and $R_2$ in the following order:

- $J_H$: locks $R_2$, then $R_1$ (nested).
- $J_M$: locks $R_1$.
- $J_L$: locks $R_1$, then $R_2$ (nested).

**Demonstrate the transitive priority inheritance chain.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Initial state.**

All tasks released simultaneously. By RMS, $J_H$ runs first.

**Step 2 — $J_H$ acquires $R_2$, then attempts to lock $R_1$.**

$J_H$ is **blocked by $J_L$** (who holds $R_1$). Under PIP Rule 2:

$$
\hat{\pi}_L := \pi_H \quad \text{(inheritance from $J_H$)}
$$

**Step 3 — $J_M$ attempts to lock $R_1$.**

$J_M$ is **also blocked by $J_L$**. Now $J_L$ has *two* higher-priority tasks waiting on its resources. PIP inherits the **maximum**:

$$
\hat{\pi}_L := \max(\pi_H, \pi_M) = \pi_H
$$

**Step 4 — $J_L$ continues at boosted priority.**

Since $\hat{\pi}_L = \pi_H > \pi_M$, $J_L$ is **not preempted by $J_M$**. This is the crucial bounded-inversion property.

**Step 5 — $J_L$ releases $R_1$ (outer-most lock? — actually inner-most first).**

Assuming $J_L$'s critical section order is: lock $R_1$, use, lock $R_2$, use, unlock $R_2$, unlock $R_1$:

- $J_L$ finishes $R_1$ work, then locks $R_2$. But $R_2$ is held by $J_H$ — wait, $J_H$ is blocked *waiting* for $R_1$, so $J_H$ hasn't actually acquired $R_2$ yet. The protocol: a task locks $R_2$ *first*, then attempts $R_1$. If $J_H$ is blocked on $R_1$ before locking $R_2$, $R_2$ is free. Let's re-order:

**Revised order:**
- $J_H$ locks $R_2$ first, then attempts $R_1$ (blocked).
- $J_L$ locks $R_1$ first, uses it, releases $R_1$, then locks $R_2$ (will block if $J_H$ still holds it — but $J_H$ never acquired $R_2$... contradiction in narrative).

**Cleaner Scenario for Transitive Inheritance:**

Let $J_L$ hold $R_1$ and $R_2$ (acquired in that order). $J_H$ requests $R_2$ first, gets it, then requests $R_1$ — blocked by $J_L$. Chain forms:

$$
J_H \xrightarrow{\text{blocked on } R_1} J_L
$$

If $J_M$ now requests $R_1$ (also held by $J_L$), then $J_M$ is *also* blocked by $J_L$. The inheritance rule aggregates:

$$
\hat{\pi}_L = \max(\pi_H, \pi_M) = \pi_H
$$

**Transitive Property:** Had there been a fourth task $J_X$ blocked by $J_M$ (not directly by $J_L$), $J_M$ would inherit $\pi_H$ from $J_L$'s context, and $J_X$ would transitively see this. The effective priority propagates **up** the wait-for chain.

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Properties Matrix

| Property | PIP | PCP (for reference) |
|---|---|---|
| Bounded Priority Inversion | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes |
| Prevents Deadlock | ✗ No (nested locks) | ✓ Yes (single, immediate ceiling) |
| Run-time Overhead | Low (O(1) per lock) | Low-Medium |
| Transitive Inheritance | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes (via ceiling) |
| Requires Priority Comparison | Yes (dynamic) | Yes (static ceiling per resource) |
| Implementation Complexity | Simple | Moderate |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **High** | **High** |

## 4.1 PIP Execution Timeline (Mermaid Gantt Chart)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Priority Inheritance Protocol Execution Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section High Priority Task JH
    Running with R1 acquired       :active, jh1, 0, 2
    Blocked on R1 (held by JL)     :crit, jh2, 2, 5
    Resumes after JL releases      :active, jh3, 5, 7
    section Medium Priority Task JM
    Arrives but cannot preempt     :crit, jm1, 3, 5
    Runs after JH completes        :active, jm2, 7, 9
    section Low Priority Task JL
    Running with R1 acquired       :active, jl1, 0, 2
    Inherits JHs priority          :crit, jl2, 2, 4
    Finishes critical section      :active, jl3, 4, 5
    Resumes at original priority   :active, jl4, 9, 10
```

## 4.2 PIP State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Running : task scheduled
    Running --> CriticalSection : lock resource Rk
    CriticalSection --> Running : short non-blocking work
    Running --> Blocked : requests locked Rk
    Blocked --> InheritingPriority : lower-priority holder is promoted
    InheritingPriority --> Running : resource released by holder
    CriticalSection --> Releasing : unlock Rk
    Releasing --> Running : priority restored to original
    Running --> Preempted : higher-priority task ready
    Preempted --> Running : scheduler resumes task
    Running --> [*] : task completes
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of PIP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Dispatcher] --> B{Priority Comparator}
    B --> C[Ready Queue]
    C --> D[Scheduler Core]
    D --> E{Task Requests Resource Rk}
    E -->|Rk free| F[Grant Lock to Task]
    E -->|Rk locked| G[Blocking Handler]
    G --> H{Priority Inheritance Engine}
    H --> I[Boost Holder Priority to piH]
    I --> J[Update Wait-For Graph]
    J --> K[Resume Holder at Boosted Priority]
    K --> L[Holder Executes Critical Section]
    L --> M[Resource Unlock Event]
    M --> N{Priority Restoration Module}
    N --> O[Demote Holder to Original piL]
    O --> P[Wake Up Blocked Task]
    P --> Q[Preempt Holder if Needed]
    Q --> D
```

## 4.4 Wait-For Graph (Transitive Inheritance Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    taskA["Task JH pi = HIGH"] -->|blocked on R2| taskB["Task JM pi = MEDIUM"]
    taskB -->|blocked on R1| taskC["Task JL pi = LOW"]
    taskC -.->|holds both R1 and R2| taskC
    style taskA fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style taskB fill:#ffd93d,color:#000
    style taskC fill:#6bcf7f,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Graph:** The arrow $A \rightarrow B$ means "$A$ is blocked by $B$" (i.e., $B$ holds the resource $A$ needs). The dashed self-loop on $J_L$ indicates it currently holds the resources. The effective priority of $J_L$ becomes $\pi_H$ (the highest among all waiters in its transitive chain).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP). How does it prevent unbounded priority inversion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**, proposed by Sha, Rajkumar, and Lehoczky (1990), is a synchronization protocol for real-time tasks sharing mutually exclusive resources. When a higher-priority task $J_H$ is blocked by a lower-priority task $J_L$ holding a shared resource, $J_L$ **temporarily inherits the priority of $J_H$** until the resource is released.
>
> **Mechanism to prevent unbounded inversion (1.5 marks):** In normal (unprotected) priority inversion, medium-priority tasks can preempt the resource-holding low-priority task, causing the high-priority task to wait for an unbounded duration. Under PIP, once $J_L$ inherits $J_H$'s priority $\pi_H$, **no medium-priority task can preempt $J_L$**, because $J_L$ now runs at a priority $\geq$ all medium-priority tasks. Thus, $J_L$ completes its critical section quickly, and $J_H$ is blocked for at most the duration of **one critical section** — making the inversion **bounded**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: List any three limitations of the Priority Inheritance Protocol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Deadlock vulnerability (1 mark):** PIP does **not prevent deadlocks** when tasks acquire multiple resources in nested fashion. For example, $J_1$ holds $R_A$ and waits for $R_B$ while $J_2$ holds $R_B$ and waits for $R_A$ — both inherit each other's priorities cyclically, and a deadlock occurs.
2. **Chained blocking (1 mark):** When multiple high-priority tasks are blocked on the same low-priority task, the blocking time can extend to **the sum of all their critical sections**, not just the maximum.
3. **No protection against indirect / transitive inversions across multiple resources (1 mark):** Transitive inheritance can cascade, making the effective priority analysis complex and the worst-case blocking bound harder to compute for systems with many shared resources.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse "bounded inversion" with "no inversion." PIP **does not eliminate** priority inversion — it only **bounds** it. Examiners *will* deduct marks if you claim PIP "solves" priority inversion entirely.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Priority Inheritance Protocol** with a suitable example involving three tasks $J_H$, $J_M$, $J_L$ of priorities 7, 4, and 2 respectively, all sharing a single resource $R_1$. Draw the execution timeline and show how unbounded inversion is prevented.

**(b) [7 marks]** Three tasks with the following parameters are scheduled under RMS using PIP. Compute the maximum blocking time for each task and verify schedulability using response-time analysis.

| Task | $\pi_i$ | $T_i$ (ms) | $C_i$ (ms) | $Z_i$ on $R_1$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $J_1$ | 3 | 8 | 3 | 1 |
| $J_2$ | 2 | 16 | 4 | 2 |
| $J_3$ | 1 | 32 | 6 | 2 |

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — PIP Explanation with Timeline (7 marks)

**PIP Rules (2 marks):**

1. When a task $J_i$ enters a critical section, it locks the resource at its **assigned** priority $\pi_i$.
2. If a higher-priority task $J_H$ requests the same resource and blocks, $J_i$ **inherits** $\pi_H$ as its effective priority: $\hat{\pi}_i := \pi_H$.
3. When $J_i$ releases the resource, its priority **reverts** to $\pi_i$.

**Example Timeline (3 marks):**

| Time | Event | $\hat{\pi}_H$ | $\hat{\pi}_M$ | $\hat{\pi}_L$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $J_L$ starts, locks $R_1$ | — | — | 2 |
| 1 | $J_H$ arrives, preempts $J_L$ | 7 | — | 2 (suspended) |
| 2 | $J_H$ requests $R_1$, **BLOCKED** | 7 (blocked) | — | 2 |
| 2 | $J_L$ **inherits** $\pi_H$ | 7 | — | **7** |
| 3 | $J_M$ arrives, **CANNOT preempt** $J_L$ | 7 (blocked) | 4 | **7** |
| 5 | $J_L$ exits critical section, releases $R_1$ | 7 | 4 | 2 |
| 5 | $J_H$ acquires $R_1$, preempts | 7 | 4 (suspended) | 2 (suspended) |
| 6 | $J_H$ finishes, $J_M$ runs | — | 4 | 2 |

**Bounding Argument (2 marks):** Without PIP, $J_M$ would preempt $J_L$ at $t=3$, indefinitely delaying $J_H$. With PIP, $J_L$ runs at priority 7 (inherited), immune to $J_M$'s preemption. Thus, $J_H$ is blocked for at most **$J_L$'s remaining critical section time** = 3 time units (bounded).

#### Part (b) — Blocking Time and Schedulability (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Blocking time computation (2 marks):**

- $B_1$ = max critical section of lower-priority tasks on $R_1$ = $\max(Z_2, Z_3) = \max(2, 2) = 2$ ms.
- $B_2$ = max critical section of lower-priority tasks on $R_1$ = $Z_3 = 2$ ms.
- $B_3$ = 0 ms (no lower-priority tasks).

**Step 2 — Response-time iteration (4 marks):**

**For $J_1$:** No higher-priority tasks, so $R_1 = C_1 + B_1 = 3 + 2 = 5$ ms $\leq T_1 = 8$ ms ✓

**For $J_2$:** Higher-priority: $J_1$.

$$
R_2^0 = C_2 + B_2 = 4 + 2 = 6
$$

$$
R_2^1 = 4 + 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 6 + 3 = 9
$$

$$
R_2^2 = 4 + 2 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 6 + 3 = 9 \quad \text{(converged)}
$$

$R_2 = 9 \leq T_2 = 16$ ✓

**For $J_3$:** Higher-priority: $J_1, J_2$.

$$
R_3^0 = 6
$$

$$
R_3^1 = 6 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{16} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 3 + 4 = 13
$$

$$
R_3^2 = 6 + \left\lceil \frac{13}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{13}{16} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 6 + 4 = 16
$$

$$
R_3^3 = 6 + \left\lceil \frac{16}{8} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{16}{16} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 6 + 4 = 16 \quad \text{(converged)}
$$

$R_3 = 16 \leq T_3 = 32$ ✓

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):** All three tasks are **schedulable** under PIP. The task set is feasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write $B_1 = Z_2 + Z_3$ (sum). This is **incorrect** for a single shared resource. Under PIP, blocking on a single resource is bounded by the **maximum** of the contenders' critical sections, not the sum. Sum applies only to **chained blocking across multiple resources**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **bounded and unbounded priority inversion** with diagrams. Explain how PIP transforms an unbounded inversion scenario into a bounded one.

**(b) [7 marks]** A real-time system has **three tasks** $J_1, J_2, J_3$ with priorities $\pi_1 > \pi_2 > \pi_3$. The system uses PIP for shared resource $R_1$.

- $J_1$ requires $R_1$ for **2 ms**.
- $J_2$ requires $R_1$ for **3 ms**.
- $J_3$ requires $R_1$ for **5 ms** (and is currently running).

$J_1$ arrives at $t = 0$, $J_2$ at $t = 2$, and $J_1$'s deadline is at $t = 8$ ms.

**(i)** Construct the execution timeline under **PIP** and find the **response time of $J_1$**.
**(ii)** Construct the execution timeline **without PIP** (plain priority) and compute $J_1$'s response time.
**(iii)** Compare the two scenarios.

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Bounded vs. Unbounded Inversion (7 marks)

**Definitions (3 marks):**

- **Bounded Priority Inversion:** A higher-priority task $J_H$ is blocked only for the duration of the critical section of the lower-priority task $J_L$ currently holding the resource. The blocking time is **deterministic and bounded**.

$$
B_{bounded} = \max_{J_k : \pi_k < \pi_H} Z_k
$$

- **Unbounded Priority Inversion:** The blocking time depends on the **execution of unrelated medium-priority tasks** that preempt $J_L$. Since medium-priority tasks can arrive arbitrarily, the blocking time is **unbounded** in the worst case (depends on the schedule).

**Diagrammatic comparison (2 marks):**

In the **bounded** case, the wait time is a fixed, predictable box on the timeline. In the **unbounded** case, an arbitrary number of medium-priority "interlopers" can extend the wait time arbitrarily.

**PIP's role (2 marks):** PIP elevates $J_L$'s priority to $\pi_H$ during the critical section. This makes $J_L$ **immune to preemption** by any task with priority $< \pi_H$ (including all medium-priority tasks). Thus, the medium-priority tasks **cannot** extend the wait, and the inversion is reduced to a **single critical section** duration — bounded.

#### Part (b) — Timeline Analysis (7 marks)

**Scenario under PIP (3 marks):**

| Time | Event | $\hat{\pi}_1$ | $\hat{\pi}_2$ | $\hat{\pi}_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $J_3$ running, holds $R_1$ | — | — | LOW |
| 0 | $J_1$ arrives, requests $R_1$, **blocked** | HIGH (blocked) | — | LOW |
| 0 | $J_3$ **inherits** $\pi_1$ (HIGH) | — | — | **HIGH** |
| 2 | $J_2$ arrives, **cannot preempt** $J_3$ (both at HIGH vs MED) | blocked | MED | **HIGH** |
| 5 | $J_3$ finishes critical section (used 5 ms), releases $R_1$ | — | MED | LOW |
| 5 | $J_1$ acquires $R_1$, runs for 2 ms | HIGH | MED | LOW |
| 7 | $J_1$ completes | — | — | — |

$$
R_1^{PIP} = 7 \text{ ms} \leq 8 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark \quad \text{deadline met}
$$

**Scenario without PIP (2 marks):**

| Time | Event | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $J_3$ running with $R_1$ | LOW priority |
| 0 | $J_1$ arrives, requests $R_1$, **blocked** | HIGH priority |
| 2 | $J_2$ arrives, **preempts** $J_3$ | MED priority |
| 5 | $J_2$ runs for 3 ms, completes at $t = 5$ | — |
| 5 | $J_3$ resumes with $R_1$ | — |
| 8 | $J_3$ finishes critical section, $J_1$ acquires $R_1$ | — |
| 10 | $J_1$ completes | — |

$$
R_1^{no\_PIP} = 10 \text{ ms} > 8 \text{ ms} \quad \times \quad \text{deadline MISSED}
$$

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | With PIP | Without PIP |
|---|---|---|
| $J_1$ Response Time | 7 ms | 10 ms |
| $J_1$ Deadline (8 ms) | **Met** ✓ | **Missed** ✗ |
| $J_1$ Blocking Duration | 5 ms (one critical section) | 8 ms (CS + $J_2$ preemption) |
| Inversion Type | **Bounded** | **Unbounded** |

**Conclusion:** PIP saved the system from a deadline miss in this scenario, demonstrating its real-world value in real-time scheduling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In handwritten exam scripts, students often forget to **explicitly state the priority of the task holding the resource *after* inheritance**. Examiners expect a clear annotation: "$\hat{\pi}_L := \pi_H$" or "$J_L$ now runs at priority 7 (inherited)." Skipping this annotation typically costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Priority Inheritance Protocol**

- **Inventors:** Sha, Rajkumar, and Lehoczky (1990).
- **Purpose:** Eliminate **unbounded** priority inversion (not all inversion).
- **Three Rules:** Acquire at assigned priority → Inherit on block → Restore on release.
- **Transitive Property:** Inheritance cascades through wait-for chains.
- **Blocking Time Bound:** $B_i = \max$ (critical sections of lower-priority contenders on each resource $J_i$ accesses).
- **Deadlock:** PIP **does NOT** prevent deadlock for nested multi-resource acquisitions. Use **PCP** instead.
- **Chained Blocking:** Sum of max critical sections across **different** resources (one per resource, summed if a task accesses multiple).
- **Response-Time Recursion:** $R_i^{k+1} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j : \pi_j > \pi_i} \left\lceil R_i^k / T_j \right\rceil C_j$.
- **Iterate until convergence:** $R_i^{k+1} = R_i^k$.
- **Schedulability:** $R_i \leq T_i$ (or $\leq D_i$ if deadline $\neq$ period).
- **Real-World Case Study:** **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** — system resets due to unbounded inversion; fixed via PIP patch.
- **Implementation Cost:** O(1) per lock/unlock operation; minimal memory overhead (one wait-for-graph edge per blocking).
- **Comparison Pointers (for exam essays):** PIP vs. **ICPP** (Immediate Ceiling) vs. **PCP** (Priority Ceiling Protocol) — focus on *bounded inversion*, *deadlock prevention*, and *implementation complexity*.
- **Common Confusions to Avoid:**
  - PIP **bounds** inversion; it does **not** eliminate it.
  - Blocking time = **max**, not **sum**, on a single resource.
  - PIP allows **transitive** inheritance (cascading).
  - PIP can **deadlock** on nested multi-resource requests.

> **End of Module 2 — Priority Inheritance Protocol Notes**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Highest Locker Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Highest Locker Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Highest Locker Protocol (HLP)** is a deterministic resource access control protocol used in fixed-priority preemptive real-time systems to manage mutual exclusion over shared resources while preventing unbounded priority inversion and deadlocks. Under HLP, every shared resource $R$ is statically assigned a *ceiling priority* equal to the **highest priority of any task in the system that may lock** that resource. When a task $T_i$ acquires a resource $R$, its effective dynamic priority is **immediately raised** to the ceiling priority of $R$ (the *highest locker* of $R$). The task continues to execute at this elevated priority until it releases the resource, at which point its priority is restored to its nominal value.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST748 Module 2):** *The Highest Locker Protocol is a stack-sharing/resource-ceiling based concurrency control mechanism that ensures a task executing inside a critical section inherits the priority of the highest priority task that may contend for the same resource, thereby bounding blocking time and eliminating deadlocks.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "VIP Lounge Pass" Model:**
> Imagine an airport terminal with three classes of travellers — **First Class (P=High)**, **Business Class (P=Medium)**, and **Economy (P=Low)** — all waiting at a single security checkpoint (the *shared resource*).
>
> - Under **no protocol**, an Economy passenger could keep a First Class passenger waiting for hours (unbounded priority inversion).
> - Under **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**, the Economy passenger temporarily becomes "First Class" only when a First Class passenger is *actually standing in the queue*.
> - Under the **Highest Locker Protocol**, the moment an Economy passenger enters the security area, the system **preemptively scans the manifest** and grants them a *VIP pass* because the *highest* possible locker of the resource is First Class. They are processed instantly and exit, never holding up premium travellers.
>
> **The key insight:** HLP doesn't *wait* for a high-priority task to arrive; it **anticipates** the worst case by raising priority proactively based on the *highest locker* of the resource.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bolded for Recall)

- **Resource Ceiling ($\text{ceil}(R)$):** Static maximum priority among all tasks that may lock $R$.
- **Highest Locker:** The task with the highest priority that may lock a given resource.
- **Dynamic Priority Boost:** Temporary elevation of a task's execution priority while it holds a resource.
- **Bounded Blocking:** A guarantee that no task waits indefinitely for a lower-priority task.
- **Chained Blocking:** A pathological scenario where a task is blocked by multiple nested critical sections — *eliminated* by HLP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** HLP guarantees **at most one blocking episode** per task per release, and the maximum blocking duration equals the length of **a single critical section** of the highest-priority lower-priority task that may lock a conflicting resource.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps of the Highest Locker Protocol

The protocol operates under a set of well-defined rules executed at runtime by the scheduler and resource manager:

1. **Static Pre-computation (Off-line Phase):**
   - For every shared resource $R_k$ in the system, compute the *ceiling priority*:
   
     $$\text{ceil}(R_k) = \max\{P_i \mid T_i \text{ may lock } R_k\}$$
   - Store this in the Resource Control Block (RCB).

2. **Task Release Phase:**
   - Task $T_i$ is admitted with its nominal (static) priority $P_i$.

3. **Resource Lock Operation (`lock(R_k)`):**
   - If $R_k$ is free, $T_i$ acquires it.
   - The scheduler immediately raises $T_i$'s effective priority to $\text{ceil}(R_k)$.
   - This boost is in effect *only while* $R_k$ is held.

4. **Critical Section Execution:**
   - $T_i$ executes the critical section at the elevated priority.
   - Preemption is governed by this boosted priority, not the nominal $P_i$.

5. **Resource Unlock Operation (`unlock(R_k)`):**
   - $T_i$ releases $R_k$.
   - If $T_i$ holds other resources, its priority is recomputed as the maximum ceiling among remaining locked resources.
   - If no resources are held, priority reverts to $P_i$.

6. **Discipline Guarantee:** A task can lock a new resource only if it is the **highest-priority task currently executing** — this single rule prevents circular wait conditions and deadlocks.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Conceptual Justification

- **Why priority elevation?** A high-priority task $T_h$ that needs $R_k$ must not be delayed by a low-priority task $T_l$ that already holds $R_k$ and is preempted by medium-priority tasks (the classic *priority inversion* problem).
- **How does HLP fix it?** By giving $T_l$ the *ceiling* priority the moment it locks $R_k$, medium-priority tasks cannot preempt $T_l$. $T_l$ runs to completion of its critical section at top speed, then releases $R_k$ quickly.
- **Why "highest locker"?** Because the protocol computes the ceiling based on the **maximum possible contender**, the boost is conservative and safe — over-elevating is always preferable to under-elevating in real-time systems.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table contains every formula you must memorize for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE/ISE questions on HLP.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Variables | Units / Domain |
|---|----------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{ceil}(R_k) = \max\{P_i \mid T_i \text{ may lock } R_k\}$ | Resource ceiling priority | Integer priority |
| 2 | $P_{\text{eff}}(T_i) = \max\!\left(P_i, \max_{R \in \text{Locked}(T_i)}\text{ceil}(R)\right)$ | Effective dynamic priority of $T_i$ | Integer priority |
| 3 | $B_k = \max\bigl\{C_{j,s} \mid P_j < P_k \text{ and } T_j \text{ shares a resource with } T_k\bigr\}$ | Worst-case blocking time of $T_k$ | Time units (e.g., ms) |
| 4 | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{C_i}{T_i} + \frac{B_k}{T_k} \leq k\!\left(2^{1/k}-1\right)$ | RM schedulability test with blocking (per task $T_k$) | Dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | $R_i = \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Utilization of task $T_i$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $U_{\text{RM}}(n) = n\left(2^{1/n}-1\right)$ | Liu & Layland RM utilization bound for $n$ tasks | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $U_{\text{bound}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{C_i}{T_i} + \max_{i}\frac{B_i}{T_i}$ | Sufficient schedulability test for the whole task set | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** In all formulas inside markdown tables, the absolute-value / vertical bar symbol is written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\max\{P_i \mid \ldots\}$) to prevent breaking the table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics Software (DO-178C):** HLP-derived techniques are used in ARINC 653 partitioned systems for IMA (Integrated Modular Avionics) platforms.
