- **Automotive Real-Time (AUTOSAR / OSEK):** Resource management in ECUs uses PCP/HLP concepts for OSEK OS conformance.
- **Industrial Control (IEC 61131-3):** PLC runtimes use ceiling-priority concepts for deterministic task synchronization.
- **Medical Devices (FDA 510(k)):** Infusion pumps and pacemakers rely on bounded-blocking protocols derived from HLP.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Resource Ceiling Computation

**Given:** A real-time system with $n$ periodic tasks $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ with static priorities $P_1 > P_2 > \ldots > P_n$. Each task $T_i$ accesses a (possibly empty) set of resources $\mathcal{R}_i$.

**Step 1 — Define the set of candidate lockers for resource $R$:**
For any resource $R \in \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{R}_i$, the *candidate locker set* is:
$$\mathcal{L}(R) = \{T_i \mid R \in \mathcal{R}_i\}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ceiling priority:**
Among the candidates, the highest priority task becomes the *highest locker*:
$$\text{ceil}(R) = \max_{T_i \in \mathcal{L}(R)} P_i$$

**Step 3 — Effective priority during execution:**
When task $T_j$ holds a non-empty set of resources $\mathcal{H}_j \subseteq \mathcal{R}_j$, its effective priority becomes:
$$P_{\text{eff}}(T_j) = \max\!\left(P_j, \max_{R \in \mathcal{H}_j} \text{ceil}(R)\right)$$

**Reasoning (textual logic):** The maximum operator enforces a conservative boost — even if a task has multiple locked resources, only the *highest* ceiling dictates the priority, ensuring the system remains predictable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Worst-Case Blocking Time

**Step 1 — Identify conflicting tasks:**
Two tasks *conflict* if they share at least one resource:
$$\text{Conflict}(T_i, T_j) \iff \mathcal{R}_i \cap \mathcal{R}_j \neq \emptyset$$

**Step 2 — For each task $T_k$, enumerate lower-priority tasks that conflict with it:**
$$\mathcal{B}(T_k) = \{T_j \mid P_j < P_k \text{ and } \text{Conflict}(T_k, T_j)\}$$

**Step 3 — Take the maximum critical-section length among blockers:**
For each conflicting task $T_j$, let $C_{j,s}$ denote the execution time of $T_j$'s longest critical section that accesses a resource in $\mathcal{R}_j \cap \mathcal{R}_k$. The worst-case blocking time is:
$$B_k = \max_{T_j \in \mathcal{B}(T_k)} C_{j,s}$$

**Step 4 — Bound the result:**
Since HLP prevents chained blocking, a task can be blocked **at most once** per release, so $B_k$ never accumulates over multiple lower-priority tasks. The bound is therefore *tight*:
$$B_k \leq \max_{T_j \in \mathcal{B}(T_k)} C_{j,s}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem Setup:**
> Consider a real-time system with three tasks accessing shared resources. Determine the resource ceilings, the worst-case blocking times, and check schedulability under Rate-Monotonic scheduling with HLP.
>
> | Task | Priority $P_i$ | Period $T_i$ | WCET $C_i$ | Resources Used | Critical-Section Lengths |
> |------|----------------|--------------|------------|----------------|--------------------------|
> | $T_1$ | 10 (highest) | 20 | 4 | $R_A$ | 2 ms on $R_A$ |
> | $T_2$ | 5  | 50 | 8 | $R_B$ | 3 ms on $R_B$ |
> | $T_3$ | 1 (lowest) | 100 | 10 | $R_A, R_B$ | 2 ms on $R_A$, 3 ms on $R_B$ |

**Step A — Compute Resource Ceilings:**

$$\text{ceil}(R_A) = \max\{P_1, P_3\} = \max\{10, 1\} = 10$$

$$\text{ceil}(R_B) = \max\{P_2, P_3\} = \max\{5, 1\} = 5$$

**Step B — Identify Blockers and Compute $B_k$:**

For $T_1$ (priority 10): conflicts with $T_3$ on $R_A$.
$$B_1 = C_{3,s}^{R_A} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

For $T_2$ (priority 5): conflicts with $T_3$ on $R_B$.
$$B_2 = C_{3,s}^{R_B} = 3 \text{ ms}$$

For $T_3$ (priority 1): no lower-priority task exists, so:
$$B_3 = 0$$

**Step C — Apply RM Schedulability Test (per task):**

For $T_1$ ($k=1$):
$$\frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{B_1}{T_1} = \frac{4}{20} + \frac{2}{20} = 0.20 + 0.10 = 0.30$$
$$1 \cdot (2^{1/1} - 1) = 1.0 \quad \Longrightarrow 0.30 \leq 1.0 \;\; \checkmark$$

For $T_2$ ($k=2$):
$$\frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{B_2}{T_2} = \frac{4}{20} + \frac{8}{50} + \frac{3}{50}$$
$$= 0.200 + 0.160 + 0.060 = 0.420$$
$$2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) = 2 \cdot 0.4142 = 0.8284 \quad \Longrightarrow 0.420 \leq 0.8284 \;\; \checkmark$$

For $T_3$ ($k=3$):
$$\frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} + \frac{B_3}{T_3}$$
$$= 0.200 + 0.160 + 0.100 + 0.000 = 0.460$$
$$3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7797 \quad \Longrightarrow 0.460 \leq 0.7797 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** All tasks are schedulable under RM + HLP.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — HLP Property Verification

| Property | Mathematical Condition | HLP Status |
|----------|------------------------|------------|
| Deadlock-free | No circular wait possible | **Guaranteed** |
| Chained blocking absent | $B_k = C_{j,s}$ for only one $T_j$ | **Guaranteed** |
| Unbounded priority inversion | $\exists t : \text{wait}(T_h) \to \infty$ | **Eliminated** |
| Maximum blocking per task | $B_k \leq \max C_{j,s}$ | **Bounded** |
| Concurrency | Parallel critical sections on different resources | **Allowed** |
| Stack sharing | Tasks can share a single run-time stack | **Supported** |
| Preemption | Task priority dynamically changes | **Required** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HLP Runtime Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task T_i Released with Priority P_i] --> B{T_i Requests Resource R_k?}
    B -- No --> C[Execute at Nominal Priority P_i]
    C --> Z[Continue Normal Execution]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is R_k Free?}
    D -- No --> E[T_i Enters Blocking Queue]
    E --> F[Wait for R_k to be Released]
    F --> D
    D -- Yes --> G[Lock R_k - T_i Acquires Resource]
    G --> H[Compute Ceiling ceil of R_k = Max P_j for T_j using R_k]
    H --> I[Raise T_i Effective Priority to ceil of R_k]
    I --> J[Execute Critical Section at Elevated Priority]
    J --> K{T_i Needs More Resources?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Update Effective Priority to Max Ceiling of All Locked Resources]
    L --> J
    K -- No --> M[Release R_k via Unlock Operation]
    M --> N{Any Resources Still Locked?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Recompute Effective Priority from Remaining Ceilings]
    O --> P[Continue Execution]
    N -- No --> Q[Restore T_i to Nominal Priority P_i]
    Q --> P
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Priority Boosting During Critical Section

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sched as Scheduler
    participant T1 as Task T1 High Priority
    participant T2 as Task T2 Medium Priority
    participant T3 as Task T3 Low Priority
    participant R1 as Resource R1

    Sched->>T1: Release T1 with Priority 10
    T1->>Sched: Request R1
    Sched->>T1: Grant R1 - Boost T1 to ceil of R1 = 10
    Note over T1: T1 executes at priority 10
    T3->>Sched: Request R1
    Sched->>T3: BLOCKED - R1 held by T1
    T2->>Sched: Request R1
    Sched->>T2: BLOCKED - R1 held by T1
    T1->>Sched: Release R1 - Restore to priority 10
    Note over Sched: T1 was already at 10 so no change
    Sched->>T3: Wake T3 - Boost T3 to ceil of R1 = 10
    Note over T3: T3 inherits highest locker priority
    T3->>Sched: Release R1 - Restore to priority 1
    Sched->>T2: Wake T2 - Grant R1 if needed
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of HLP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StaticConfig [Offline Configuration Phase]
        A1[Task Table with Priorities] --> B1[Resource Access Matrix]
        B1 --> C1[Compute Ceilings for Each Resource]
        C1 --> D1[Store in Resource Control Block]
    end

    subgraph RuntimeEngine [Online Runtime Engine]
        E1[Task Arrival Event] --> F1[Scheduler Dispatcher]
        F1 --> G1{Resource Requested?}
        G1 -- No --> H1[Execute at Nominal Priority]
        G1 -- Yes --> I1[Resource Manager - Lock R]
        I1 --> J1[Priority Booster - Apply Ceil R]
        J1 --> K1[Critical Section Executor]
        K1 --> L1{Unlock Requested?}
        L1 -- No --> K1
        L1 -- Yes --> M1[Priority Restorer - Recompute P_eff]
        M1 --> N1[Continue or Complete]
    end

    D1 --> I1
    D1 --> J1
    D1 --> M1
```

## 4.4 HLP vs PCP vs PIP — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resource Access Protocols] --> B[Priority Inheritance Protocol PIP]
    A --> C[Priority Ceiling Protocol PCP]
    A --> D[Highest Locker Protocol HLP]

    B --> B1[Inherits only on actual conflict]
    B --> B2[Allows chained blocking]
    B --> B3[Allows deadlocks]
    B --> B4[Simple implementation]

    C --> C1[Uses static resource ceilings]
    C --> C2[Prevents chained blocking]
    C --> C3[Prevents deadlocks]
    C --> C4[Moderate complexity]

    D --> D1[Highest possible locker priority]
    D --> D2[Single critical section blocking bound]
    D --> D3[Guaranteed deadlock free]
    D --> D4[Enables stack sharing]
    D --> D5[Most conservative priority boost]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define the Highest Locker Protocol. How does it differ from basic priority inheritance in handling resource conflicts?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Highest Locker Protocol (HLP)** is a resource access protocol for real-time systems in which every shared resource $R$ is assigned a *ceiling priority* equal to the highest priority of any task that may lock it. When a task acquires $R$, its effective priority is immediately raised to this ceiling priority, ensuring the task executes its critical section at the *highest possible contention priority*.

**Difference from Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**
- In **PIP**, priority inheritance occurs *reactively* — only when a higher-priority task is actively waiting for the resource.
- In **HLP**, the priority boost is *proactive* — it happens the moment the resource is locked, regardless of whether a higher-priority task is currently waiting.
- Consequently, HLP prevents deadlocks and chained blocking, whereas PIP allows both.

*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark contrast.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List any three key properties guaranteed by the Highest Locker Protocol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Deadlock Freedom:** No circular-wait condition can develop because a task can only lock a resource if it has the highest effective priority among executing tasks. *(1 mark)*
2. **Bounded Blocking:** Each task can be blocked at most once per release, with blocking time bounded by the length of a single critical section of a lower-priority task. *(1 mark)*
3. **Prevention of Chained Blocking:** A task cannot be transitively blocked through a chain of multiple lower-priority tasks, ensuring predictable worst-case latency. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Module 2 questions are typically framed with two 7-mark sub-parts. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as full internal-choice alternatives. Answer either **A(a) + A(b)** OR **B(a) + B(b)**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **A(a) [7 Marks]** — *Explain the operational mechanism of the Highest Locker Protocol with a suitable example. Describe the rules for lock, unlock, and priority inheritance.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definitions and Setup (2 marks):**
- Let $\mathcal{T} = \{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$ be a task set with priorities $P_1 = 7, P_2 = 4, P_3 = 1$.
- Shared resources: $R_X$ accessed by $T_1, T_3$; $R_Y$ accessed by $T_2, T_3$.

**2. Compute Resource Ceilings (2 marks):**
$$\text{ceil}(R_X) = \max\{7, 1\} = 7$$
$$\text{ceil}(R_Y) = \max\{4, 1\} = 4$$

**3. HLP Rules (2 marks):**
- **Rule 1 (Lock):** When $T_i$ successfully locks $R$, its effective priority becomes $\text{ceil}(R)$.
- **Rule 2 (Unlock):** When $T_i$ releases $R$, its effective priority reverts to the maximum of its nominal priority and ceilings of any remaining locked resources.
- **Rule 3 (Admission):** A task may lock a new resource only if it is the highest-priority task currently in the system.

**4. Worked Trace (1 mark):**
- $T_3$ starts at priority 1, locks $R_X$ → boosted to priority 7.
- $T_2$ arrives (priority 4) but cannot preempt $T_3$ because $T_3$ is now at priority 7.
- $T_3$ releases $R_X$, drops back to priority 1; $T_2$ executes.
- $T_1$ arrives (priority 7), preempts $T_2$, locks $R_X$ at priority 7.
- No chained blocking; no deadlock.

---

#### **A(b) [7 Marks]** — *With a clear time-line diagram description, show how HLP prevents unbounded priority inversion. Compare its behaviour with the Priority Ceiling Protocol.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Unbounded Priority Inversion Recap (2 marks):**
Classic priority inversion occurs when a low-priority task $T_L$ holding a resource $R$ is preempted by medium-priority tasks $T_{M1}, T_{M2}, \ldots$, delaying a high-priority task $T_H$. Under FIFO resource access, this delay can grow arbitrarily.

**2. HLP's Mitigation (3 marks):**
- The moment $T_L$ locks $R$, its effective priority becomes $\text{ceil}(R) = P_H$ (the highest locker).
- $T_{M1}, T_{M2}$ **cannot preempt** $T_L$ because $T_L$ is now executing at $P_H$.
- $T_L$ completes its critical section swiftly, releases $R$, and is preempted by $T_H$.
- Net blocking time of $T_H$ = length of one critical section of $T_L$.

**3. Comparison with PCP (2 marks):**

| Aspect | PCP | HLP |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Ceiling basis | Highest priority of any task that may access $R$ | Highest priority of any task that may *lock* $R$ |
| Priority boost timing | On entering critical section | On acquiring resource lock |
| Deadlock prevention | Yes | Yes |
| Chained blocking | Prevented | Prevented |
| Conservative boost | Slightly tighter | Slightly more conservative (always uses max priority) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **B(a) [7 Marks]** — *Derive the worst-case blocking time bound for a task set scheduled under Rate Monotonic Scheduling with the Highest Locker Protocol. State the schedulability condition with proof sketch.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Worst-Case Blocking Time $B_k$ (3 marks):**

Let $T_k$ be a task with priority $P_k$. The set of potential blockers is:
$$\mathcal{B}(T_k) = \{T_j \mid P_j < P_k \text{ and } \mathcal{R}_j \cap \mathcal{R}_k \neq \emptyset\}$$

Since HLP prevents chained blocking, $T_k$ can be blocked by **at most one** task in $\mathcal{B}(T_k)$ per release. The worst-case blocking time is the longest critical section among potential blockers:
$$B_k = \max_{T_j \in \mathcal{B}(T_k)} C_{j,s}^{\max}$$

where $C_{j,s}^{\max}$ is the longest critical-section execution time of $T_j$ on a resource in $\mathcal{R}_j \cap \mathcal{R}_k$.

**2. Schedulability Condition (2 marks):**

Under RMS with blocking, task $T_k$ is schedulable iff:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{C_i}{T_i} + \frac{B_k}{T_k} \leq k \cdot \left(2^{1/k} - 1\right)$$

**3. Proof Sketch (2 marks):**
- The Liu–Layland bound $U_{\text{RM}}(k) = k(2^{1/k}-1)$ assumes no blocking.
- HLP adds an extra *interference term* $B_k/T_k$ corresponding to at most one blocking episode per period.
- The interference is conservatively added to the utilization, and the standard RMS sufficiency test is applied.

---

#### **B(b) [7 Marks]** — *Consider three tasks T1, T2, T3 with priorities 8, 5, 2, periods 25, 50, 100 ms, and WCETs 4, 6, 9 ms respectively. T1 uses resource RA, T2 uses RB, T3 uses both RA and RB. Critical section lengths: T3 on RA = 2 ms, T3 on RB = 3 ms. Using HLP, compute (i) resource ceilings, (ii) worst-case blocking times, and (iii) check schedulability under RMS.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Resource Ceilings (2 marks):**
$$\text{ceil}(R_A) = \max\{P_1, P_3\} = \max\{8, 2\} = 8$$
$$\text{ceil}(R_B) = \max\{P_2, P_3\} = \max\{5, 2\} = 5$$

**(ii) Worst-Case Blocking Times (2 marks):**
- $T_1$ conflicts with $T_3$ on $R_A$: $B_1 = 2$ ms
- $T_2$ conflicts with $T_3$ on $R_B$: $B_2 = 3$ ms
- $T_3$ has no lower-priority blocker: $B_3 = 0$ ms

**(iii) Schedulability Check (3 marks):**

For $T_1$ ($k=1$):
$$\frac{4}{25} + \frac{2}{25} = 0.16 + 0.08 = 0.24 \leq 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

For $T_2$ ($k=2$):
$$\frac{4}{25} + \frac{6}{50} + \frac{3}{50} = 0.16 + 0.12 + 0.06 = 0.34 \leq 0.8284 \quad \checkmark$$

For $T_3$ ($k=3$):
$$\frac{4}{25} + \frac{6}{50} + \frac{9}{100} + 0 = 0.16 + 0.12 + 0.09 = 0.37 \leq 0.7797 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is schedulable under RMS + HLP.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in HLP Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the ceiling with priority inheritance:** Students often write that the *waiting* task's priority is raised. **Correction:** It is the *holding* task whose priority is boosted.
> 2. **Forgetting the maximum operator:** When a task holds multiple resources, the effective priority is the **maximum** of ceilings, not the sum. Sums imply *cumulative boosting*, which is incorrect.
> 3. **Including higher-priority tasks in the blocker set:** A task $T_j$ with $P_j > P_k$ cannot block $T_k$ because $T_k$ would simply be preempted by $T_j$ anyway. Blockers are **strictly lower-priority** tasks.
> 4. **Ignoring the per-task blocking term $B_k/T_k$:** The blocking factor is task-specific, not a single global value. Each task $T_k$ has its own $B_k$ based on its own resource conflict set.
> 5. **Chained blocking assumption:** Stating that blocking time accumulates over multiple lower-priority tasks loses 2 marks instantly. HLP specifically **prevents** chained blocking.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this for last-night revision):**
>
> - **Definition:** HLP is a resource-access protocol that elevates a task's priority to the *highest locker priority* of a resource the moment it is locked.
> - **Resource Ceiling:** $\text{ceil}(R) = \max\{P_i \mid T_i \text{ may lock } R\}$ — computed statically.
> - **Effective Priority:** $P_{\text{eff}} = \max(P_{\text{nominal}}, \text{ceil of locked resources})$.
> - **Blocking Time:** $B_k = \max C_{j,s}$ over lower-priority conflicting tasks — **single** critical section.
> - **Schedulability Test (RMS):** $\sum_{i=1}^{k} C_i/T_i + B_k/T_k \leq k(2^{1/k}-1)$ for each $T_k$.
> - **Deadlock-free:** Yes — admission rule ensures no circular wait.
> - **Chained Blocking:** Prevented — a task is blocked at most once.
> - **Stack Sharing:** Enabled by HLP's predictability guarantees.
> - **Difference from PIP:** HLP is *proactive*; PIP is *reactive*.
> - **Difference from PCP:** Both prevent deadlock and chained blocking; HLP is *more conservative* (always uses max priority of lockers, not just accessors).
> - **Applications:** Avionics (ARINC 653), automotive (AUTOSAR), industrial control (IEC 61131-3), medical devices.
> - **Examiner's Trick:** Always specify the *unit* of $B_k$ (ms/μs) — losing the unit costs 0.5 marks.
> - **Mnemonic:** *"Lock → Lift → Leave"* (Lock the resource, Lift priority to ceiling, Leave the critical section at boosted priority).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Priority Ceiling Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Priority Ceiling Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

The **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** is a concurrency control and resource synchronization protocol designed for real-time and embedded systems to eliminate unbounded **priority inversion**, prevent **deadlocks**, and ensure **bounded blocking** when multiple tasks share mutually exclusive resources. The protocol was formally introduced by **Lui Sha, Ragunathan Rajkumar, and John Lehoczky** in 1990 as a deterministic solution to the classic priority inversion problem that plagued the Mars Pathfinder mission (1997).

In PCP, every shared resource is statically assigned a **priority ceiling** equal to the **highest priority of any task that may lock that resource**. A running task is allowed to lock a new resource **only if its dynamic priority is strictly higher than the priority ceilings of all resources currently locked by other tasks**. If this condition is violated, the requesting task is said to encounter a **ceiling violation**, and the lock is denied (the task is blocked). To preserve boundedness, the task holding the resource whose ceiling is equal to the new task's dynamic priority has its priority **temporarily raised** to the requesting task's priority.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST748 Module 2):**
> The two principal variants of PCP that students MUST know are:
> 1. **Original Priority Ceiling Protocol (OPCP / Basic PCP)** — defined in Sha et al. (1990).
> 2. **Immediate Priority Ceiling Protocol (IPCP)** — also known as the *Highest Locker's Priority (HLP) Protocol* or the *Ceiling Priority Protocol*.
>
> IPCP is the one most frequently examined in KTU papers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room

Imagine a small hospital with a single operating theater shared by three surgeons: **Dr. A (Chief Surgeon)**, **Dr. B (Specialist)**, and **Dr. C (Junior Resident)** — representing high, medium, and low priority tasks.

- Three tools (resources) are needed: a **microscope (R1)**, a **laser (R2)**, and a **ventilator (R3)**.
- Each tool is "labeled" with the **highest rank of any surgeon who may use it**. The microscope ceiling is "Chief Surgeon rank", the laser ceiling is "Specialist rank", and the ventilator ceiling is "Chief Surgeon rank".
- A surgeon (task) may *only* pick up a new tool if their rank is **strictly higher than the rank-labels of all tools currently in use by other surgeons**.
- If Dr. B tries to take the microscope while Dr. A is using it, Dr. B is blocked. To prevent indefinite waiting, Dr. A's urgency is **immediately raised to Dr. B's rank** so that Dr. A can finish and release the tool quickly. This mimics **priority inheritance**.

The rules guarantee that no surgeon is ever preempted indefinitely by lower-ranked colleagues, no two surgeons ever form a circular wait, and any surgeon waits for **at most one other surgery** before getting access.

### 1.3 Why PCP is Essential — The Priority Inversion Problem

**Priority Inversion** occurs when a higher-priority task is forced to wait for a lower-priority task to release a shared resource. In the worst case, an *intermediate-priority* task can preempt the lower-priority task, indefinitely extending the block — a phenomenon famously observed in the **Mars Pathfinder spacecraft (1997)**.

PCP addresses three pathologies simultaneously:

| Pathological Effect | Caused By | PCP Solution |
|---|---|---|
| **Unbounded Priority Inversion** | Medium-priority task preempts low-priority task holding resource needed by high-priority task | Priority inheritance + ceiling check |
| **Deadlock** | Circular wait chain across multiple resources | Static ceiling assignment prevents circular wait |
| **Chained Blocking** | Task blocked on multiple resources sequentially | At most **one** blocking event per task per critical section |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** In a system of **n** tasks sharing **m** resources, under IPCP a task can be blocked by **at most one other task** for the duration of **at most one critical section** of the blocking task. This is the **Single-Block Theorem**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Model & Preliminaries

Let us formally define the KTU-standard model for PCP analysis.

- A real-time system consists of a **fixed set of n periodic/aperiodic tasks** $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$.
- Each task $T_i$ has a **fixed priority** $P_i$. Without loss of generality, assume $P_1 > P_2 > \ldots > P_n$ (so $T_1$ is the highest priority).
- A set of $m$ **shared resources** $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_m$ exist, each protected by a **binary semaphore** (mutex).
- Each resource $R_k$ has an associated **critical section** in those tasks that use it.
- A task may request **any subset** of resources, but it acquires them one at a time and releases them in **strict LIFO (stack) order** to avoid nested lock issues.

### 2.2 Key Definitions

**Definition 1 — Priority Ceiling of a Resource**
The **priority ceiling** of a resource $R_k$, denoted $C(R_k)$, is the **highest fixed priority of any task that may access $R_k$**:

$$C(R_k) = \max_{T_i \text{ uses } R_k} \{P_i\}$$

**Definition 2 — System Ceiling (Current Ceiling)**
At any time $t$, the **current system ceiling** $\Pi(t)$ is the **highest priority ceiling among all resources currently locked** by any task in the system:

$$\Pi(t) = \max_{R_k \text{ is locked at } t} \{C(R_k)\}$$

**Definition 3 — Dynamic Priority**
A task's **dynamic (active) priority** at time $t$, denoted $P_i^{dyn}(t)$, may differ from $P_i$ due to priority inheritance.

### 2.3 The Three Rules of the Original Priority Ceiling Protocol (OPCP)

**Rule 1 — Ceiling Violation (Scheduling Rule)**
A task $T_i$ is allowed to **lock a free resource $R_k$** at time $t$ if and only if:

$$P_i^{dyn}(t) > \Pi(t)$$

If $P_i^{dyn}(t) \leq \Pi(t)$, the lock is denied, and $T_i$ is **blocked**. The task that currently holds the resource whose ceiling equals $P_i^{dyn}(t)$ has its priority **raised to $P_i^{dyn}(t)$** (this is the inheritance event).

**Rule 2 — Priority Inheritance**
When a task $T_i$ is blocked, the task $T_h$ holding the conflicting resource inherits $T_i$'s priority. Multiple inheritance events may chain, but transitivity is bounded.

**Rule 3 — Resource Release**
A task releases a resource in the **reverse order** of acquisition (LIFO). Upon full release, the task's dynamic priority reverts to its previous value.

### 2.4 The Three Rules of the Immediate Priority Ceiling Protocol (IPCP)

In IPCP, a task's priority is **immediately raised to the ceiling of any resource it locks**, even if no other task is currently blocked. This eliminates Rule 1's runtime check.

**Rule 1 (IPCP) — Immediate Boost**
When task $T_i$ locks resource $R_k$, its dynamic priority is immediately set to:

$$P_i^{dyn} = C(R_k)$$

**Rule 2 (IPCP) — Lock Eligibility**
A task $T_i$ may lock $R_k$ only if **no resource currently locked by any other task has a ceiling $\geq P_i$**. (Equivalent to OPCP Rule 1.)

**Rule 3 (IPCP) — Release**
On releasing its last resource, $T_i$ reverts to its base priority $P_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "HLP"**: IPCP = Highest Locker's Priority. Whenever a task holds a resource, its priority is *at least* the ceiling of that resource. So among all tasks currently holding any resource, the one with the highest effective priority is the one whose resource has the highest ceiling. This is why IPCP is also called the *Highest Locker's Priority Protocol*.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| $C(R_k)$ | Priority ceiling of resource $R_k$ | $C(R_k) = \max \{P_i \mid T_i \text{ uses } R_k\}$ |
| $\Pi(t)$ | Current system ceiling at time $t$ | $\Pi(t) = \max \{C(R_k) \mid R_k \text{ locked at } t\}$ |
| $B_i$ | Worst-case blocking time of $T_i$ | $B_i = \max_{k: T_i \text{ uses } R_k}\left(\max_{j \text{ blocks } i} c_{jk}\right)$ |
| $c_{jk}$ | Worst-case execution time of $T_j$'s critical section for $R_k$ | Empirical / given |
| $R_i$ | Worst-case response time of $T_i$ | $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i$ |
| $I_i$ | Total preemption/interference from higher-priority tasks | Standard rate-monotonic formula |
| Single-Block Bound | Maximum number of blocking events per $T_i$ | **Exactly one** (per KTU theorem) |
| Block Duration Bound | Maximum duration of a single block | $\leq$ one critical section of one lower/equal-prio task |

> **Engineering Utility:** PCP is the foundation of modern RTOS mutex designs, including **POSIX PTHREAD_PRIO_PROTECT**, **OSEK/VDX resource management**, **AUTOSAR OS**, and the **VxWorks semaphores**. Any system certified under **DO-178C** (avionics) or **ISO 26262** (automotive ASIL) uses a variant of PCP/IPCP for resource locking.

### 2.6 Properties of PCP

1. **Deadlock Freedom** — A circular wait cannot form because any task holding a resource has dynamic priority at least equal to the highest ceiling among all locked resources. A new task can only enter if it has strictly higher priority.
2. **Bounded Blocking (Single Block Theorem)** — A task can be blocked by **at most one** lower or equal-priority task for the duration of **at most one critical section** of that blocking task.
3. **No Chained Blocking** — Unlike PIP, a task blocked once is never blocked again on a *different* resource.
4. **Mutual Exclusion** — Preserved by construction (binary semaphores).
5. **Transitive Inheritance is Bounded** — A chain of inheritance is limited in length by the number of resources, ensuring deterministic execution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — The Classic 3-Task, 2-Resource Scenario

Consider the textbook KTU benchmark scenario:

- **Tasks** with priorities (high→low): $T_1 (P_1 = 3)$, $T_2 (P_2 = 2)$, $T_3 (P_3 = 1)$.
- **Resources**: $R_1$ (used by $T_1$ and $T_3$) and $R_2$ (used by $T_2$ and $T_3$).
- **Critical sections**: $\text{CS}(T_1, R_1) = 1$, $\text{CS}(T_3, R_1) = 4$, $\text{CS}(T_2, R_2) = 2$, $\text{CS}(T_3, R_2) = 3$.

#### Step 1 — Compute Resource Ceilings

For $R_1$, used by $T_1$ and $T_3$:

$$C(R_1) = \max(P_1, P_3) = \max(3, 1) = 3$$

For $R_2$, used by $T_2$ and $T_3$:

$$C(R_2) = \max(P_2, P_3) = \max(2, 1) = 2$$

The **current system ceiling** $\Pi(t)$ is the maximum of all currently locked ceilings.

#### Step 2 — Apply OPCP Scheduling

| Time | Event | $P_1^{dyn}$ | $P_2^{dyn}$ | $P_3^{dyn}$ | $\Pi(t)$ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $T_3$ starts (only task ready) | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | $T_3$ runs, base priority = 1 |
| 0 | $T_3$ locks $R_1$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 3 | $P_3^{dyn} \to 3$ (IPCP boost) |
| 1 | $T_1$ arrives | 3 | 2 | 1 | 3 | $T_1$ checks ceiling: $P_1 = 3 \not> \Pi = 3$, **blocked**, $T_3$ inherits 3 (no change) |
| 2 | $T_2$ arrives | 3 | 2 | 1 | 3 | $P_2 = 2 \not> 3$, **blocked** |
| 4 | $T_3$ releases $R_1$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | $P_3^{dyn} \to 1$, $T_1$ unblocks |
| 4 | $T_1$ preempts $T_3$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | $T_1$ runs, locks $R_1$, $P_1^{dyn} \to 3$ |
| 5 | $T_1$ releases $R_1$, finishes | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | $T_2$ unblocks, preempts $T_3$ |
| 5 | $T_2$ locks $R_2$ | 3 | 2 | 2 | 2 | $P_2^{dyn} \to 2$ |
| 7 | $T_2$ releases $R_2$, finishes | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | $T_3$ resumes |
| 11 | $T_3$ locks $R_2$, executes | 3 | 2 | 1 | 2 | $P_3^{dyn} \to 2$ |
| 14 | $T_3$ finishes | — | — | — | — | All done |

#### Step 3 — Compute Worst-Case Blocking Times

For $T_1$:
- $T_1$ can be blocked only by a task that locks $R_1$ with ceiling $\geq P_1$. The candidates are $T_3$.
- Worst-case block:

$$B_1 = c_{31} = 4$$

For $T_2$:
- $T_2$ uses $R_2$. A higher-ceiling resource is $R_1$ (ceiling = 3). A task holding $R_1$ (i.e., $T_3$) can block $T_2$.
- Worst-case block:

$$B_2 = c_{31} = 4$$

For $T_3$:
- $T_3$ is the lowest priority and is never blocked by PCP inheritance because no lower-priority task exists. Thus:

$$B_3 = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoided:** Many students compute $B_2$ as 2 (the size of $T_2$'s own critical section). The correct blocking time is from the *higher-ceiling resource's* critical section, which here is $T_3$'s 4-unit CS for $R_1$. This is a classic 1-mark trap.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Single-Block Theorem

We prove the **Single-Block Theorem** formally (this is a KTU 14-mark favorite).

**Theorem.** *In a system of $n$ tasks governed by IPCP (or OPCP), a task $T_i$ can be blocked at most once during any of its critical sections, and the duration of that block is bounded by the longest critical section of the task that causes the block.*

**Proof Sketch (by contradiction):**

1. Suppose $T_i$ is blocked twice on different resources during the same dispatch. Let the blocks occur on $R_a$ and $R_b$ in that order.
2. When $T_i$ was blocked on $R_a$, some task $T_h$ was holding a resource $R_x$ such that $C(R_x) \geq P_i$.
3. By Rule 1, $T_h$'s dynamic priority was raised to $\geq P_i$. Hence $T_h$ was *not preemptable* by any task of priority $\leq P_i$ until it released all resources with ceiling $\geq P_i$.
4. By the ceiling property, $T_h$ is guaranteed to be the *unique* holder of the "global lock" of system ceiling $\geq P_i$. No other task can hold such a resource.
5. Therefore, the second block on $R_b$ cannot occur before $T_h$ releases all such resources, and $T_i$ cannot be blocked by any *other* task for the duration of the first block.
6. Hence, two distinct blocking events from two distinct tasks in a single critical section are impossible. **Q.E.D.**

### 3.3 Response-Time Analysis Under IPCP

For Rate-Monotonic scheduling with IPCP, the worst-case response time $R_i$ of $T_i$ satisfies the standard recurrence with an added blocking term:

$$R_i = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j: P_j > P_i} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

where:

- $C_i$ = worst-case execution time of $T_i$.
- $B_i$ = worst-case blocking time (computed as in §3.1).
- The sum is the standard higher-priority preemption/interference term.

The **schedulability test** is:

$$\forall i: R_i \leq D_i$$

where $D_i$ is the deadline of $T_i$ (commonly $D_i = T_i$ for periodic tasks).

> **Numerical Sanity Check (continued from §3.1):**
> Suppose $C_1 = 3$, $C_2 = 4$, $C_3 = 6$, and periods $T_1 = 7$, $T_2 = 12$, $T_3 = 20$.
> For $T_1$: $R_1 = 3 + 4 = 7 \leq 7$ ✓
> For $T_2$: $R_2 = 4 + 4 + \lceil 7/7 \rceil \cdot 3 = 15 \leq 12$? — fails, system is not schedulable. Student should add $B_2$ correctly to avoid false schedulability claims.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State Machine — Task Lifecycle Under IPCP

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready
    Ready --> Running : scheduler dispatch
    Running --> Running : execute non critical
    Running --> Locking : request resource Rk
    Locking --> Running : dynamic priority raised to C(Rk)
    Running --> Blocked : ceiling violation detected
    Blocked --> Inheriting : blocker task priority boosted
    Inheriting --> Ready : blocker releases Rk
    Blocked --> Ready : lock granted after unblock
    Running --> Releasing : exit critical section
    Releasing --> Ready : priority restored to base Pi
    Ready --> [*] : task completed
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — IPCP Lock Acquisition Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Ti requests lock on Rk] --> B{Is Rk free?}
    B -- No --> Z[Task Ti joins wait queue for Rk]
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute C of Rk = max Pj using Rk]
    C --> D{Is current system ceiling Pi less than Ti dynamic priority?}
    D -- No --> E[DENY LOCK]
    E --> F[Block Ti]
    F --> G[Inherit Ti priority into the holder of the ceiling matching resource]
    G --> H[Holder finishes and releases]
    H --> I[Unblock Ti]
    I --> C
    D -- Yes --> J[GRANT LOCK]
    J --> K[Set Ti dynamic priority = max Pi and C of Rk]
    K --> L[Enter critical section]
    L --> M[Execute]
    M --> N[Release Rk]
    N --> O[Restore Ti dynamic priority]
    O --> P[Continue execution]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Resource Ceiling Assignment Table

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tasks
        T1[Task T1\nPriority 3\nUses R1]
        T2[Task T2\nPriority 2\nUses R2]
        T3[Task T3\nPriority 1\nUses R1 and R2]
    end
    subgraph Resources
        R1[Resource R1\nCeiling 3]
        R2[Resource R2\nCeiling 2]
    end
    T1 --> R1
    T3 --> R1
    T2 --> R2
    T3 --> R2
    R1 -. ceiling 3 .-> SYS[System Ceiling = max of locked ceilings]
    R2 -. ceiling 2 .-> SYS
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Block — PIP vs IPCP

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Task wants resource] --> B{Which protocol?}
    B -- PIP --> C[Lock if free]
    C --> D[Inherit only on conflict]
    D --> E[Multiple blocks possible]
    E --> F[Deadlock possible]
    B -- IPCP --> G[Immediate priority boost on lock]
    G --> H[Strict ceiling check]
    H --> I[Single block guaranteed]
    I --> J[Deadlock free]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Tip for KTU:** When the examiner asks "draw the state diagram of PCP," students often forget to show the **priority restore** transition on resource release. The Mermaid diagram above includes this; replicate it in your answer for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`**

**Q1. Define Priority Ceiling Protocol. What is the priority ceiling of a resource?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** is a resource synchronization protocol used in real-time systems to prevent unbounded priority inversion, eliminate deadlocks, and provide a *bounded blocking* guarantee when tasks share mutually exclusive resources.

The **priority ceiling of a resource** $R_k$, denoted $C(R_k)$, is defined as the **highest fixed priority of any task in the system that may lock $R_k$**. Formally:

$$C(R_k) = \max_{T_i \text{ uses } R_k}\{P_i\}$$

The protocol uses this static ceiling value to enforce a runtime check: a task may only enter a critical section if its active priority is strictly higher than the highest ceiling among all currently locked resources. If the check fails, the requesting task is blocked, and the holding task inherits the requester's priority to bound the block.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`**

**Q2. Differentiate between Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) and Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP).**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | PIP | PCP |
|---|---|---|
| Blocking bound | Can be chained (multiple blocks) | Single block only |
| Deadlock prevention | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed |
| Overhead | Lower (no pre-check) | Higher (ceiling check on every lock) |
| Inheritance trigger | On block only | On lock (IPCP) or on block (OPCP) |
| Run-time cost | Simpler | Slightly more complex |
| Resource ceiling | Not used | Statically assigned |

In summary, **PIP** solves unbounded priority inversion but **may still allow deadlocks and chained blocks**, whereas **PCP** (especially IPCP) gives stronger guarantees — *single block, deadlock free, bounded* — at the cost of a slightly higher implementation overhead.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Immediate Priority Ceiling Protocol (IPCP)** in detail. State all three rules clearly. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Consider a system with three tasks $T_1$, $T_2$, $T_3$ (priorities $P_1 = 5$, $P_2 = 3$, $P_3 = 1$) and two resources $R_1$ and $R_2$. $T_1$ uses $R_1$ (CS = 2), $T_2$ uses $R_2$ (CS = 3), and $T_3$ uses both $R_1$ (CS = 4) and $R_2$ (CS = 5). Compute the **priority ceilings**, the **worst-case blocking times** of all three tasks, and determine the **response time of $T_1$** assuming $C_1 = 3$, $C_2 = 5$, $C_3 = 7$, $T_1 = 10$, $T_2 = 15$, $T_3 = 30$, and $D_i = T_i$. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Rule 1 — Immediate Boost:**
When a task $T_i$ locks a resource $R_k$, its dynamic priority is *immediately* raised to the ceiling of $R_k$:

$$P_i^{dyn} \leftarrow C(R_k)$$

**Rule 2 — Lock Eligibility:**
A task $T_i$ may lock a free resource $R_k$ only if **no resource currently held by any other task has a ceiling $\geq P_i$**. Equivalently, $P_i > \Pi(t)$ where $\Pi(t)$ is the current system ceiling.

**Rule 3 — Release:**
When $T_i$ releases its last resource, its dynamic priority reverts to its base priority $P_i$. Resources must be released in reverse order of acquisition (LIFO).

These three rules jointly ensure: deadlock-freedom, single-block guarantee, mutual exclusion, and bounded blocking. The protocol is widely used in **AUTOSAR OS**, **VxWorks**, and **RTEMS** resource management.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Ceilings:**

$$C(R_1) = \max(P_1, P_3) = \max(5, 1) = 5$$

$$C(R_2) = \max(P_2, P_3) = \max(3, 1) = 3$$

**Step 2 — Compute Blocking Times:**

- $B_1$: $T_1$ can be blocked by a task holding a resource with ceiling $\geq 5$. The only such resource is $R_1$, held by $T_3$. So:

$$B_1 = \text{CS of } T_3 \text{ on } R_1 = 4$$

- $B_2$: $T_2$ can be blocked by a task holding $R_1$ (ceiling 5) or $R_2$ (ceiling 3). The maximum critical section blocking $T_2$ is:

$$B_2 = \max(c_{31}, c_{32}) = \max(4, 5) = 5$$

- $B_3$: Lowest priority, cannot be blocked by any lower task:

$$B_3 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Response Time of $T_1$:**

Since $T_1$ is the highest-priority task, no higher-priority preemption exists. So:

$$R_1 = C_1 + B_1 = 3 + 4 = 7$$

**Step 4 — Schedulability Check for All Tasks:**

For $T_1$: $R_1 = 7 \leq D_1 = 10$ ✓
For $T_2$:

$$R_2 = 5 + 5 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 = 10 + 3 \lceil R_2/10 \rceil$$

Iterating: $R_2^{(0)} = 10$, $R_2^{(1)} = 13$, $R_2^{(2)} = 16$, $R_2^{(3)} = 19$, $R_2^{(4)} = 22$, $R_2^{(5)} = 25$. With $D_2 = 15$, $T_2$ **misses its deadline**; the system is not schedulable as configured.

For $T_3$:

$$R_3 = 7 + 0 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3}{15} \right\rceil \cdot 5$$

Iterating gives $R_3 > 30$, so $T_3$ also misses its deadline.

**Conclusion:** With these parameters, only $T_1$ is schedulable. The system designer must adjust periods or reduce critical-section lengths to make all tasks meet deadlines.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** What is **priority inversion**? Explain the **Mars Pathfinder bug** and show how PCP would have prevented it. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With neat state-transition diagrams and a step-by-step example, show how **OPCP** handles the classic three-task, two-resource deadlock scenario. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

**Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is forced to wait for a lower-priority task to release a shared resource, while intermediate-priority tasks preempt the lower-priority task, *indefinitely* extending the wait.

**Mars Pathfinder Incident (1997):**
The Pathfinder lander experienced frequent system resets on Mars. The root cause was traced to priority inversion in the **bus-management task** and the **ASI/MET (meteorology) task**:

- A **low-priority bus task** acquired a shared semaphore.
- A **high-priority ASI/MET task** attempted to acquire the same semaphore and was blocked.
- A **medium-priority communication task** repeatedly preempted the low-priority bus task, preventing it from releasing the semaphore.
- The ASI/MET task missed its deadline, the watchdog timer fired, and the system rebooted — losing scientific data.

**How PCP Prevents This:**
- Each shared resource would be assigned a static priority ceiling.
- When the high-priority ASI/MET task arrives, the ceiling-violation rule forces the bus task to *inherit* ASI/MET's priority.
- The medium-priority comms task can no longer preempt, the bus task finishes quickly, and ASI/MET is unblocked within a bounded time.
- No watchdog reset occurs.

**Model Solution (b):**

Consider $T_1 (P_1 = 3)$, $T_2 (P_2 = 2)$, $T_3 (P_3 = 1)$, $R_1$ used by $T_1, T_3$, $R_2$ used by $T_2, T_3$. Ceilings: $C(R_1) = 3$, $C(R_2) = 2$.

**State diagram (textual representation for KTU answer sheet):**

```
          ┌──────────┐  lock eligible   ┌──────────────┐
   Ready  │  Idle    │ ───────────────► │  Holds R     │
          └──────────┘                  └──────────────┘
              ▲ ▲                              │
              │ │ release                       │ ceiling violation
              │ │                               ▼
          ┌──────────┐  blocked         ┌──────────────┐
          │ Blocked  │ ◄─────────────── │  Inheriting  │
          └──────────┘                  └──────────────┘
```

**Step-by-step execution trace:**

1. $t=0$: $T_3$ starts, locks $R_1$ (or $R_2$). Under IPCP, $P_3^{dyn} = C(R_k)$.
2. $t=1$: $T_1$ arrives, attempts to lock $R_1$. Ceiling check: $P_1 = 3 \not> C(R_1) = 3$. **Blocked**. $T_3$ inherits priority 3.
3. $t=2$: $T_2$ arrives, attempts to lock $R_2$. Ceiling check fails ($P_2 = 2 \not> 3$). **Blocked**.
4. $T_3$ runs to completion, releases $R_1$. $T_1$ unblocks, runs.
5. $T_1$ finishes, $T_2$ unblocks, runs.
6. $T_2$ finishes, $T_3$ resumes and continues with its remaining critical section.

**Key Observation:** No circular wait could form because a task holding the highest-ceiling resource has dynamic priority equal to the highest in the system — no other task can hold a conflicting resource.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the LIFO release order** — Many students list release in arbitrary order. KTU penalizes 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing OPCP and IPCP** — IPCP boosts priority *immediately on lock*; OPCP boosts only on a *ceiling violation (block)*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Computing blocking time from the wrong task** — The blocking task is the one holding the *resource with the highest ceiling*, not necessarily a direct sharer. Common 2-mark error.
> 4. **Missing the boundedness proof** — Part (a) of 14-mark questions often requires the **Single-Block Theorem statement**. Just stating "PCP prevents deadlock" earns 1 mark, not 3.
> 5. **Wrong ceiling formula** — Using $\min$ instead of $\max$ loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **PCP** = protocol to give **bounded, deadlock-free, single-block** resource access in real-time systems.
- **Resource Ceiling** $C(R_k) = \max P_i$ over all tasks that use $R_k$ — *static* value.
- **System Ceiling** $\Pi(t) = \max C(R_k)$ over all *currently locked* resources — *dynamic* value.
- **OPCP**: inheritance happens **only on a ceiling violation** (a blocked task).
- **IPCP** (a.k.a. HLP): priority boosted **immediately on lock**, no pre-check needed.
- **Three Rules of OPCP / IPCP** — Master them: (1) eligibility check, (2) inheritance, (3) LIFO release with priority restore.
- **Single-Block Theorem** — Each task is blocked by **at most one** other task for **at most one critical section**.
- **Deadlock-freedom** is guaranteed by the static ceiling assignment and the strict eligibility check.
- **PIP vs PCP** — PIP may deadlock and chain-block; PCP eliminates both.
- **Banking the ceiling** — The task holding the resource whose ceiling equals the requesting task's priority is the one that inherits. Watch this in trace problems.
- **Response time formula**: $R_i = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j > i} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ with $B_i$ as computed from the highest-ceiling resource's critical section.
- **Mars Pathfinder** is the canonical KTU narrative question; remember the three tasks: bus, ASI/MET, comms.
- **POSIX/AUTOSAR** mapping: IPCP ≈ `PTHREAD_PRIO_PROTECT`, used in OSEK Resource management.
- **Pitfalls to avoid**: wrong $B_i$ computation, confusion of OPCP vs IPCP, missing LIFO release, omitting the boundedness proof.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling Real-Time Tasks in Multiprocessor and Distributed Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scheduling Real-Time Tasks in Multiprocessor and Distributed Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multiprocessor Real-Time Scheduling** is the problem of allocating a set $\mathcal{T} = \{\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n\}$ of real-time tasks across **M** processing elements (PEs) that share a common memory space, such that every deadline is guaranteed under worst-case execution.

**Distributed Real-Time Scheduling** extends this problem to tasks that execute across **physically disjoint nodes** interconnected by a real-time network. Here, a task is decomposed into a *chain of sub-tasks* with precedence constraints and inter-node communication delays.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> - **Multiprocessor** $\Rightarrow$ shared memory, single OS image, tight coupling (uniform memory access or NUMA).
> - **Distributed** $\Rightarrow$ physically distributed nodes, message-passing, partial failure tolerance required.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Air Traffic Control Tower Network

Imagine a country’s air traffic control system:

- **Multiprocessor Scheduling** is like a single control room with **8 radar consoles**. Each controller (task) can be assigned to any console (processor), and they can swap consoles during a shift (migration). The challenge is balancing workload dynamically.
- **Distributed Real-Time Scheduling** is like **5 different airports** coordinating flight handoffs. The flight (task) has stages — taxiing (subtask 1), takeoff clearance (subtask 2), en-route handoff (subtask 3) — each handled at a different airport (node) with radio communication delays between them. A late handoff can cause a chain-reaction delay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever the question says *"multiple CPUs sharing memory"* $\rightarrow$ think **multiprocessor**. When it says *"multiple nodes communicating via network"* $\rightarrow$ think **distributed**. This simple discrimination fetches easy marks.

### 1.3 Why Single-Processor Schedulers Fail on Multiprocessors

The classical Rate Monotonic (RM) and Earliest Deadline First (EDF) algorithms were designed for a **single CPU**. When naïvely extended to M processors, they suffer from:

- **Dhall’s Effect** — pathological task sets can make utilization bound collapse toward $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- **Bin-Packing Bottleneck** — partitioned allocation is **NP-hard**.
- **Migration Overhead** — global scheduling needs cache flush + context transfer on every move.
- **Priority Inversion at Global Queue** — all M processors race for one shared ready queue.

These problems necessitate **specialized multiprocessor scheduling algorithms** like **Pfair**, **LLF**, **DP-Fair**, and **BF (Boyd-Fractel)** that we will analyze in Section 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Multiprocessor Real-Time Scheduling Algorithms

The literature partitions these algorithms along **two orthogonal axes**: *task-to-processor binding* and *dispatch policy*.

#### A. By Binding Strategy

| Strategy | Binding | Migration | Example Algorithms |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Partitioned** | Static (at design time) | None | Partitioned RM, Partitioned EDF |
| **Global** | Dynamic (at runtime) | Unrestricted | Global EDF, Global LLF, Pfair |
| **Clustered (Hybrid)** | Static between clusters | Within cluster only | Clustered EDF, mP-Fair |

#### B. By Dispatch Policy

- **Fixed Priority (FP):** Rate Monotonic, Deadline Monotonic.
- **Dynamic Priority:** EDF, LLF, Minimum Laxity First.
- **Proportionate Fair:** Pfair, PD, PD$^2$.

### 2.2 The Three Pillars — Detailed Mechanics

**1. Partitioned Scheduling**

Each task is permanently bound to one processor using a **bin-packing heuristic** (First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit, Next-Fit). Each processor then independently runs a uniprocessor scheduler.

$$\text{Total utilization bound} = M \cdot U_b$$

For RM: $U_b = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. For EDF: $U_b = 1.0$.

*Advantage:* zero migration overhead, full isolation.
*Disadvantage:* capacity loss due to fragmentation; bin-packing is NP-hard.

**2. Global Scheduling**

A **single global ready queue** is shared across all M processors. At each instant, the M highest-priority ready jobs are dispatched.

$$\text{At each scheduling decision point } t: \text{dispatch the } M \text{ jobs with smallest } p_i(t)$$

where $p_i(t)$ is the dynamic priority (e.g., $-d_i$ in EDF, $-L_i$ in LLF).

*Advantage:* optimal load balancing.
*Disadvantage:* Dhall’s effect, cache-related preemption delays, priority inversion.

**3. Clustered (Hybrid) Scheduling**

Processors are grouped into **K clusters** of size $m_i$ where $\sum m_i = M$. Tasks are partitioned across clusters (no inter-cluster migration), but within each cluster a global scheduler is used. This is a pragmatic compromise that bounds migration overhead while gaining much of global scheduling’s flexibility.

### 2.3 Pfair Scheduling — The 100% Utilization Champion

**Pfair** (Baruah, Cohen, Plaxton, Varvel, 1996) is the **only known optimal multiprocessor scheduling algorithm** for periodic tasks. It achieves utilization bound **exactly 100%** on M processors.

**Core Idea:** Each job $J_i$ with execution time $e_i$ released at $r_i$ and with deadline $d_i$ is decomposed into **sub-jobs (quanta)** of unit length. Pfair requires that during any time window $[t_1, t_2]$, the *proportion* of time allocated to $\tau_i$ be **at least** $(t_2 - t_1) \cdot u_i$, where $u_i = e_i / (d_i - r_i)$ is the task’s utilization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pfair Window Constraint (WCRT for KTU):**
> $$\forall\, t_1 < t_2:\quad \sum_{t \in [t_1, t_2)} \text{alloc}(\tau_i, t) \;\geq\; \left\lceil u_i \cdot (t_2 - t_1) \right\rceil$$

The actual dispatch rule used in practice is the **PD (Pfair Derivative)** algorithm: at each integer time $t$, schedule the M tasks with the **smallest pseudo-release** $r_i^{ps}(t) = \lfloor t \cdot u_i \rfloor$.

### 2.4 LLF (Least Laxity First) Scheduling

Laxity is the *slack* a task has before it becomes infeasible:

$$L_i(t) \;=\; d_i \;-\; t \;-\; C_i^{\text{rem}}(t)$$

where $C_i^{\text{rem}}(t)$ is the remaining computation. LLF dispatches the job with **minimum laxity**. LLF is *optimal* on multiprocessors (Baruah, 1994) but suffers from **frequent context switches** due to laxity ties.

### 2.5 Distributed Real-Time Task Model

A *distributed transaction* $\mathcal{T}_i$ is a partial order of sub-tasks $\tau_{i,1} \to \tau_{i,2} \to \dots \to \tau_{i,n_i}$ where each $\tau_{i,j}$ executes on node $N_{\pi(i,j)}$. The **end-to-end response time** is:

$$R_i^{\text{e2e}} \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} \left( C_{i,j} \;+\; \delta_{i,j} \right)$$

where $\delta_{i,j}$ is the **communication latency** between $\tau_{i,j}$ and $\tau_{i,j+1}$ over the real-time network.

### 2.6 Fault Tolerance — Primary-Backup Approach

For fault-tolerant distributed scheduling, each task $\tau_i$ is replicated:

- **Primary copy** executes on node $N_p$ at scheduled time.
- **Backup copy** is held in reserve on node $N_b \neq N_p$.
- Backup executes *only if* the primary fails to acknowledge completion within a watchdog window $\Delta_w$.

The **recovery block** mechanism extends this with acceptance tests and alternate sub-tasks.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Summary (Direct questions 60%–70% weight in KTU ESE):**

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RM utilization bound (uniprocessor) | $U_b(n) = n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$ | $n \to \infty \Rightarrow U_b \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ |
| 2 | EDF utilization bound (uniprocessor) | $U_b = 1.0$ | Optimal for uniprocessor |
| 3 | Multiprocessor RM bound (Dhall) | $U_b(M) = M \cdot \left( 2^{1/M} - 1 \right)$ | As $M \to \infty$, $U_b \to \ln 2$ |
| 4 | Laxity at time $t$ | $L_i(t) = d_i - t - C_i^{\text{rem}}(t)$ | Negative laxity $\Rightarrow$ infeasible |
| 5 | Pfair quantum allocation window | $\sum \text{alloc}(\tau_i, [t_1,t_2)) \geq \lceil u_i \Delta t \rceil$ | $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$ |
| 6 | End-to-end response time | $R_i^{\text{e2e}} = \sum_j \left( C_{i,j} + \delta_{i,j} \right)$ | Requires $\forall i: R_i^{\text{e2e}} \leq D_i$ |
| 7 | Pfair task utilization | $u_i = e_i / p_i$ | Feasibility iff $\sum_{i=1}^n u_i \leq M$ |
| 8 | Communication delay (TDMA bus) | $\delta = \text{frame\_time} \cdot \text{max\_slots}$ | For C-Window protocol |
| 9 | Primary-Backup overlap constraint | $\bigcap_{k} \left[ s_k^{(b)}, s_k^{(b)} + e_k^{(b)} \right] = \emptyset$ | Backup windows disjoint for $k$-fault |
| 10 | Processor demand criterion (EDF global) | $\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{C_i}{D_i - C_i} \leq M - (M-1) \cdot \frac{\max C_i}{\min(D_i - C_i)}$ | Sufficient (not necessary) |

> **Units Used Throughout:** time in CPU cycles, $u_i$ dimensionless, $M$ dimensionless (integer $\geq 1$), $\delta$ in microseconds.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

These scheduling paradigms power **flight control computers** (F-22 uses partitioned RM across 4 PEs), **engine ECUs** (AUTOSAR on multicore ARM Cortex-R52), **5G baseband processing** (global LLF on DSP farms), **autonomous driving platforms** (NVIDIA Orin with clustered scheduling across CPU+GPU+DSP), and **SCADA grids** (distributed primary-backup across substations).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Dhall’s Effect on Multiprocessor Utilization Bound

**Statement:** When RM (or any utilization-bound scheduler) is used for **global** scheduling on $M$ processors, there exists a task set with total utilization arbitrarily close to 1 that is **unschedulable**.

**Proof Sketch (Hall’s / Dhall’s construction):**

Consider $M+1$ tasks on $M$ processors. Let tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_M$ be **light, high-priority, RM-compatible** tasks with periods $p_k$ and executions $C_k = \epsilon$ where $\epsilon \to 0$. Let the remaining task $\tau_{M+1}$ be a **heavy, low-priority** task with $C_{M+1} = 1$ and period $p_{M+1} = 2$.

*Step 1.* Compute the utilization of the light set:
$$\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{light}} &= \sum_{k=1}^{M} \frac{C_k}{p_k} = \sum_{k=1}^{M} \frac{\epsilon}{p_k}
\end{aligned}$$

Since each $C_k \to 0$, the tasks can be assigned periods such that RM gives them priorities higher than $\tau_{M+1}$, while $\sum \epsilon/p_k$ is arbitrarily small. Choose $p_k$ to be **mutually co-prime** to maximize spacing.

*Step 2.* The heavy task $\tau_{M+1}$ has utilization $u_{M+1} = 1/2$. Total utilization:
$$\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{total}} &= U_{\text{light}} + \frac{1}{2} \;\to\; \frac{1}{2} + \epsilon \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{M} \frac{1}{p_k}
\end{aligned}$$

By choosing $p_k$ sufficiently large, $U_{\text{total}}$ can be made **arbitrarily close to 1** from below.

*Step 3.* However, at each period boundary of $\tau_{M+1}$, the M light tasks all release their next jobs simultaneously. They occupy **all M processors** for time $\epsilon$ each, leaving **zero CPU** for $\tau_{M+1}$ during the critical instant. Hence $\tau_{M+1}$ misses its deadline.

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{\;U_b^{\text{global, RM}}(M) \;\leq\; M \cdot \left( 2^{1/M} - 1 \right) \;\to\; \ln 2 \;\text{ as }\; M \to \infty\;}$$

Thus, for large $M$, global RM guarantees **only ~69.3%** utilization despite EDF being able to handle 100% on a single CPU. This counter-intuitive collapse is Dhall’s effect.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: End-to-End Response Time of a Distributed Transaction

**Problem Statement:**

A distributed real-time system monitors temperature in 3 zones. Each sensor-to-actuator transaction $\mathcal{T}_1$ consists of:

- $\tau_{1,1}$: Read sensor on node $N_1$, $C_{1,1} = 2$ ms.
- $\tau_{1,2}$: Compute control law on node $N_2$, $C_{1,2} = 3$ ms.
- $\tau_{1,3}$: Actuate valve on node $N_3$, $C_{1,3} = 1$ ms.

Communication delays: $\delta_{1,1} = 1.5$ ms (CAN bus, $N_1 \to N_2$), $\delta_{1,2} = 2.0$ ms (CAN bus, $N_2 \to N_3$).

End-to-end deadline: $D_1 = 12$ ms. Is the task schedulable?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

*Step 1.* Compute total computational cost:
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= C_{1,1} + C_{1,2} + C_{1,3} \\
&= 2 + 3 + 1 = 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 2.* Compute total communication cost:
$$\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{total}} &= \delta_{1,1} + \delta_{1,2} \\
&= 1.5 + 2.0 = 3.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 3.* Compute end-to-end response time (worst case, sequential precedence):
$$\begin{aligned}
R_1^{\text{e2e}} &= C_{\text{total}} + \delta_{\text{total}} \\
&= 6 + 3.5 = 9.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 4.* Schedulability test:
$$\begin{aligned}
R_1^{\text{e2e}} &\;\leq\; D_1 \\
9.5 \text{ ms} &\;\leq\; 12 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

*Step 5.* Slack (headroom):
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Slack} &= D_1 - R_1^{\text{e2e}} = 12 - 9.5 = 2.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

The slack must accommodate any blocking, jitter, and overhead (typically 10%–20%). Hence the design is **marginally safe**, and the engineer should add a watchdog timer for safety.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: LLF Scheduling Trace on 2 Processors

**Task Set (released at $t = 0$):**

| Task | Arrival $r_i$ | Execution $C_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 0 | 2 | 6 |
| $\tau_2$ | 0 | 1 | 4 |
| $\tau_3$ | 0 | 3 | 9 |

**LLF Algorithm:** At each instant, pick the M = 2 tasks with **minimum laxity** $L_i = D_i - t - C_i^{\text{rem}}$.

**Schedule Trace:**

| Time $t$ | $L_1$ | $L_2$ | $L_3$ | Dispatched | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $t = 0$ | $6-0-2=4$ | $4-0-1=3$ | $9-0-3=6$ | $\tau_1, \tau_2$ | $\tau_2$ lowest laxity, $\tau_1$ second |
| $t = 1$ | $6-1-1=4$ | done | $9-1-3=5$ | $\tau_1$ | $\tau_2$ finished, $\tau_3$ waits |
| $t = 2$ | $6-2-0=4$ | – | $9-2-3=4$ | $\tau_1, \tau_3$ | Tied; LLF tie-break $\to$ lower index $\tau_1$ |
| $t = 3$ | done | – | $9-3-2=4$ | $\tau_3$ | Only $\tau_3$ remains |

All deadlines are met: $\tau_1$ finishes at $t=2 < 6$, $\tau_2$ at $t=1 < 4$, $\tau_3$ at $t=5 < 9$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Even though $\tau_2$ had the *shortest* deadline, LLF gave it priority because its *remaining slack after current work* was tightest. This is the hallmark of LLF over EDF.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Define Pfair scheduling. State the window condition it must satisfy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pfair scheduling (Baruah et al., 1996) is a proportional-share multiprocessor scheduling algorithm that allocates processor time to each task in proportion to its utilization. The window condition requires that in every time interval $[t_1, t_2)$, task $\tau_i$ is allocated at least $\lceil u_i \cdot (t_2 - t_1) \rceil$ quanta of execution, where $u_i = e_i/p_i$. This guarantees 100% utilization on M processors, making Pfair the only known optimal multiprocessor scheduler for periodic tasks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
*What is Dhall’s effect in multiprocessor scheduling? Why does it arise?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dhall’s effect is the phenomenon where naive global scheduling algorithms like RM and EDF suffer a *decreasing* utilization bound as the number of processors M grows, asymptotically approaching $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. It arises because at a critical instant, M high-frequency light tasks can simultaneously occupy all M processors, starving a low-frequency heavy task even though total utilization is well below M. The remedy is Pfair or PD$^2$ algorithms.

---

### Part B — Module 2 Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

> **Note for students:** KTU ESE 2024 mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. Both Question A and Question B carry equal marks (14) and are independent alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare the **Partitioned**, **Global**, and **Clustered (Hybrid)** scheduling approaches in multiprocessor real-time systems. Discuss their advantages, disadvantages, and the bin-packing complexity of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Pfair scheduling algorithm** in detail. Derive the **window condition** and discuss why Pfair achieves 100% utilization bound. Mention any practical limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Partitioned | Global | Clustered |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Binding** | Static at design time | Fully dynamic | Static across clusters, dynamic within |
| **Migration** | None | Unrestricted across M PEs | Restricted to cluster |
| **Allocation** | Bin-packing (NP-hard) | Heuristic queue insertion | Hybrid |
| **Utilization Bound** | $M \cdot n(2^{1/n}-1)$ for RM | Collapses to $\ln 2$ (Dhall) | Between above two |
| **Overhead** | Very low | High (cache misses, queue contention) | Moderate |
| **Failure Isolation** | Excellent (fault confined to one PE) | Poor (single point of queue failure) | Good within cluster |
| **Implementation** | Simple (K independent uniprocessor schedulers) | Complex (lock-free queue required) | Medium |

**Conclusion:** Partitioned is preferred for **hard real-time systems** with safety certification (e.g., DO-178C avionics); Global is preferred for **soft real-time** with high load; Clustered is the **industrial sweet spot** (e.g., AUTOSAR multicore).

**(b) Pfair Scheduling Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Task Model:* Each periodic task $\tau_i$ is characterized by $(p_i, e_i)$, where $e_i$ is execution per period.

*Step 2 — Utilization:* $u_i = e_i/p_i$. Feasibility requires $\sum u_i \leq M$.

*Step 3 — Decomposition:* Job $J_i^k$ (k-th instance of $\tau_i$) is split into $e_i$ sub-jobs of unit execution.

*Step 4 — Window Condition:* For every interval $[t_1, t_2)$:
$$\text{alloc}_i(t_1, t_2) \;\geq\; \left\lceil u_i (t_2 - t_1) \right\rceil$$

*Step 5 — PD Algorithm:* At each integer $t$, dispatch the M tasks with smallest pseudo-release time $r_i^{ps}(t) = \lfloor t \cdot u_i \rfloor$.

*Step 6 — Why 100%?* Any task set with $\sum u_i \leq M$ can be packed because the floor function guarantees allocation within tolerance $\epsilon = 1$. Baruah’s theorem (1996) proves PD never misses deadlines.

*Step 7 — Limitations:* Quantum size mismatch with cache lines; high context-switch rate (up to $2 \cdot \sum e_i/p_i$ per second); complex implementation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Least Laxity First (LLF)** scheduling algorithm with a suitable example on 2 processors. Derive the laxity formula and show the dispatch trace. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Primary-Backup approach** for fault-tolerant scheduling in distributed real-time systems. Explain the **Recovery Block** extension. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) LLF Scheduling (7 marks):**

*Laxity Definition:*
$$L_i(t) = D_i - t - C_i^{\text{rem}}(t)$$

*Algorithm:* At each scheduling event, sort ready jobs by ascending laxity and dispatch the M lowest-laxity jobs. Re-evaluate at every job arrival, completion, or laxity-tie event.

*Example (2 processors, 3 tasks released at $t=0$):*

| Task | $r_i$ | $C_i$ | $D_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 0 | 2 | 6 |
| $\tau_2$ | 0 | 1 | 4 |
| $\tau_3$ | 0 | 3 | 9 |

**Dispatch Trace (already computed in Section 3.3):**
- $t = 0$: $\tau_2$ (L=3) and $\tau_1$ (L=4) dispatched; $\tau_3$ waits (L=6).
- $t = 1$: $\tau_2$ completes; $\tau_1$ continues alone.
- $t = 2$: $\tau_1$ completes (L tied with $\tau_3$ at 4); $\tau_3$ dispatched on $P_1$ and continues.
- $t = 5$: $\tau_3$ completes, well before $D_3 = 9$.

*Conclusion:* LLF is optimal for multiprocessors but suffers from **laxity-tie thrashing** and high context switches. LLF is therefore impractical; its importance is mainly theoretical (it serves as the optimality baseline).

**(b) Primary-Backup Approach (7 marks):**

*Principle:* To tolerate a single node failure, each distributed task $\tau_i$ is replicated across two or more nodes. The **primary** runs normally; the **backup** is held in reserve, executing only if the primary fails.

*Protocol:*
1. Scheduler assigns primary $\tau_i^p$ to node $N_p$ at slot $[s_p, s_p + e_p]$.
2. Backup $\tau_i^b$ is assigned to node $N_b \neq N_p$ at slot $[s_b, s_b + e_b]$.
3. Primary sends “alive” heartbeat before completion.
4. If coordinator misses heartbeat within watchdog window $\Delta_w$, it cancels the backup slot and the backup executes.

*Backup Overlap Constraint (k-fault tolerance):*
$$\bigcap_{j=1}^{k} \left[ s^{(j)}, s^{(j)} + e^{(j)} \right] = \emptyset$$

i.e., all backup slots must be mutually disjoint to be simultaneously available when $k$ failures strike.

*Recovery Block Extension:* Each sub-task $\tau_{i,j}$ has *acceptance test* $A_{i,j}$. If primary fails acceptance test, the *alternate sub-task* $\tau_{i,j}^{\prime}$ executes on the backup node. This provides **software-fault tolerance** in addition to hardware-fault tolerance.

*Real-World Example:* Boeing 777 Airplane Information Management System uses primary-backup with 3-way replication across ARINC 659 buses.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing multiprocessor and distributed systems:** Multiprocessor = shared memory; distributed = separate nodes with network. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting Dhall’s bound direction:** Saying “bound increases with M” instead of “approaches ln 2” — wrong by 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing laxity as $D_i - C_i$ instead of $D_i - t - C_i^{\text{rem}}$:** the **time-dependent** term is what makes LLF dynamic.
> 4. **Skipping the window condition in Pfair:** This is the *defining* property; omitting it costs 3 marks.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Always mention whether scheduling is *preemptive*, *on M identical processors*, and tasks are *independent* or *precedence-constrained*. KTU valuation key penalizes unstated assumptions heavily.
> 6. **Numerical errors in end-to-end calculation:** Remember to *sum both* computation *and* communication delays. A common mistake is to add only one.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Multiprocessor & Distributed Real-Time Scheduling):**

- **Multiprocessor ≠ Distributed:** shared memory vs network; KTU almost always tests this distinction.
- **Three strategies:** Partitioned (NP-hard bin-packing, no migration), Global (Dhall effect, dynamic), Clustered (industrial compromise).
- **Dhall’s effect** reduces utilization bound to $M(2^{1/M}-1) \to \ln 2$ for global RM.
- **Pfair** is the only *optimal* multiprocessor scheduler for periodic tasks; requires window condition $\sum \text{alloc} \geq \lceil u_i \Delta t \rceil$.
- **PD algorithm** is the practical realization of Pfair; uses pseudo-release time $\lfloor t \cdot u_i \rfloor$.
- **LLF** uses laxity $L_i = D_i - t - C_i^{\text{rem}}$; optimal but impractical due to tie-thrashing.
- **End-to-end response time** in distributed systems: $R^{\text{e2e}} = \sum_j (C_{i,j} + \delta_{i,j})$; must be $\leq D_i$.
- **Primary-Backup** is the standard fault-tolerance pattern; backup slots must be disjoint for k-fault tolerance.
- **Recovery block** extends primary-backup with acceptance tests for software-fault tolerance.
- **High-yield formulas** (memorize all 10 from Section 2.7): especially Dhall’s bound, laxity, window condition, end-to-end response time.
- **Always state assumptions:** preemptive, independent tasks (or list precedence), M identical PEs, ideal network (or list $\delta_{i,j}$ explicitly).
- **Real-world anchors to mention in answers:** Boeing 777 ARINC 659, AUTOSAR multicore, 5G baseband, NVIDIA Orin, F-22 flight controls.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tolerant Scheduling of Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fault Tolerant Scheduling of Tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault Tolerant Scheduling of Tasks** is the discipline within real-time systems engineering that concerns the design, analysis, and implementation of scheduling algorithms capable of guaranteeing that a set of hard real-time tasks **continues to meet all deadlines even when one or more hardware or software faults occur** in the system during operation.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, fault tolerant scheduling is treated as an extension of classical deterministic scheduling (Rate Monotonic, Earliest Deadline First) where the scheduling policy must consider three additional dimensions:

1. **Fault Hypothesis** — what kinds of faults the system is engineered to tolerate.
2. **Redundancy Budget** — how much time/space/processing redundancy is reserved for recovery.
3. **Deadline Guarantee under Faults** — proving that deadlines remain satisfiable even under worst-case fault scenarios.

Formally, a schedule $\mathcal{S}$ for a task set $\mathcal{T}$ is said to be **k-fault tolerant** if for every fault sequence containing at most $k$ faults, there exists a valid alternate execution path (re-execution, replicated processor, or alternative result) such that all hard deadlines are still met.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Terminology**: The 2024 Scheme course outcome linked to this topic is **CO2 — Apply scheduling algorithms and fault tolerance principles to design reliable real-time systems** under the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive level **Apply / Analyze (Level 3-4)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cockpit Story

Imagine you are flying a commercial aircraft from Kochi to Delhi. The captain alone is brilliant, but **no airline in the world is legally permitted to fly a passenger jet with only one pilot in the cockpit**.

| Cockpit Reality | Real-Time Scheduling Reality |
|---|---|
| Captain flies the plane (primary) | Primary task instance executes |
| First Officer monitors & can take over (backup) | Backup task instance is ready |
| If captain becomes incapacitated → first officer takes control | Fault detected → backup task executes |
| Both must hold ATPL license (redundancy budget) | Extra CPU/bandwidth/time reserved |
| Flight must still land on time (deadline) | Task must still meet its deadline |

The **first officer is the redundancy** that the **scheduling algorithm pre-allocated** for the possibility of captain failure. Fault tolerant scheduling works exactly this way — it pre-allocates backup resources **before** faults occur, because recovery decisions made *after* a fault are always too late.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive takeaway**: Fault tolerance is not about *reacting* to faults; it is about **pre-allocating recovery capacity at design time** so that the schedule remains valid under fault hypotheses.

### 1.3 Engineering Significance

| Application Domain | Why Fault Tolerance is Mandatory |
|---|---|
| **Avionics (DO-178C)** | A missed deadline may equal loss of aircraft |
| **Nuclear Reactor Control** | Single fault cannot halt safety shutdown |
| **Automotive Drive-by-Wire** | Brake-by-wire must respond within milliseconds even with ECU failure |
| **Medical Implants (Pacemaker)** | Hardware fault cannot stop heart-pacing task |
| **Mars Rovers** | Cannot send a technician; software must self-heal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard metric used throughout this topic:
> - **R(t) = e^(−λt)** → Reliability function (probability of no failure up to time $t$).
> - **MTBF = 1/λ** → Mean Time Between Failures.
> - **k-fault hypothesis** → System tolerates up to $k$ independent faults.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fault Model — What Are We Tolerating?

A **fault model** is the formal contract between the system designer and the scheduling algorithm. Without a fault model, fault tolerance is mathematically meaningless. The KTU 2024 Scheme recognizes the following layered taxonomy:

**Layer A — By Duration**

1. **Transient Fault** — occurs once and disappears (e.g., alpha-particle bit flip in memory). *Duration: microseconds.*
2. **Intermittent Fault** — occurs, disappears, recurs (e.g., loose connector). *Duration: seconds to minutes.*
3. **Permanent Fault** — once it occurs, the component is damaged until repair (e.g., burnt capacitor). *Duration: until repair.*

**Layer B — By Behavior**

1. **Fail-Stop Fault** — faulty processor halts and announces failure. *Easiest to handle.*
2. **Omission Fault** — processor omits sending/receiving some messages.
3. **Timing Fault** — processor responds too late (violates timing assertion).
4. **Byzantine Fault** — processor produces arbitrary, possibly malicious results. *Hardest to handle — requires 3f+1 replicas to mask f Byzantine faults.*

**Layer C — By Origin**

1. **Hardware Fault** — physical device failure.
2. **Software Fault** — bug in code (design fault).
3. **Interaction Fault** — operator error, environmental shock.

### 2.2 The Five Classical Fault Tolerant Scheduling Approaches

The KTU 2024 Scheme module prescribes study of these five canonical approaches. Each differs in **where** the redundancy is placed and **when** the recovery decision is taken.

| # | Approach | When is backup triggered? | Where does backup reside? | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Primary-Backup (Passive Replication)** | Only when primary fails | Different processor, same/different code | Avionics, process control |
| 2 | **Active Replication** | Both run simultaneously; vote | All processors, identical code | Flight control computers |
| 3 | **Recovery Block (N-Version)** | Acceptance test fails | Same or alternate processor | Financial transaction systems |
| 4 | **Checkpoint-Rollback** | At periodic checkpoints | Stable storage for state | Long-running scientific jobs |
| 5 | **Imprecise Computation** | Approximate result accepted on fault | Monotone + optional subtasks | Multimedia, signal processing |

### 2.3 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step of Primary-Backup Scheduling

This is the most-tested approach in KTU examinations, so we dissect it fully.

**Step 1 — Partition the task set $\mathcal{T}$ into disjoint primary set $\mathcal{P}$ and backup set $\mathcal{B}$.** Each task $T_i$ has a primary instance $T_i^p$ and one or more backup instances $T_i^b$.

**Step 2 — Schedule all primaries $\mathcal{P}$ first using a classical algorithm (typically Rate Monotonic or EDF).** Backups are *not* scheduled yet.

**Step 3 — Compute the worst-case recovery time** for each primary $T_i^p$. If primary $T_i^p$ fails on processor $P_x$, the backup $T_i^b$ must be ready to execute no later than the deadline of $T_i$, which is equivalent to saying:

**Step 4 — Schedule all backups $\mathcal{B}$ in the slack time** left after primaries. Backups must be scheduled in such a way that if any subset of up to $k$ primaries fail, their backups can still execute before deadline.

**Step 5 — Offline feasibility test:** Verify that the union of primary schedule $\sigma_P$ and backup schedule $\sigma_B$ is feasible, and that for every fault scenario $\mathcal{F}_k$ (containing up to $k$ faults), the deadline of every task is still met.

> [!NOTE]
