```c
    overrun_count = timer_getoverrun(timerid);
    printf("Timer fired! Overruns: %u\n", overrun_count);
}

int main(void) {
    struct sigaction sa;
    struct sigevent sev;
    struct itimerspec its;

    // Step 1: Install signal handler for SIGRTMIN
    sa.sa_flags = SA_SIGINFO;
    sa.sa_sigaction = timer_handler;
    sigemptyset(&sa.sa_mask);
    sigaction(SIGRTMIN, &sa, NULL);

    // Step 2: Create timer that will notify via SIGRTMIN
    sev.sigev_notify = SIGEV_SIGNAL;
    sev.sigev_signo = SIGRTMIN;
    sev.sigev_value.sival_ptr = &timerid;
    timer_create(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &sev, &timerid);

    // Step 3: Arm timer — 500 ms period, 0 initial expiration
    its.it_value.tv_sec = 0;
    its.it_value.tv_nsec = 500000000;   // 500 ms initial
    its.it_interval.tv_sec = 0;
    its.it_interval.tv_nsec = 500000000; // 500 ms periodic

    timer_settime(timerid, 0, &its, NULL);

    // Sleep to let timer fire
    sleep(3);
    timer_delete(timerid);
    return 0;
}
```

**Code Walkthrough:**
- `SA_SIGINFO` flag allows the three-argument handler signature `(int sig, siginfo_t *si, void *uc)`.
- `SIGEV_SIGNAL` notification means the timer fires by sending `SIGRTMIN`.
- `timer_getoverrun()` reports the number of missed expirations if the handler was delayed.
- `CLOCK_MONOTONIC` ensures jitter-free timing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing `sem_timedwait` timeout semantics** — Students often pass a **relative** timeout instead of an **absolute** timestamp. Always add the current time: `clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &ts); ts.tv_nsec += 500000000; if (ts.tv_nsec >= 1000000000) { ts.tv_sec++; ts.tv_nsec -= 1000000000; }`.
> 2. **Forgetting to set `SA_SIGINFO`** — Without this flag, your handler receives only `(int sig)` and you **cannot access** `si_value` or `si_code`.
> 3. **Using `CLOCK_REALTIME` for periodic timers** — Subject to backward jumps, causing incorrect intervals. **Always use `CLOCK_MONOTONIC`**.
> 4. **Not calling `mlockall(MCL_CURRENT | MCL_FUTURE)`** — A page fault during a real-time task causes millisecond-scale jitter, violating deadlines. Real-time processes **must** lock memory.
> 5. **Confusing `SCHED_FIFO` priority semantics** — Higher number = higher priority in POSIX (opposite of some textbooks). Priority 99 is the highest.
> 6. **Forgetting to set the scheduling policy before creating threads** — `pthread_setschedparam()` must be called *after* thread creation if the default policy is `SCHED_OTHER`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for POSIX (Module 3):**

- ✅ **POSIX = IEEE 1003** = portable API standard for Unix-like systems.
- ✅ **POSIX.1b** = real-time extensions (clocks, timers, mqueues, semaphores, mlock, `SCHED_FIFO`/`SCHED_RR`).
- ✅ **POSIX.1c** = pthreads (POSIX threads).
- ✅ **Minimum 32 real-time priority levels** mandated by POSIX.1b (Linux: 1–99).
- ✅ **`CLOCK_MONOTONIC`** is **non-settable, monotonically non-decreasing**; preferred over `CLOCK_REALTIME` for real-time intervals.
- ✅ **Time is stored in `struct timespec`** = `{tv_sec, tv_nsec}` with nanosecond resolution.
- ✅ **Real-time signals** = `SIGRTMIN` to `SIGRTMAX`, **queued (not merged)**, **prioritized (lower number = higher priority)**, support **payload via `sigqueue()`**.
- ✅ **`SCHED_FIFO`** = run-to-completion, no time slicing; **hard real-time**.
- ✅ **`SCHED_RR`** = round-robin with time quantum; **soft real-time**.
- ✅ **`sem_timedwait()`** accepts an **absolute** timeout (not relative).
- ✅ **POSIX mqueues** deliver **highest-priority message first**, FIFO within same priority.
- ✅ **POSIX shared memory** uses `shm_open()` + `mmap()` — **zero-copy** IPC.
- ✅ **`mlockall(MCL_CURRENT | MCL_FUTURE)`** eliminates page-fault jitter in RT processes.
- ✅ **Priority inversion** is mitigated by `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT` on mutexes (not directly on semaphores).
- ✅ **`timer_getoverrun()`** reports missed timer expirations — critical for detecting deadline misses.
- ✅ **RT Linux priority order:** `1 < 60 < 99` (higher number = higher priority).
- ✅ **`SIGEV_SIGNAL` + `SA_SIGINFO`** = standard pattern for async timer-based RT tasks.

> **End of Module 3 — POSIX Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PSOS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** PSOS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of pSOS (PSOS) **pSOS** (pronounced "pea-sauce") is a commercially developed, modular, **real-time operating system (RTOS)** originally created by **Software Components Group (SCG)** in the 1980s, later acquired by **Integrated Systems Inc. (ISI)** and subsequently by **Wind River Systems**. It is engineered specifically for **embedded real-time applications** requiring deterministic response and high reliability. The most common variant studied under the KTU syllabus is **pSOS+**, which represents the modular microkernel component. The full pSOS family consists of tightly integrated, but functionally separated, subsystems: | Subsystem | Function | |-----------|----------| | **pSOS+** | Microkernel (task, memory, time, interrupt management) | | **pNA+** | Network Manager (TCP/IP stack) | | **pHILE+** | File System Manager | | **pREPC+** | Multiprocessor / RPC Manager | | **pRISM+** | Integrated Development Environment | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** pSOS is grouped under **Commercial / Off-the-Shelf Hard Real-Time Operating Systems**. It is treated in Module 3 alongside other RTOS architectures like VxWorks, QNX, and RT-Linux. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Air-Traffic Controller" Imagine an **Air-Traffic Control Tower** managing dozens of aircraft simultaneously: - Each **aircraft** = a **task** with its own flight plan, priority, and deadline. - The **controller** = the **pSOS+ microkernel**, which decides *exactly* which plane gets clearance *next*, without ambiguity. - The **ground crew, refuelers, caterers** = peripheral modules like **pHILE+** (file system) and **pNA+** (network). - The **radar system** = the **interrupt subsystem**, immediately detecting and signalling urgent events (e.g., emergencies). Just as the controller must respond deterministically (a delayed clearance could crash a plane), pSOS guarantees bounded response times so that no critical task misses its deadline. ### 1.3 Key Design Philosophy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Microkernel Architecture**: All essential services (task scheduling, inter-task communication, memory management, interrupt handling) reside in a small, fast, protected kernel. Non-essential services (drivers, network stacks, file systems) execute as user-level tasks. This makes pSOS both **predictable** and **modular**. Key distinguishing properties: - **Preemptive, priority-based scheduling** (default), with optional round-robin - **Fixed-priority scheduling** support (Rate Monotonic / Deadline Monotonic semantics) - **Deterministic interrupt latency** (claimed in the order of microseconds on embedded hardware) - **Tightly bounded context-switch overhead** - **Scalable** from deeply embedded 8/16-bit controllers to 32-bit multiprocessor systems - **POSIX-compliant subset** (later versions: pSOSystem/pSOS) - **ROMable** (kernel can reside in ROM/Flash) > [!TIP] > For KTU exams, always mention that **pSOS+ is ROMable** and uses a **microkernel architecture** — these two phrases alone secure easy marks in definition questions. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Layered Architecture of the pSOS Family The pSOS system follows a strict **layered modular architecture**: ``` +-------------------------------------------------------+ | APPLICATION TASKS | +-------------------------------------------------------+ | pHILE+ | pNA+ | pREPC+ | USER DRIVERS | | (File Sys) | (Network)| (RPC/Multi)| | +-------------------------------------------------------+ | pSOS+ MICROKERNEL | | Scheduler | Memory | Timer | Interrupts | IPC | I/O | +-------------------------------------------------------+ | HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER (BSP) | +-------------------------------------------------------+ | PHYSICAL HARDWARE | +-------------------------------------------------------+ ``` The **microkernel (pSOS+)** itself is built around four core objects: 1. **Tasks** — basic units of execution 2. **Memory Partitions** — fixed-size blocks for predictable allocation 3. **Queues & Messages** — for inter-task communication 4. **Semaphores & Events** — for synchronization ### 2.2 Task States in pSOS+ A task in pSOS+ traverses a finite set of well-defined states: | State | Description | |-------|-------------| | **Ready** | Eligible to run; waiting in the scheduler's ready queue | | **Running** | Currently executing on the CPU | | **Suspended** | Blocked waiting for an event/resource | | **Asleep** | Delayed (waiting for timer to expire) | | **Terminated** | Task has finished or been killed | Transitions occur due to: kernel API calls, timeouts, interrupts, or higher-priority preemption. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse "Suspended" with the same name used in μC/OS-II. In pSOS+, **Suspended** means *blocked on a resource/event*, not suspended-time. The time-delayed state is called **Asleep**. ### 2.3 Scheduling Algorithms Supported pSOS+ natively supports: - **Preemptive Priority Scheduling (PPS)** — the default and most common for hard real-time systems - **Time-Slice (Round-Robin)** scheduling among tasks of *equal* priority - **Hybrid** — priority-based with optional time-slicing within a priority level The scheduler uses a **bitmap-priority queue** for $O(1)$ selection of the highest-priority ready task. Given $N$ priority levels, the latency of selecting the next task is bounded and independent of the number of tasks. ### 2.4 Memory Management Model pSOS+ provides two memory primitives: 1. **Fixed-Size Memory Partitions** — used in real-time contexts because allocation/deallocation time is **deterministic** ($O(1)$). 2. **Variable-Size Memory Regions** — used less frequently, useful for dynamic data structures when fragmentation is acceptable. > [!NOTE] > **Deterministic Allocation Principle:** Because allocation/deallocation in fixed partitions takes constant time, pSOS is suitable for hard real-time systems where worst-case memory acquisition time must be bounded. ### 2.5 Inter-Task Communication (IPC) pSOS+ provides: | Mechanism | Use Case | |-----------|----------| | **Semaphores** (counting) | Mutual exclusion, resource locking | | **Events** (16-bit flags per task) | Task-level signalling | | **Message Queues** (variable-length) | Structured data exchange | | **Asynchronous Signals** | Interrupt-to-task notification | ### 2.6 Interrupt Handling pSOS+ supports nested interrupts and uses a two-level interrupt structure: - **ISRs** (Interrupt Service Routines) — run at interrupt level, kernel services restricted - **ISTs** (Interrupt Service Threads) — user-defined tasks triggered by ASR/Asynchronous Service Routines that perform the deferred work, allowing the ISR itself to be ultra-short. ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula & Constants Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Below are the **critical formulas, constants, and bounds** that KTU examiners expect for any pSOS numerical problem. Master these. | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes / Units | |---------|--------------------|---------------| | Task selection time (priority queue) | $T_{select} = O(1)$ | Bitmap-indexed, **microseconds** | | Context switch time | $T_{cs}$ | Typically $5$–$20\ \mu s$ (board-dependent) | | Interrupt latency | $T_{lat} = T_{max\ ISR}$ | Bounded by max ISR execution | | Fixed partition allocation time | $T_{alloc} = c$ (constant) | Independent of partition size | | Memory waste (internal fragmentation) | $W = B_{part} - \overline{S}_{obj}$ | Per partition, $B_{part}$ = block size | | Priority inversion (unbounded case) | $T_{delay} \le T_{lowest\ critical}$ | Preventable via PIP/PCP | | CPU utilization (Rate Monotonic) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $C_i$ = computation, $T_i$ = period | | Liu & Layland bound | $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Necessary but not sufficient for RM schedulability | | Response time of task $\tau_i$ | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{\tau_j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Smallest fixed-point solution | | Priority inheritance duration (bounded) | $T_{inherit} \le T_{cs\ low} \cdot k$ | $k$ = number of priority promotions | > [!WARNING] > When writing absolute values in the LaTeX equations above, **never use the vertical pipe `|` inside a markdown table** — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead to avoid breaking the table parser. KTU answer sheets use similar rendering engines. ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace & Defence:** Avionics, flight-control computers, missile guidance - **Telecommunications:** Routers, base stations, switches (where deterministic latency is critical) - **Medical Devices:** Patient monitors, infusion pumps, imaging systems - **Industrial Automation:** PLC controllers, robotic assembly lines - **Automotive:** Engine control units (ECUs), braking systems (early versions) --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section walks through three classical KTU numerical/analytical derivations that examiners love to pose on pSOS scheduling. ### 3.1 Derivation: Response-Time Analysis for a pSOS-Scheduled Task Set **Given:** A task set $\tau = \{\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3\}$ scheduled by **fixed priority (RM mapping)** on pSOS+: | Task | Period $T_i$ | WCET $C_i$ | |------|-------------|-----------| | $\tau_1$ | $20$ | $4$ | | $\tau_2$ | $50$ | $8$ | | $\tau_3$ | $100$ | $10$ | Assume deadline equals period ($D_i = T_i$). Determine the response time $R_i$ of each task using the iterative formula: $$ R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{\tau_j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j $$ where $hp(i)$ is the set of tasks with priority higher than $\tau_i$. #### Step 1 — Compute $R_1$ (highest priority) $\tau_1$ has no higher-priority tasks, so: $$ R_1 = C_1 = 4 $$ Initial check: $R_1 = 4 \le T_1 = 20$. ✅ Task $\tau_1$ is schedulable. #### Step 2 — Compute $R_2$ Higher-priority tasks: only $\tau_1$. Initial guess $R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 8$. **Iteration 1:** $$ R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 8 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 8 + (1)(4) = 12 $$ Wait — $\lceil 8/20 \rceil = 1$ because $8 \le 20$. Actually the **floor** operation applies (number of completed periods of $\tau_1$ before $R_2$). The convention is: $$ \left\lceil \frac{8}{20} \right\rceil = 1 $$ So $R_2^{(1)} = 8 + 1 \cdot 4 = 12$. **Iteration 2:** $$ R_2^{(2)} = 8 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 8 + (1)(4) = 12 $$ Fixed-point reached. So $R_2 = 12 \le T_2 = 50$. ✅ Task $\tau_2$ is schedulable. #### Step 3 — Compute $R_3$ Higher-priority tasks: $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$. Initial guess $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 10$. **Iteration 1:** $$ R_3^{(1)} = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 8 = 10 + (1)(4) + (1)(8) = 22 $$ **Iteration 2:** $$ R_3^{(2)} = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{22}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{22}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 8 = 10 + (2)(4) + (1)(8) = 26 $$ **Iteration 3:** $$ R_3^{(3)} = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{26}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 + \left\lceil \frac{26}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 8 = 10 + (2)(4) + (1)(8) = 26 $$ Fixed-point reached. So $R_3 = 26 \le T_3 = 100$. ✅ Task $\tau_3$ is schedulable. **Conclusion:** All tasks meet their deadlines — the task set is **schedulable** under pSOS+ fixed-priority scheduling. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Utilization Bound vs Liu-Layland Limit Compute the total CPU utilization: $$ U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{4}{20} + \frac{8}{50} + \frac{10}{100} $$ $$ U = 0.200 + 0.160 + 0.100 = 0.460 $$ $$ U = 46\% $$ Liu & Layland sufficient bound for $n = 3$: $$ U_{LL}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798 \approx 78\% $$ Since $U = 0.460 \le 0.7798$, the test is **passed** — but this is *sufficient*, not *necessary*. The exact response-time test (Section 3.1) is the **necessary and sufficient** condition. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Students often quote Liu-Layland and forget to state that it is **sufficient but not necessary**. Always add the phrase "*sufficient condition, exact test requires response-time analysis*". --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Bounded Priority Inversion under pSOS+ **Setup:** Suppose pSOS+ uses **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** for mutex-style semaphores. A low-priority task $\tau_L$ holds a resource needed by $\tau_H$. Compute the maximum blocking time on $\tau_H$. Let: - $C_L$ = WCET of $\tau_L$ = $30$ - $T_L$ = Period of $\tau_L$ = $100$ - $C_H$ = WCET of $\tau_H$ = $5$ - One critical section of $\tau_L$ accesses the shared resource for $d_{crit} = 8$ time units. **Without PIP:** $\tau_H$ can be blocked for the *entire* duration of $\tau_L$, i.e., up to $C_L = 30$ time units. **With PIP:** $\tau_H$ can only be blocked for the **duration of the critical section of $\tau_L$**, since $\tau_L$ inherits $\tau_H$'s priority while inside the critical section and therefore cannot be preempted by intermediate-priority tasks. Therefore: $$ B_{max}(\tau_H) = d_{crit} = 8\ \text{time units} $$ Including this in the response-time formula: $$ R_H = C_H + B_{max} + \sum_{\tau_j \in hp(H)} \left\lceil \frac{R_H}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** pSOS+ documentation mentions support for **Priority Inheritance** on its mutex semaphores. This is one of the most-asked topics — be ready to compute the blocking bound and contrast it with the unbounded case. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of pSOS+ with Other RTOS (often asked) | Feature | **pSOS+** | VxWorks | RT-Linux | QNX | |---------|-----------|---------|----------|-----| | Kernel type | Microkernel | Monolithic (with modules) | Hybrid | Microkernel | | Scheduler | Fixed priority + RR | Priority + RR | Linux CFS + PREEMPT_RT | Priority + FIFO/RR | | Memory model | Fixed partitions | Partition + page | Virtual memory (paged) | Protected address space | | POSIX-compliant | Partial | Yes | Yes (mostly) | Yes | | IPC | Queues, events, semaphores | Pipes, sockets, semaphores | Standard Linux IPC | Msg passing (native) | | Footprint | Small–Medium | Small–Medium | Large | Small–Medium | | Hard real-time | Yes | Yes | Soft (configurable for hard) | Yes | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 pSOS+ Kernel Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP[APPLICATION LAYER] T1[Task 1 - High Priority] T2[Task 2 - Med Priority] T3[Task 3 - Low Priority] end subgraph SVC[SYSTEM SERVICES] SEM[Semaphores] EVT[Event Flags] Q[Message Queues] MEM[Memory Partitions] TMR[Timer Manager] end subgraph KRN[pSOS+ MICROKERNEL] SCH[Priority Scheduler - Bitmap indexed] CCTX[Context Switch Engine] IPC[IPC Dispatcher] INT[Interrupt Controller] end subgraph HW[HARDWARE ABSTRACTION - BSP] BSP1[CPU Drivers] BSP2[Peripheral Drivers] BSP3[Board Initialization] end T1 --> SCH T2 --> SCH T3 --> SCH SCH --> CCTX SEM --> IPC EVT --> IPC Q --> IPC IPC --> CCTX TMR --> INT INT --> CCTX CCTX --> BSP1 CCTX --> BSP2 BSP1 --> BSP3 BSP2 --> BSP3 ``` ### 4.2 Task State Transition Diagram in pSOS+ ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Ready: Task Created Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatches Running --> Ready: Preempted by Higher Priority Running --> Suspended: Blocked on Semaphore or Queue Suspended --> Ready: Resource Available or Event Set Running --> Asleep: Sleep or Delay Called Asleep --> Ready: Timer Expired Running --> [*: Task Deleted Suspended --> [*: Task Deleted Ready --> [*: Task Deleted ``` ### 4.3 Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant H as Task H - Priority 8 participant M as Task M - Priority 5 participant L as Task L - Priority 2 participant R as Shared Resource R Note over L: L acquires R and enters critical section L->>R: Lock Resource R Note over L: L inherits H's priority 8 H->>H: Requests R - blocks Note over H: H is now BLOCKED M->>M: Tries to run - preempts L?
NO because L is at priority 8 Note over L: L continues critical section at priority 8 L->>R: Release Resource R Note over L: L reverts to priority 2 Note over H: H is unblocked and runs at priority 8 H->>R: Lock Resource R H->>H: Execute Critical Section H->>R: Release Resource R ``` ### 4.4 Memory Partition Allocation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Application Request: malloc 60 bytes] --> B{Partition 1: 64 bytes?} B -- YES --> C[Allocate from Partition 1] B -- NO --> D{Partition 2: 128 bytes?} D -- YES --> E[Allocate from Partition 2] D -- NO --> F{Partition 3: 256 bytes?} F -- YES --> G[Allocate from Partition 3] F -- NO --> H[Allocation Fails - Return NULL] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Rationale:** Because pSOS+ uses *fixed-size partitions*, allocation is $O(1)$ — the kernel simply hands out the first free block from the matching partition. There is no fragmentation, no best-fit search, no paging overhead. This is exactly why pSOS+ is classified as deterministic. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** What is **pSOS+**? List its **four core objects** managed by the microkernel. **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** > **pSOS+** is the microkernel component of the pSOS real-time operating system family. It provides a small, fast, ROMable kernel suitable for embedded hard real-time systems. > > The **four core objects** managed by pSOS+ are: > > 1. **Tasks** — independent threads of execution > 2. **Memory Partitions** — fixed-size blocks for deterministic allocation > 3. **Queues & Messages** — variable-length inter-task messages > 4. **Semaphores & Events** — for synchronization and signalling (Word-limit: 4–6 lines. Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for one-line explanation of each.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** Differentiate between **Suspended** and **Asleep** states of a task in pSOS+. **Model Answer (for 3 marks):** > | Aspect | Suspended | Asleep | > |--------|-----------|--------| > | Reason | Task is blocked waiting for an *event* or *resource* (semaphore, queue, event-flag) | Task explicitly called a *time-delay* primitive (e.g., `t_sleep`) | > | Wake-up Trigger | Another task, ISR, or the event itself | Timer expiration | > | Predictability | Depends on external producer | Fully deterministic (timer-driven) | > | Typical Use | IPC, mutual exclusion | Periodic polling, debouncing, throttling | > > Both states place the task in a non-ready condition, preventing it from being scheduled. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — pSOS Architecture Deep Dive **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Describe the **layered architecture of the pSOS family** with the help of a neat diagram. Mention the function of each subsystem. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain **task scheduling in pSOS+** with reference to (i) supported scheduling algorithms, (ii) priority queue implementation, and (iii) how response time is bounded. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Layered Architecture (7 marks):** The pSOS system follows a strict 4-layer architecture, from hardware upward: **Layer 1 — Hardware:** The physical CPU, memory, and peripherals (e.g., Motorola 68xxx, Intel x86, MIPS, ARM). **Layer 2 — Board Support Package (BSP):** Hardware Abstraction Layer containing CPU-specific initialization code, peripheral drivers, and interrupt vector installation. Provides a uniform API to the kernel above. **Layer 3 — pSOS+ Microkernel:** The smallest, most privileged layer implementing: - Task scheduler (priority-based) - Task creation, deletion, suspension, resumption - Memory partition manager - Message queues - Semaphores, events - Timer manager - Interrupt dispatch **Layer 4 — System Services and Application:** - **pHILE+** — file system manager (MS-DOS-compatible, NFS, RAW) - **pNA+** — TCP/IP networking - **pREPC+** — RPC/multiprocessor support - Application tasks (user code) (Examiner's note: award 1 mark each for correctly describing BSP, kernel services, system modules, and the layering principle. 3 marks for diagram.) **(b) Task Scheduling (7 marks):** **(i) Supported algorithms (2 marks):** - **Preemptive, fixed-priority scheduling** (default) - **Time-slice (round-robin)** among equal-priority tasks - **Hybrid** — priority with optional time-slicing inside a level **(ii) Priority queue implementation (3 marks):** The scheduler maintains a **bitmap-indexed priority queue**. With $N$ priority levels, a bitmap of $N$ bits tracks which priorities have ready tasks. To select the next task, the kernel performs a *find-first-set* instruction, which is implemented in a single CPU cycle on most processors. Hence: $$ T_{select} = O(1) $$ This guarantees **bounded selection latency** independent of the number of tasks, satisfying hard real-time constraints. **(iii) Response-time bounding (2 marks):** The response time of the highest-priority task in a busy period satisfies: $$ R_i = C_i + \sum_{\tau_j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j + B_i $$ where $B_i$ is the worst-case blocking from lower-priority tasks via shared resources. The fixed-point of this equation is found iteratively, and the system is schedulable iff $R_i \le D_i$ for every task. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — IPC, Memory & Priority Inversion **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Discuss the **inter-task communication mechanisms** in pSOS+. Compare message queues and events. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain **priority inversion** in the context of pSOS+. Describe how the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** is supported, and derive the **maximum blocking time** for a high-priority task under PIP. Use the following data: Task H (priority 10, $C_H = 4$), Task M (priority 5, $C_M = 10$), Task L (priority 1, $C_L = 30$, with one critical section of duration $d_{crit} = 7$). *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) IPC Mechanisms (7 marks):** pSOS+ provides four primary IPC mechanisms: 1. **Semaphores (counting):** Used for mutual exclusion and resource counting. Supports `sm_create`, `sm_p` (wait), `sm_v` (signal). Supports **priority inheritance** to mitigate priority inversion. 2. **Events (16-bit flags):** Each task has an associated 16-bit event register. Tasks can `ev_receive` on a mask of bits. Lightweight, useful for synchronization without data transfer. 3. **Message Queues:** Variable-length messages passed between tasks via `q_receive` / `q_send`. Support both FIFO and priority-based delivery. 4. **Asynchronous Signals:** Allow ISRs to wake or notify a specific task without busy-waiting. **Comparison — Message Queues vs Events (2 marks):** | Property | Message Queues | Events | |----------|----------------|--------| | Data transfer | Yes (variable payload) | No (16-bit flag only) | | Overhead | Higher | Minimal | | Use case | Producer–consumer with data | Synchronization, signalling | | Blocking semantics | Wait for queue non-empty | Wait for mask match | | Multiple consumers | Yes | One task per event register | **(b) Priority Inversion & PIP (7 marks):** **Problem statement (2 marks):** Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority task $\tau_H$ is forced to wait for a lower-priority task $\tau_L$ to release a shared resource, while one or more intermediate-priority tasks $\tau_M$ preempt $\tau_L$. The blocking time of $\tau_H$ becomes unbounded, depending on the duration of $\tau_M$'s execution — a fatal scenario in hard real-time systems. **Priority Inheritance Protocol (3 marks):** Under PIP: - When $\tau_L$ acquires a resource that $\tau_H$ needs, $\tau_L$'s priority is **temporarily raised** to $\tau_H$'s priority. - $\tau_M$ can no longer preempt $\tau_L$ because $\tau_L$ now runs at $\tau_H$'s priority. - $\tau_L$ releases the resource and reverts to its original priority. - $\tau_H$ is then unblocked and runs. **Bounding the blocking time (2 marks):** The maximum blocking time of $\tau_H$ under PIP equals the duration of **one critical section of $\tau_L$**: $$ B_{max}(\tau_H) = d_{crit} = 7\ \text{time units} $$ Compare this with the *unbounded* case where $B_{max}$ could be as large as $C_L = 30$ time units — a 4× improvement in worst-case determinism. The updated response time of $\tau_H$: $$ R_H = C_H + B_{max} + \text{interference from higher-priority tasks} $$ Since no task has higher priority than $\tau_H$, there is no interference, and: $$ R_H = 4 + 7 = 11\ \text{time units} $$ This is the **bounded, deterministic** response — a hallmark of pSOS+ design. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones for pSOS Questions:** > > 1. **Misnaming the state.** Writing "blocked" instead of "suspended" loses ½ mark. Always use the exact pSOS+ terminology: **Ready, Running, Suspended, Asleep, Terminated**. > > 2. **Confusing pSOS+ with pSOSystem.** pSOS+ is the *kernel*. pSOSystem is the *integrated package* containing pSOS+, pNA+, pHILE+, pREPC+. Many students interchange them — examiners deduct marks. > > 3. **Forgetting priority inheritance.** Whenever asked about mutex/semaphore behaviour, **always** mention Priority Inheritance. Skipping this is the most common single mark-loss reason. > > 4. **Confusing Liu-Layland sufficiency with necessity.** $U \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$ is *sufficient only*. Always state the response-time test is the *necessary-and-sufficient* condition. > > 5. **Omitting BSP.** The Board Support Package is *part of the architecture*. A diagram without BSP is incomplete. > > 6. **Pipes inside markdown tables.** LaTeX with `|x|` breaks the markdown parser. Always use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` in tables. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist for PSOS:** > > - **Definition:** pSOS+ is a microkernel-based RTOS for embedded systems, ROMable and deterministic. > - **Family members:** pSOS+ (kernel), pNA+ (network), pHILE+ (file system), pREPC+ (RPC), pRISM+ (IDE). > - **Architecture:** Layered — Hardware → BSP → pSOS+ microkernel → System services + Apps. > - **4 core kernel objects:** Tasks, Memory partitions, Queues/Messages, Semaphores/Events. > - **Task states:** Ready, Running, Suspended, Asleep, Terminated. > - **Scheduler:** Preemptive fixed-priority (default), Round-Robin among equal-priority, bitmap-indexed → $O(1)$ selection. > - **Memory model:** Fixed-size partitions (deterministic) + variable-size regions (when needed). > - **IPC:** Semaphores (with priority inheritance), Event flags (16-bit), Message queues (variable-length), Async signals. > - **Interrupts:** Two-level — short ISR + deferred IST; nested interrupts supported. > - **Priority inversion mitigation:** Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) supported on mutex semaphores. > - **Blocking bound under PIP:** $B_{max}(\tau_H) = $ duration of *one* critical section of the lowest-priority contending task. > - **Response time formula:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil R_i / T_j \right\rceil C_j + B_i$. > - **Liu-Layland bound:** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — sufficient but not necessary. > - **Determinism guarantee:** Allocation $O(1)$, scheduler $O(1)$, bounded interrupt latency. > - **Applications:** Aerospace, telecom, medical, industrial automation, automotive. > - **Comparison edge:** Microkernel (pSOS+, QNX) vs Monolithic (VxWorks) — know this for "compare & contrast" questions. > - **Compliance:** Later versions partially POSIX-compliant. > - **Vendors:** Originally SCG → ISI → Wind River Systems. ---

topics.title: VRTX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** VRTX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**VRTX** (Versatile Real-Time eXecutive) is a **commercial, preemptive, priority-based real-time operating system kernel** originally developed by **Ready System (1981)** and later maintained by **Microtec Research, Mentor Graphics, and Siemens EDA**. It is engineered for **embedded systems** where deterministic response to external events within strict timing deadlines is non-negotiable.

In strict KTU 2024 terminology, VRTX is classified as a **hard real-time executive** providing:
- Preemptive, priority-driven task scheduling
- Deterministic interrupt latency
- Inter-task communication primitives (semaphores, mailboxes, queues, events)
- Memory management with fixed/block partitioning
- Support for up to **256 task priority levels** (0 being highest)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** VRTX is a flagship topic in Module 3 (Real-Time Operating Systems). Students must be able to compare VRTX with μC/OS-II and describe its kernel architecture, scheduling policy, and IPC mechanisms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **hospital emergency ward** operating 24/7:
- **Patients** = Tasks (each with a different illness/urgency)
- **Triage Nurse** = The VRTX Scheduler (decides who gets the doctor next)
- **Doctors** = CPU execution time (only one critical resource)
- **ICU Beds** = Memory blocks (limited, must be allocated fairly)
- **Intercom System** = Mailboxes/Semaphores (doctors and nurses coordinate)
- **Stat alarms** = Hardware Interrupts (must be answered immediately)

Just as the most critical patient (heart attack) is treated **before** a patient with a mild fever, VRTX always runs the **highest-priority READY task**. The "versatile" part means the same kernel can scale from a tiny 8-bit microcontroller to a 32-bit DSP — it adapts like a swiss army knife for embedded developers.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Priority Levels** | **256 (0–255)** | 0 = highest urgency |
| **Interrupt Latency** | **< 10 µs** (typical) | Time from IRQ to first instruction |
| **Context Switch Time** | **< 20 µs** | Saving/restoring task state |
| **Kernel ROM Footprint** | **~4–8 KB** | Smallest config |
| **Max Tasks (configurable)** | **Up to 256** | Scalable |

> [!NOTE]
> VRTX is **POSIX 1003.1 compliant** in its later versions (VRTXsa, VRTXmc), making it portable across processors like ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, x86, and various DSPs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 VRTX Kernel Architecture

VRTX follows a **microkernel-style modular architecture**, with the core scheduler and dispatcher running in privileged/kernel mode while user tasks run in protected user mode.

**Core Components:**

1. **Task Control Block (TCB):** Each task has a TCB storing:
   - Task ID
   - Current priority
   - Stack pointer
   - Task state (READY, RUNNING, PENDED, DORMANT, DELAYED)
   - Register save area
   - Event flags
   - Timeout counters

2. **Scheduler:** Implements **fixed-priority preemptive scheduling** with optional **round-robin** tie-breaking for tasks of equal priority.

3. **Dispatcher:** Performs the actual context switch (saves current TCB, loads new TCB, restores CPU registers).

4. **Interrupt Handler:** Minimal latency — defers non-critical work via deferred procedure calls (DPCs).

5. **IPC Primitives:** Semaphores, Mailboxes, Queues, Events, Partitions.

6. **Memory Manager:** Fixed-size block partition allocator (no fragmentation).

### 2.2 Task States in VRTX

VRTX recognizes **5 canonical task states**:

- **DORMANT:** Task created but not yet started (akin to "not born yet").
- **READY:** Eligible to run, waiting for CPU.
- **RUNNING:** Currently executing on the CPU.
- **PENDED:** Blocked, waiting on a resource (semaphore, mailbox, event).
- **DELAYED:** Sleeping for a specified tick count (self-suspension).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The state transition `READY → RUNNING → PENDED` on `SC_PEND` and `PENDED → READY` on `SC_POST` is the **most frequently asked** VRTX concept in KTU examinations.

### 2.3 Scheduling Algorithm

VRTX uses a **fixed-priority preemptive scheduler** with the following rules:

1. Always select the **highest-priority READY** task.
2. On `SC_POST` (resource freed) or interrupt completion, re-evaluate.
3. If two tasks share priority, **round-robin** (time-sliced) execution applies (optional, configurable).
4. The **running task** is preempted only when a higher-priority task becomes READY.

**Priority Inversion Problem:** VRTX addresses this via the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** in advanced versions (VRTXsa).

### 2.4 Inter-Task Communication (IPC) Primitives

| Primitive | System Call | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Semaphore** | `SC_PEND`, `SC_POST` | Mutual exclusion, resource signaling |
| **Mailbox** | `SC_QPOST`, `SC_QPEND` | 1-word message passing |
| **Queue** | `SC_QPOST`, `SC_QACCEPT` | Multi-message FIFO buffer |
| **Event Flags** | `SC_EPOST`, `SC_EWAIT` | Task synchronization via bit-masks |
| **Partition** | `SC_GPART`, `SC_RPART` | Fixed-size memory block allocation |

> [!NOTE]
> All IPC primitives in VRTX are **counting semaphores** by default and support **timeout-based blocking** (e.g., `pend with 100 ticks`).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** All pipe symbols in equations are written as `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption.

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Response Time** | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Worst-case response time analysis |
| **CPU Utilization Bound (Rate Monotonic)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Schedulability test for n tasks |
| **Context Switch Time** | $T_{cs} = 2 \cdot T_{reg\_save} + T_{sched} + T_{cache\_flush}$ | System overhead calculation |
| **Interrupt Latency** | $T_{lat} = T_{irq\_detect} + T_{kernel\_entry} + T_{save\_ctx}$ | Worst-case interrupt response |
| **Semaphore Wait Time (PIP)** | $T_{wait}^{PIP} \leq \sum_{k \in hp(i)} C_k$ | Bounded priority inversion |
| **Effective Priority (Inheritance)** | $P_{eff}(T) = \max(P_{T}, P_{H})$ | Boosted task priority |
| **Deadline Monotonic Ordering** | $D_i \leq D_j \implies P_i \geq P_j$ | Static priority assignment |
| **Memory Partition Size** | $S_{partition} = N_{blocks} \cdot S_{block} + O(hdr)$ | RAM requirement calculation |
| **Round-Robin Quantum** | $Q = \dfrac{T_{tick}}{f_{tick\_rate}}$ | Time-slice length |
| **Task Wake-up Jitter** | $J = \max \vert T_{wake} - T_{expected} \vert$ | Real-time jitter specification |

**Explanation of Key Equations:**

- **Response Time $R_i$** for task $i$ sums its own computation time $C_i$ plus the preemption overhead from all higher-priority tasks. The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ counts how many full periods of a higher-priority task $j$ can fit within $R_i$.
- **Utilization Bound $U$** for Rate Monotonic Scheduling (Liu & Layland, 1973). For $n \to \infty$, the bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- **Priority Inheritance:** When task $T$ (low priority) holds a resource needed by task $H$ (high priority), $T$ inherits $P_H$ temporarily, preventing medium-priority tasks from preempting it.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

VRTX powers mission-critical systems in:
- **Aerospace & Defense:** Avionics flight control (e.g., F-16, F-18)
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemakers, MRI controllers
- **Telecommunications:** Base station routers, DSLAMs
- **Industrial Automation:** PLCs, robotic arms
- **Automotive:** Engine control units (ECUs), ABS modules
- **Consumer Electronics:** Set-top boxes, PDAs (early Palm OS used a VRTX derivative)

> [!NOTE]
> **VRTX vs. μC/OS-II (a classic KTU comparison question):**
>
> | Feature | VRTX | μC/OS-II |
> |---|---|---|
> | Source | Commercial (licensed) | Open-source (educational use) |
> | Priority Levels | 256 | 64 |
> | Max Tasks | 256 | 255 (in v2.x) |
> | Scheduling | Preemptive + Round-robin | Preemptive only |
> | IPC | Sem, Mbox, Queue, Event, Part | Sem, Mbox, Queue, Flag, Mutex |
> | Memory Mgmt | Fixed partitions | Fixed partitions |
> | POSIX | Yes (VRTXsa) | Partial |
> | Footprint | ~4–8 KB | ~3–6 KB |
> | Cost | $$$$ (commercial) | Free |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Response Time for a VRTX Task

Consider a task set $\tau = \{\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3\}$ scheduled under **fixed-priority preemptive** VRTX scheduling. Task $\tau_3$ has the lowest priority and is being analyzed.

**Given:**
- $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 1$, $T_1 = 4$, $D_1 = 4$
- $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 2$, $T_2 = 6$, $D_2 = 6$
- $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 3$, $T_3 = 12$, $D_3 = 12$

Higher-priority tasks (interfering with $\tau_3$): $\tau_1, \tau_2$

**Step 1:** Initialize the response time estimate with the task's own computation time.

$$
R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the interference from higher-priority tasks in the first iteration.

$$
R_3^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2
$$

Substitute values:

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \lceil 0.75 \rceil \cdot 1 + \lceil 0.5 \rceil \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 3 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 6
$$

**Step 3:** Iterate with the updated $R_3$.

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \lceil 1.5 \rceil + \lceil 1.0 \rceil \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + 2 + 1 \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 7
$$

**Step 4:** Next iteration.

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \dfrac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \dfrac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + 2 + 2
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 7
$$

**Step 5:** The iteration has converged at $R_3 = 7$. Since $R_3 = 7 \leq D_3 = 12$, **task $\tau_3$ is schedulable** under VRTX.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Termination condition:** Iteration stops when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (converged) or $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (unschedulable). If $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ at any step, the task is **guaranteed to miss its deadline**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound (Liu-Layland)

For $n$ periodic tasks under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling** (shorter period = higher priority — VRTX's default mapping):

**Step 1:** Define utilization.

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

**Step 2:** Liu & Layland's bound.

$$
U \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 3:** Limiting case.

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} n (2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$

**Step 4:** Numerical values for typical $n$:

| n | $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.000 | Single task always meets deadline |
| 2 | 0.828 | 2 tasks: ≤ 82.8% utilization |
| 3 | 0.780 | 3 tasks: ≤ 78.0% |
| 4 | 0.757 | 4 tasks: ≤ 75.7% |
| 5 | 0.743 | 5 tasks: ≤ 74.3% |
| ∞ | 0.693 | Asymptotic floor |

> [!NOTE]
> This bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. Tasks can still be schedulable even when $U > n(2^{1/n}-1)$ — this is called the **"schedulable but not RMS-bounded"** case, testable via Response Time Analysis (Section 3.1).

### 3.3 Derivation: Priority Inheritance — Bounding Inversion

**Scenario:** Task $L$ (low priority, P=10) holds a mutex needed by task $H$ (high priority, P=2). Task $M$ (medium priority, P=5) is also active.

**Without Inheritance:**
- $H$ blocks on mutex
- $M$ preempts $L$ (since P=5 > P=10)
- $L$ delayed indefinitely → **unbounded priority inversion**

**With PIP (VRTXsa):**
- $H$ blocks on mutex
- $L$ inherits $P_{eff} = \max(P_L, P_H) = 2$
- $M$ cannot preempt $L$ (P=5 < 2? No, P=2 wins since lower number = higher priority)
- $L$ finishes critical section, releases mutex, $P_{eff}$ restored to 10
- $H$ acquires mutex, runs to completion

**Bounding:** Under PIP, the blocking time $B_i$ for task $i$ is bounded by:

$$
B_i \leq \max_{k \in resources(i)} \left( C_{cs,k}^{L_k} \right)
$$

where $C_{cs,k}^{L_k}$ is the critical section length of the lowest-priority task that holds resource $k$.

**Extended formula (with chained blocking):**

$$
B_i \leq \sum_{k=1}^{m} \text{(number of times task } i \text{ accesses resource } k \text{)} \times C_{cs,k}^{\max}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: VRTX Scheduling Decision Table

| Condition | Action by VRTX Scheduler |
|---|---|
| Higher-priority task becomes READY | Preempt current; save TCB; dispatch higher-priority |
| Current task calls `SC_PEND` (resource unavailable) | Move task to PENDED state; run next highest-priority READY |
| `SC_POST` releases resource | Wake PENDED task(s); if higher priority than current, preempt |
| `SC_DELAY(n)` called | Move task to DELAYED state; decrement tick counter |
| Tick interrupt fires | Decrement DELAYED timers; re-evaluate priority queue |
| ISR completes | Re-evaluate scheduler; potentially dispatch new task |
| `SC_SUSPEND` called | Task becomes DORMANT (or special suspend state) |
| `SC_RESUME` called on DORMANT task | Move to READY |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 VRTX Kernel Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Tasks User Mode] --> B[VRTX System Calls API]
    B --> C[Kernel Supervisor Mode]
    C --> D[Task Scheduler]
    C --> E[Dispatcher Context Switch]
    C --> F[IPC Manager Sem Mailbox Queue Event]
    C --> G[Memory Manager Partitions]
    C --> H[Interrupt Handler]
    H --> I[Hardware Peripherals]
    D --> J[Ready Queue Priority Ordered]
    E --> K[CPU Core]
    F --> L[Resource Tables]
    G --> M[Memory Pool RAM]
    H --> N[Vector Table]
    J --> D
    L --> F
    M --> G
    N --> H
```

### 4.2 VRTX Task State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DORMANT: SC_CTCB create
    DORMANT --> READY: SC_START
    READY --> RUNNING: Dispatcher selects
    RUNNING --> READY: Preempted by higher priority
    RUNNING --> PENDED: SC_PEND resource unavailable
    RUNNING --> DELAYED: SC_DELAY n ticks
    RUNNING --> DORMANT: SC_STOP or termination
    PENDED --> READY: SC_POST or timeout
    PENDED --> RUNNING: Direct resume higher priority
    DELAYED --> READY: Timer expires
    DELAYED --> PENDED: SC_PEND during delay
```

### 4.3 VRTX Context Switch Flow (Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Tick as Tick Interrupt
    participant ISR as ISR Handler
    participant Sched as Scheduler
    participant Disp as Dispatcher
    participant Old as Old Task TCB
    participant New as New Task TCB
    participant CPU

    Tick->>ISR: IRQ fired
    ISR->>CPU: Save current registers
    ISR->>Sched: Post ISR exit
    Sched->>Sched: Find highest priority READY
    Sched->>Disp: Dispatch decision
    Disp->>Old: Save context to TCB
    Disp->>New: Load context from TCB
    Disp->>CPU: Restore new task registers
    CPU-->>New: Begin execution
```

### 4.4 VRTX Inter-Task Communication Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Task 1 Producer] -->|SC_QPOST| Q[(Message Queue Buffer)]
    Q -->|SC_QPEND| T2[Task 2 Consumer]
    T2 -->|SC_POST| S[(Semaphore)]
    S -->|SC_PEND| T3[Task 3 Resource User]
    T1 -->|SC_EPOST| E[(Event Flags)]
    E -->|SC_EWAIT| T4[Task 4 Synchronized]
    T1 -->|SC_GPART| P[(Memory Partition)]
    P -->|SC_RPART| T5[Task 5 Allocator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1` style) and labels contain raw uppercase text only — no markdown formatting inside quoted strings, ensuring successful diagram compilation.

### 4.5 VRTX Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Task H High Priority P2] -->|Request Mutex| B{Mutex Available?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Acquire and Run]
    B -->|No| D[Task H PENDED]
    D --> E[Task L Holds Mutex]
    E --> F{L inherits P2}
    F --> G[Task M Cannot Preempt P5 < P2]
    G --> H[Task L Finishes Critical Section]
    H --> I[Release Mutex Restore P10]
    I --> J[Task H Acquires Mutex and Runs]
    C --> K[Normal Completion]
    J --> K
```
