| 1 | Token Bucket — packet conforms if | $L_i \leq b + r \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1})$ | $L_i$ = packet size (bytes), $r$ = rate (bytes/s), $b$ = bucket size (bytes) | Requires $b \geq L_{max}$ |
| 2 | Leaky Bucket — output rate | $R_{out} = \min(R_{in}, R_{max})$ | $R_{in}$ = input rate, $R_{max}$ = max output | Always $R_{out} \leq R_{max}$ |
| 3 | Token Bus — Worst-Case Token Rotation Time | $T_{WCRT} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} THT_i$ | $N$ = stations, $THT_i$ = token hold time at station $i$ | $T_{WCRT}$ is the maximum delay between two consecutive token arrivals at any station |
| 4 | Token Bus — Utilization Bound (for real-time schedulability) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq N \cdot (2^{1/N} - 1)$ | $C_i$ = transmission time, $T_i$ = period | Liu & Layland bound adapted for token bus |
| 5 | End-to-End Delay (PSN) | $D_{e2e} = D_{prop} + D_{trans} + D_{queue} + D_{proc}$ | All in seconds (s or ms) | $D_{queue}$ is the dominant term in congested networks |
| 6 | Effective Bandwidth (Pareto source) | $E[B] = \frac{\alpha \cdot K}{(\alpha - 1) \cdot K_{min}^{\alpha-1}}$ | $\alpha$ = shape param, $K$ = rate, $K_{min}$ = min rate | Valid for $\alpha > 1$ |
| 7 | Jitter | $J = D_{max} - D_{min}$ | In seconds | Smaller is better for real-time |
| 8 | RSVP Bandwidth Reservation | $BW_{reserved} \geq r_{peak} + \text{overhead}$ | bytes/sec | Includes header overhead |
| 9 | Token Generation Rate | $r_{tokens} = r_{avg} \cdot 8 \text{ (bits/byte)}$ | tokens/sec | If $r$ given in bits/sec |
| 10 | M/G/1 Queue — Mean Waiting Time | $W_q = \frac{\lambda \cdot E[S^2]}{2 \cdot (1 - \rho)}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $E[S^2]$ = 2nd moment of service, $\rho = \lambda E[S]$ | Pollaczek-Khinchine formula |
| 11 | Network Calculus — Delay Bound | $D_{max} = \max_{t \geq 0} \left\{ \inf\{ d \geq 0 : \alpha(t) \leq \beta(t+d) \} \right\}$ | $\alpha$ = arrival curve, $\beta$ = service curve | $h(\alpha,\beta) = $ horizontal deviation |
| 12 | RETHER — PROFINET IRT Cycle Time | $T_{cycle} \geq 31.25 \text{ μs} \times N_{slots}$ | $N_{slots}$ = number of time slots | Minimum 31.25 μs per slot |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Token Rotation Time in IEEE 802.4

**Problem:** Given a Token Bus with $N$ stations, each with Token Holding Time $THT_i$ and propagation delay $T_{prop}$, derive the Worst-Case Token Rotation Time ($T_{WCRT}$).

**Step 1: Define the token rotation cycle.**

A single complete cycle of the token involves:
- Transmission by each station $i$ (bounded by $THT_i$).
- Propagation of the token from station $i$ to station $i+1$ (bounded by $T_{prop,i}$).
- Reception and processing of the token at each station ($T_{proc}$).

**Step 2: Express the total cycle time as a sum.**

$$T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( THT_i + T_{prop,i} + T_{proc} \right)$$

**Step 3: Define the worst-case upper bound.**

The worst case occurs when **every station transmits for its maximum holding time** $THT_{max}$:

$$T_{WCRT} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} THT_{max} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{prop,i} + N \cdot T_{proc}$$

**Step 4: Simplify assuming uniform delays.**

Assuming $T_{prop,i} = T_{prop}$ for all $i$ and $T_{proc}$ constant:

$$T_{WCRT} = N \cdot THT_{max} + N \cdot T_{prop} + N \cdot T_{proc}$$

**Step 5: Final compact form.**

$$T_{WCRT} = N \cdot \left( THT_{max} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} \right)$$

**Interpretation:** A real-time message with deadline $D$ is schedulable if and only if $D \geq T_{WCRT}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This bound is *pessimistic* (assumes all stations are backlogged). In practice, the *average* token rotation time is significantly lower, which is why IEEE 802.4 was chosen for real-time manufacturing (e.g., General Motors' MAP protocol in the 1980s).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Token Bucket Conformance Condition

**Problem:** A source generates packets of sizes $L_1, L_2, \ldots$ at times $t_1, t_2, \ldots$. Determine the condition for the stream to be **token-bucket conformant** with parameters $(r, b)$.

**Step 1: Token bucket state equation.**

Let $B(t)$ = number of tokens in bucket at time $t$. Bucket fills at rate $r$ tokens/sec, capped at $b$:

$$B(t) = \min\left(b, \; B(t_{prev}) + r \cdot (t - t_{prev})\right)$$

**Step 2: To send a packet of size $L_i$, the sender must have:**

$$L_i \leq B(t_i)$$

**Step 3: Worst-case analysis over the inter-arrival time.**

The maximum tokens available at time $t_i$ since the last packet at $t_{i-1}$ are:

$$B_{max}(t_i) = \min\left(b, \; B(t_{i-1}) + r \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1})\right)$$

**Step 4: To guarantee conformance regardless of prior state (assume $B(t_{i-1}) = 0$):**

$$L_i \leq \min(b, \; r \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1}))$$

**Step 5: Split into two regimes.**

- If $r \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1}) \geq b$: $L_i \leq b$ (limited by bucket size).
- If $r \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1}) < b$: $L_i \leq r \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1})$ (limited by refill rate).

**Step 6: Unified condition.**

$$L_i \leq b + r \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1})$$

**Interpretation:** A packet of size $L_i$ arriving $\Delta t = t_i - t_{i-1}$ after the previous packet conforms if it does not exceed the maximum possible bucket fill over $\Delta t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Use:** This conformance test is used by **traffic policers** in routers (e.g., Three-Color Marker — RFC 2698) to decide whether to admit, remark, or drop a packet.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: End-to-End Delay Bound using Network Calculus

**Problem:** A flow traverses $H$ hops. Each hop $i$ provides a service curve $\beta_i(t) = C_i \cdot [t - T_i]^+$ (rate-latency service curve), where $[x]^+ = \max(0, x)$. Find the end-to-end delay bound for an arrival curve $\alpha(t) = r \cdot t + b$ (token bucket).

**Step 1: Apply the concatenation property of service curves.**

The end-to-end service curve of the concatenation is:

$$\beta_{e2e}(t) = C_{min} \cdot \left[ t - \sum_{i=1}^{H} T_i \right]^+$$

where $C_{min} = \min(C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_H)$.

**Step 2: Compute the horizontal deviation $h(\alpha, \beta_{e2e})$.**

The horizontal deviation is the smallest $d$ such that $\alpha(t) \leq \beta_{e2e}(t + d)$ for all $t \geq 0$.

**Step 3: Set up the inequality.**

$$r \cdot t + b \leq C_{min} \cdot \left[ t + d - \sum_{i=1}^{H} T_i \right]^+$$

For $t + d \geq \sum T_i$:

$$r \cdot t + b \leq C_{min} \cdot \left( t + d - \sum T_i \right)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $d$.**

$$r \cdot t + b \leq C_{min} \cdot t + C_{min} \cdot d - C_{min} \cdot \sum T_i$$

Rearranging:

$$C_{min} \cdot d \geq b + (r - C_{min}) \cdot t + C_{min} \cdot \sum T_i$$

Since $r \leq C_{min}$ (otherwise the system is unstable), the term $(r - C_{min}) \cdot t \leq 0$, and worst case at $t \to 0$:

$$C_{min} \cdot d \geq b + C_{min} \cdot \sum T_i$$

**Step 5: Final delay bound.**

$$D_{max} = \frac{b}{C_{min}} + \sum_{i=1}^{H} T_i$$

**Interpretation:** The end-to-end delay bound is the sum of the *bottleneck-induced backlog time* plus the *total latency* across all hops. This is the formula used by **TSN schedulability analysis** tools.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Token Bucket — Burst Tolerance for Real-Time Traffic

**Problem:** A real-time flow has rate $r$ Mbps and maximum burst $B_{max}$ bits. What bucket size $b$ guarantees no loss due to rate policing?

**Step 1: Identify the bottleneck link capacity $C_{bottleneck}$.**

**Step 2: Time to drain the burst at the bottleneck rate:**

$$T_{drain} = \frac{B_{max}}{C_{bottleneck}}$$

**Step 3: Tokens accumulated during the burst emission time** ($T_{emit} = B_{max} / r$):

$$\text{Refill during emission} = r \cdot T_{emit} = B_{max}$$

Wait, this is a tautology. Let's redo with peak rate $p > r$:

**Step 3 (corrected): Peak emission time** $T_{peak} = B_{max} / p$:

$$\text{Tokens available} = b + r \cdot T_{peak}$$

**Step 4: Conformance condition** $L \leq$ tokens available:

$$B_{max} \leq b + r \cdot \frac{B_{max}}{p}$$

**Step 5: Solve for $b$:**

$$b \geq B_{max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{r}{p}\right)$$

**Interpretation:** The minimum bucket size must accommodate the burst that exceeds the average rate. Smaller $b$ causes more drops; larger $b$ allows more burstiness.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IntServ vs DiffServ

| Parameter | Integrated Services (IntServ) | Differentiated Services (DiffServ) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Granularity** | Per-flow | Per-class (aggregate) |
| **Signaling** | RSVP (explicit setup) | None (implicit via DSCP) |
| **Router State** | Per-flow state (large memory) | Per-class state (small memory) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (state $\propto$ flows) | Excellent (state $\propto$ classes) |
| **Guarantee** | Hard (mathematical) | Soft (statistical) |
| **QoS Class Types** | Guaranteed, Controlled Load, BE | EF, AF1–AF4, BE |
| **Configuration** | End-to-end | Edge + Core |
| **Overhead** | High (per-flow signaling) | Low (only edge policing) |
| **Best For** | Small LANs, premium flows | Large WANs, ISP backbones |
| **Coexistence** | Can use RSVP+DiffServ in hybrid mode | Works with IntServ at edges |

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — IEEE 802.4 vs IEEE 802.3 vs RETHER

| Parameter | IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet) | IEEE 802.4 (Token Bus) | IEEE 802.5 (Token Ring) | RETHER (TSN/PROFINET IRT) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Topology** | Bus / Star | Bus (logical ring) | Ring (physical ring) | Star (switched) |
| **MAC** | CSMA/CD | Token pass | Token pass | TDMA / Priority gating |
| **Determinism** | None (statistical) | Bounded | Bounded | Hard real-time |
| **Worst-Case Delay** | Unbounded | $N \cdot THT$ | $N \cdot THT$ | Cycle-time bound |
| **Bottleneck** | Collisions | Token rotation | Token rotation | Time-slot exhaustion |
| **Bandwidth** | 10 Mbps–100 Gbps | 1–10 Mbps | 4–16 Mbps | 100 Mbps–10 Gbps |
| **Cost** | Very low | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate–high |
| **Modern Use** | Universal IT | Legacy (MAP) | Legacy (IBM) | Industry 4.0, automotive |

---

### 3.7 RSVP Reservation State Machine

**Mathematical representation:**

Let $R_f$ = reservation state for flow $f$ at a router. $R_f = (r, b, style, filter)$ where:
- $r$ = reserved bandwidth
- $b$ = burst tolerance
- $style \in \{WF, FF, SE\}$
- $filter$ = list of sender addresses (for FF/SE) or empty (for WF)

**State transition functions:**

1. **$Init(R_f)$:** Default state — no reservation.
2. **$Path(S, D)$:** PATH message from sender $S$ to destination $D$ sets up $R_f$ in PATH state.
3. **$Resv(D, S, r, b)$:** RESV message from $D$ back to $S$ triggers $Transmit(R_f, r, b)$.
4. **$Transmit(R_f, r, b)$:** Admission control:
   - If $\sum_{existing} r_{existing} + r \leq C_{link}$: **ADMIT**, install reservation.
   - Else: **REJECT**, send RESV ERROR back to receiver.
5. **$Refresh(R_f)$:** Soft-state refresh — every $T_{refresh}$ (typically 30 sec).
6. **$Timeout(R_f)$:** If no refresh in $T_{timeout}$ (typically 3 × $T_{refresh}$ = 90 sec), remove $R_f$.

**Block diagram (textual):**

```
PATH arrives -> [Check PSB exists?] -> Install PHOP
RESV arrives -> [Admission Control?] -> ADMIT/REJECT
              -> if ADMIT: install reservation, classify packets
Timeout fires -> Remove reservation
Refresh arrives -> Reset timer
```

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 QoS Framework Reference Model

This diagram shows the layered QoS framework for real-time communication over packet-switched networks.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER<br>Real-Time Apps: VoIP, Video, Control Loops"]
    specLayer["QOS SPECIFICATION LAYER<br>TSpec: rate, burst, peak, MTU<br>RSpec: bandwidth, delay bound"]
    signalLayer["SIGNALING LAYER<br>RSVP PATH and RESV messages<br>Resource negotiation"]
    enforceLayer["QOS ENFORCEMENT LAYER<br>Admission Control<br>Packet Classification and Marking<br>Queue Scheduling: PQ, WFQ, CBS"]
    monLayer["MONITORING AND ADAPTATION LAYER<br>QoS measurement, SLA verification<br>ECN, AQM, Renegotiation"]
    netLayer["NETWORK SUBSTRATE<br>Packet Switched Network: IP, ATM, MPLS, Ethernet"]

    appLayer --> specLayer
    specLayer --> signalLayer
    signalLayer --> enforceLayer
    enforceLayer --> monLayer
    monLayer --> netLayer
    netLayer -. feedback .- enforceLayer
```

**Figure Explanation:**
- Vertical flow represents the request/negotiation direction (top → bottom).
- Dashed feedback line represents monitoring & adaptation (bottom → top).
- Each layer maps to a specific protocol/mechanism in KTU Module 4.

---

### 4.2 Token Bus (IEEE 802.4) Logical Ring Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stationA["Station A<br>Addr 0x10"]
    stationB["Station B<br>Addr 0x20"]
    stationC["Station C<br>Addr 0x30"]
    stationD["Station D<br>Addr 0x40"]
    stationE["Station E<br>Addr 0x50"]
    tokenAnim["TOKEN<br>Circulating"]

    stationA -- token pass --> stationB
    stationB -- token pass --> stationC
    stationC -- token pass --> stationD
    stationD -- token pass --> stationE
    stationE -- token pass --> stationA
    tokenAnim -. rotates .- stationA
```

**Figure Explanation:**
- Physical bus topology (linear cable connecting all stations).
- Logical ring: Token passes in address order regardless of physical position.
- Only token holder can transmit → no collisions → deterministic access.

---

### 4.3 RSVP Reservation Setup Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as SENDER
    participant Rout1 as R1
    participant Rout2 as R2
    participant Recv as RECEIVER

    Sender->>Rout1: PATH message (TSpec, PHOP)
    Note over Rout1: Install path state,<br>store PHOP = S
    Rout1->>Rout2: PATH message (TSpec, PHOP=R1)
    Note over Rout2: Install path state,<br>store PHOP = R1
    Rout2->>Recv: PATH message
    Note over Recv: Calculate required<br>bandwidth

    Recv->>Rout2: RESV message (RSpec, style=FF)
    Note over Rout2: Admission Control<br>ADMIT or REJECT
    Rout2->>Rout1: RESV message
    Note over Rout1: Install reservation<br>Classify packets
    Rout1->>Sender: RESV message
    Note over Sender: Reservation established<br>Begin data transfer

    loop Periodic Refresh
        Sender->>Rout1: PATH refresh
        Recv->>Rout2: RESV refresh
    end
```

**Figure Explanation:**
- Solid lines show message flow directions.
- Each router performs admission control independently.
- Soft-state: refresh required every $T_{refresh}$ (30 s typical).

---

### 4.4 Token Bucket Rate Control Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pktArr["Incoming Packet<br>Size L bytes"]
    bucket{"Bucket has<br>at least L tokens?"}
    accept["ACCEPT Packet<br>Deduct L tokens<br>Transmit immediately"]
    reject["REJECT Packet<br>Drop or mark<br>or queue"]
    refill["Token Refill<br>Rate r tokens/sec<br>Capacity b"]
    bucketStore["TOKEN BUCKET<br>Capacity b tokens<br>Current level B"]

    pktArr --> bucket
    bucket -- YES --> accept
    bucket -- NO --> reject
    refill --> bucketStore
    bucketStore --> bucket
```

**Figure Explanation:**
- Token bucket acts as a "credit-based" admission filter.
- Tokens accumulate at rate $r$; capped at $b$.
- Packets consume tokens equal to their size.

---

### 4.5 RETHER Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph syncPlane["SYNCHRONIZATION PLANE"]
        gPTP["IEEE 802.1AS gPTP<br>Grand Master Clock<br>Sub-microsecond sync"]
    end

    subgraph schedPlane["SCHEDULING PLANE"]
        TAS["IEEE 802.1Qbv<br>Time-Aware Shaper<br>Gate Control Lists"]
        CQF["IEEE 802.1Qch<br>Cyclic Queuing and Forwarding"]
        FP["IEEE 802.1Qcr<br>Asynchronous Traffic Shaping"]
    end

    subgraph resvPlane["RESERVATION PLANE"]
        SRP["IEEE 802.1Qat<br>Stream Reservation Protocol"]
        MSRP["Multiple Stream Reservation"]
    end

    subgraph relPlane["RELIABILITY PLANE"]
        FRER["IEEE 802.1CB<br>Frame Replication and Elimination"]
        Qbu["IEEE 802.1Qbu<br>Frame Preemption"]
    end

    gPTP --> TAS
    SRP --> TAS
    FRER --> TAS
    Qbu --> TAS
    TAS --> CQF
    CQF --> FP
```

**Figure Explanation:**
- Four planes work together: time sync, scheduling, reservation, and reliability.
- TSN is the modern evolution of RETHER for industrial IoT.
- Gate Control Lists (GCLs) in TAS implement TDMA-like behavior over Ethernet.

---

### 4.6 QoS Routing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["New Flow Request<br>src, dst, delay_bound, bw_min"]
    gather["Gather Network State<br>Link delays, available bw"]
    compute["Run QoS Routing Algorithm<br>Dijkstra, Q-OSPF, or PNNI"]
    pathExist{"Path satisfying<br>all constraints?"}
    admit["Admit Flow<br>Reserve resources<br>Install forwarding state"]
    reject["Reject Flow<br>Send rejection to source"]
    monitor["Monitor Flow<br>Periodic SLA check"]

    start --> gather
    gather --> compute
    compute --> pathExist
    pathExist -- YES --> admit
    pathExist -- NO --> reject
    admit --> monitor
    monitor -. violation .--> start
```

**Figure Explanation:**
- QoS routing is a *constraint-based shortest path* problem (NP-hard).
- After admission, ongoing monitoring detects SLA violations and triggers re-routing.

---

### 4.7 Block-Level Functional Architecture: End-to-End Real-Time Communication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph srcSide["SOURCE SIDE"]
        app1["Real-Time App<br>e.g., Video Stream"]
        ts1["Traffic Shaper<br>Token Bucket r,b"]
        cls1["Classifier<br>Mark DSCP / VLAN PCP"]
    end

    subgraph netCore["NETWORK CORE"]
        r1["Router 1<br>WFQ Scheduler<br>DSCP-based"]
        r2["Router 2<br>WFQ Scheduler<br>DSCP-based"]
        r3["Router 3<br>WFQ Scheduler<br>DSCP-based"]
    end

    subgraph dstSide["DESTINATION SIDE"]
        cls2["Classifier<br>Reverse mapping"]
        app2["Real-Time App<br>Receiver buffer / Jitter comp"]
    end

    app1 --> ts1 --> cls1
    cls1 -- ETHERNET/IP --> r1
    r1 --> r2
    r2 --> r3
    r3 -- ETHERNET/IP --> cls2
    cls2 --> app2
```

**Figure Explanation:**
- Edge nodes do heavy lifting (shaping, classification, policing).
- Core nodes do fast forwarding (DSCP-based WFQ scheduling).
- Receiver uses jitter buffer to compensate for delay variation.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define Quality of Service (QoS). List the four primary QoS parameters used to characterize real-time network communication.**

**Model Answer:**

**Quality of Service (QoS)** is a set of measurable service parameters that quantify the performance and reliability of a network from the application’s perspective.

**Four primary QoS parameters for real-time communication:**

1. **Bandwidth** — Minimum data rate (bits/sec) that must be available end-to-end.
2. **End-to-End Delay (Latency)** — Maximum time for a packet to travel from source to destination.
3. **Jitter** — Variation in inter-packet arrival times (delay variance). Defined as $J = D_{max} - D_{min}$.
4. **Packet Loss Ratio (PLR)** — Fraction of transmitted packets lost in transit. Typical real-time requirement: $\text{PLR} \leq 10^{-3}$ for voice, $\leq 10^{-5}$ for control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **throughput** with **bandwidth**. Throughput is *achieved* rate; bandwidth is *available* capacity.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Briefly explain the differences between IntServ and DiffServ QoS models.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | IntServ | DiffServ |
|--------|---------|----------|
| **Granularity** | Per-flow | Per-class (aggregate) |
| **Signaling Protocol** | RSVP | None (DSCP-based) |
| **State in Routers** | Per-flow (high memory) | Per-class (low memory) |
| **Guarantee Type** | Hard QoS | Soft QoS |
| **Scalability** | Limited to small networks | Highly scalable for ISP/core |
| **Configuration** | End-to-end setup needed | Edge policers + core PHBs |

**Key insight:** IntServ uses **RSVP** to reserve resources for every individual flow; DiffServ uses **6-bit DSCP** field to mark packets into a small number of service classes (EF, AF, BE).

---

### PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture and operation of the IEEE 802.4 Token Bus** standard. Derive the **worst-case token rotation time** for a Token Bus network with $N$ stations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **RETHER (Real-Time Ethernet)** concept. Compare the **PROFINET IRT** and **EtherCAT** protocols in terms of their synchronization mechanism, minimum cycle time, and typical industrial application. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — IEEE 802.4 Token Bus Architecture:**

IEEE 802.4 defines a MAC protocol for a bus topology LAN where stations are logically organized into a **ring**. A special control frame called a *token* is passed sequentially from station to station in address order, regardless of physical position on the bus.

**Key components:**
- **Logical Ring** — Implicit ordering of stations (0x10 → 0x20 → 0x30 → ...).
- **Token Frame** — 3-byte frame granting transmission right.
- **Token Holder** — Only station with token may transmit.
- **THT (Token Holding Time)** — Maximum duration a station may hold the token.
- **TRT (Token Rotation Time)** — Time for token to make one complete cycle.
- **4 Priority Classes** — Classes 0, 2, 4, 6 (6 highest) for traffic differentiation.

**Operations:**
- Station receives token → starts transmission timer → sends frames of highest priority class first.
- After transmitting or THT expires, station passes token to next logical neighbor.
- Ring maintenance handled by **Ring Manager** (a designated station).

**Step 2 — Derivation of Worst-Case Token Rotation Time:**

Let $N$ = number of stations, $THT_i$ = token hold time at station $i$, $T_{prop}$ = bus propagation delay (one-way), $T_{proc}$ = token processing time at each station.

A single token rotation cycle = sum of (transmission + propagation + processing) for every station.

**Worst case** — every station uses its maximum $THT_{max}$:

$$T_{WCRT} = N \cdot THT_{max} + N \cdot T_{prop} + N \cdot T_{proc}$$

**Compact form:**

$$T_{WCRT} = N \cdot (THT_{max} + T_{prop} + T_{proc})$$

**For a real-time message with deadline $D$ to be schedulable:**

$$D \geq T_{WCRT}$$

**Step 3 — Application Example:**

A factory has $N=10$ stations on an IEEE 802.4 bus. Each station has $THT_{max} = 2$ ms. The bus length is 500 m ($T_{prop} = 500/2 \times 10^8 = 2.5$ μs, negligible). $T_{proc} = 50$ μs. Find $T_{WCRT}$.

$$T_{WCRT} = 10 \cdot (2 \text{ ms} + 2.5 \text{ μs} + 50 \text{ μs}) \approx 20.05 \text{ ms}$$

Any real-time control message with deadline $\geq 20.05$ ms is guaranteed to be delivered before its deadline.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — RETHER Definition:**

**RETHER (Real-Time Ethernet)** is a class of Ethernet-based protocols engineered to deliver deterministic, time-bounded communication suitable for hard real-time industrial and embedded systems. It overcomes the non-deterministic CSMA/CD of standard Ethernet using synchronization, scheduling, and traffic shaping.

**Step 2 — PROFINET IRT:**

- **PROFINET IRT (Isochronous Real-Time)** uses a **time-slotted** cycle divided into three phases:
  1. **Standard traffic phase** (non-real-time, e.g., diagnostics).
  2. **Real-time phase (RT)** — Reserved for RT class frames (cyclic).
  3. **Isochronous phase (IRT)** — Reserved for time-critical frames with hardware cut-through.
- **Synchronization:** IEEE 1588 PTP (Precision Time Protocol), with hardware timestamping for sub-microsecond accuracy.
- **Minimum cycle time:** $31.25$ μs per slot.
- **Application:** Motion control, high-speed CNC machines, robotics.

**Step 3 — EtherCAT:**

- **EtherCAT (Ethernet for Control Automation Technology)** uses a **"processing on the fly"** technique. The master sends one frame; each slave reads its data and inserts its response **as the frame passes through**, in nanoseconds.
- **Synchronization:** **Distributed Clocks (DC)** — Each slave has its own clock; master periodically synchronizes them with sub-μs precision.
- **Minimum cycle time:** $\leq 100$ μs (achievable $\approx 12.5$ μs in some setups).
- **Application:** High-speed I/O scanning, hydraulic control, semiconductor manufacturing.

**Step 4 — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | PROFINET IRT | EtherCAT |
|-----------|--------------|----------|
| Synchronization | IEEE 1588 PTP | Distributed Clocks (DC) |
| Cycle Time | $\geq 31.25$ μs | $\leq 100$ μs (typ. 12.5 μs) |
| Topology | Star, line, ring | Daisy-chain (line, ring) |
| Frame Handling | Per-slot scheduling | "On-the-fly" processing |
| Bandwidth Efficiency | Lower (multiple frames) | Very high (single frame) |
| Primary Use | Motion control, CNC | High-speed I/O, robotics |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **RSVP (Resource Reservation Protocol)** with its two key message types: **PATH** and **RESV**. Discuss the three **reservation styles** in RSVP. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Token Bucket rate control mechanism** with its parameters. A real-time flow has token bucket parameters $(r, b) = (10 \text{ Mbps}, 1 \text{ Mbit})$ and peak rate $p = 50$ Mbps. A burst of $B_{max} = 5$ Mbits is observed. Verify whether the burst is **conformant**. If a 2-Mbyte packet arrives 50 ms after the previous packet, check conformance. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Step 1 — RSVP Overview:**

RSVP is a **receiver-oriented, soft-state, unidirectional** signaling protocol that supports resource reservation for unicast and multicast flows in IP networks. It works in conjunction with IntServ to provide end-to-end QoS guarantees.

**Step 2 — Two Key Message Types:**

**PATH message (sent from Sender → Receiver):**
- Propagates a description of the flow's traffic profile (**TSpec**).
- At each router, RSVP creates **Path State Block (PSB)** and records the **PHOP (Previous Hop)** address.
- Used by receivers to determine reverse path.

**RESV message (sent from Receiver → Sender):**
- Carries the **RSpec** (Reservation Specification) — requested QoS.
- Travels back to the sender along the **reverse path** (using stored PHOPs).
- At each router, RSVP performs:
  1. **Admission Control** — does the link have capacity for this request?
  2. **Policy Control** — is the request authorized?
- If both pass, the router installs **reservation state** and configures its **packet classifier and scheduler** for this flow.
- Returns RESV ERROR if rejected.

**Step 3 — Soft State Maintenance:**
- Both PSB and RSB are **soft state** — must be refreshed every 30 sec.
- If no refresh within 90 sec, state is deleted.

**Step 4 — Three Reservation Styles:**

| Style | Filter | Sharing | Use Case |
|-------|--------|---------|----------|
| **WF (Wildcard Filter)** | No specific sender | Shared across all senders | Audio conference, multicast video |
| **FF (Fixed Filter)** | Specific sender | Not shared (dedicated) | Distinct high-quality video feeds |
| **SE (Shared Explicit)** | Explicit list of senders | Shared among listed senders | Selected sender pools |

**Step 5 — Diagram (drawn in solution):**

```
SENDER --PATH--> R1 --PATH--> R2 --PATH--> RECEIVER
SENDER <--RESV-- R1 <--RESV-- R2 <--RESV-- RECEIVER
```

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — Token Bucket Definition:**

A token bucket of size $b$ (bits) fills at rate $r$ (bits/sec). To transmit a packet of size $L$, the sender consumes $L$ tokens. If insufficient tokens, the packet is non-conformant.

**Conformance condition:**

$$L_i \leq b + r \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1})$$

**Step 2 — Verify Burst Conformance:**

Given: $r = 10$ Mbps, $b = 1$ Mbit, $B_{max} = 5$ Mbits, $p = 50$ Mbps.

Time to emit burst at peak rate:

$$T_{peak} = \frac{B_{max}}{p} = \frac{5 \text{ Mbits}}{50 \text{ Mbps}} = 100 \text{ ms}$$

Tokens accumulated during $T_{peak}$:

$$\Delta_{tokens} = r \cdot T_{peak} = 10 \text{ Mbps} \times 100 \text{ ms} = 1 \text{ Mbit}$$

Maximum tokens available at end of burst:

$$B_{avail} = b + \Delta_{tokens} = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ Mbits}$$

**Conclusion:** $B_{max} = 5$ Mbits $>$ $B_{avail} = 2$ Mbits.

$$\boxed{\text{The burst is NOT CONFORMANT. The packet will be policed/dropped.}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify 2-Mbyte Packet Conformance:**

Given: $L = 2$ Mbytes $= 16$ Mbits, $\Delta t = 50$ ms $= 0.05$ s, $r = 10$ Mbps, $b = 1$ Mbit.

Tokens accumulated since last packet:

$$\Delta_{tokens} = r \cdot \Delta t = 10 \text{ Mbps} \times 0.05 \text{ s} = 0.5 \text{ Mbits}$$

Maximum tokens available:

$$B_{avail} = b + \Delta_{tokens} = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ Mbits}$$

**Compare:** $L = 16$ Mbits $>$ $B_{avail} = 1.5$ Mbits.

$$\boxed{\text{The 2-Mbyte packet is NOT CONFORMANT.}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Insight:**

To make the 2-Mbyte packet conformant, either:
- Increase bucket size to $b \geq 15.5$ Mbits.
- Increase inter-arrival time to $\Delta t \geq 1.55$ s.
- Reduce packet size to $L \leq 1.5$ Mbits (e.g., fragment).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit consistency** is the #1 reason students lose marks in numerical questions. Always convert: Mbps ↔ Mbits/sec, Mbyte ↔ Mbit (= × 8).
> - In step 2, students often use $r$ (10 Mbps) instead of $p$ (50 Mbps) to compute $T_{peak}$. Use $p$ for *emission time*, $r$ for *refill rate*.
> - The conformance test is **asymmetric**: a flow that *was* conformant may suddenly become non-conformant if a large packet arrives after a long quiet period (bucket caps at $b$).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. Use it as your last-day revision sheet.**

**Core Concepts to Memorize:**

- [ ] **QoS** = quantifiable, predictable service delivery over a network.
- [ ] **Four QoS Parameters:** Bandwidth, Delay, Jitter, Packet Loss.
- [ ] **QoS Framework Phases:** Specification → Negotiation → Reservation → Enforcement → Monitoring.
- [ ] **IntServ** = per-flow, RSVP, hard QoS, low scalability.
- [ ] **DiffServ** = per-class, DSCP-based, soft QoS, high scalability.
- [ ] **IEEE 802.4** = Token Bus, logical ring, deterministic.
- [ ] **Token Rotation Time** = $N \cdot (THT_{max} + T_{prop} + T_{proc})$.
- [ ] **RETHER** = family of protocols (PROFINET IRT, EtherCAT, TSN) that add determinism to Ethernet.
- [ ] **RSVP** = receiver-oriented, soft-state, two messages (PATH, RESV).
- [ ] **RSVP Styles** = WF (shared wildcard), FF (fixed), SE (shared explicit).
- [ ] **Token Bucket Conformance:** $L_i \leq b + r \cdot \Delta t$.
- [ ] **Leaky Bucket** = output rate capped at $R_{max}$.
- [ ] **Rate Control** = open-loop (shaping) vs closed-loop (AIMD).
- [ ] **Routing for Real-Time** = multi-constraint (delay, bandwidth, loss) — NP-hard.

**Key Formulae to Memorize:**

$$T_{WCRT}^{802.4} = N \cdot (THT_{max} + T_{prop} + T_{proc})$$

$$\text{Token Bucket Conformance: } L_i \leq b + r \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1})$$

$$b_{min} \geq B_{max} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{r}{p}\right)$$

$$D_{e2e}^{NC} = \frac{b}{C_{min}} + \sum_{i=1}^{H} T_i$$

**Key Acronyms:**

- TSpec = Traffic Specification
- RSpec = Reservation Specification
- PSB = Path State Block
- RSB = Reservation State Block
- PHOP = Previous Hop
- DSCP = Differentiated Services Code Point
- PHB = Per-Hop Behavior
- EF = Expedited Forwarding
- AF = Assured Forwarding
- BE = Best Effort
- TSN = Time-Sensitive Networking
- PTP = Precision Time Protocol
- DC = Distributed Clocks
- IRT = Isochronous Real-Time
- TTP = Time-Triggered Protocol
- MAP = Manufacturing Automation Protocol
- SLA = Service Level Agreement
- ECN = Explicit Congestion Notification
- AIMD = Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease

**Common Comparison Traps in Exams:**

- IntServ vs DiffServ (granularity, scalability, signaling).
- IEEE 802.3 vs 802.4 vs 802.5 vs 802.11 (access method, determinism).
- RSVP vs DiffServ (signaling vs marking).
- PROFINET IRT vs EtherCAT vs TSN (sync method, cycle time).
- Token Bucket vs Leaky Bucket (rate cap vs burst cap).
- Open-loop vs Closed-loop rate control (proactive vs reactive).

**Final Exam Strategy:**

- For **3-mark questions:** Use bullet points, mention 2–3 distinct technical terms, and use the 4-quadrant table format if comparing two items.
- For **14-mark questions:** Always start with a **definition**, then a **diagram** (even if simple), then a **table/comparison**, then a **numerical example**. This structure alone guarantees 80% of marks.
- Always include **units** in numerical answers. State assumptions clearly.
- For derivations, write **every algebraic step** — partial credit is heavily awarded.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RT Databases - Applications, Characteristics of Temporal Data, Concurrency Control, Commercial RT Databases, Special Topics in Real-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST748 - REAL TIME SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** RT Databases - Applications, Characteristics of Temporal Data, Concurrency Control, Commercial RT Databases, Special Topics in Real-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Real-Time Database (RTDB) – Formal Definition

A **Real-Time Database (RTDB)** is a database system where transactions have explicit timing constraints (deadlines) and whose correctness depends not only on the logical results of committed transactions but also on the **time at which these results are delivered**. An RTDB simultaneously preserves the *ACID properties* of conventional databases (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) while honoring **temporal consistency** of data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** *"A Real-Time Database is a database system whose state includes time-dependent data, where transactions are associated with deadlines, and where the system's correctness depends on producing logically correct results within specified time bounds."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The Air Traffic Control Tower

Imagine an **Air Traffic Control (ATC) Tower** as an RTDB:
- Each flight's data (altitude, speed, heading) is a *temporal data record*.
- The controller must update flight positions **before the next radar sweep** (deadline).
- A late update is as useless as no update at all — a stale radar reading can cause a collision.
- The system must process hundreds of updates per second, prioritizing the most urgent (planes closest to each other).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In conventional DBs, *consistency* is about logical correctness. In RTDBs, *consistency = logical correctness + temporal correctness*. Stale data = incorrect data.

## 1.3 Characteristics of Temporal Data

Temporal data has **two time dimensions** that govern its validity:

| Time Dimension | Definition | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| **Valid Time (VT)** | The real-world time interval during which a fact is true | "When the fact is **true in reality**" |
| **Transaction Time (TT)** | The time interval during which the fact was stored in the database | "When the fact is **current in the DB**" |
| **Bitemporal** | A fact indexed by both valid time AND transaction time | "Double-stamped facts" |

**Temporal Consistency Invariant (Formal Definition):**

$$\text{Age}(x) \le \text{Absolute\_Validity\_Interval}(AVI)$$

Where $\text{Age}(x) = \text{CurrentTime} - \text{LastUpdateTime}(x)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Properties of Temporal Data:**
> 1. **Absolute Consistency:** Data must be *absolutely* up-to-date (Age $\le$ AVI).
> 2. **Relative Consistency:** A set of related data items used together must all be *relatively* fresh within a *Relative Validity Interval (RVI)*.
> 3. **Temporal Integrity Constraints:** Application-specific timing rules (e.g., stock price must update within 5 seconds).

## 1.4 RT Database Applications – Industrial Domains

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Applications (Frequently Asked):**

1. **Telecommunications** – Call routing records, billing, HLR/VLR updates.
2. **Aviation & Air Traffic Control** – Flight data, radar tracks.
3. **Process Control / SCADA** – Chemical plant sensor data, nuclear reactor monitoring.
4. **Stock Trading / E-Commerce** – Bid/ask prices, order books (microsecond deadlines).
5. **Medical / Healthcare** – Patient monitoring (ECG, ICU telemetry).
6. **Military / Defense** – Missile guidance, radar tracking systems.
7. **Industrial Automation** – Manufacturing control systems (e.g., assembly line robots).

## 1.5 Special Topics in Real-Time Systems – Definition Block

The term *"Special Topics"* in KTU's Module 4 refers to emerging and cross-cutting concerns beyond classical scheduling theory:

- **Real-Time Security & Cryptographic Protocols** – bounded-time encryption.
- **Fault-Tolerant Real-Time Computing** – redundancy, recovery blocks.
- **Energy-Aware Real-Time Systems** – Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS).
- **Multiprocessor / Multicore Real-Time Scheduling** – partitioned vs. global scheduling.
- **Real-Time Communication & Networking** – CAN bus, TSN, EtherCAT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Concurrency Control in Real-Time Databases – Deep Dive

Concurrency Control (CC) ensures the **isolation property** of transactions when multiple transactions execute concurrently. In RTDBs, classical CC protocols are *insufficient* because they ignore deadlines. RTDBs require **Deadline-Aware Concurrency Control**.

### 2.1.1 Taxonomy of Concurrency Control Protocols

```
Concurrency Control in RTDBs
        │
        ├── 1. Lock-Based (Pessimistic)
        │      ├── Priority Inheritance (PI)
        │      ├── Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)
        │      ├── Stack Resource Policy (SRP)
        │      └── Aborted Restart on Conflict
        │
        ├── 2. Optimistic (Optimistic Concurrency Control - OCC)
        │      ├── OPT-BC (Backward Validation, Conflict-priority)
        │      ├── OPT-WP / OPT-Wait (Wait-based)
        │      └── OPT-Sacrifice (Restart loser)
        │
        └── 3. Timestamp-Based
               ├── Basic Timestamp Ordering (TO)
               └── Multiversion Timestamp Ordering
```

### 2.1.2 The Priority Inversion Problem

**Priority Inversion** occurs when a higher-priority transaction is *blocked* waiting for a lower-priority transaction to release a lock. If an intermediate-priority transaction preempts the lower one, the high-priority task waits indefinitely — this is the **unbounded priority inversion problem** (famously caused the Mars Pathfinder fault in 1997).

### 2.1.3 Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) – Theory

In PIP, when a high-priority task $T_H$ is blocked by a lower-priority task $T_L$ holding a lock, $T_L$ **temporarily inherits** $T_H$'s priority until it releases the lock.

**Formal Priority Inheritance Rule:**

$$\text{Priority}(T_L) = \max\big(\text{Priority}(T_L),\ \text{Priority}(T_H)\big)$$

This continues transitively if $T_L$ is blocked by an even lower-priority task $T_{LL}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of PIP:** PIP prevents unbounded inversion but allows **deadlock** and **chained blocking** (a high-priority task can be blocked by multiple lower-priority tasks sequentially).

### 2.1.4 Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) – Theory

Each resource (lock) is assigned a **priority ceiling** equal to the highest priority of any task that may lock it:

$$\text{Ceiling}(R) = \max_{T_i \text{ accesses } R} \text{Priority}(T_i)$$

A task $T_i$ can lock a resource $R$ **only if** its priority is *strictly higher* than the ceilings of **all** resources currently locked by other tasks.

**Properties of PCP:**
- Deadlock-free ✓
- At most **one** level of blocking per task ✓
- Prevents chained blocking ✓

### 2.1.5 Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC) in RTDBs

OCC has three phases:
1. **Read Phase** – Transaction reads data into a local workspace, performing all computations speculatively.
2. **Validation Phase** – Check if the read set conflicts with any concurrently committed transaction.
3. **Write Phase** – If validation passes, apply local writes to the database; else restart.

**Conflict Resolution Policies in RTDBs:**

| Policy | Strategy | KTU Exam Note |
|---|---|---|
| **OPT-Forward** | Restart the *arriving* (later) transaction | Loser waits/restarts |
| **OPT-Backward** | Restart the *committed* (earlier) transaction, but only if its priority is *lower* | Respects priorities |
| **OPT-Sacrifice** | Restart the transaction with **lower priority** | Most deadline-aware |
| **OPT-Wait** | Lower-priority transaction waits | Causes priority inversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **OPT-BC (OPT-Backward with Conflict priority)** is the most commonly tested variant in KTU exams.

## 2.2 Transaction Scheduling in RTDBs – Key Concepts

**EDF (Earliest Deadline First)** is the optimal uniprocessor scheduling algorithm for *preemptive* real-time tasks.

**Utilization Bound (Necessary & Sufficient Condition for EDF schedulability):**

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) of task $i$
- $T_i$ = Period of task $i$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS (Module 4)**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Domain | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temporal Consistency (Absolute) | $\text{Age}(x) \le AVI$ | RTDB | seconds / ms |
| Relative Consistency | $\vert t_1 - t_2 \vert \le RVI$ | Temporal Data | seconds / ms |
| EDF Utilization Bound | $U = \sum C_i / T_i \le 1$ | Scheduling | dimensionless |
| Response Time | $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i$ | Task Analysis | ms |
| Priority Inheritance | $P_L = \max(P_L, P_H)$ | CC Protocol | dimensionless |
| Priority Ceiling | $\text{Ceiling}(R) = \max P_i$ | CC Protocol | dimensionless |
| Slack Time | $\text{Slack} = D_i - R_i$ | Admission Control | ms |
| Preemption Cost | $C_{\text{eff}} = C_i + \text{Preemption\_Overhead}$ | Schedulability | ms |
| MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) | $\lambda^{-1}$ | Fault Tolerance | hours |
| Deadline Miss Ratio | $DMR = \frac{N_{\text{missed}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | QoS Metrics | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Special Topics in Detail

### 2.4.1 Energy-Aware Real-Time Scheduling

The energy consumption per task execution is governed by:

$$E = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$$

where $C$ is the switched capacitance, $V$ is the supply voltage, and $f$ is the clock frequency. **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)** exploits the relationship $f \propto V$ to reduce energy.

**Energy-Response Time Trade-off (DVS):**

$$E = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{eff}} \cdot f_k^2$$

The slowdown factor when reducing frequency:

$$s = \frac{f_{\max}}{f_{\text{scaled}}} \implies R' = s \cdot R$$

### 2.4.2 Fault-Tolerant Real-Time Systems

The **Recovery Block Scheme** uses multiple alternative algorithms:

$$\text{Primary} \rightarrow \text{Acceptance Test} \rightarrow \text{Backup}_1 \rightarrow \text{Backup}_2$$

Each level is wrapped with an acceptance test. The system proceeds until a backup passes the test.

**Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) Reliability:**

$$R_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$$

### 2.4.3 Real-Time Security – Bounded Cryptography

Security services (encryption, authentication) must complete within transaction deadlines. Key parameter is **encryption throughput**:

$$T_{\text{enc}} = \frac{\text{Block Size}}{\text{Throughput (bytes/s)}}$$

For RT systems: $T_{\text{enc}} + T_{\text{trans}} \le \text{Deadline}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Schedulability Test Under Priority Inheritance Blocking

**Problem:** Three real-time transactions access a shared database with the following parameters:

| Task | Priority | WCET ($C_i$) | Period ($T_i$) | Lock Holding | Resource |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | High (3) | 1 ms | 6 ms | $R_1, R_2$ | $R_1, R_2$ |
| $T_2$ | Medium (2) | 2 ms | 8 ms | $R_2$ | $R_2$ |
| $T_3$ | Low (1) | 3 ms | 12 ms | $R_1$ | $R_1$ |

Higher number = higher priority. Compute the worst-case blocking time $B_i$ for $T_1$ under **PCP** (Priority Ceiling Protocol).

### Step 1: Compute the Priority Ceiling of Each Resource

The priority ceiling of a resource $R$ is the highest priority of any task that may lock $R$.

For Resource $R_1$:
- $T_1$ locks $R_1$ → priority 3
- $T_3$ locks $R_1$ → priority 1
- Therefore: $\text{Ceiling}(R_1) = \max(3, 1) = 3$

For Resource $R_2$:
- $T_1$ locks $R_2$ → priority 3
- $T_2$ locks $R_2$ → priority 2
- Therefore: $\text{Ceiling}(R_2) = \max(3, 2) = 3$

### Step 2: Identify Possible Blocking Sources for $T_1$

Under PCP, a task can be blocked only by tasks holding resources whose ceilings are **greater than or equal to** the task's priority.

For $T_1$ (priority 3):
- $\text{Ceiling}(R_1) = 3 \ge 3$ → Block possible
- $\text{Ceiling}(R_2) = 3 \ge 3$ → Block possible

However, because both ceilings equal $T_1$'s priority, $T_1$ can suffer blocking only **once** (from one critical section of the lower-priority tasks).

### Step 3: Calculate Worst-Case Blocking Time $B_1$

The blocking time is bounded by the longest critical section of lower-priority tasks on the conflicting resources.

- Critical section of $T_2$ on $R_2$: estimated as $C_2^{\text{cs}} = 1$ ms
- Critical section of $T_3$ on $R_1$: estimated as $C_3^{\text{cs}} = 2$ ms

The blocking time for $T_1$ under PCP is the **maximum** of these single blocking durations:

$$B_1 = \max\big(C_2^{\text{cs}},\ C_3^{\text{cs}}\big) = \max(1,\ 2) = 2 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4: Worst-Case Response Time of $T_1$

Using the standard worst-case response time recurrence:

$$R_i = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

For $T_1$ (no higher-priority tasks), this simplifies to:

$$R_1 = C_1 + B_1 = 1 + 2 = 3 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 5: Verify Deadline Feasibility

Assuming deadline $D_1 = T_1 = 6$ ms:

$$R_1 = 3 \text{ ms} \le D_1 = 6 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

$T_1$ is **schedulable**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Optimistic Concurrency Control Validation Logic

**Problem:** Transaction $T_A$ (priority 4) and $T_B$ (priority 2) run concurrently under OPT-BC. Both reach the validation phase. Show the conflict resolution decision step-by-step.

**Read Sets and Write Sets:**
- $\text{RS}(T_A) = \{x, y\}$, $\text{WS}(T_A) = \{x\}$
- $\text{RS}(T_B) = \{y, z\}$, $\text{WS}(T_B) = \{y\}$

### Step 1: Detect Read-Write Conflict

A conflict exists between $T_B$ (validating) and $T_A$ (committed earlier) if:

$$\text{RS}(T_B) \cap \text{WS}(T_A) \neq \emptyset$$

Evaluating:

$$\text{RS}(T_B) \cap \text{WS}(T_A) = \{y, z\} \cap \{x\} = \emptyset$$

**No conflict on $T_A$'s writes.**

### Step 2: Detect Write-Read Conflict (Symmetric)

$$\text{WS}(T_B) \cap \text{RS}(T_A) = \{y\} \cap \{x, y\} = \{y\} \neq \emptyset$$

**Conflict detected on item $y$.**

### Step 3: Apply OPT-BC Priority Rule

Under OPT-BC, the validation of $T_B$ checks if it conflicts with a *higher-priority* committed transaction.

$$P(T_A) = 4 \quad > \quad P(T_B) = 2$$

Since $T_A$ has **higher priority** than $T_B$, $T_B$ must be **restarted** (sacrificed).

### Step 4: Outcome

- $T_A$ commits successfully.
- $T_B$ is **aborted and restarted** with a possibly new priority or after a wait.
- Result: Priority of high-priority transaction is preserved; system remains RT-correct.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Energy-Delay Optimization with DVFS

**Problem:** A real-time task $T$ has deadline $D = 50$ ms, nominal WCET at $f_{\max} = 1$ GHz is $C = 25$ ms. Compute the optimal frequency to minimize energy while meeting the deadline.

### Step 1: Identify Constraint

The task execution time at scaled frequency $f$ is:

$$R(f) = C \cdot \frac{f_{\max}}{f}$$

### Step 2: Apply Deadline Constraint

We require $R(f) \le D$, i.e.:

$$C \cdot \frac{f_{\max}}{f} \le D \implies f \ge \frac{C \cdot f_{\max}}{D}$$

Plugging in numerical values:

$$f \ge \frac{25 \cdot 1000}{50} = 500 \text{ MHz}$$

### Step 3: Compute Minimum Energy

Energy is proportional to $f^2$ (CMOS model). Minimum energy occurs at the **lowest** frequency satisfying the constraint:

$$f_{\text{opt}} = 500 \text{ MHz}$$

### Step 4: Energy Savings Calculation

Energy ratio (scaled vs. max):

$$\frac{E(f_{\text{opt}})}{E(f_{\max})} = \left(\frac{f_{\text{opt}}}{f_{\max}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{500}{1000}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{4}$$

Thus, **75% energy is saved** by scaling down.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Scaling frequency to the lowest feasible value gives 75% energy reduction while still meeting the deadline exactly.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix – CC Protocols

| Protocol | Deadlock-Free | Bounded Blocking | Priority Awareness | Overhead | KTU Preference |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Basic 2PL | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | Low | Never for RT |
| **PIP** | ✗ | Partial (chained) | ✓ | Low | Common |
| **PCP** | ✓ | One level max | ✓ | Medium | **Board-favorite** |
| **SRP** | ✓ | One level max | ✓ | Medium | Advanced topic |
| **OCC (Basic)** | N/A | None (restart) | ✗ | High | Avoid in RT |
| **OPT-BC** | N/A | None (restart) | ✓ | High | **Board-favorite** |
| **OPT-Sacrifice** | N/A | None (restart) | ✓ (best) | High | Best RT-CC |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram – Real-Time Database Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Real-Time Application Layer] --> B[Transaction Manager with Deadline Awareness]
    B --> C{Scheduler EDF or Priority}
    C -->|Accept| D[Concurrency Control Layer]
    C -->|Reject| E[Admission Rejected Task Dropped]
    D --> F[Pessimistic CC PCP PI]
    D --> G[Optimistic CC OPT BC]
    F --> H[Buffer Pool with Temporal Indexing]
    G --> H
    H --> I[(Persistent Storage Disk or SSD)]
    H --> J[Temporal Index Valid Time]
    H --> K[Temporal Index Transaction Time]
    H --> L[Temporal Index Bitemporal]
    I --> M[Recovery and Logging Module]
    M --> N[Fault Tolerance TMR Replication]
    style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style I fill:#277da1,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Temporal Data Model (Bitemporal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Fact e.g. Salary of Employee X]
    F --> VT[Valid Time When fact is true]
    F --> TT[Transaction Time When fact is stored]
    VT --> VT1[VT1 From To dates]
    TT --> TT1[TT1 From To dates]
    VT1 --> B[Bitemporal Table]
    TT1 --> B
    B --> Q1[Query as of Transaction Time]
    B --> Q2[Query when fact was Valid]
    B --> Q3[Query both Combined]
    style F fill:#577590,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#f3722c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Q3 fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram – Priority Inheritance Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TH as Task T_H High Priority
    participant TM as Task T_M Medium Priority
    participant TL as Task T_L Low Priority
    participant R as Resource R Lock

    TH->>R: Request Lock on R
    R-->>TH: Lock Busy held by T_L
    Note over TH,TL: T_H is BLOCKED

    TL->>R: Continues Execution
    Note over TL: T_L INHERITS priority of T_H

    TM-->>TL: Preemption Attempt
    Note over TM,TL: T_M CANNOT preempt T_L now higher priority

    TL->>R: Releases Lock
    Note over TL: T_L reverts to original priority

    R-->>TH: Lock Granted to T_H
    TH->>TH: Executes and Finishes
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram – OCC Three-Phase Workflow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Read
    Read --> Validation: Compute complete
    Validation --> Write: No conflict or low priority loser
    Validation --> Restart: Conflict with HIGHER priority tx
    Write --> [*]: Committed
    Restart --> Read: New Read Phase

    note right of Read: Local workspace\nno DB writes
    note right of Validation: Conflict check\nRS and WS overlap
    note right of Write: Atomic apply\nto database
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram – Fault-Tolerant RT System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input Sensor] --> B[Channel 1 Primary]
    A --> C[Channel 2 Backup 1]
    A --> D[Channel 3 Backup 2]
    B --> E[Voter Majority Logic TMR]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{Acceptance Test}
    F -->|Pass| G[Output to Actuator]
    F -->|Fail| H[Switch to Backup Channel]
    H --> E
    style E fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#f3722c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style G fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define temporal consistency in real-time databases. Differentiate between absolute and relative consistency.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Temporal Consistency (1 mark):** Temporal consistency is the property that data values are *logically correct AND* up-to-date with respect to time. Formally, a data item $x$ is temporally consistent if:

$$\text{Age}(x) = \text{CurrentTime} - \text{LastUpdateTime}(x) \le AVI(x)$$

**Absolute Consistency (1 mark):** Refers to a *single* data item — it must be updated within its **Absolute Validity Interval (AVI)**. Example: A radar track must be refreshed every 500 ms.

**Relative Consistency (1 mark):** Refers to a *set* of related data items — they must be updated together within a **Relative Validity Interval (RVI)**. Example: In an aircraft, position $(x, y, z)$ must be temporally coherent; otherwise, the plane's computed velocity is incorrect.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Q: What is priority inversion? Explain briefly how the Priority Inheritance Protocol solves it.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Priority Inversion (1.5 marks):** Priority inversion occurs when a higher-priority task is *indirectly preempted* by lower-priority tasks — i.e., a high-priority task waits for a lock held by a low-priority task, while medium-priority tasks preempt the low one. The high-priority task's wait becomes *unbounded*.

**Solution via PIP (1.5 marks):** The Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) solves *unbounded* inversion by **temporarily raising the priority** of the low-priority task holding the lock to that of the highest-priority waiter. This prevents medium-priority tasks from preempting and ensures the lock is released promptly. However, PIP does *not* prevent deadlock and allows *chained blocking*.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** ESE Module 4 questions carry **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Real-Time Database System. Discuss the role of each module.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│       Real-Time Application Layer            │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│     Transaction Manager (Deadline-Aware)     │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Scheduler  (Priority/EDF/Admission Ctrl)   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Concurrency Control (PIP / PCP / OCC)       │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Buffer Pool + Temporal Indexing             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Persistent Storage (Disk / SSD / NVM)      │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Recovery, Logging & Fault Tolerance Module  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**2. Module Roles (4 marks):**

- **Application Layer:** Issues transactions with deadlines and data requirements.
- **Transaction Manager:** Receives transactions, assigns priority (based on deadline or criticality), and decides admission.
- **Scheduler:** Schedules accepted transactions using EDF or fixed priority; guarantees timeliness.
- **Concurrency Control:** Manages concurrent access using PCP, PIP, or OCC; resolves conflicts respecting priorities.
- **Buffer Pool & Temporal Index:** Caches pages in memory and indexes by valid/transaction time for fast access.
- **Storage:** Physical persistence; supports I/O bandwidth for timely commits.
- **Recovery Module:** Handles faults, replays logs, supports replication for fault tolerance.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Compare the Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) and the Stack Resource Policy (SRP). Why is PCP preferred in hard real-time systems?** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | PCP | SRP |
|---|---|---|
| Resource Ceiling | Static (assigned at compile-time) | Static (per resource) |
| Preemption Ceiling | Not defined | Yes — system-wide preemption level |
| Deadlock-Free | ✓ | ✓ |
| Max Blocking | One critical section | One critical section |
| Multiprocessor Support | Limited | Better partitioning |
| Stacks Required | No | Yes (resource stack per task) |

**Why PCP is preferred in hard RT (3 marks):**

1. **Bounded and Predictable Blocking** — A high-priority task suffers at most *one* blocking delay, simplifying worst-case execution time (WCET) analysis.
2. **Deadlock Avoidance by Construction** — A task can lock a resource only if its priority exceeds the ceiling of *all* currently held resources — this rule is deadlock-free.
3. **Analytical Simplicity** — Worst-case response time can be computed analytically, satisfying hard-RT verification (required by standards like DO-178C, IEC 61508).
4. **Stack-Free Operation** — Unlike SRP, PCP does not require runtime stacks for resource tracking, reducing memory overhead.

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Describe the characteristics of temporal data. Explain bitemporal data with an example. List the differences between valid time and transaction time.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Characteristics (2 marks):**
- Time is an *integral attribute* of every data item.
- Data evolves over time; old states are retained as history.
- Queries are time-relative ("as of", "during", "before").
- Both logical and temporal consistency must be maintained.

**2. Bitemporal Example (3 marks):**

Consider an employee **John** whose salary is **50,000 INR from Jan 2020** but the HR system recorded this change only on **March 2021**.

| Employee | Salary | Valid Time (VT) | Transaction Time (TT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| John | 50,000 | [Jan 2020, ∞) | [Mar 2021, ∞) |

Now consider a *correction*: HR realizes it should be **55,000 INR**, and fixes it in **June 2024**.

| Employee | Salary | Valid Time (VT) | Transaction Time (TT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| John | 50,000 | [Jan 2020, June 2024) | [Mar 2021, June 2024) |
| John | 55,000 | [June 2024, ∞) | [June 2024, ∞) |

This **two-dimensional timeline** is bitemporal.

**3. Differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Valid Time | Transaction Time |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | When fact was *true in reality* | When fact was *stored in DB* |
| Mutable? | Can be backdated/corrected | Monotonically increasing |
| Control | Application | Database system |
| Example | "Salary was 50K from Jan 2020" | "We recorded this in Mar 2021" |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the Optimistic Concurrency Control (OCC) protocol for real-time databases. How does OPT-BC handle priority conflicts?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. OCC Phases (3.5 marks):**

OCC operates in three phases:
- **Read Phase:** Transaction reads data into a local workspace; writes are buffered locally — no DB updates occur.
- **Validation Phase:** At commit time, the transaction checks if its *Read Set* overlaps with the *Write Set* of any concurrently committed transaction.
- **Write Phase:** If validation succeeds, buffered writes are atomically applied; else transaction is **aborted and restarted**.

**2. OPT-BC Priority Handling (3.5 marks):**

In **OPT-BC (Backward Validation with Conflict priorities)**:
- When $T_i$ validates, it checks conflicts with *already committed* transactions $T_j$.
- If a conflict is found:
  - If $P(T_j) > P(T_i)$ → $T_i$ is **restarted** (the committed higher-priority transaction's work is preserved).
  - If $P(T_j) < P(T_i)$ → Optionally, $T_j$ can be **rolled back** to accommodate $T_i$.
- This policy ensures that **higher-priority transactions always succeed**, preserving temporal correctness for critical tasks.
- Drawback: Possible **starvation** of low-priority tasks; mitigated by aging mechanisms.

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Temporal Consistency with ACID Consistency** — Students write the ACID definition when asked for temporal consistency. **Marks Deducted: 2–3 out of 7.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Priority Inheritance "Transitive" property** — When asked about PIP, students forget to mention that inheritance propagates down a chain (Task H blocked by L, which is blocked by LL).
> 3. **Drawing 2PL instead of PCP** — A common mix-up. 2PL is *not* deadlock-free; PCP *is*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping the Bitemporal Diagram** — For 7-mark questions on temporal data, a *diagram is mandatory* (worth 2 marks alone).
> 5. **Misquoting EDF Utilization** — The bound is $U \le 1$, not $U \le n$. Many students confuse this.
> 6. **Not mentioning Real-Time Constraints in OCC** — KTU expects *RT-specific* OCC variants (OPT-BC, OPT-Sacrifice), not generic textbook OCC.

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MODULE 4 — HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY**

- **RTDB Definition** — DB where correctness depends on both logical results *and* timely delivery.
- **Temporal Data** — Time is a first-class attribute; characterized by valid time, transaction time, or both (bitemporal).
- **Temporal Consistency Invariant** — $\text{Age}(x) \le AVI$; relative consistency: $\vert t_i - t_j \vert \le RVI$.
- **Applications** — Telecom, ATC, SCADA, stock trading, healthcare, military, industrial automation.
- **Concurrency Control Protocols:**
  - **PIP** — Priority inheritance solves unbounded inversion, but allows deadlock.
  - **PCP** — Priority ceilings ensure deadlock-freedom + single-level blocking (preferred for hard RT).
  - **SRP** — Adds preemption levels; supports multiprocessors better.
  - **OCC** — Speculative; uses OPT-BC / OPT-Sacrifice for RT-awareness.
- **Priority Inversion Problem** — High-priority task blocked by low-priority holder; solved by PIP (bounded) and PCP (deadlock-free).
- **OPT-BC Rule** — When conflict is found in validation, lower-priority transaction is restarted.
- **Commercial RTDBs** — eXtremeDB, Oracle TimesTen, Velocidata, IBM DB2 (with RT extensions), Polyhedra.
- **Special Topics:**
  - **Energy-Aware RT** — DVFS: $f \ge C \cdot f_{\max} / D$; energy scales as $f^2$.
  - **Fault Tolerance** — TMR reliability: $R_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$.
  - **RT Security** — Encryption time + transmission time $\le$ deadline.
- **EDF Optimality** — Utilization bound $U = \sum C_i / T_i \le 1$.
- **Worst-Case Response Time** — $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i$ where $I_i$ is interference from higher-priority tasks.
- **Stack Resource Policy (SRP)** — Preemption level concept; allows group blocking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks one question on **Concurrency Control (PIP/PCP/OCC)** and one on **Temporal Data Characteristics**. Memorize the priority inheritance *chain rule* and the OPT-BC conflict resolution algorithm — these are the most-asked sub-topics in Module 4.
